footnotes.html: (∗) This presentation and the following one, written in 1968 and 1969, take up and complete the oral presentations of A. GROTHENDIECK (spring 1964). The editor, who did not attend the oral seminar, was partially inspired by the notes of A. GROTHENDIECK.

blocks.html: Introduction

In this presentation the formalism of cohomology with proper support is developed. The questions of variance were handled quite carefully, although they were only made plausible in the oral seminar. This explains the length of the presentation, paragraphs 1 to 4 of which are devoted to the categories and the general topology. The reader is very strongly recommended to read only paragraphs 5 and 6, where the geometric substance of the presentation is concentrated (§ 5: construction and variance of cohomology with proper support, theorems of change of base, of finitude, of Tor-finite dimension and of comparison; § 6: theory of the morphism drawn for a quasi-finite and flat morphism).

In paragraphs 1 and 2, we "recall" some results on the derived categories. § 1 n°1 is devoted to the formalism of signs. In § 3, we deal with the problem of putting together two variance formalisms. In § 4, we introduce flat resolutions and we study the special properties of flaccid resolutions of GODEMENT.

In this presentation, the functors \(R^q f_!\), direct images higher than proper supports, and the functor \(Rf_!\) which gives rise to them, are only defined for \(f\) a compactifiable morphism (3.2.1). In the appendix, written by B. SAINT-DONAT, we show how to extend the definition to separated morphisms of finite type of quasi-compact quasi-separate goal.
§ 5 n°5 (cohomology of a symmetric product) will no longer be used in this seminar. It will be used in SGA 5 to refine the rationality theorem of L functions.

§ 6 n°3 (trace theory for continuous coefficients) will only be used in presentation XVIII in the relatively easy case of smooth groups; the reader interested in Poincaré's duality theorem (global duality), and ready to admit a transcendent argument, can even dispense completely with reading this § 6 no. 3, as well as most of § 1 of XVIII.
0. Terminological preliminaries.

0.1. The sign \(=\) placed between two groups of symbols designating objects of a category will sometimes mean (by abuse of notations) that these objects are canonically isomorphic. The category and the canonical isomorphism must in principle have been defined beforehand. In a diagram, the sign \(=\) will then designate the isomorphism itself.

0.2.0. Let \(f : S \longrightarrow S'\) be a morphism of sites (IV 4.9.3). If \(U\) and \(U'\) are objects of \(S\) and \(S'\), a \(f\)-morphism from \(U\) into \(U'\) will by definition be a morphism from \(U\) in \(f^*U'\). For sites with sparse diagrams, we find the usual notion.
0.2.1. If \(\mathfrak{F}\) and \(\mathfrak{F}'\) are sheaves on \(S\) and \(S'\), a \(f\)-morphism from \(\mathfrak{F}\) into \(\mathfrak{F}'\) will be indifferently

1. (i) a morphism of \(f^*\mathfrak{F}'\) into \(\mathfrak{F}\)
  2. (ii) a morphism of \(\mathfrak{F}'\) into \(f_*\mathfrak{F}\)
  3. (iii) a function which, to each \(f\)-morphism \(\varphi\) of an object \(U\) of \(S\) in an object \(U'\) of \(S'\) associates a function of \(\mathfrak{F}'(U')\) in \(\mathfrak{F}(U)\), in a manner compatible with the composition of \(\varphi\) with an arrow of \(S\) or \(S'\) (∗).
We see in (i), (ii) and (iii) that the overall bundles form a fibered and cofibered category on the category of sites.

Itou for bundles of modules on the left on ringed sites. Itou for spread beams on diagrams; this is not immediately a special case of the above, because the sites actually form a 2-category and "slack site of \(X\)" is just a pseudo-functor in \(X\) (VII 1.4).

The previous definition makes bundles of sets (resp. ...) on variable sites a fibered category on that of the sites, having for fiber categories the categories opposite to the usual categories of bundles of sets (resp. ...).

0.3. Let \(I\) be a finite set, \(\varepsilon\) a function of \(I\) with values in \(\{+1, -1\}\), \((\mathcal{A}_i)_{i \in I}\) a family of additive categories graded by translation functors \(T_i\) (CD I 1.10) and \(F\) a multiadditive multifunctor of the categories \(\mathcal{A}_i\) in an additive category \(\mathcal{A}\) graded by the translation functor \(T\). We assume \(F\) covariant (resp. contravariant) in \(i\) such that \(\varepsilon_i = 1\) (resp. \(\varepsilon_i = -1\)). Modifying and completing ([CD] I 1.11), we will say that \(F\) is a graded functor if we have given ourselves a family \((\varphi_i)_{i \in I}\) of isomorphisms of functors between \(T \circ F\) and \(F \circ T_i^{\varepsilon_i}\), such that the following diagrams are anticommutative, for \(i \neq j\) in \(I\),

\[\xymatrix{ F \circ (T_i^{\varepsilon_i}, T_j^{\varepsilon_j}) \ar[rr]^-{\varphi_j * T_i^{\varepsilon_i}} \ar[d]_-{\varphi_i * T_j^{\varepsilon_j}} & & T \circ F \circ T_i^{\varepsilon_i} \ar[d]^-{T * \varphi_i} \\ T \circ F \circ T_j^{\varepsilon_j} \ar[rr]_-{T * \varphi_j} & & T \circ T \circ F }\]
We leave it to the reader to define the compound of two graded functors and to verify that it is still a graded functor.

0.4. Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an additive category and \(I\) be a finite set. For \(i \in I\), we denote by \(1_i\) the \(i\)-th basis vector of \(\mathbb{Z}^I\). A \(I\)-uple complex (resp. a naive \(I\)-uple complex) of \(\mathcal{A}\) consists of

1. (a) a family \((K^k)_{k \in \mathbb{Z}^I}\) of objects of \(\mathcal{A}\);
  2. (b) for each \(i \in I\) and each \(k \in \mathbb{Z}^I\), an arrow \(d_i^k : K^k \longrightarrow K^{k+1_i}\), these arrows verifying \(d_i^{k+1_i} d_i^k = 0\), and, for \(i \neq j\), \(d_i^{k+1_j} d_j^k + d_j^{k+1_i} d_i^k = 0\) (resp. \(d_i^{k+1_j} d_j^k = d_j^{k+1_i} d_i^k\)).

0.5. We will call pro-object of a category \(C\) a functor of \(C\) in (Ens) which is a filtering inductive limit (according to a small filtering category) of representable functors (cf. I 8.10). Every pro-object is an inductive limit according to a small filtering ordered set of representable functors. If \(X_i\) is a projective system of objects of \(C\), indexed by a small filtering category, we designate by "\(\varprojlim\)" \(X_i\) the pro-object \(\varinjlim h^{X_i}\).
0.6. The reader will dualize 0.5 in the case of ind-objects (Cf. I 8.2).
0.7. In accordance with the new terminology, we call schema what was formerly called pre-schema, and we call separate schema what was formerly called schema.

0.8. The category of ringed schemes is the category whose objects are the schemes whose spread site is provided with a bundle of rings, an arrow of \((S, \mathcal{A})\) in \((T, \mathcal{B})\) being a pair formed from a morphism of schemes \(f\) of \(S\) in \(T\), and a homomorphism \(\omega\) of ring bundles of \(f^*\mathcal{B}\) in \(\mathcal{A}\). The schema morphism \(f\) is said to be induced by \((f, \omega)\).
0.9. If \(X\) is a schema on \(Y\), we denote by \((X/Y)^n\) the fiber product \(n\)-uple of \(X\) on \(Y\).

0.10. Let \(S\) be a schema. We will call large slack site (resp. large fppf site, resp. large fpqc site) of \(S\) the site \(\mathrm{Sch}_{/S}\), equipped with the slack topology (resp. fppf, resp. fpqc) (SGA 3 IV 6.3). Please note that this is not a \(U\)-site (\(U\) being the fixed universe, \(\mathrm{Sch}_{/S}\) consisting of the schematics \(\in U\)).

We call small fpqc site (resp. small fppf site) of \(S\) the site of the flat diagrams on \(S\) (resp. finished presentation flats) equipped with the fpqc (resp. fppf) topology. When it is necessary to avoid confusion, we will call small spread site of \(S\) the site \(S_{\mathrm{et}}\) (VII 1.2).

0.11. In many cases, the editor has allowed himself to speak of commutative diagrams of site morphisms where it would have been necessary to speak of essentially commutative diagrams, i.e. commutative up to isomorphism (cf. IV 3.2.2). The reader will be able to verify that the arguments we give also apply to the general situation.
0.12. The terminology "coherent scheme" for "quasi-compact quasi-separate scheme" was surreptitiously introduced in places by A. Grothendieck.

0.13. A torsion beam \(F\) on a diagram \(S\) will be said to be prime with the residual characteristics of \(S\) if it is the inductive limit of its sub-sheaves canceled by invertible integers \(n\) on \(S\).

footnotes.html: (∗) These terminologies are not compatible with the identification of objects from \(S\) to the associated beams.

blocks.html: 1. Derived categories.
1.1. Exact functors (the sign rules).
1.1.1. For the fundamental theorems relating to derived categories, I refer to VERDIER [CD] and [2]. In this issue, special attention has been paid to problems of signs.
Recall that if \(f : X \longrightarrow Y\) is a morphism of complexes (in an additive category, implied subsequently), its cone \(C(f)\) is defined by
When \(X = 0\) (resp. \(Y = 0\)), we have \(C(f) = Y\) (resp. \(C(f) = X[1]\)), so that the commutative diagram
\[\xymatrix{ 0 \ar@{=}[r] \ar[d] & 0 \ar[r] \ar[d] & X \ar@{=}[r] \ar[d]^{f} & X \ar[d] \\ X \ar[r]_{f} & Y \ar@{=}[r] & Y \ar[r] & 0 }\]
defines a “triangle”
\[X \xrightarrow{\;f\;} Y \longrightarrow C(f) \xrightarrow{\;j\;} X[1] \, .\]

In the category of complexes up to homotopy, we call distinguished a triangle isomorphic to a triangle of this type, and antidistinguished a triangle which becomes distinguished when we change the sign of its arrows. We verify that the triangle defined by an exact sequence of complexes split into each degree ([CD] p. 10, cf. the proof of 1.1.5.4) is antidistinguished and that, up to isomorphism, any antidistinguished triangle is obtained thus.

∗ 1.1.2. Sign calculations are most easily carried out using the following "formal rules". We write an element of \(C(f)^n\) in the form \(1 \otimes x + y\) (\(x \in X^{n+1}\), \(y \in Y^n\)), \(1\) being a "cell" of dimension \(1\), and we set \(d(1) \otimes x = f(x)\). We therefore have
\[d(1 \otimes x + y) = d1 \otimes x - 1 \otimes dx + dy = -1 \otimes dx + f(x) + dy. \quad ∗\]

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.1.3

(i) A graded functor (0.3) of a triangulated category \(\mathcal{A}\) in a triangulated category \(\mathcal{B}\) is said to be exact if it transforms distinguished triangles into distinguished triangles.

(ii) A contravariant graded functor of a triangulated category \(\mathcal{A}\) in a triangulated category \(\mathcal{B}\) is said to be exact if for every triangle distinguished \((X,Y,Z,u,v,w)\) from \(\mathcal{A}\), the triangle \((F(Z), F(Y), F(X), F(v), F(u), TF(w))\) is distinguished (in this definition, we identify \(F X\) to \(T F T X\) thanks to the graduation of \(F\)).

(iii) Let \((\mathcal{A}_i)_{i \in I}\) be a finite family of triangulated categories, \(\varepsilon\) be a function of \(I\) in \(\{+1, -1\}\) and \(\mathcal{A}\) be a triangulated category. A graded functor \(F\) of the product of the \(\mathcal{A}_i\) in \(\mathcal{A}\), covariant in the \(i\) such that \(\varepsilon(i) = 1\) and contravariant in the \(i\) such that \(\varepsilon(i) = -1\) is said exact if whatever \(i \in I\), the functors of \(\mathcal{A}_i\) in \(\mathcal{A}\) deduced from \(F\) by setting all the variables, except the \(i\)-th, are exact functors.
A compound of exact functors is still an exact functor.
1.1.4. If \((K^{n_1 \ldots n_p}, d_1, \ldots, d_p)\) is a multiple complex (0.4), the associated simple complex is defined by
\[(1.1.4.0)\qquad K^n = \prod_{\sum n_i = n} K^{(n_i)} \, , \qquad d = \sum d_i \, .\]
When, exceptionally, it is defined by a sum rather than by a product, this fact will be explicitly indicated.
Let \(I\) be a finite set. We propose, according to CARTAN-EILENBERG and J.P. SERRE, to explain how to any naive \(I\)-uple \(K\) complex is canonically associated with a \(I\)-uple complex (0.4).
For any total order \(<\) on \(I\), let \(K(<)\) the following complex \(I\)-tuple:
\[(1.1.4.1)\qquad \begin{cases} K(<)^k = K^k \\[4pt] d(<)_i^k = (-1)^{\sum_{j<i} k_j}\, d_i^k \, . \end{cases}\]
If \(<_1\) and \(<_2\) are two total orders on \(I\) the canonical isomorphism \(\tau\) between \(K(<_1)\) and \(K(<_2)\) is by definition the given one by
\[(1.1.4.2)\qquad \tau^k = (-1)^{\varepsilon(<_1, <_2)} \, , \quad \text{où } \varepsilon(<_1, <_2) = \sum_{\substack{i <_1 j \\ i >_2 j}} k_i k_j \, .\]

1.1.4.2.1. We verify that these canonical isomorphisms establish a transitive system of isomorphisms between the \(K(<)\) for \(<\) total order on \(I\); these \(K(<)\) therefore form a unique \(I\)-uple complex with unique isomorphism, which we will designate in this issue by the notation \(K(*)\) and which we call the complex \(I\)-uple associated with the naive complex \(I\)-uple \(K\). Please note that \(K(*)\) is not canonically isomorphic to \(K\): only the choice of a total order on \(I\) allows these two objects to be identified.
If \(K\) is a naive \(I\)-fold complex, and if \(i \in I\), we denote by \(K[1_i]\) the following naive complex, of differentials denoted \(d_j[1_i]\):
\[(1.1.4.3)\qquad \begin{cases} K[1_i]^k = K^{k+1_i} \\[4pt] d_j[1_i]^k = d_j^{k+1_i} & \text{si } i \neq j \\[4pt] d_i[1_i]^k = -d_i^{k+1_i} \, . \end{cases}\]
If \(K\) is a \(I\)-ple complex, we set
\[(1.1.4.4)\qquad \begin{cases} K[1_i]^k = K^{k+1_i} \\[4pt] d_j[1_i]^k = -d_j^{k+1_i} \, . \end{cases}\]

If \(K\) is a naive complex \(I\)-tuple and \(<\) a total order on \(I\), so that \(K(*)\) is given by (1.1.4.1), we define an isomorphism \(\sigma\) between \(K[1_i](*)\) and \(K(*)[1_i]\) by the formula
1.1.4.6. We verify that this isomorphism does not depend on the choice of the total order \(<\); it therefore defines an isomorphism, called canonical, between \(K[1_i](*)\) and \(K(*)[1_i]\).
1.1.4.7. Whatever \(i,j\) in \(I\), if \(K\) is a complex or a naive complex \(I\)-ple, we clearly identify \(K[1_i][1_j]\) and \(K[1_j][1_i]\).
For \(i \neq j\) in \(I\), the following diagram of canonical isomorphisms of type 1.1.4.6 or 1.1.4.7 is then anticommutative:

1.1.5. Let \(F : \mathcal{A} \longrightarrow \mathcal{B}\) be an additive contravariant functor of additive categories. We define the extension of \(F\) to complexes as follows: \(F : C(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow C(\mathcal{B})\). If \(K\) is a complex of \(\mathcal{A}\), we set
The canonical isomorphism \(\sigma\) between \(F(K)[1]\) and \(F(K[-1])\) is defined by

If \(F : \mathcal{A} \longrightarrow \mathcal{B}\) and \(G : \mathcal{B} \longrightarrow \mathcal{C}\) are two additive contravariant functors, we define the canonical isomorphism \(\rho\) between \(G \circ F(K)\) and \(G(F(K))\) by

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.1.5.4

(i) For any additive contravariant functor \(F : \mathcal{A} \longrightarrow \mathcal{B}\), formulas 1.1.5.1, 1.1.5.2 define an exact contravariant functor of \(K(\mathcal{A})\) in \(K(\mathcal{B})\).

(ii) For any pair \((G,F)\) of composable additive contravariant functors, the formula (1.1.5.3) defines an isomorphism of graded functors between the extension to the complexes of \(GF\) and the compound of the extensions to the complexes of \(G\) and \(F\).
Proof. A morphism of degree \(i\): \(f : K \longrightarrow L\) is your choice

1. (a) a morphism of \(K\) into \(L[i]\)
  2. (b) a morphism of \(K[-i]\) into \(L\)
  3. (c) a family \(f^n : K^n \longrightarrow L^{n+i}\) which commutes or anticommutes to differentials according to the parity of \(i\).
If \(f : K \longrightarrow L[i]\) is a morphism of degree \(i\), then \(F(f) : F(L)[-i] \longrightarrow K\) is still a morphism of degree \(i\); for \(i=1\), we have

If \(0 \longrightarrow K \xrightarrow{u} L \xrightarrow{v} M \longrightarrow 0\) is an exact sequence of complexes split degree by degree, the choice of a split allows us to write the differential of \(L\) in the form
where \(f\) is a degree one morphism of \(M\) into \(K\). Likewise, the dual exact sequence
\[0 \longrightarrow F(M) \longrightarrow F(L) \longrightarrow F(K) \longrightarrow 0\]

defines a degree one morphism \(f^*\) from \(F(K)\) into \(F(L)\). We have \(f^* = F(f)\). It follows that the image by \(F\) of a triangle \((K,L,M,u,v,f)\) defined by a short exact sequence of complexes is still a triangle of the same type, according to 1.1.1, this proves (i).

Under hypotheses (ii), for \(f\) of degree one, we have \(G(F(f))^n = (-1)^{n+1}(-1)^{-n} G \circ F(f)^n = -G \circ F(f)^n\); in particular, the graduation isomorphism \(G(F(K[1])) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} G(F(K))[1]\) is the isomorphism with components \(-1\).
The diagram
\[\xymatrix{ G \circ F(K)[1] \ar@{=}[d] & G(F(K))[1] \ar[l]_-{\rho[1]} \ar[d] \\ G \circ F(K[1]) & G(F(K[1])) \ar[l]^-{\rho} }\]
is therefore commutative, which proves (ii).

1.1.6. Either \(s = +\), or \(s = -\); are \(I\) a finite set, \(\varepsilon : I \longrightarrow \{+,-\}\) a function, \((\mathcal{A}_i)_{i \in I}\) and \(\mathcal{A}\) additive categories, and \(F\) a multiadditive multifunctor of the categories \(\mathcal{A}_i\) in \(\mathcal{A}\), covarying in the arguments of index \(i\) such as \(\varepsilon(i) = +\) and contravariant in the others. We propose to define the extension \(K^s(F)\) (or simply \(F\)) of \(F\) to complexes as an exact multifunction of the categories \(K^{s\varepsilon(i)}(\mathcal{A}_i)\) (\(s\varepsilon(i) = \pm\), according to the rule of signs) in \(K^s(\mathcal{A})\) presenting the same variance as \(F\) (∗). We will then explain in what sense this construction is compatible with the composition of functors. Other degree conventions are possible, and will be used; this point is independent of the questions of sign considered here.
1.1.6.1. If \(F\) is contravariant to a variable, we define \(K^s(F)\) by the formulas (1.1.5).

1.1.6.2. If \(F\) is covariant, we define the functor \(K^s(F)\) by passing to the quotient from the compound of the obvious functor of the product of the categories \(C^s(\mathcal{A}_i)\) in the category of complex \(I\)-naive tuples, of the functor (1.1.4) from the category of naive complex \(I\)-tuples into that of complex \(I\)-tuples (1.1.4.2.1), and of the associated simple complex functor (1.1.4.0):
Let complexes be \(K_j \in K^s(\mathcal{A}_j)\), and \(i \in I\); we set \(K_j[1_i] = K_j\) for \(j \neq i\), and \(K_i[1_i] = K_i[1]\). We then have obvious isomorphisms:
and
hence, via (1.1.4.5), a canonical isomorphism \(\sigma\) between \(K^s(F)(K_j[1_i])\) and \(K^s(F)(K_j)[1]\). It follows from 1.1.4.7 that these isomorphisms define a graduation of \(K^s(F)\).

1.1.6.3. In the general case, \(F\) is the product of canonical contravariant functors \(\mathcal{A}_i \longrightarrow \mathcal{A}_i^o\) (for \(\varepsilon(i) = -\)) and a covariant functor \(F^+\); we define the functor \(K^s(F)\) by composition from 1.1.6.1 and 1.1.6.2.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1.1.7

Under the previous assumptions, the functor \(K^s(F)\) is an exact multiadditive functor.

Just check 1.1.7 under the assumptions of 1.1.6.1 (where the proposition is 1.1.5.4 (i)) or 1.1.6.2. To verify that the functors deduced from \(K^s(F)\) by fixing all the variables, except the \(i\)-th, are exact, we can explain \(K^s(F)\) in terms of an order on \(I\) for which \(i\) is the smallest element; a typical distinguished triangle (1.1.1.1) is then transformed into a typical distinguished triangle.

1.1.8. Let \(I\) and \(J\) be two finite sets, \(\psi : I \longrightarrow J\) a surjective map, \(\varepsilon_I : I \longrightarrow \{+,-\}\) and \(\varepsilon_J : J \longrightarrow \{+,-\}\) functions, and multiadditive multifunctors, covariant in the arguments for which \(\varepsilon = +\) and contravariant in the others:
\[G_j : \prod_{\psi(i)=j} \mathcal{A}_i \longrightarrow \mathcal{B}_j \qquad (j \in J)\] \[F : \prod_{j \in J} \mathcal{B}_j \longrightarrow \mathcal{C}\]

Let's choose total orders on \(I\) and \(J\), such that \(\psi\) is an increasing application. We then define the canonical isomorphism \(\rho\) between the extension to the complexes of \(F \circ (G_j)\) and the compound of the extensions to the complexes of \(F\) and of \(G_j\) by the formula
\[(1.1.8.1)\qquad \rho^k = \prod_i \eta_i^{k_i} \; : \; \text{automorphisme de } F \circ (G_j)\big(K_i^{k_i}\big)^{\varepsilon(i)\,\varepsilon(\psi(i))}\]
with \(\eta_i = -1\) if \(\varepsilon_I(i) = \varepsilon_J(\psi(i)) = -\), \(\eta_i = 1\) otherwise.
This formula generalizes (1.1.5.3), we verify that \(\rho\) does not depend on the orders chosen on \(I\) and \(J\), and is an isomorphism of graded functors (cf. 1.1.5.4 (ii)).
These isomorphisms \(\rho\) satisfy the obvious cocycle condition for a triple compound.

1.1.9. Let \(I\) be a finite set, \(i\) and \(j\) two distinct elements of \(I\), \(I' = I \smallsetminus \{i,j\}\), \((\mathcal{A}_i)_{i \in I}\) and \(\mathcal{A}\) additive categories and \(F : (\mathcal{A}_i)_{i \in I} \longrightarrow \mathcal{A}\) and \(G : (\mathcal{A}_i)_{i \in I'} \longrightarrow \mathcal{A}\) multiadditive multifunctors. We assume that \(\mathcal{A}_i = \mathcal{A}_j\) and that \(F\) is contravariant in the \(i\)-th variable and covariant in the \(j\)-th. For \(X, Y \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{A}_i\), we will designate by \(F_{X,Y}\) the multifunctor of \((\mathcal{A}_i)_{i \in I'}\) in \(\mathcal{A}\) obtained by fixing the \(i\)-th and \(j\)-th arguments:
\[F_{X,Y}(\ldots) = F(\ldots X, \ldots, Y, \ldots) \, .\]
We call contraction morphism \(c : F \longrightarrow G\) the data for all \(X \in \mathcal{A}_i\) of a morphism of functors
\[c_X : F_{X,X} \longrightarrow G \, ,\]
such that for any arrow \(f: X \longrightarrow Y\) of \(\mathcal{A}_i\), the diagram
\[\xymatrix{ F_{Y,X} \ar[r] \ar[d] & F_{X,X} \ar[d]^{c_X} \\ F_{Y,Y} \ar[r]_{c_Y} & G }\]
be commutative.
The 1.1.6 conventions are motivated by

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.1.9.1

Let \(c : F \longrightarrow G\) be a contraction morphism and \((K_\ell)_{\ell \in I}\) be complexes in the \(\mathcal{A}_\ell\), with \(K_i = K_j\). Let \(<\) be a total order on \(I\) for which \(i\) is the predecessor of \(j\). Or, with the notations of 1.1.6, for \(k \in \mathbb{Z}\),

\[c^k : K^s(F)(K_\ell)^k \longrightarrow K^s(G)(K_\ell)^k\]

the morphism whose restriction to \(F(\ldots K_i^{k_i}\, K_j^{k_j} \ldots)\) is worth \(0\) if \(k_i \neq k_j\), and is worth \(c_{K_i^{k_i}}\) if \(k_i = k_j\). Then, \((c^k)_{k \in \mathbb{Z}}\) is a complex morphism.
We leave it to the reader to verify this lemma, and the fact that the contraction morphism \((c^k)\) does not depend on the order chosen on \(I\).
The standard example of contraction morphism is that of the law of composition
\[\operatorname{Hom}(Y,Z) \otimes \operatorname{Hom}(X,Y) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}(X,Z)\]

blocks.title: Examples
blocks.html:
Examples 1.1.10

(i) If we apply the definitions of 1.1.6 to the functor tensor product, we find the usual notion of tensor product of two complexes, the differential being given by

\[d(x \otimes y) = dx \otimes y + (-1)^{\deg x} x \otimes dy \, .\]

(ii) If we apply the definitions of 1.1.6 to the functor \(\operatorname{Hom}(X,Y)\), considering \(Y\) as the first variable and \(X\) as the second variable, a convention that we will always follow, this convention makes it possible to identify, for two complexes \(X,Y\), the \(k\)-th component of the complex \(\operatorname{Hom}(X,Y)\) defined in 1.1.6 to \(\prod_n \operatorname{Hom}(X^n, Y^{n+k})\), and we find the usual differential; for \(f \in \operatorname{Hom}(X^n, Y^{n+k})\), we have

\[df = d \circ f - (-1)^k f \circ d \, .\]

(iii) The preceding conventions have the virtue that the following systems of arrows are morphisms of complexes, without having to disrupt them by any sign (1.1.9):

\[\operatorname{Hom}^\bullet(L,M) \otimes \operatorname{Hom}^\bullet(K,L) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}^\bullet(K,M) : f \otimes g \mapsto f \circ g\]

\[\operatorname{Hom}^\bullet(L,M) \otimes L \longrightarrow M : f \otimes x \mapsto f(x) \, .\]

∗ 1.1.10.1. The convention (1.1.4.5)-(1.1.6.2) is justified as follows, for \(F = \otimes\): an element of \(((K \otimes L)[1])^n\) is written in the form (1.1.2) (remembering that \((K \otimes L)[1] = C(K \otimes L \longrightarrow 0)\) (1.1.1.1)) \(\sum 1 \otimes (x^p \otimes y^q)\), an element of \((K[1] \otimes L)^n\) in the form \(\sum (1 \otimes x^p) \otimes y^q\) and an element of \((K \otimes L[1])^n\) in the form \(\sum x^p \otimes (1 \otimes y^q)\); the sign appears when we interchange \(x^p\) and \(1\). For \(F = \text{dual}\), the convention (1.1.5.2) is justified as follows: an element of \(K^*[1]\) is written \(1 \otimes \omega\); its value on an element \(1' \otimes x\) of \(K[-1]\) (\(1'\) cell of dimension \(-1\)) is \(\langle 1 \otimes \omega, 1' \otimes x \rangle = (-1)^{\deg \omega} \langle 1, 1' \rangle \cdot \langle \omega, x \rangle = (-1)^{\deg \omega} \langle \omega, x \rangle\); this is the adopted convention. ∗

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 1.1.11

"Recall" that a subadditive category is a category whose sets of arrows \(\operatorname{Hom}(X,Y)\) are provided with laws of abelian groups, the composition of the morphisms being biadditive. An additive functor \(F\) between subadditive categories is a functor that satisfies the formula

\[F(f + g) = F(f) + F(g)\]

for \(f,g \in \operatorname{Hom}(X,Y)\). The additive functors of a subadditive category \(\mathcal{A}\) in another \(\mathcal{B}\) form a category, denoted \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{\mathrm{add}}(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B})\).
Let \((D)\) be the unique subadditive category, set of objects indexed by \(\mathbb{Z}\), \((X^n)_{n \in \mathbb{Z}}\), with

\[(1.1.11.1)\qquad \begin{cases} \operatorname{Hom}(X^n, X^n) = \mathbb{Z} \, , \text{ engendré par l'identité} \\[4pt] \operatorname{Hom}(X^n, X^{n+1}) = \mathbb{Z} \, , \text{ engendré par une flèche notée } d_n \\[4pt] \operatorname{Hom}(X^i, X^j) = 0 \text{ si } j - i \neq 0 \text{ ou } 1 \, . \end{cases}\]
Let \(D\) be the complex of \((D)\) defined by \(D^n = X^n\) and \(d^n = d_n\).
For any subadditive category \(\mathcal{A}\), the functor \(G \mapsto G(D)\) of \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{\mathrm{add}}((D), \mathcal{A})\) in \(C(\mathcal{A})\) is an isomorphism.
Construction 1.1.6 extends to the case of a multiadditive functor of subadditive categories \(\mathcal{A}_i\) in an additive category \(\mathcal{A}\).
Either
\[F^\bullet = (F^n, d^n)_{n \in \mathbb{Z}}\]

a complex of multiadditive functors of additive categories \(\mathcal{A}_i\) in an additive category \(\mathcal{A}\). We can consider \(F^\bullet\) as a functor of \((D)\) in a category of multifunctors, or even as a multiadditive functor
\[F_1 : \textstyle\prod \mathcal{A}_i \times (D) \longrightarrow \mathcal{A} \, .\]
We define the extension of \(F\) to complexes by the formula
\[(1.1.11.2)\qquad F^\bullet(K_1 \ldots K_n) = F_1(K_1 \ldots K_n, D) \, .\]
1.1.12. Let \(\mathcal{A}\) and \(\mathcal{B}\) be two additive categories and
\[F^\bullet = (F^n, d^n)_{n \in \mathbb{Z}}\]

a complex of additive functors from \(\mathcal{A}\) in \(\mathcal{B}\). Suppose that the functors \(F^i\) have right adjoints \(F_i\); if \({}^t d^i : F_{i+1} \longrightarrow F_i\) is the transpose of \(d^i\), let \(F_\bullet\) be the complex of additive functors of \(\mathcal{B}\) in \(\mathcal{A}\), of components \(F_i\), and of differential operator
\[(d_\bullet)^i = (-1)^{i+1}\, {}^t d^{-i-1} : F_{-i} \longrightarrow F_{-i-1}\]
Let \({}^t\) be the contravariant functor of \((D)\) in \((D)\) given by
\[{}^t X^n = X^{-n} \quad \text{et} \quad {}^t d^n = d^{-n-1} \, .\]
The following diagram is commutative up to canonical isomorphism

\[(1.1.12.1)\qquad \xymatrix@C=3em{ \mathcal{A} \times (D) \times \mathcal{B} \ar[r]^-{(F^n(A), \mathrm{Id})} \ar[d]_-{(\mathrm{Id}, {}^t, \mathrm{Id})} & \mathcal{B} \times \mathcal{B} \ar[r]^-{\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{B}}} & (\mathrm{Ab}) \ar@{=}[d] \\ \mathcal{A} \times (D) \times \mathcal{B} \ar[r]_-{(\mathrm{Id}, (F_\bullet)^n(B))} & \mathcal{A} \times \mathcal{A} \ar[r]_-{\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{A}}} & (\mathrm{Ab}) }\]
This expresses the addition formula
\[(1.1.12.2)\qquad \operatorname{Hom}(F^n(A), B) \; \overset{\sim}{\longleftrightarrow} \; \operatorname{Hom}(A, F_n(B)) \, .\]
The general conventions 1.1.3 therefore provide an isomorphism
\[(1.1.12.3)\qquad \operatorname{Hom}^\bullet(F^\bullet(K), L) \; \overset{\sim}{\longleftrightarrow} \; \operatorname{Hom}^\bullet(K, F_\bullet(L)) \, .\]

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.1.13

If \(K\) is a complex of an abelian category \(\mathcal{A}\), we call right truncated (resp. left) of \(K\) in dimension \(n\), and we denote by \(\tau_{\leqslant n}(K)\) (resp. \(\tau_{\geqslant n}(K)\)) the following complexes

\[\tau_{\leqslant n}(K) : \; \ldots K^{n-2} \longrightarrow K^{n-1} \longrightarrow \operatorname{Ker}(d^n) \longrightarrow 0\]

\[\tau_{\geqslant n}(K) : \; 0 \longrightarrow \operatorname{coker}(d^{n-1}) \longrightarrow K^{n+1} \longrightarrow K^{n+2} \ldots \, .\]

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1.1.14

(i) \(H^i(\tau_{\leqslant n}(K)) = 0\) for \(i > n\) and \(H^i(\tau_{\geqslant n}(K)) = 0\) for \(i < n\).

(ii) The canonical morphism \(\tau_{\leqslant n}(K) \longrightarrow K\) (resp. \(K \longrightarrow \tau_{\geqslant n} K\)) induces isomorphisms on the \(H^i\) for \(i \leqslant n\) (resp. \(i \geqslant n\)).

The functor \(\tau_{\leqslant n}\) (resp. \(\tau_{\geqslant n}\)) transforms homotopic morphisms into homotopic morphisms. Therefore, it follows from 1.1.4 that the functor 1.1.13 retains meaning in the derived category.
1.1.15. If \(K\) is a double complex, of first (resp. second) differential \(d'\) (resp. \(d''\)), we denote by \(\tau''_{\leqslant n}(K)\) the double subcomplex of \(K\) such that
\[\tau''_{\leqslant n}(K)^{pq} = \begin{cases} K^{pq} & \text{si } q < n \\[4pt] \operatorname{Ker}(d'') & \text{si } q = n \\[4pt] 0 & \text{si } q > n \end{cases}\]
we similarly define \(\tau''_{\geqslant n}(K)\), \(\tau'_{\leqslant n}(K)\) and \(\tau'_{\geqslant n}(K)\).
1.1.16. We will also have to consider the "truncated beasts" of a complex \(K\), defined by

\[\sigma_{\leqslant n}(K)^i = \begin{cases} K^i & \text{si } i \leqslant n \\[4pt] 0 & \text{si } i > n \end{cases} \qquad\qquad \sigma_{\geqslant n}(K)^i = \begin{cases} K^i & \text{si } i \geqslant n \\[4pt] 0 & \text{si } i < n \end{cases}\]
1.2. Derived functors.

Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be a triangulated category and \(S\) be a saturated multiplicative system (CD 2.1.2). If \(A \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{A}\), we will designate by \(A^+\) and \(A^-\) the ind and pro-objects ((0.5) and (0.6))
\[A^+ = \underset{A \xrightarrow{s} A'}{\text{"}\varinjlim\text{"}}\, A' \qquad\qquad A^- = \underset{A' \xrightarrow{s} A}{\text{"}\varprojlim\text{"}}\, A' \, ,\]
where \(s\) browses the filtering category of arrows of \(S\) of source or goal \(A\).

The functors \(A \mapsto A^+\) and \(A \longrightarrow A^-\) make the elements of \(S\) invertible and define fully faithful functors of \(\mathcal{A}(S^{-1})\) in the categories of Ind and pro-objects of \(\mathcal{A}\) respectively.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.2.1

Let \(F\) be an exact covariant multifunctor of the triangulated categories \((\mathcal{A}_i)_{0 \leqslant i \leqslant n}\) in the triangulated category \(\mathcal{A}\) and let \(S_i\) and \(S\) be saturated multiplicative systems of the categories \(\mathcal{A}_i\) and \(\mathcal{A}\).

(i) The right derivative functor \(RF\) of \(F\) (relative to \(S_i\) and to \(S\)) is the functor of the categories \(\mathcal{A}_i(S_i^{-1})\) in the category of Ind-objects of \(\mathcal{A}(S^{-1})\) which makes the following diagram commutative

\[\xymatrix{ (\mathcal{A}_i) \ar[r]^-{(A_i) \,\mapsto\, F(A_i^+)} \ar[d] & \operatorname{Ind} \mathcal{A} \ar[d] \\ \mathcal{A}_i(S_i^{-1}) \ar[r]_-{RF} & \operatorname{Ind} \mathcal{A}(S^{-1}) \, . }\]

(ii) We say that \(RF\) is defined in \((A_i)\) if the Ind-object \(RF(A_i)\) of \(\mathcal{A}(S^{-1})\) is "essentially constant", i.e. comes from an object of \(\mathcal{A}(S^{-1})\); this object is called the value of \(RF\) in \((A_i)\).

(iii) We say that \(F\) is right differentiable if \(RF\) is defined everywhere; we will then designate again by \(RF\) the functor of \(\mathcal{A}_i(S_i^{-1})\) in \(\mathcal{A}(S^{-1})\) that it defines.

(iv) A family \((A_i)\) of objects of \(\mathcal{A}_i\) is said to be deployed for \(RF\) if the canonical morphism of \(F(A_i)\) in \(RF(A_i)\) is an isomorphism.

We leave it to the reader to define by duality the left derivative \(LF\) of \(F\) (with value in Pro \(\mathcal{A}(S^{-1})\)) and to extend the previous definitions to cases where \(F\) is covariant in certain arguments and contravariant in others.
When \(RF\) is defined everywhere, the definition given here coincides with that of Verdier (CD p.39), as can easily be verified.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1.2.2

Let \((A_1', A_1, A_1'')\) be a triangle distinguished in \(\mathcal{A}_1\), and \(A_i \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{A}_i\) (\(i \neq 1\)).

(i) If \((A_1',(A_i))\) and \((A_1'',(A_i))\) are deployed for \(RF\), then the family \((A_1,(A_i))\) is deployed as well.

(ii) If \(RF\) is defined in \((A_1',(A_i))\) and in \((A_1'',(A_i))\), it is also defined in \((A_1,(A_i))\) and the triangle \((RF(A_1',(A_i)), RF(A_1,(A_i)), RF(A_1'',(A_i)))\) is distinguished in \(\mathcal{A}(S^{-1})\).
Let us prove that (ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) (i). Since \(F\) is correct, the arrow
is a morphism of distinguished triangles. By hypothesis, this arrow is an isomorphism in two vertices; so it's one on the third, as we wanted.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.2.2.1

If \(\Delta = (X,Y,Z)\) is a triangle distinguished from a triangulated category \(\mathcal{A}\) and \(S\) a multiplicative system saturated with \(\mathcal{A}\), then the triangle \(\Delta^+ = (X^+, Y^+, Z^+)\) de Ind \(\mathcal{A}\) is limit of triangles distinguished from \(\mathcal{A}\).
Let us designate by \(\mathcal{L}\) the category of triangle morphisms distinguished from source \(\Delta\) whose arrows belong to \(S\).

(a) If \(\Delta = (X,Y,Z)\) and \(\Delta' = (X',Y',Z')\) are two triangles distinguished from \(\mathcal{A}\) and if \(f : \Delta \longrightarrow \Delta'\) is a \(\mathcal{A}(S^{-1})\)-morphism of triangles, there exist in \(\mathcal{A}\) morphisms of triangles \(s : \Delta' \longrightarrow \Delta''\) and \(f_1 : \Delta \longrightarrow \Delta''\) such that the components of \(s\) are in \(S\) and the diagram
\[(1)\qquad \xymatrix{ \Delta \ar[d]_{f} \ar[dr]^{f_1} & \\ \Delta' \ar[r]_{s} & \Delta'' }\]
be commutative in \(\mathcal{A}(S^{-1})\).
There is a commutative diagram
\[(2)\qquad \xymatrix{ X \ar[d]_{f} \ar[dr]^{f_1} & \\ X' \ar[r]_{s} & X'' } \qquad\qquad (s \in S, \; f_1 \in \operatorname{Fl} \mathcal{A})\]

and a morphism \(\Delta' \xrightarrow{s_1} \Delta_1''\) in \(S\), admitting \(s\) as component. Let us reason in the same way about the other vertices: we obtain a diagram of type (1), in which however \(f_1\) is not yet a triangle morphism in \(\mathcal{A}\), the diagrams
\[(3)\qquad \xymatrix{ Z \ar[r]^{f} \ar[d]_{w} & Z_o'' \ar[d]^{w'} \\ X \ar[r]_{f} & X_o'' }\]

for example which may not be commutative in \(\mathcal{A}\), only in \(\mathcal{A}(S^{-1})\). There exists \(t_o : X_o'' \longrightarrow X_1''\) in \(S\) such that \(t_o\, w' f = t_o\, f w\) and \(t : \Delta_o'' \longrightarrow \Delta_1''\) admitting \(t_o\) as component. Let's replace \(\Delta_o''\) with \(\Delta_1''\), \(s\) with \(ts\) and \(f_1\) with \(tf_1\); in the diagram of type (1) obtained, (3) is this time commutative; proceeding in the same way to the other vertices, we obtain (1). We leave it to the reader to verify the same:

(b) If \(f,g : \Delta \rightrightarrows \Delta'\) are two morphisms of distinguished triangles in \(\mathcal{A}\), equal in \(\mathcal{A}(S^{-1})\), then there exists a morphism of distinguished triangles with arrows in \(S\), \(s : \Delta' \longrightarrow \Delta''\), such that \(Sf = Sg\).
(c) The category \(\mathcal{L}\) is filtering on the right.
(d) We complete the proof of the lemma by noting that
\[\Delta^+ = \underset{s \in \mathcal{L}}{\text{"}\varinjlim\text{"}} (\text{but de } s) \, .\]

Let us prove 1.2.2 (ii). Let \(X'\) and \(X''\) be objects of \(\mathcal{A}(S^{-1})\) representing \(RF(A_1',(A_i))\) and \(RF(A_1'',(A_i))\), and let us complete the degree 1 morphism of \(X''\) into \(X'\) into a distinguished triangle \((X',X,X'')\). Replacing \(A_i\), \(A_1'\), \(A_1\) and \(A_1''\) with the aim of an arrow from \(S\) from source \(A_i\), \(A_1'\), \(A_1\) or \(A_1''\), we return to the case where there exists a commutative diagram

where \(a''\) and \(a'\) are sections of arrows from \(F(A_1'',(A_i))\) in \(X''\) and from \(F(A_1',(A_i))\) in \(X'\). By virtue of the axiom TR 3 of [C.D] we can complete this diagram in a morphism of triangles

The compound arrow is a morphism of triangles, of source a distinguished triangle and of goal a limit (in Ind \(\mathcal{A}(S^{-1})\)) of distinguished triangles (1.2.2.1). Whatever \(Y\) is in \(\mathcal{A}(S^{-1})\), the following diagram will be commutative and its lines exact

\[\xymatrix@C=1em@R=2.5em{ \operatorname{Hom}(Y,X''[1]) \ar[r] \ar[d] & \operatorname{Hom}(Y,X') \ar[r] \ar[d] & \operatorname{Hom}(Y,X) \ar[r] \ar[d] & \operatorname{Hom}(Y,X'') \ar[r] \ar[d] & \operatorname{Hom}(Y,X'[1]) \ar[d] \\ \operatorname{Hom}(Y,RF(A_1'',(A_i))[1]) \ar[r] & \operatorname{Hom}(Y,RF(A_1',(A_i))) \ar[r] & \operatorname{Hom}(Y,RF(A_1,(A_i))) \ar[r] & \operatorname{Hom}(Y,RF(A_1'',(A_i))) \ar[r] & \operatorname{Hom}(Y,RF(A_1',(A_i))[1]) }\]

By hypothesis, arrows other than the central arrow are isomorphisms. The lemma of 5 shows that the central arrow is also an isomorphism, whatever \(Y\), therefore that the distinguished triangle \((X',X,X'')\) is isomorphism to the triangle \((RF(A_1',(A_i)), RF(A_1,(A_i)), RF(A_1'',(A_i)))\). This completes the demonstration.

If \(\mathcal{A}\) and \(\mathcal{B}\) are two abelian categories and \(F\) an additive covariant functor of \(\mathcal{A}\) in \(\mathcal{B}\), \(F\) defines exact functors of \(K(\mathcal{A})\) in \(K(\mathcal{B})\), from \(K^+(\mathcal{A})\) in \(K^+(\mathcal{B})\) and from \(K^-(\mathcal{A})\) in \(K^-(\mathcal{B})\) (1.1.7).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.2.3

(i) We call right derivative functors of \(F\) the functors of \(D^+(\mathcal{A})\) in Ind \(D^+(\mathcal{B})\) and of \(D(\mathcal{A})\) in Ind \(D(\mathcal{B})\) right derivatives of the extensions of \(F\) to the complexes \(K^+(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow K^+(\mathcal{B})\) and \(K(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow K(\mathcal{B})\).

(ii) An object \(A\) of \(\mathcal{A}\) is called acyclic on the right for \(F\), or, by abuse of language, acyclic for \(RF\), if the complex reduced to \(A\) in degree \(0\) is deployed for \(RF\).

We will be especially interested in the case where \(RF\) is defined everywhere; we can then consider it as a functor of \(D^+(\mathcal{A})\) in \(D^+(\mathcal{B})\), or of \(D(\mathcal{A})\) in \(D(\mathcal{B})\), depending on the case.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1.2.4

The following diagram is commutative.

\[\xymatrix{ D^+(\mathcal{A}) \ar[r] \ar[d]_{RF} & D(\mathcal{A}) \ar[d]^{RF} \\ \operatorname{Ind} D^+(\mathcal{B}) \ar[r] & \operatorname{Ind} D(\mathcal{B}) \, . }\]

If \(K\) is a zero complex in degree \(< n\) and if \(s : K \longrightarrow L\) is a quasi-isomorphism, the compound morphism \(K \xrightarrow{s} L \longrightarrow \sigma_{\geqslant n}(L)\) (1.1.16) is still a quasi-isomorphism. The quasi-isomorphisms of \(K\) in a lower bounded complex therefore form a (full) cofinal subcategory of the category of all quasi-isomorphisms of source \(K\). The proposition results formally.

In particular, if the functor \(RF\) is everywhere defined to \(D(\mathcal{A})\), it is everywhere defined to \(D^+(\mathcal{A})\), and the derived functor \(RF : D^+(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow D^+(\mathcal{B})\) is restriction of the derived functor \(RF : D(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow D(\mathcal{B})\).
(1.2.5) We leave it to the reader to:
(i) Generalize definition 1.2.3 to the case of a complex of multifunctors, covariant in certain variables and contravariant in others;
(ii) Dualize definition 1.2.5 (i) to define left derivatives;
(iii) Extend proposition 1.2.4 to the case of a bounded complex of multifunctors;
(iv) Dualize proposition 1.2.5 (iii) by considering left derivative functors and the inclusion of \(D^-(\mathcal{B})\) in \(D(\mathcal{B})\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1.2.6

Let \(\mathcal{A}\) and \(\mathcal{B}\) two abelian categories, \(P\) a part of \(\operatorname{Ob}(\mathcal{A})\) and \(F\) an additive functor of \(\mathcal{A}\) in \(\mathcal{B}\). It is assumed that

(a) every object of \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quotient of an element of \(P\)

(b) for any short exact sequence

\[0 \longrightarrow P \longrightarrow Q \longrightarrow R \longrightarrow 0 \, ,\]

where \(Q\) and \(R\) are in \(P\), \(P\) also belongs to \(P\) and the \(0\)-continued

\[0 \longrightarrow FP \longrightarrow FQ \longrightarrow FR \longrightarrow 0 \quad \underline{\text{est exacte}}.\]

Then, any object of \(P\) is acyclic for \(LF\).

If \(A \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{A}\), left resolutions of \(A\) by objects of \(P\) will form a cofinal system in the filtering category of left resolutions of \(A_o\). It is therefore sufficient to prove that if a complex
\[\ldots \longrightarrow A_2 \longrightarrow A_1 \longrightarrow A_o \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow 0\]
is acyclic and with objects in \(P\), its image is acyclic. Cutting it into short exact sequences
\[0 \longrightarrow \operatorname{Im}(d_{i-1}) \longrightarrow A_i \longrightarrow \operatorname{Im}(d_i) \longrightarrow 0 \qquad (1.2.6.\text{ i})\quad (i \geqslant 0)\]
we see by induction on \(i\) that \(\operatorname{Im}(d_i) \in P\) and that the image of (1.2.6 i) by \(F\) is a short exact sequence. The image of the complex is therefore acyclic.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1.2.7

Let \(F\) an additive functor of an abelian category \(\mathcal{A}\) in an abelian category \(\mathcal{B}\). If any object of \(\mathcal{A}\) is quotient (resp. subobject) of an acyclic object for \(LF\) (resp. \(RF\)) (1.2.3), the derived functor \(LF : D^-(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow D^-(\mathcal{B})\) (resp. \(RF : D^+(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow D^+(\mathcal{B})\)) is everywhere defined and any upper bounded complex (resp. lower) of acyclic objects for \(LF\) (resp. \(RF\)) is deployed.

Just consider the left derivative \(LF\). If \(K\) is an upper bounded complex, the quasi-isomorphisms \(s : L \longrightarrow K\) with \(L\) upper bounded and with acyclic objects form a cofinal system in the category of goal quasi-isomorphisms \(K\). It suffices to show that for \(K\) with acyclic objects, \(F(s) : F(L) \longrightarrow F(K)\) is a quasi-isomorphism; we will then have in the general case \(F(L) = LF(K)\). Let \(M\) be the cone of \(s\). The complex has acyclic objects and \(H^*(M) = 0\); it is necessary to prove that \(H^*(F(M)) = 0\).

Let \(i\) be an integer. Expanded complexes form a triangulated subcategory, such that any bounded complex of acyclic objects, notably \(\tau_{\geqslant i}(M)\), is deployed. Since \(H^j(\tau_{\geqslant i}(L)) = 0\) for \(j > i\), we have, for \(j > i\), \(H^j(F(M)) = H^j(F(\tau_{\geqslant i}(M))) = H^j LF(\tau_{\geqslant i}(M)) = 0\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1.2.8

Let \(F^\bullet\) be a lower bounded complex of functors from \(\mathcal{A}\) in \(\mathcal{B}\). If for all \(K \in K^+(\mathcal{A})\) exists a quasi-isomorphism \(L \xrightarrow{s} K\), where \(L \in K^+(\mathcal{A})\) is deployed for all \(RF^i\), then the derived functor \(RF^\bullet : D^+(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow D^+(\mathcal{B})\) exists and any lower bounded complex deployed for all \(RF^i\) is deployed for \(RF\).

The proof is left to the reader, as well as that of the dual statement, or of the variants obtained by taking \(F\) contravariant, or by taking a bounded complex of functors and working in \(D(\mathcal{A})\) and \(D(\mathcal{B})\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.2.9

Let \(F\) be a functor of \(D^b(\mathcal{A})\) in \(D(\mathcal{B})\) and \(d\) an interval of \(\mathbb{Z}\). We say that \(F\) is of cohomological amplitude \(\subset d\) if for all \(A \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{A}\), \(H^i F(A) = 0\) for \(i \notin d\).

Very often, \(d\) will be an interval of the form \([0,x]\) or \([-x,0]\) and we will then say that \(F\) is of cohomological dimension resp. homology \(\leqslant x\). We will say that \(F\) is of finite dimension if there exists a finite interval \(d\) of \(\mathbb{Z}\) such that \(F\) is of amplitude \(\subset d\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1.2.10

Let us assume that the assumptions of 1.2.7 are met, and that the functor \(RF : D^+(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow D^+(\mathcal{B})\) (resp. \(LF : D^-(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow D^-(\mathcal{B})\)) is of finite dimension. Then any complex of acyclic objects for \(RF\) (resp. \(LF\)) is deployed, and the functor \(RF : D(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow D(\mathcal{B})\) (resp. \(LF : D(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow D(\mathcal{B})\)) is everywhere defined.
For the demonstration, we refer to Verdier [CD]. There is an analogous statement for bounded complexes of functors. We can still replace the exponent \(+\) (resp \(-\)) by \(b\).

footnotes.html: (∗) The construction also provides an extension of \(F\) into a multifunction of the categories \(C^{s\varepsilon(i)}(\mathcal{A}_i)\) in \(C^s(\mathcal{A})\).

blocks.html: 2. Categories fiberized into derived categories.
2.1. Introduction.
The editor insists that the reader refrain from reading this §. We give the formalism of the trivial duality theorem and we resolve the perplexity raised by Artin in XII 4.

For the notions of categories on top of another, of fibered category, of cofibered category and of cleavage, we refer to SGA 1 VI. Among other things, we establish in this presentation an equivalence between the notions of split fiber categories normalized on a category \(C\), and pseudo-functor of \(C^o\) in (Cat).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.1.1

If \(X\) is a ringed site, we denote by \(D(X)\) the category derived from the abelian category of the module bundles on the left on \(X\).
When there is reason to avoid confusion, we instead write \(D(X, \mathcal{A})\), \(\mathcal{A}\) designating the bundle of rings.
If \(f : X \longrightarrow Y\) is a morphism of ringed sites, \(f\) defines derived functors
\[(1)\qquad Rf_* : D^+(X) \longrightarrow D^+(Y)\] \[(2)\qquad Lf^* : D^-(Y) \longrightarrow D^-(X) \, ,\]
and, by passing to opposite categories, functors
\[(1^o)\qquad Rf_* : D^+(X)^o \longrightarrow D^+(Y)^o\] \[(2^o)\qquad Lf^* : D^-(Y)^o \longrightarrow D^-(X)^o \, ,\]

"functional" in \(f\). More precisely, (1°) and (2°) define two pseudo-functors of the category of sites in that of triangulated categories, one covariant and the other contravariant. We thus find two categories on that of the sites, one cofibered, of fibers the \(D^+(X)^o\), and of direct image functors the \(Rf_*\), the other fibered, of fibers the \(D^-(X)^o\), and of reciprocal image functors the \(Lf^*\). If the functor \(f^*\) is of finite homological dimension (i.e. of "Finite Tor-dimension") we can in (2) replace the exponent by an exponent \(+\). We therefore obtain two categories on the category \(\mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{tdf}}\) of ringed sites and finite-dimensional Tor-ringed site morphisms, categories whose fibers are the categories \(D^+(X)^o\). VERDIER's "trivial duality theorem" ([CD] p. 48) expresses that these two categories are canonically isomorphic; they are therefore fibered and cofibered; the "direct image" functors are the \(Rf_*\)(sic) functors, and the "reciprocal image" functors are the \(Lf^*\)(sic) functors.

2.1.2. The language of fiber categories makes it possible to easily resolve the perplexity raised by ARTIN in XII 4: it suffices to apply the following proposition to the fiber and co-fiber category on that of the fiber sites opposite the categories of bundles on the corresponding sites (cf. 0.2).

Let \(\mathcal{E}\) be a category on \(\mathcal{B}\), \(f: y \longrightarrow x\) an arrow of \(\mathcal{B}\), and \(F \in \operatorname{Ob}(\mathcal{E}_x)\). Recall that a pair \((G,\varphi)\) (\(G \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{E}_y\), \(\varphi \in \operatorname{Hom}_f(G,F)\)) is a reciprocal image in the strict sense of \(F\) by \(f\) if, whatever \(g: Z \longrightarrow y\) and \(H \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{E}_Z\), we a
\[\operatorname{Hom}_g(H,G) \xrightarrow[\;\sim\;]{\varphi} \operatorname{Hom}_{fg}(H,F) \, .\]
The same goes for direct images. If \(\mathcal{E}\) is fibered or cofibered on \(\mathcal{B}\), this notion is reduced to that of reciprocal image.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.1.3

Let \(\mathcal{E}\) be a category on \(\mathcal{B}\), \(y \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{B}\), \(F \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{E}_y\) and a commutative diagram in \(\mathcal{B}\):

\[(2.1.3.1)\qquad \xymatrix{ y' \ar[r]^{g'} \ar[d]_{f'} & y \ar[d]^{f} \\ x' \ar[r] & x \, . }\]

We assume that the direct and reciprocal images \(f_*F\), \(g^*f_*F\), \(g'^*F\) and \(f'_* g'^*F\) exist in the strict sense. There then exists one and only one arrow \(\varphi \in \operatorname{Fl} \mathcal{E}_{x'}\), called base change arrow, making the diagram commutative

\[(2.1.3.2)\qquad \xymatrix@R=2.2em@C=1.5em{ g'^*F \ar[rr] \ar[d] & & F \ar[d] \\ f'_* g'^*F \ar[dr]_{\varphi} & & f_*F \\ & g^*f_*F \ar[ur] & }\]

If in addition the direct images \(g'_*g'^*F\) and \(g'_*f'_*g'^*F\) exist in the strict sense, then the arrow \(\varphi\) coincides with that defined in XII 4. If \(f'^*f_*g'^*F\) and \(f'^*f'_*F\) exist in the strict sense, it still coincides with the 2nd arrow defined in XII 4.
In particular the two base change arrows XII 4 coincide.
By hypothesis, the arrows in the diagram (2.1.3.2) induce bijections

\[\operatorname{Hom}_y(F,F) = \operatorname{Hom}_{g'}(g'^*F,F) = \operatorname{Hom}_{g'}(g'^*F, f_*F) \quad \text{et}\] \[\operatorname{Hom}_{x'}(f'_*g'^*F, g^*f_*F) = \operatorname{Hom}_{f'}(g'^*F, g^*f_*F) = \operatorname{Hom}_{gf'}(g'^*F, f_*F) \, .\]
Since \(fg' = gf'\), \(\operatorname{Hom}_{fg'}(g'^*F, f_*F)\) is identical to \(\operatorname{Hom}_{gf'}(g'^*F, f_*F)\) and the arrows in the diagram (2.1.3.2) induce bijections
\[\operatorname{Hom}_y(F,F) = \operatorname{Hom}_{fg'}(g'^*F, f_*F) = \operatorname{Hom}_{x'}(f'_*g'^*F, g^*f_*F),\]
and the image of \(1_F\) is the only arrow of \(\mathcal{E}_{x'}\) making (2.1.3.2) commutative.
By definition, in XII 4, the base change arrow \(\psi\) was the one making the following diagram commutative

\[(2.1.3.3)\quad \xymatrix@C=1.3em@R=3em{ f'_*g'^*F \ar[d]_{\psi} & g'^*F \ar[l] \ar@{=}[r] \ar[d]|-{u_4} & g'^*F \ar[r] \ar[d]|-{u_3} & F \ar@{=}[r] \ar[d]|-{u_2} & F \ar[d]^{u_1} \\ g^*f_*F & f'^*g^*f_*F \ar[l] & g^*f_*f^*F \ar[l] & f'^*f_*F \ar[l] \ar[r] & f_*F }\]

The horizontal arrows and \(u_1\) are the canonical arrows; \(u_2\) is therefore the addition arrow, \(u_3 = g^*(u_2)\), \(u_4\) is deduced from the isomorphism between \(f'^*g^*\) and \(g'^*f^*\), and the arrow \(\psi\) is deduced from \(u_4\) by addition.

The map composed of source \(g'^*F\) and goal \(f_*F\) is the one that appears in (2.1.3.2) and it is therefore the same in the following commutative diagram, the left square of which is taken from (2.1.3.3)
\[\xymatrix{ g'^*F \ar[r]^-{u_4} \ar[d] & f'^*g^*f_*F \ar[r] \ar[d] & f_*F \ar@{=}[d] \\ f'_*g'^*F \ar[r]_-{\psi} & g^*f_*F \ar[r] & f_*F }\]
This means that \(\psi\) verifies the characteristic property of \(\varphi\).
The definition of \(\varphi\) is autodual, which exempts us from proving the second part of 2.1.3.
2.2. Categories fiberized into triangulated categories.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.2.1

We call "additive category on a category \(\mathcal{B}\)" a category \(\mathcal{A}\) on \(\mathcal{B}\) whose sets of arrows \(\operatorname{Hom}_f(X,Y)\) (for \(f \in \operatorname{Fl}(\mathcal{B})\), \(X\) (resp. \(Y\)) above the source (resp. the goal) of \(f\)) have been provided with laws of abelian groups, so that:

(i) Whatever \(x \xrightarrow{f} y \xrightarrow{g} z\) in \(\mathcal{B}\) and \(X, Y, Z\) in \(\mathcal{A}\) above \(x\), \(y\) and \(z\) respectively, the law of composition

\[\operatorname{Hom}_g(Y,Z) \times \operatorname{Hom}_f(X,Y) \xrightarrow{\;\circ\;} \operatorname{Hom}_{gf}(X,Z) \quad \text{est biadditive.}\]

(ii) Fiber categories are additive.

We will not have to use here that these abelian group laws, when they exist, are solely determined by \(\mathcal{A}\), \(\mathcal{B}\) and \(\rho\). A \(\mathcal{B}\)-functor \(F : \mathcal{A}_1 \longrightarrow \mathcal{A}_2\) between additive categories on \(\mathcal{B}\) is said to be additive if it induces homomorphisms
\[F : \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{A}_1, f}(X,Y) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{A}_2, f}(X,Y) \, .\]

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.2.2

We call "abelian category on a category \(\mathcal{B}\)" an additive category \(\mathcal{A}\) on \(\mathcal{B}\), whose fibers are abelian, and such that for any arrow \(f: x \longrightarrow y\) of \(\mathcal{B}\) the bifuctor

\[\operatorname{Hom}_f(X,Y) : \mathcal{A}_x^o \times \mathcal{A}_y \longrightarrow (\mathrm{Ab})\]

is exact on the left in \(X\) and \(Y\).
We will not have to use here that if \(\mathcal{A}\) is a fibered and cofibered category on \(\mathcal{B}\) (SGA 1 VI 6) it is abelian on \(\mathcal{B}\) as soon as its fibers are abelian categories.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.2.3

We call "category triangulated on a category \(\mathcal{B}\)" an additive category \(\mathcal{A}\) on \(\mathcal{B}\), provided with a functor \(T : \mathcal{A} \longrightarrow \mathcal{A}\) and whose fibers \(\mathcal{A}_x\) for \(x \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{B}\) are provided of sets \(\Delta_x\) of triangles, so that ;

(i) \(T\) is an \(\mathcal{B}\)-additive automorphism of \(\mathcal{A}\)

(ii) each fiber category \(\mathcal{A}_x\), provided with the functor \(T_x\) induced by \(T\) and the set \(\Delta_x\), is a triangulated category (CD 1.1),

(iii) if the triangles \((X,Y,Z,u,v,w)\) and \((X',Y',Z',u',v',w')\) belong to \(\Delta_x\) and \(\Delta_{x'}\), respectively, any diagram

\[\xymatrix@R=1.8em@C=1.6em{ X \ar[r]^{u} \ar[d]_{f} & Y \ar[r]^{v} \ar[d]^{g} & Z \ar[r]^{w} \ar@{-->}[d]^{h} & X[1] \ar[d]^{f[1]} \\ X' \ar[r]_{u'} & Y' \ar[r]_{v'} & Z' \ar[r]_{w'} & X'[1] }\]

where \(u'f = gu\) can be extended into a morphism of triangles \((f,g,h) : (X,Y,Z) \longrightarrow (X',Y',Z')\).
\(T\) will be called the translation functor, we will often write \(A[1]\) rather than \(T(A)\), \(A[n]\) then designating \(T^n(A)\) (\(n \in \mathbb{Z}\)).
The triangles belonging to the sets \(\Delta_x\) will be said to be distinguished or exact.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.2.4

Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be a triangulated category on \(\mathcal{B}\), \(x\) an object of \(\mathcal{B}\), \((X,Y,Z,u,v,w)\) a triangle distinguished from \(\mathcal{A}_x\). Whatever \(f: y \longrightarrow x\) and the object \(A \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{A}_y\), the following infinite sequence is exact

\[\ldots \to \operatorname{Hom}_f(A,X) \to \operatorname{Hom}_f(A,Y) \to \operatorname{Hom}_f(A,Z) \to \operatorname{Hom}_f(A,X[1]) \to \operatorname{Hom}_f(A,Y[1]) \to \ldots\]
Analogous statement for \(f : x \longrightarrow y\) and \(A \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{A}_y\).
We leave it to the reader to demonstrate this proposition by paraphrasing VERDIER [CD] p. 4. We also leave it to him to give meaning to the following proposition and to verify it:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.2.5

Let \(\mathcal{A}\) an additive category (resp. abelian, resp. triangulated) on \(\mathcal{B}\). For any functor \(\mathcal{B}' \longrightarrow \mathcal{B}\) of goal \(\mathcal{B}\), the category \(\mathcal{A}' = \mathcal{A} \times_{\mathcal{B}} \mathcal{B}'\) is additive (resp. abelian, resp. triangulated) on \(\mathcal{B}'\).

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
Example 2.2.6

The category of module bundles on variable ringed sites is abelian on the category of ringed sites.

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
Example 2.2.7

Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an additive category on \(\mathcal{B}\). The category \(C(\mathcal{A}/\mathcal{B})\) complexes of \(\mathcal{A}\) on \(\mathcal{B}\) has for objects the complexes of the fiber categories. An arrow \(f\) of \((X^n,d)\) in \((Y^n,d)\) is a system of arrows \(f^n : X^n \longrightarrow Y^n\) such that \(fd^n = f^{n+1} d\). We set \(p(f) = p(f^n)\) (this arrow does not depend on \(n\) because the \(p(d)\) are identities). \(C(\mathcal{A}/\mathcal{B})\) is an additive category on \(\mathcal{B}\). We define a "translation" functor by setting

\[(K[1])^n = K^{n+1} \, , \quad (d[1])_n = -d_{n+1} \, .\]

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
Example 2.2.8

Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an additive category on \(\mathcal{B}\). The category \(K(\mathcal{A}/\mathcal{B})\) complexes of \(\mathcal{A}\) on \(\mathcal{B}\) up to homotopy has the same objects as \(C(\mathcal{A}/\mathcal{B})\). We obtain it by decreeing zero, in \(C(\mathcal{A}/\mathcal{B})\), the arrows homotopic to zero; in other words, for \(f : x \longrightarrow y\) in \(\mathcal{B}\), \(X^\bullet \in \operatorname{Ob} C(\mathcal{A}/\mathcal{B})_x\) and \(Y^\bullet \in \operatorname{Ob} C(\mathcal{A}/\mathcal{B})_y\), we set

\[\operatorname{Hom}_f(X^\bullet,Y^\bullet) = H^0 \operatorname{Hom}_f^\bullet(X^\bullet,Y^\bullet)\]

The translation functor passes to the quotient.

If \(F : \mathcal{B}' \longrightarrow \mathcal{B}\) is a goal functor \(\mathcal{B}\), and if \(\mathcal{A}' = \mathcal{A} \times_{\mathcal{B}} \mathcal{B}'\), we leave it to the reader to identify \(C(\mathcal{A}'/\mathcal{B}')\) with \(C(\mathcal{A}/\mathcal{B}) \times_{\mathcal{B}} \mathcal{B}'\) and \(K(\mathcal{A}'/\mathcal{B}')\) with \(K(\mathcal{A}/\mathcal{B}) \times_{\mathcal{B}} \mathcal{B}'\). In particular, fibers of category \(K(\mathcal{A}/\mathcal{B})\) are categories \(K(\mathcal{A}_x)\) and, as such, are triangulated.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.2.9

(i) For the additional structures defined in 2.2.8, the category \(K(\mathcal{A}/\mathcal{B})\) is triangulated on \(\mathcal{B}\).

(ii) The formation of the additive category (resp. triangulated) \(C(\mathcal{A}/\mathcal{B})\) (resp. \(K(\mathcal{A}/\mathcal{B})\)) is compatible with any change of base category \(\mathcal{B}' \longrightarrow \mathcal{B}\).
Conditions (i) and (ii) of definition 2.2.3 are verified fiber by fiber. (iii) is verified by paraphrasing VERDIER [CD] p.4.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 2.2.10

The above also applies to the categories \(K^+(\mathcal{A}/\mathcal{B})\), \(K^-(\mathcal{A}/\mathcal{B})\) and \(K^b(\mathcal{A}/\mathcal{B})\) obtained by limiting ourselves to lower bounded, upper bounded, or bounded complexes respectively.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 2.2.11

Taking \(\mathcal{B}\) as the final category, we find the usual categories of complexes.

2.2.12. Let \(F\) be a pseudo-functor of a category \(\mathcal{B}^o\) in (Cat). We know (SGA 1 VI) that \(F\) defines a fiber category on \(\mathcal{B}\), of fibers the categories \(F(x)\). Whatever \(f: x \longrightarrow y\) in \(\mathcal{B}\) and \(X \in \operatorname{Ob} F(x)\), \(Y \in \operatorname{Ob} F(y)\), we have by definition
\[(2.2.12.1)\qquad \operatorname{Hom}_f(X,Y) = \operatorname{Hom}_{F(x)}(X, F(f)(Y)) \, .\]

We know (ibidem) that the previous construction defines an equivalence between the 2-category of pseudo-functors of \(\mathcal{B}^o\) in (Cat) and the 2-category of categories fiberized on \(\mathcal{B}\).

Let's start with a pseudo-functor of \(\mathcal{B}^o\) in the 2-category having for objects the additive categories (resp. abelian, resp. triangulated) and for 1-arrows the additive functors (resp. left-exact, resp. triangulated). We immediately verify that the category defined by (2.2.12.1) is additive (resp. abelian, resp. triangulated). Conversely:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.2.13

The previous construction establishes an equivalence between

(a) The 2-category of pseudo-functors of \(\mathcal{B}^o\) in the 2-category having as objects the additive categories (resp. abelian, resp. triangulated), and for 1-arrows the additive functors (resp. exact to left, resp. triangulated).

(b) The 2-category of additive categories (resp. abelian, resp. triangulated) on \(\mathcal{B}\), which are fibered on \(\mathcal{B}\).
The theory developed in SGA 1 VI allows us to verify only the following point:

2.2.13.1. Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an additive category (resp. abelian, resp. triangulated) on \(\mathcal{B}\), \(f : x \longrightarrow y\) an arrow of \(\mathcal{B}\) and suppose that a reciprocal image functor \(f^* : \mathcal{A}_y \longrightarrow \mathcal{A}_x\) exists; \(f^*\) is then additive (resp. left exact, resp. triangulated).
The functor \(f^*\) gives rise to isomorphisms
\[\operatorname{Hom}_f(X,Y) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_x(X,f^*(Y)) \, .\]
It is necessary to prove in the triangulated case, that \(f^*\) is triangulated for the isomorphism \(Tf^* = f^* T\) (\(T\) translation functor) which makes the following diagrams commutative

\[(2.2.13.2)\quad \xymatrix@C=2.5em@R=2.5em{ \operatorname{Hom}_f(X,Y) \ar@{=}[r] \ar@{=}[d] & \operatorname{Hom}_x(X,f^*Y) \ar[dr] & \\ \operatorname{Hom}_f(TX,TY) \ar@{=}[r] & \operatorname{Hom}_x(TX, f^*TY) \ar[r] & \operatorname{Hom}_x(TX, Tf^*Y) }\]
Additive case: Consider a diagram
\[(2.2.13.3)\quad \xymatrix@C=3em@R=2.5em{ f^*X \ar[r]^{p} \ar@<0.5ex>[d]^{f^*(u)} \ar@<-0.5ex>[d]_{f^*(v)} & X \ar@<0.5ex>[d]^{u} \ar@<-0.5ex>[d]_{v} \\ f^*X' \ar[r]_{p'} & X' }\]
We have \(p'f^*(u+v) = (u+v)p = up+vp = p'f^*(u)+p'f^*(v) = p'(f^*(u) + f^*(v))\) and therefore \(f^*(u+v) = f^*(u) + f^*(v)\).
Abelian case: by definition (2.2), the functor \(\operatorname{Hom}(X,f^*Y) \simeq \operatorname{Hom}_f(X,Y)\) is left exact at \(Y\), whatever \(X\), therefore \(f^*\) is left exact.

Triangulated case: let \((Y',Y,Y'')\) be a triangle distinguished in \(\mathcal{A}_y\) and let \((f^*Y', f^*Y)\). By virtue of 2.2.3, the canonical morphism between the bases of these triangles can be extended into a morphism of triangles, giving rise to the following diagram
\[\xymatrix@C=1.8em@R=1.8em{ f^*Y' \ar[r] \ar@{=}[d] & f^*Y \ar[r] \ar@{=}[d] & X'' \ar[r] \ar[d]^{s} & f^*Y'[1] \ar@{=}[d] \\ f^*Y' \ar[r] & f^*Y \ar[r] & f^*Y'' \ar[r] & f^*Y'[1] }\]
Let's prove that \(s\) is an isomorphism, which will complete the proof. Whatever \(X\) in \(\operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{A}_x\), we have the commutative diagram:

\[\xymatrix@C=0.7em@R=2em{ \operatorname{Hom}_x(X,f^*Y') \ar[r] \ar@{=}[d] & \operatorname{Hom}_x(X,f^*Y) \ar[r] \ar@{=}[d] & \operatorname{Hom}_x(X,X'') \ar[r] \ar[d] & \operatorname{Hom}_x(X,f^*Y'[1]) \ar[r] \ar@{=}[d] & \operatorname{Hom}_x(X,f^*Y[1]) \ar@{=}[d] \\ \operatorname{Hom}_x(X,f^*Y') \ar[r] \ar@{=}[d] & \operatorname{Hom}_x(X,f^*Y) \ar[r] \ar@{=}[d] & \operatorname{Hom}_x(X,f^*Y'') \ar[r] \ar@{=}[d] & \operatorname{Hom}_x(X,f^*Y'[1]) \ar[r] \ar@{=}[d] & \operatorname{Hom}_x(X,f^*Y[1]) \ar@{=}[d] \\ \operatorname{Hom}_f(X,Y') \ar[r] & \operatorname{Hom}_f(X,Y) \ar[r] & \operatorname{Hom}_f(X,Y'') \ar[r] & \operatorname{Hom}_f(X,Y'[1]) \ar[r] & \operatorname{Hom}_f(X,Y[1]) }\]

The first and last lines are correct under 2.2.4. By virtue of the five lemma, the map induced by \(s\) from \(\operatorname{Hom}(X,X'')\) to \(\operatorname{Hom}(X,f^*Y'')\) is always bijective and \(s\) is therefore an isomorphism.
We obviously have dual results for the cofibered categories.
2.3. Trivial duality formula.

Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be a category on a base category \(\mathcal{B}\), and let \(S\) be a set of arrows of \(\mathcal{A}\), each projecting onto an identity of \(\mathcal{B}\). The category \(\mathcal{A}(S^{-1})\) deduced from \(\mathcal{A}\) by calculating fractions is a category on \(\mathcal{B}\) (seeing the universal property).

Let \(\mathcal{B}' \longrightarrow \mathcal{B}\) be a functor; we denote by \(\mathcal{A}'\) the fiber product \(\mathcal{A} \times_{\mathcal{B}} \mathcal{B}'\) and by \(S'\) the reciprocal image of \(S\) in \(\mathcal{A}'\). The compound functor \(\mathcal{A}' \longrightarrow \mathcal{A} \longrightarrow \mathcal{A}(S^{-1})\) makes the arrows of \(S'\) invertible, so it is factorized by \(\mathcal{A}'(S'^{-1})\). We thus find a \(\mathcal{B}'\)-canonical functor
\[(2.3.1)\qquad \mathcal{A}'(S'^{-1}) \longrightarrow \mathcal{B}' \times_{\mathcal{B}} \mathcal{A}(S^{-1}) \, .\]

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.3.2

If \(\mathcal{B}'\) is a subcategory of \(\mathcal{B}\), and if each time a compound arrow \(h = f \circ g\) of \(\mathcal{B}\) belongs to \(\mathcal{B}'\), \(f\) and \(g\) belong to \(\mathcal{B}'\), then the functor (2.3.1) is an isomorphism.
Note that (2.3.1) always induces a bijection on objects, and that, in the present case, \(\mathcal{B}' \times_{\mathcal{B}} \mathcal{A}(S^{-1})\) is a subcategory of \(\mathcal{A}(S^{-1})\).

Recall the "explicit" construction of \(\mathcal{A}(S^{-1})\) [1, Chap. I]. Given two objects \(X\) and \(Y\) of \(\mathcal{A}\), we consider the "formal compounds" \(f_1 \ldots f_n\) where each \(f_i\) is either an arrow of \(\mathcal{A}\), or the inverse of an arrow of \(S\). The set \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{A}(S^{-1})}(X,Y)\) will be the quotient of all of these "formal compounds" by the equivalence relation generated by the relations

\[\begin{aligned} & f_1 \ldots uv \ldots f_n \equiv f_1 \ldots (u \circ v) \ldots f_n && \text{pour } u,v \text{ dans } \operatorname{Fl}(\mathcal{A}) \\ & f_1 \ldots ss^{-1} \ldots f_n \equiv f_1 \ldots f_n && \text{pour } s \in S \\ & f_1 \ldots s^{-1}s \ldots f_n \equiv f_1 \ldots f_n && \text{pour } s \in S \, . \end{aligned}\]

If we apply these constructions to \(\mathcal{A}\) and to the subcategory \(\mathcal{A}'\), we verify that \(\mathcal{A}'(S'^{-1})\) is identical to the subcategory of \(\mathcal{A}(S^{-1})\) reciprocal image of \(\mathcal{B}'\), from the fact that if a "formal compound" \(f_1 \ldots f_n\) has for composed in \(\mathcal{B}\) an arrow of \(\mathcal{B}'\), the image of each \(f_i\) will also be an arrow of \(\mathcal{B}'\).

Now suppose that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a triangulated category on \(\mathcal{B}\), and that the sets \(S_x = S \cap \operatorname{Fl}(\mathcal{A}_x)\) for \(x \in \operatorname{Ob}(\mathcal{B})\) are saturated multiplicative systems (CD Ch.1 §2.1).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.3.3

Under the previous hypotheses the category \(\mathcal{A}/\mathcal{B}\) will be said to be almost differentiable (resp. differentiable, resp. coderivable) relative to \(S\) if, whatever the functor \(F : \mathcal{B}' \longrightarrow \mathcal{B}\), the functor (2.3.1) is an isomorphism (resp. and if \(\mathcal{A}'(S'^{-1})\) is fibered, resp. cofibered on \(\mathcal{B}'\)).
We will designate by \(I\) the category of the following diagram
\[(2.3.4)\qquad I : \quad x_o \xrightarrow{\;F\;} x_1 \, .\]

According to 2.2.13, it is the same to give a triangulated category \(\mathcal{A}\) on \(I\), fibered (resp. cofibered) on \(I\), or to give its fibers \(\mathcal{A}_o\) and \(\mathcal{A}_1\) and the triangulated reciprocal image functor \(F^* : \mathcal{A}_1 \longrightarrow \mathcal{A}_o\) (resp. and the direct image triangulated functor \(F_* : \mathcal{A}_o \longrightarrow \mathcal{A}_1\)). If \(A_o \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{A}_o\) and \(A_1 \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{A}_1\), we have (2.2.12.1)
\[\operatorname{Hom}_F(A_o, A_1) = \operatorname{Hom}(A_o, F^*A_1)\]
(resp. \(\operatorname{Hom}_F(A_o, A_1) = \operatorname{Hom}(F_*A_o, A_1)\)).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.3.5

Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be a triangulated category on \(I\) and \(S\) as in (2.3.3).

(i) \(\mathcal{A}\) is almost differentiable relative to \(S\), and \(\mathcal{A}(S^{-1})\) is triangulated on \(I\).

(ii) If \(\mathcal{A}\) is fibered (resp. cofibered), the functor \(F^*\) (resp. \(F_*\)) is differentiable on the right (resp. on the left) relative to \(S_o\) and \(S_1\) (1.2.1) if and only if \(\mathcal{A}\) is derivable (resp. coderivable) on \(I\).

Under 2.3.2, the fiber categories of \(\mathcal{A}(S^{-1})\) are the categories \(\mathcal{A}_o(S_o^{-1})\) and \(\mathcal{A}_1(S_1^{-1})\), where \(S_i = S \cap \operatorname{Fl}(\mathcal{A}_i)\) (\(i = 0,1\)). With the notations of § 1 n°2, the functors \(A_o \longrightarrow A_o^-\) and \(A_1 \longrightarrow A_1^+\) identify \(\mathcal{A}_o(S_o^{-1})\) and \(\mathcal{A}_1(S_1^{-1})\) to categories of pro and ind-objects of \(\mathcal{A}_o\) and \(\mathcal{A}_1\). This allows you to define a category on \(I\), fibers \(\mathcal{A}_o(S_o^{-1})\) and \(\mathcal{A}_1(S_1^{-1})\), by setting, for \(A_o \in \mathcal{A}_o\) and \(A_1 \in \mathcal{A}_1\):
\[\operatorname{Hom}_F(A_o, A_1) = \operatorname{Hom}_F(A_o^-, A_1^+) = \varinjlim_{A_o \xrightarrow{s} A_o'} \; \varprojlim_{A_1 \xrightarrow{t} A_1'} \operatorname{Hom}(A_o', A_1') \, ,\]

where \(s\) and \(t\) iterate over the elements of \(S_o\) and \(S_1\) of goal (source) \(A_o\) (\(A_1\)). The category \(\mathcal{A}\) is sent in this category, and we immediately check that it verifies the universal property of \(\mathcal{A}(S^{-1})\), on \(I\) and after any base change.

To check (i), it is enough to prove that this category, namely \(\mathcal{A}(S^{-1})\), is triangulated, and it is enough to check condition 2.2.3 (iii). Let \((X_o,Y_o,Z_o)\) and \((X_1,Y_1,Z_1)\) be triangles distinguished from \(\mathcal{A}_o\) and \(\mathcal{A}_1\), giving rise to the triangles distinguished \((X_o^-,Y_o^-,Z_o^-)\) and \((X_1^+,Y_1^+,Z_1^+)\) from \(\mathcal{A}_o(S_o^{-1})\) and \(\mathcal{A}_1(S_1^{-1})\).
By virtue of Lemma 1.2.2.1, the triangles \((X_o^-,Y_o^-,Z_o^-)\) and \((X_1^+,Y_1^+,Z_1^+)\) are limits of distinguished triangles; there therefore exist distinguished triangle morphisms
\[(2.3.5.2)\qquad \begin{aligned} (X_o^-, Y_o^-, Z_o^-) & \longrightarrow (X_o', Y_o', Z_o') \\ (X_1', Y_1', Z_1') & \longrightarrow (X_1^+, Y_1^+, Z_1^+) \, , \end{aligned}\]
and a diagram
\[\xymatrix{ X_o' \ar[r]^{u'} \ar[d] & X_1' \ar[d] \\ Y_o' \ar[r]_{v'} & Y_1' \, , }\]
compatible with (2.3.5.1). The category \(\mathcal{A}\) being triangulated on \(I\), this diagram can be extended into a morphism of triangles
\[(X_o', Y_o', Z_o') \longrightarrow (X_1', Y_1', Z_1') \, .\]
Composing the latter with the morphisms (2.3.5.2), we see that the diagram (2.3.5.1) can be extended into a morphism of triangles, which establishes 2.2.3 (iii).

Let's check (ii), assuming for example \(\mathcal{A}\) fibered on \(I\). If \(A_1 \in \operatorname{Ob} (\mathcal{A}_1(S_1^{-1}))\), a reciprocal image of \(A_1\) in \(\mathcal{A}(S^{-1})\) is an object \(B\) of \(\mathcal{A}_o(S_o^{-1})\) such that, in \(\mathcal{A}_o(S_o^{-1})\), we have
\[\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{A}_o(S_o^{-1})}(A_o,B) = \operatorname{Hom}_F(A_o^-, A_1) = \operatorname{Hom}(A_o^-, F^*(A_1)) = \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{A}_o(S_o^{-1})}(A_o, F^*(A_1^+)).\]

There exists such an object \(B\) if and only if the Ind-object \(F^*(A_1^+)\) of \(\mathcal{A}_o(S_o^{-1})\) is essentially constant (i.e. representable), and \(B\) then represents this ind-object, which is none other than \(RF(A_1)\) (1.2.1), hence the assertion.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.3.6

With the notations of 2.3.3, if \(\mathcal{A}\) is almost differentiable on \(\mathcal{B}\), then \(\mathcal{A}(S^{-1})\) is a triangulated category on \(\mathcal{B}\).

First of all, the fiber category \(\mathcal{A}(S^{-1})_x\) can be identified with \(\mathcal{A}_x(S_x^{-1})\): we apply the hypothesis to the change of base \(\{x\} \longrightarrow \mathcal{B}\). This category is triangulated, provided with a set \(\Delta_x\) of triangles, which gives meaning to the previous statement. To check condition 2.2.3 (iii), just do it after any change of base \(I \longrightarrow \mathcal{B}\), and the assertion results from 2.3.5 (i).
It immediately follows from the demonstration of 2.3.5 (ii) that:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.3.7

(trivial duality formula).

Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be a triangulated category fibered and cofibered on \(I\), and let us designate by \(F_*\) and \(F^*\) the direct image and reciprocal image functors. Let \(S = S_o \cup S_1\) a multiplicative system as in 2.3.3. We assume that \(LF_*\) is defined in \(A_o \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{A}_o(S_o^{-1})\) and that \(RF^*\) is defined in \(A_1 \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{A}_1(S_1^{-1})\). We then have

\[\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{A}_o(S_o^{-1})}(A_o, RF^*A_1) = \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{A}(S^{-1})}(A_o, A_1) = \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{A}_1(S_1^{-1})}(LF_* A_o, A_1) \, .\]

The position of the symbols \(R\), \(L\), \(*\) and \({}_*\) in 2.3.7 is aberrant only in appearance; in fact, in the fibered and cofibered 0.2 category of bundles on variable sites, the fiber categories are the opposite categories of the usual categories of bundles.
2.4. Categories fiberized into derived categories.

2.4.0. In this number we designate by \(\mathcal{A}\) a triangulated category cofibered on \(\mathcal{B}\) and by \(S\) a multiplicative system of morphisms of \(\mathcal{A}\), all above an identity, and such that \(S_x = S \cap \operatorname{Fl}(\mathcal{A}_x)\) is a multiplicative system saturated with \(\mathcal{A}_x\) [CD 1.2.1] for all \(x \in \operatorname{Ob}(\mathcal{B})\).

We are looking for a criterion so that \(\mathcal{A}\) is co-differentiable relative to \(S\) (2.3.3), which implies that \(\mathcal{A}(S^{-1})\) is triangulated on \(\mathcal{B}\) when it is provided with the set of triangles isomorphic to the image of a triangle distinguished from \(\mathcal{A}\) (2.3.5).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.4.1

Let \(\mathcal{A}\), \(\mathcal{B}\) and \(S\) as above. For \(\mathcal{A}\) to be coderivable relative to \(S\), it is sufficient that the following condition be verified:

(*) \(\forall x \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{B}, \forall A \in \operatorname{Ob}(\mathcal{A}_x), \exists\, s : A' \longrightarrow A\) in \(S\) \(\forall g,f : x \xrightarrow{f} y \xrightarrow{g} z\), \(f_*A'\) is left deployed for the functor \(g_*\).

Let us denote by \(\mathcal{A}^1\) the full subcategory of \(\mathcal{A}\) formed of objects whose all direct images are deployed relative to all direct image functors. Then 1.2.2 shows that \(\mathcal{A}^1\) is a triangulated subcategory of \(\mathcal{A}\), obviously cofibered. We set \(S^1 = S \cap \operatorname{Fl}(\mathcal{A}^1)\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.4.1.1

Suppose that \(S\) is stable by direct images. Then, \(\mathcal{A}/\mathcal{B}\) is co-differentiable and \(\mathcal{A}(S^{-1})\) can be calculated by calculating fractions on the right.
Let's check condition c) of [1, I 2.2], if possible less trivial than the others. We give ourselves \(f\) and \(s\) (\(s \in S\)) and we must find
\[(2.4.1.2)\qquad \xymatrix{ A' \ar@{-->}[r] & B' \\ A \ar[r] \ar[u]^{s} & B \ar[u]_{t} }\]

a commutative diagram (2.4.1.2) with \(t \in S\). Let \(F\) be the image of \(f\) in \(\mathcal{B}\). By hypothesis \(F_*(s)\) is still in \(S\), so that, according to VERDIER, there exists a commutative diagram
\[\xymatrix{ F_*(A') \ar[r] & B' \\ F_*(A) \ar[r]_{f} \ar[u]^{s} & B \ar[u]_{t} }\]
so also a diagram (2.4.1.2).
Because \(\mathcal{A}(S^{-1})\) is calculated by calculating fractions on the right, it is trivial that its formation commutes to the change of base \(c : \mathcal{B}' \longrightarrow \mathcal{B}\).
This lemma applies in particular to \(\mathcal{A}^1\) and \(S^1\); the following lemma therefore implies 2.4.1:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.4.1.3

On \(\mathcal{B}\) and after any base change \(c : \mathcal{B}' \longrightarrow \mathcal{B}\), the canonical functor \(\varphi : \mathcal{A}^1(S^{1-1}) \longrightarrow \mathcal{A}(S^{-1})\) is a category equivalence.

Let's construct a quasi-inverse functor to the functor \(\varphi\). Whatever \(A \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{A}\), let's choose \(s_A \in S\) with goal \(A\) and source \(A^1 \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{A}^1\) with \(s_A = \operatorname{Id}_A\) for \(A \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{A}^1\). Let \(f\) be an arrow of \(\mathcal{A}\), of goal \(A\), of source \(B\) and of image \(F\) in \(\mathcal{B}\). Since \(s_B \in S\) and \(F_*A^1 \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{A}^1\), we can find \(B^1\), \(v\) and \(s \in S^1\) making the following diagram commutative
\[(2.4.1.4)\qquad \xymatrix{ A \ar[r]^{f} & B \ar[r]^{s_B} & B_1 \\ A_1 \ar[r]_{a} \ar[u]^{s_A} & F_*A_1 \ar[u] & B' \ar[l]^{s} \ar[u]_{u} }\]
The arrow \(f^1 = us^{-1}a\) of \(\mathcal{A}^1(S^{1-1})\) then only depends on \(f\).

Let \(f\) and \(g\) be two composable arrows, with images \(F\) and \(G\) in \(\mathcal{B}\). To prove that \((g \circ f)^1 = g^1 \circ f^1\), consider the following diagram, which shows diagrams (2.4.1.4) for \(f\) and \(g\):

\[\xymatrix@C=1.5em@R=2.4em{ A \ar[r]^{f} & B \ar[r]^{g} & C & & & C^1 \ar[dd]^{v} \\ A^1 \ar[u]^{s_A} \ar[r]^{a} & F_*A^1 \ar[u] \ar[r]^{a'} & G_*F_*A^1 \ar[u] & & & \\ B \ar[u]^{u} \ar[r]_{b'} & B^1 \ar[u]_{s} \ar[r]_{b} & G_*B^1 \ar[u] & G_*B' \ar[l]^{G_*s} & C'' \ar[l]_{r} \ar[r]_{w} & C' }\]

Since \(t \in S\), there exists \(C''\), \(r \in S^1\) and \(w\) which makes it commutative. By definition, \(g^1 \circ f^1 = vt^{-1} b u s^{-1}a\) and \((g \circ f)^1 = vw\, (G_*(s)r)^{-1} s'a\), and we conclude with the commutativity of the diagram.

We have therefore defined a functor of \(\mathcal{A}\) in \(\mathcal{A}^1(S^{1-1})\); it factors into \(\psi : \mathcal{A}(S^{-1}) \longrightarrow \mathcal{A}^1(S^{1-1})\), the universal property of \(\mathcal{A}(S^{-1})\). The compound \(\varphi\, \psi\) is isomorphic to the identity because its compound with the functor \(J : \mathcal{A} \longrightarrow \mathcal{A}(S^{-1})\) is isomorphic to \(J\). We also check that \(\psi\, \varphi\) is isomorphic to the identity.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.4.2

With the notations of 2.4.1, \(\mathcal{A}\) is coderivable relative to \(S\) as soon as, for any finite set \(B\) of arrows of \(\mathcal{B}\), the condition (*) is verified when we limit ourselves to considering only the direct images by the arrows of \(B\).

Regardless of the \(\mathcal{B}'\) category of finite arrow set, the prop. 2.4.1 will be applicable after any change of base category \(\mathcal{B}' \longrightarrow \mathcal{B}\). We will take for \(\mathcal{B}'\) the categories of the following diagrams:
\[(0)\; * \qquad\quad (1)\; * \to * \qquad\quad (2)\; * \to * \to * \qquad\quad (3)\; * \to * \to * \to * \, .\]

Consider the categories \(\mathcal{A}_x(S_x^{-1})\) (\(x \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{B}\)). Given \(f \in \operatorname{Fl}(\mathcal{B})\) and \(A_1, A_2\) in these categories above the source and purpose of \(f\), we define \(\operatorname{Hom}_f(A_1,A_2)\) to be the analogous set defined after the base category change of type (1) defined by \(f\).
