We define the composition of morphisms using base category changes of type (3). We thus construct a category \(\mathcal{A}(S^{-1})^*\) on \(\mathcal{B}\), whose formation is compatible with any change of base; we verify that it is a solution to the universal problem which defines \(\mathcal{A}(S^{-1})\) using base changes of type (0), (1) and (2).
The following result is the dual of 2.4.2.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.4.3

Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be a triangulated category fibered on \(\mathcal{B}\) and \(S\) as in 2.4.1. For \(\mathcal{A}\) to be differentiable relatively to \(S\), it is sufficient that for any finite set \(B\) of arrows of \(\mathcal{B}\), we have

(*') \(\forall x \in \operatorname{Ob}(\mathcal{B}), \forall A \in \operatorname{Ob}(\mathcal{A}_x), \exists\, s : A \longrightarrow A'\) in \(S\), \(\forall g,f : Z \xrightarrow{g} Y \xrightarrow{f} X\) in \(B\), \(f^*A'\) is deployed to the right for the functor \(g^*\).

pages.title: 3. Joining of fibered or cofibered categories.

blocks.html: 3. Joining of fibered or cofibered categories.
3.1. Introduction.

Let \(f : Y \longrightarrow X\) be a separate morphism of finite type from a schema \(Y\) into a schema \(X\), or a continuous map from a locally compact space to a locally compact space. We then know how to define a "direct image with proper support" functor, denoted \(f_!\), from the category of abelian sheaves on \(Y\) in the category of abelian sheaves on \(X\) (see 5.3 in the case of diagrams, and VERDIER [3] for the case of separated locally compact spaces). When \(Y\) and \(X\) are schemes of finite type over \(\mathbb{C}\), these two definitions are compatible in an obvious sense. Unfortunately in the case of schemes, contrary to what happens in the case of locally compact spaces, the functors derived from the functor \(f_!\) are pathological; we can however define a reasonable "direct image with proper support" functor, directly from the category \(D^+(Y)\) in \(D^+(X)\). To define it, we factor the morphism \(f\) into an open immersion and a proper morphism: \(f = \bar{f} j\). The searched functor \(Rf_!\) is then defined as composed of the "extension by 0" \(j_!\) and the functor \(R\bar{f}_*\), derived from the direct image functor by \(\bar{f}\).
These functors \(Rf_!\), "direct image with proper support" can only be used if we verify for them a "variance formalism" including, for a compound \(f = gh\), a formula

which is best expressed in terms of cofibered categories. We have such a formalism separately for "extensions by zero" and for direct images by a proper morphism, and the problem is to put these formalisms together. To avoid being bogged down later by stupid calculations, we propose in this § to draw up a list of the basic checks required to carry out such a joining successfully. We will do it in a slightly more abstract framework than is essential, hoping that the result will be useful again.

The construction sketched above of the functor \(Rf_!\) requires that the morphism \(f\) can be factorized into an open immersion and a proper morphism. On the HARTSHORNE model [RD III 8 p.189], we introduce in No. 2 the adequate notion of compactifiable morphism.
3.2. Compactifiable morphisms.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.2.1

A \(f\) morphism of a quasi-compact, quasi-separated \(S\)-schema \(X\) into a \(S\)-schema \(Y\) is said to be \(S\)-compactifiable if it exists a proper \(S\)-scheme \(P\) on \(S\) and a factorization of \(f\) into a separate quasi-finite morphism \(j\) of \(X\) into \(P \times_S Y\) followed by the projection of \(P \times_S Y\) in \(Y\)

\[\xymatrix@C=1.4em@R=1.8em{ & & P \times_S Y \ar[ddl] \ar[drr] & & \\ X \ar[urr]^{j} \ar[dr]_{f} & & & & P \ar[dl] \\ & Y \ar[rr] & & S & }\]
When \(S = Y\), we will simply speak of a compactifiable morphism.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 3.2.2

(i) A morphism \(S\)-compactifiable is separated from finite type.

(ii) If \(Y\) is quasi-compact, quasi-separated, a quasi-finite morphism separated from goal \(Y\) is compactifiable.

(iii) The compound of two morphisms \(S\)-compactifiable is still \(S\)-compactifiable.

(iv) Let be a diagram of \(S\)-quasi-separated quasi-compact schemes

\[\xymatrix@R=2.2em@C=2em{ X \times_Y Y' \ar[r] \ar[d]_{f'} & X \ar[d]^{f} \\ Y' \ar[r] & Y \, . }\]

If \(f\) is \(S\)-compactifiable, then \(f'\) is \(S\)-compactifiable.

(v) Let \(g\) a morphism \(S\)-compactifiable; for a compound morphism \(g \circ h\) to be \(S\)-compactifiable, it is necessary that \(h\) be so.
Assertions (i) (ii) and (iv) are trivial. To prove (iii) and (v), consider a compound \(f = gh\), \(g\) being compactifiable.
Assume \(gh\) compactifiable, hence a diagram
\[\xymatrix@C=2.4em@R=2em{ & P \times_S Y \ar[r]^{v} \ar[d] & P \times_S Z \ar[d] \\ X \ar[ur]^{u} \ar[r]_{h} & Y \ar[r]_{g} & Z \, . }\]
The compound \(vu\) is almost finished; \(u\) is therefore quasi-finite and (v) is proven.
If \(h\) is compactifiable, we have a diagram

\[\xymatrix@C=1em@R=1.5em{ & & (Q \times_S P) \times_S Z \ar[dr] & & \\ Q \times_S Y \ar[ur] \ar[d] \ar[drrr] & & & P \times_S Z \ar[dl] \ar[dr] & \\ X \ar[u] \ar[r]_-{h} & Y \ar[rr]_-{g} \ar[dr] & & Z \ar[d] & P \ar[dl] \\ & & Q \ar[r] & S & }\]
and \(f = gh\) is factorized by a separate quasi-finite morphism into \((Q \times_S P) \times_S Z\).
Nagata asserts in [5] that any finite type morphism between separate integral Noetherian schemes is compactifiable. The editor admits not having understood the demonstration.

Let us now fix a schema \(S\) and designate by \((S)\) the subcategory of the category of \(S\)-schemas whose objects are the \(S\)-quasi-compact, quasi-separated schemas and whose arrows are the \(S\)-\(S\)-compactifiable morphisms.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 3.2.3

In category \((S)\):

(i) Open immersions (resp. proper morphisms) define a subcategory \((S,i)\) (resp. \((S,p)\)) of \((S)\) having the same objects as \((S)\).

(ii) Fiber products exist in \((S,p)\) and are fiber products in \((S)\).

(iii) Every morphism \(f\) is the compound \(f = pj\) of a proper morphism \(p\) and of an open immersion.

Under 3.2.2 (iii) (iv) and (v), fiber products exist in \((S)\) and are fiber products in the all schemes category. Since the fiber product \(X = X_1 \times_Y X_2\) of two proper schemas on \(Y\) is still proper on \(Y\), and a schema is proper on \(X\) if and only if it is proper on \(X_1\) and \(X_2\), condition (ii) is satisfied.

If \(f\) is a morphism of \((S)\), it admits by hypothesis, in \((S)\), a factorization \(f = pj'\), where \(p\) is proper and \(j\) quasi-finitely separated. Under EGA IV 18.12.13, \(j'\) admits a factorization \(j' = qj\), where \(q\) is finite and \(j\) is an open immersion; furthermore, by virtue of 3.2.2 (ii), \(q\) and \(j\) are in \((S)\). We then have \(f = (pq)j\), which proves (iii). Assertion (i) follows from 3.2.2 (iii).
3.2.4. In the remainder of this §, we assume given a category \((S)\), provided with two subcategories \((S,i)\) and \((S,p)\), such that
(i) \(\operatorname{Ob}(S) = \operatorname{Ob}(S,i) = \operatorname{Ob}(S,p)\);
(ii) fiber products exist in \((S,p)\) and are fiber products in \((S)\);
(iii) \(\forall f \in \operatorname{Fl}(S), \exists\, p \in \operatorname{Fl}(S,p), \exists\, j \in \operatorname{Fl}(S,i), \; f = pj\).
The arrows of \((S,i)\) (resp. of \((S,p)\)) will be called open immersions (resp. proper morphisms).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.2.5

(i) A compactification of a morphism \(f\) of \((S)\) (resp. of a pair \((f,g)\), resp. of a triple \((f,g,h)\) of composable morphisms) is a commutative diagram (3.2.5.1) (resp. (3.2.5.2), resp. (3.2.5.3)) whose horizontal arrows are open immersions and whose oblique arrows are clean.

(ii) A morphism of compactifications is a morphism of diagrams (3.2.5.1) (resp. ...) which is the identity on the 1st vertical line and proper in all vertices

\[(3.2.5.1) \qquad \xymatrix@C=1.6em@R=1.8em{ Y \ar@{^{(}->}[r]^{i} \ar[d]_{f} & \bar{Y} \ar[dl]^{\bar{f}} \\ X & }\]

\[(3.2.5.2) \qquad \xymatrix@C=1.6em@R=1.8em{ Z \ar@{^{(}->}[r]^{j} \ar[d]_{g} & \bar{Z} \ar[dl]_{\bar{g}} \ar@{^{(}->}[r]^{j'} & \bar{\bar{Z}} \ar[dll]^{\bar{\bar{g}}} \\ Y \ar@{^{(}->}[r]_{i} \ar[d]_{f} & \bar{Y} \ar[dl]^{\bar{f}} & \\ X & & }\]

\[(3.2.5.3) \qquad \xymatrix@C=1.4em@R=1.6em{ T \ar@{^{(}->}[r]^{h} \ar[d]_{k} & \bar{T} \ar[dl]_{\bar{h}} \ar@{^{(}->}[r]^{h'} & \bar{\bar{T}} \ar[dll]|(0.4){\bar{\bar{h}}} \ar@{^{(}->}[r]^{h''} & \bar{\bar{\bar{T}}} \ar[dlll]^{\bar{\bar{\bar{h}}}} \\ Z \ar@{^{(}->}[r]^{j} \ar[d]_{g} & \bar{Z} \ar[dl]_{\bar{g}} \ar@{^{(}->}[r]^{j'} & \bar{\bar{Z}} \ar[dll]^{\bar{\bar{g}}} & \\ Y \ar@{^{(}->}[r]_{i} \ar[d]_{f} & \bar{Y} \ar[dl]^{\bar{f}} & & \\ X & & & }\]

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 3.2.6

(i) Any morphism (resp. any pair \((f,g)\), resp. any triple \((f,g,h)\), of composable morphisms) admits compactification.

(ii) The category of compactifications of \(f\) is left filtering.

(iii) If diagrams \((3.2.4.2)_i\) \((i=1,2)\) are compactifications of \((f,g)\), there exists a compactification \((3.2.4.2)_3\) of \((f,g)\) and morphisms of compactifications of \((3.2.4.2)_3\) in compactifications \((3.2.4.2)_i\).

(i) The case of a morphism is none other than 3.2.4 (iii). Let us deal with the case of a triple; with the notations of (3.2.5.3), it suffices to successively construct compactifications of \(f\), \(g\) and \(h\), of \(i\bar{g}\) and \(j\bar{h}\), and finally of \(j'\bar{\bar{h}}\).

(ii) Let \((3.2.5.1)_1\) and \((3.2.5.1)_2\) be two compactifications of \(f\). The projection \(\rho\) of \(\bar{Y}_1 \times_X \bar{Y}_2\) into \(X\) is clean and \(Y\) is sent there by \((i_1,i_2)\), which (3.2.4 (iii)) can be compactified into \((i_1,i_2) = qj\). The compactification \(f = (\rho \cdot q) j\) dominates \((3.2.4.1)_1\) and \((3.2.4.1)_2\).

If \(r\) and \(s\) are two morphisms of \((3.2.4.1)_1\) in \((3.2.4.1)_2\), the kernel \(K\) of the double arrow \(\bar{Y}_1 \xrightarrow[s]{\;r\;} \bar{Y}_2\) is sent by a proper morphism in \(\bar{Y}_1\) and \(i_1\) is factorized by \(K\): i.e. \(i_1 = k\, i\). If \(i = p\, i_3\) is a compactification of \(i\), the existence of the compactification \(f = (\bar{f}_1\, k\, p) i_3\) shows that the axiom \(L_2\) is satisfied.

(iii) Let us apply the axiom \(L_1\) of filtering categories to the compactifications of \(f\) and \(g\) deduced from \((3.2.4.2)_i\) \((i = 1,2)\). We thus find \(\bar{Y}_3\) and \(\bar{Z}_3\) giving rise to commutative diagrams \((3.2.5.1)_i\) \((i=1,2)\)

\[(3.2.5.1)_i \qquad \xymatrix@C=1.6em@R=1.6em{ & \bar{Z}_i \ar@{^{(}->}[r] \ar[d] & \bar{\bar{Z}}_i \ar[d] \\ \bar{Z}_3 \ar[ur] \ar[dr]_{\bar{g}_3} & Y \ar[r] & \bar{Y}_i \\ & Y \ar[r]_{i_3} & \bar{Y}_3 \ar[u] }\]

and it remains to find a compactification of \(i_3\, \bar{g}_3\) by \(\bar{Z}_3\), such that \(\bar{Z}_3\) can be sent by a proper morphism in \(\bar{Z}_i\), so as to give rise to a commutative cube \((i = 1,2)\). If the goal of a morphism between compactifications of \(i_3\bar{g}_3\) has this property, the source still has it and by virtue of (ii), it will suffice to deal separately with the cases \(i = 1\) and \(i = 2\).

Let \(T\) be the fibered product of \(\bar{Y}_3\) and \(\bar{\bar{Z}}_i\) on \(\bar{Y}_i\): then \(T\) appears in a commutative cube similar to the one we are looking for, except that the arrow \(k\) of \(\bar{Z}_3\) in \(T\) might not be open immersion. Setting \(k = pj\) (\(p\) clean, \(j\) open immersion) and replacing \(T\) with the source \(\bar{Z}_3\) of \(p\), we find the cube we are looking for.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 3.2.7

Proposition 3.2.3 would still have been valid if, in definition 3.2.1 of compactifiable morphisms, we had omitted to require that \(Y\) be quasi-compact quasi-separated and demanded in compensation that \(j\) be a quasi-compact open immersion and not just a morphism almost finished.
3.3. Regluing.
Remember that assumptions 3.2.4 are satisfied.
3.3.1. Suppose given:
\(\alpha\)) for each \(X \in \operatorname{Ob}(S)\), a category \(F(X)\),
\(\beta\)) a \((S,i)\)-cofibered category \(F_i\) and a \((S,p)\) cofibered category \(F_p\),
\(\gamma\)) for each \(X \in \operatorname{Ob}(S)\), isomorphisms between \(F(X)\), \((F_i)_X\) and \((F_p)_X\),
\(\delta\)) a normalized split of \(F_i\) and a normalized split of \(F_p\).
Under (SGA 1 VI), it "is the same" to give instead \(\alpha\)) and:
(i) for any proper morphism \(p : X \longrightarrow Y\), a functor \(p_* : F(X) \longrightarrow F(Y)\),
(ii) for any composable pair \((p,q)\) of proper morphisms, an isomorphism of functor \(c_{p,q} : p_* q_* \longleftrightarrow (pq)_*\),
(i)' and (ii)', analogous data for \((S,i)\),
this data verifies the following conditions:
(a) if \(p\) is an identity, then \(p_*\), \(c_{p,q}\) and \(c_{q,p}\) are identities,
(b) for any composable triple of proper morphisms, we have
\[c_{p,qr} \bullet (p_* \ast c_{q,r}) = c_{pq,r} \circ (c_{p,q} \ast r_*) \, ,\]
(a)' and (b)', analogous conditions for \((S,i)\).
Suppose given more,
(iii) for any commutative diagram \(D\)
\[\xymatrix@C=2em@R=2em{ X \ar[r]^{j'} \ar[d]_{p'} & Y \ar[d]^{p} \\ Z \ar[r]_{j} & T } \qquad j,j' \in \operatorname{Fl}(S,i) \; ; \; p,p' \in \operatorname{Fl}(S,p),\]
an isomorphism of functor \(d(D) : p_* j'_* \longleftrightarrow j_* p'_*\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 3.3.2

If data (i) (ii) (i') (ii') (iii) satisfies the conditions (a) (b) (a') (b') as well as (c) and (c') stated below, then there is one and "essentially" only one category \(F\) cofibered on \(S\), equipped with isomorphisms \(F \times_S (S,i) = F_i\) and \(F \times_S (S,p) = F_p\), compatible with the data 3.3.1 \(\gamma\)) and such as the isomorphisms \(d(D)\) of (iii) be the compound isomorphisms \(j_* p'_* = (jp')_* = (pj')_* = p_* j'_*\).
(c) For any commutative diagram of the following type
whose horizontal arrows are open immersions and whose vertical arrows are proper, the following triangle of isomorphisms \(d\) of (iii) is commutative:
\[\xymatrix@C=1.5em@R=2.2em{ p_{3*} i'_* i_* \ar[rr] \ar[dr] & & j'_* j_* p_{1*} \ar[dl] \\ & j'_* p_{2*} i_* & } \qquad .\]
(c') For any commutative diagram of the following type
whose horizontal arrows are proper and whose vertical arrows are open immersions, the following triangle of isomorphisms \(d\) of (iii) is commutative:
\[\xymatrix@C=1.5em@R=2.2em{ i_{3*} p'_* p_* \ar[rr] \ar[dr] & & q'_* q_* i_{1*} \ar[dl] \\ & q'_* i_{2*} p_* & } \qquad .\]
3.3.2.1. Definition of\(f_*\).

Let \(f\) be a morphism of \(Y\) into \(X\). Each compactification of \(f\), of diagram (3.2.5(i)), defines a functor composed \(\bar{f}_* i_*\) of \(F(Y)\) in \(F(X)\). A morphism of compactifications defines a commutative diagram
Data (iii) therefore provides us with an isomorphism

The axiom (c') further guarantees that the isomorphism associated with a compound of compactification morphisms is the compound of the isomorphisms associated with each of them. The category of compactification morphisms being filtering (3.2.6 (ii)), we thus obtain a transitive system of isomorphisms between the functors associated with the various compactifications of \(f\). Choosing one of them, we define the functor \(f_*\).
3.3.2.3. Transitivity homomorphisms.

Any compactification of a pair \((f,g)\) of composable morphisms (diagram (3.2.5.2)) defines a compactification of \(f\), one of \(g\), one of \(f \circ g = (\bar{f}\,\bar{\bar{g}})(j'j)\), and an isomorphism \(c_{f,g} : (fg)_* \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} f_* g_*\), composed of the isomorphisms
(where the median isomorphism comes from (iii)).

Let us prove that this isomorphism does not depend on the chosen compactification of \((f,g)\). By virtue of 3.2.6 (iii), it will be sufficient to compare the isomorphisms \(c_{f,g}\) obtained from two compactifications \((3.2.4.2)_i\) \((i=1,2)\) such that there exists a compactification morphism of the first in the second. The arrows of these morphisms will be designated by \(p\).
Consider the following diagram of isomorphisms of functors. To make it easier to read, we omitted writing the \(\ast\).

\[\xymatrix@C=0.5em@R=2.4em{ \bar{f}_2\bar{\bar{g}}_2 j'_2 j_2 \ar[rr] \ar[dd] & & \bar{f}_2\bar{\bar{g}}_2\, p\, j'_1 j_1 \ar[rr] \ar[dd] & & \bar{f}_1\bar{\bar{g}}_1 j'_1 j_1 \ar[dd] \\ & \bar{f}_2\bar{\bar{g}}_2 j'_2\, p\, j_1 \ar[dl] & (4) & \bar{f}_2\, p\, \bar{g}_1 j'_1 j_1 \ar[dr] & \\ \bar{f}_2 i_2 \bar{g}_2 j_2 \ar[r] & \bar{f}_2 i_2 \bar{g}_2\, p\, j_1 \ar[drr] & (3)\quad(5) & \bar{f}_2\, p\, i_1 \bar{g}_1 j_1 \ar[r] & \bar{f}_1 i_1 \bar{g}_1 j_1 \\ & & \bar{f}_2 i_2 1_Y \bar{g}_1 j_1 \ar[u] & & }\]
a) The compound isomorphism of the 1st line is isomorphism 3.3.2.1 between two definitions of \((fg)_*\).
b) The compound isomorphism in the last line is isomorphism 3.3.2.1 between two definitions of \(f_* g_*\).
c) The extreme vertical isomorphisms are the isomorphisms \(c_{f,g}\) between \((fg)_*\) and \(f_* g_*\) deduced from one or the other compactification.

The problem is therefore to prove that the outer edge of the diagram is commutative. A commutative diagram of functors remains commutative when we compose each of them with the same functor. This recalled, the commutativity of (i) results from (c) applied to the diagram
The commutativity of (3) and (5) is trivial, that of (2), which essentially concerns proper morphisms, is also trivial. It remains to consider the hexagon (4).
Consider the following commutative cube

\[\xymatrix@!0@C=4em@R=3.2em{ & \bar{Z}_2 \ar[rr]^{j'_2} \ar[dd]_(0.6){\bar{g}_2} & & \bar{\bar{Z}}_2 \ar[dd]^{\bar{\bar{g}}_2} \\ \bar{Z}_1 \ar[ur] \ar[rr]^(0.7){j'_1} \ar[dd]_{\bar{g}_1} & & \bar{\bar{Z}}_1 \ar[ur] \ar[dd]^(0.3){\bar{\bar{g}}_1} & \\ & Y \ar@{=}[dl] \ar'[r][rr] & & \bar{Y}_2 \\ Y \ar[rr]_{i_1} & & \bar{Y}_1 \ar[ur]_{i_2} & }\]

There are six ways to traverse the edges of this cube from \(\bar{Z}_1\) to \(\bar{Y}_2\), each "adjacent" to two others; we have an isomorphism between the compound functors associated with two adjacent paths (i.e. separated by a single face) and the commutativity of (4) will result from the commutativity of the hexagon thus obtained.

The moral of this kind of diagram is that an edge represents a functor, a face an isomorphism between compound functors and a volume a compatibility condition (between the faces which are the edge). We will decompose the cube into two prisms with a triangular base (by the "plane" \(\bar{Z}_1\, \bar{\bar{Z}}_1\, Y\, \bar{Y}_2\)) to return to the hypotheses (c').
Precisely, we complete the hexagon in the following isomorphism diagram (to make it easier to read, the \(\ast\) have been omitted):

\[\xymatrix@C=1.4em@R=1.8em{ & & \bar{\bar{g}}_2 j'_2\, p \ar[dll] \ar[drr] & & \\ i_2 \bar{g}_2\, p \ar[dd] & & & & \bar{\bar{g}}_2\, p\, j'_1 \ar[dd] \\ & i_2 (\bar{g}_2 p) \ar@{-}[rr] & & (\bar{\bar{g}}_2 p) j'_1 & \\ i_2 1_Y \bar{g}_1 \ar[drr] & & & & p\, \bar{g}_1 j'_1 \ar[dll] \\ & & p\, i_1 \bar{g}_1 & & }\]
Triangles are trivially commutative and pentagons are by virtue of (c').
3.3.2.4. Cocycle condition.

Let's check that the isomorphisms \(c_{f,g}\) constructed in 3.3.2.3 satisfy a cocycle condition for a triple \((f, g, h)\) of composable morphisms. Let us introduce a compactification of \((f, g, h)\), of diagram (3.2.4.3) and consider the following diagram of isomorphisms of functors:

\[\xymatrix@C=2.2em@R=2.4em{ \bar{f} i \bar{g} j \bar{h} k \ar[rr] \ar[dd] & & \bar{f} \bar{g} j' j \bar{h} k \ar[dd] \\ & \bar{f} \bar{\bar{g}} j' \bar{\bar{h}} k' k \ar[ul] \ar[dr] & \\ \bar{f} i \bar{g} \bar{h} k' k \ar[rr] & & \bar{f} \bar{g} \bar{h} k'' k' k \, . }\]
The commutativity of the square is trivial, that of the triangles results from (c) and (c'). The commutativity of the outer edge being what had to be demonstrated, this completes the proof of 3.3.2.

3.3.3. Let us assume data on \((S,i)\) and \((S,p)\) respectively fiber categories with a normalized split \(F_i\) and \(F_p\), and suppose that \(F_i\) and \(F_p\) have the same fiber \(F(X)\) in all \(X \in \operatorname{Ob} S\).
According to SGA 1 VI, it “is the same thing” to give instead:
(i bis) for any proper morphism \(p : X \longrightarrow Y\), a functor \(p^* : F(Y) \longrightarrow F(X)\),
(ii bis) for any composable pair \((p,q)\) of proper morphisms, an isomorphism of functor \(c_{p,q} : q^* p^* \longleftrightarrow (pq)^*\),
(i' bis) and (ii' bis) analogous data for \((S,i)\),
these data satisfy the dual conditions (a bis) (b bis) (a' bis) (b' bis) of 3.3.1 (a)(b)(a')(b').
Suppose given more,
(iii bis) for any commutative diagram \(D\)
\[\xymatrix@C=2em@R=2em{ X \ar[r]^{j'} \ar[d]_{p'} & Y \ar[d]^{p} \\ Z \ar[r]_{j} & T } \qquad j,j' \in \operatorname{Fl}(S,i) \; ; \; p\, p' \in \operatorname{Fl}(S,p)\]
an isomorphism of functor \(d(D) : j'^* p^* \longleftrightarrow p'^* j^*\).
We leave it to the reader to state the dual conditions (c bis) and (c' bis) of 3.3.2 (c) and (c').
Reasonings parallel to those which precede then prove the following proposition.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 3.3.4

If data (i bis)(ii bis)(i' bis)(ii' bis)(iii bis) satisfies the conditions (a bis)(b bis)(a' bis)(b' bis)(c bis)(c' bis), there exists one and essentially a category \(F\) fibered on \(S\) and provided with isomorphisms \(F \times_S (S,i) = F_i\), \(F \times_S (S,p) = F_p\), these isomorphisms being compatible with the identifications \(F_{iX} = F_{pX}\) \((X \in \operatorname{Ob} S)\) and such as isomorphisms \(d(D)\) of (iii bis) become the compound isomorphisms \(p'^* j^* = (jp')^* = (pj')^* = j'^* p^*\).
Note however that 3.3.4 is not dual to 3.3.2, because the assumptions made on \((S,i)\) and \((S,p)\) are not autodual.

pages.title: 4. Resolutions. Application to the base change arrow.

blocks.html: 4. Resolutions. Application to the base change arrow.
4.1. Flat resolutions.

Recall that if \(f : (X, \mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow (Y, \mathcal{B})\) is a morphism of ringed sites (or topos), the reciprocal image by \(f\) of a bundle of \(\mathcal{B}\)-modules on the left \(\mathcal{M}\) is the bundle of \(\mathcal{A}\)-modules on the left \(\mathcal{A} \otimes_{f^*\mathcal{B}} f^*\mathcal{M}\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 4.1.1

Let \(f : (X, \mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow (Y, \mathcal{B})\) a morphism of ringed sites, \(K\) a sheaf of \(\mathcal{A}\)-modules on the right, and \(0 \longrightarrow L \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow N \longrightarrow 0\) an exact sequence of sheaves of \(\mathcal{B}\)-modules on the left. If \(M\) and \(N\) are flat, then

(i) \(L\) is flat,

(ii) the following sequence is correct:

\[0 \longrightarrow K \otimes_{\mathcal{A}} f^*L \longrightarrow K \otimes_{\mathcal{A}} f^*M \longrightarrow K \otimes_{\mathcal{A}} f^*N \longrightarrow 0 \, .\]

Let's factor \(f\) into the arrows \((X, \mathcal{A}) \xrightarrow{\;u\;} (X, f^*\mathcal{B}) \xrightarrow{\;v\;} (Y, \mathcal{B})\). The functor \(v^*\) is exact, and transforms Flat Modules into Flat Modules (IV 15) so that it suffices to prove 4.1.1 for \(u\). Isomorphism
\[(4.1.1.1) \qquad K \otimes_{\mathcal{A}} (\mathcal{A} \otimes_{f^*\mathcal{B}} P) = K \otimes_{f^*\mathcal{B}} P\]
then brings us back to the case where \(f\) is the identity.
Let us be an exact sequence \(0 \longrightarrow R \longrightarrow P \longrightarrow K \longrightarrow 0\) with flat \(P\) (4.1.2). The rows and columns of the following diagram are correct:

\[\xymatrix@C=1.6em@R=1.6em{ & & 0 \ar[d] & & \\ & R \otimes L \ar[r] \ar[d] & P \otimes L \ar[r] \ar[d] & K \otimes L \ar[r] \ar[d] & 0 \\ 0 \ar[r] & R \otimes M \ar[r] \ar[d] & P \otimes M \ar[r] \ar[d] & K \otimes M \ar[r] \ar[d] & 0 \\ 0 \ar[r] & R \otimes N \ar[r] \ar[d] & P \otimes N \ar[r] \ar[d] & K \otimes N \ar[r] \ar[d] & 0 \\ & 0 & 0 & 0 & }\]
Let's apply the snake diagram to the first two columns: we find
\[0 \longrightarrow K \otimes L \longrightarrow K \otimes M \, ,\]

which proves (ii). Without further assuming \(P\) flat, we know that in the previous diagram the three columns and two rows are short exact sequences; the first line is therefore exact, i.e. \(L\) is flat.

4.1.2. Let \(\mathcal{S}\) be a subcategory of the category of ringed sites. The bundles of modules on the ringed sites of \(\mathcal{S}\) form a category \(\mathcal{M}\), abelian on \(\mathcal{S}\) (2.2.2), fibered and cofibered, whose fibers are the opposites of the usual categories of bundles of modules on a site. We will designate by \(K(\mathcal{S})\) the category \(K(\mathcal{M})\) (2.2.8) and by \(K^+(\mathcal{S})\) (resp. \(K^-(\mathcal{S})\)) the full subcategory of \(K(\mathcal{S})\), whose fiber categories are the opposites of the categories \(K^+(S)\) (resp. \(K^-(S)\)) for \(S \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{S}\). The categories \(K(\mathcal{S})\), \(K^+(\mathcal{S})\) and \(K^-(\mathcal{S})\) are triangulated on \(\mathcal{S}\), fibered and co-fibered.

Let \(f : S \longrightarrow S'\) be an arrow of \(\mathcal{S}\). Every injective is acyclic (1.2.3) for the functor \(Rf_*\), and any bundle of flat modules is acyclic for the functor \(Lf^*\) (4.1.1 and 1.2.6), hence derived functors (1.2.7)
\[(4.1.2.1) \qquad Rf_* : D^+(S) \longrightarrow D^+(S')\] \[(4.1.2.2) \qquad Lf^* : D^-(S) \longrightarrow D^-(S') \, .\]
If \(Rf_*\) (resp. \(Lf^*\)) is of finite dimension, we can in 4.1.2.1 (resp. 4.1.2.2) replace the exponent \(+\) (resp. \(-\)) by \(b\), by \(-\) (resp. \(+\)) or remove it (1.2.10).

We denote by \(D(\mathcal{S})\) (resp. \(D^+(\mathcal{S})\), resp. \(D^-(\mathcal{S})\)) the category deduced from \(K(\mathcal{S})\) (resp. \(K^+(\mathcal{S})\), resp. \(K^-(\mathcal{S})\)) by reversing the quasi-isomorphisms.

blocks.title: Scholia
blocks.html:
Scholia 4.1.3

("Trivial duality theorem").

(a) We assume that all functors \(Rf_*\) (resp. \(Lf^*\)) are of finite dimension. So:

(i) The formation of \(D(\mathcal{S})\) commutes on any change of base category \(\mathcal{S}' \longrightarrow \mathcal{S}\). The category \(D(\mathcal{S})\) is triangulated on \(\mathcal{S}\) (2.2.3), and its fibers are identified with the opposites of the categories \(D(S)\) for \(S \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{S}\).

(ii) The category \(D(\mathcal{S})\) is co-fibered (resp. fibered) on \(\mathcal{S}\). Let \(f : S \longrightarrow S'\); if \(Lf^*\) (resp. \(Rf_*\)) is of finite dimension, then every object of \(D(S')\) (resp. \(D(S)\)) has a reciprocal (resp. direct) image by \(f\) in the sense of the category \(D(\mathcal{S})\) on \(\mathcal{S}\). The reciprocal image and direct image functors identify with \(Lf^*\) and \(Rf_*\).

(iii) Every object of \(D^-(S')\) (resp. \(D^+(S)\)) has a reciprocal (resp. direct) image in the sense of \(D(\mathcal{S})\) and the reciprocal (resp. direct) image functor is identified with \(Lf^*\) (resp. \(Rf_*\)).

(b) (i) The formation of \(D^-(\mathcal{S})\) (resp. \(D^+(\mathcal{S})\)) commutes on any change of base category \(\mathcal{S}' \longrightarrow \mathcal{S}\). The category \(D^-(\mathcal{S})\) (resp. \(D^+(\mathcal{S})\)) is triangulated on \(\mathcal{S}\), and its fibers identify with the opposites of the categories \(D^-(S)\) (resp. \(D^+(S)\)).

(ii) The category \(D^-(\mathcal{S})\) (resp. \(D^+(\mathcal{S})\)) is fibered (resp. cofibered) on \(\mathcal{S}\); the "reciprocal image" (resp. "direct image") functors identify with the derived functors \(Lf^*\) (resp. \(Rf_*\)).

(iii) If \(f : S \longrightarrow S'\) is such that \(Rf_*\) (resp. \(Lf^*\)) is of finite dimension, any object of \(D^-(S)\) (resp. \(D^+(S')\)) has a direct image in the sense of \(D^-(\mathcal{S})\) (resp. a reciprocal image in the sense of \(D^+(\mathcal{S})\)), and the functor "direct image" (resp. "reciprocal image") is identified with \(Rf_*\) (resp. \(Lf^*\)).

It will suffice to verify that in each case the hypotheses of 2.4.1 or 2.4.3 are met, the other assertions being deduced after a change of base \(\{S\} \longrightarrow \mathcal{S}\), or \(I \longrightarrow \mathcal{S}\), of 2.3.5 and 1.2.10. In case (b), the flat (resp. flaccid) bundle complexes satisfy the assumptions of 2.4.1. In case (a), we will use 2.4.3 so as to only have to consider \(f\) such that the dimension of \(Rf_*\) (resp. \(Lf^*\)) is increased by \(N\) fixed. Let us prove that there exist "enough" complexes \(K\) whose components have their direct images (resp. reciprocals) all acyclic for the direct image (reciprocal) functors considered. Let \(K\) be a complex and \(K^*\) (resp. \(K_*\)) a Cartan-Eilenberg resolution of \(K\) by complexes with flaccid (resp. flat) components. If \(n > N\), we see that \(f_* \tau''_{\leq n}(K^*) = \tau''_{\leq n} f_*(K^*)\) (resp. \(f^* \tau''_{\geq n}(K_*) = \tau''_{\geq n} f^*(K_*)\)) where \(\tau''\) designates a truncated relative to the second differential. For \(n > 2N\), we deduce that \(\tau''_{\leq n}(K^*)\) (resp. \(\tau''_{\geq n}(K_*)\)) verifies the hypothesis of 2.4.3, and every complex \(K\) is the source (resp. the goal) of a quasi-isomorphism of goal (resp. source) of such a complex.
4.1.4. Consider a commutative square of ringed sites (4.1.4.1) (cf. 0.11)
\[(4.1.4.1) \qquad \xymatrix@C=2.4em@R=2em{ X' \ar[r]^{g'} \ar[d]_{f'} & X \ar[d]^{f} \\ S' \ar[r]_{g} & S } \qquad .\]
We can consider the subcategory of the category of sites reduced to this diagram; we then use 2.1.2 to define a "base change morphism" of \(Lg^* Rf_*\) in \(Rf'_* Lg'^*\) when 4.1.3 is applicable:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 4.1.5

The arrow 2.1.2 of change of base, from \(Lg^* Rf_*\) in \(Rf'_* Lg'^*\), is defined in each of the following cases:

(i) We work in the categories \(D^+\), and \(Lg^*\), \(Lg'^*\) are of finite dimension.

(ii) We work in the categories \(D^-\), and \(Rf_*\), \(Rf'_*\) are of finite dimension.

(iii) We work in integer derived categories, and \(Rf_*\), \(Rf'_*\), \(Lg^*\), \(Lg'^*\) are of finite dimension, as well as \(Lf^*\) and \(Lf'^*\) (or: as well as \(Rg_*\) and \(Rg'_*\)).

Note that the last hypothesis of (iii) seems hoax. The editor does not know how to get rid of them except when the sites considered have enough points (4.2.12). The functorial properties of this base change arrow are immediately obtained from description 2.1.2.
4.1.6. Let \((S, \mathcal{A})\) be a ringed site, and consider the "tensor product" functor
\[\otimes : \operatorname{Mod}_d(S, \mathcal{A}^o) \times \operatorname{Mod}_g(S, \mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Mod}(S, C)\]
where \(C\) is the center of \(\mathcal{A}\).

The recipe of 1.1.6, for the application of which we will define the simple complex associated with a double complex by a sum, allows us to extend this functor into a functor still denoted \(\otimes\):
\[\otimes : K(S, \mathcal{A}^o) \times K(S, \mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow K(S, C) \, .\]

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 4.1.7

With the previous notations:

(i) The tensor product functor is left differentiable (1.2.1) into a "total tensor product" functor

\[\overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} : D^-(S, \mathcal{A}^o) \times D^-(S, \mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow D^-(S, C) \; ;\]

(ii) a pair \((K,L)\) of upper bounded complexes is deployed for \(\overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes}\) as soon as \(K\) or \(L\) has flat components.
The demonstration is standard.

blocks.title: Rating
blocks.html:
Notation 4.1.8

If \(K \in D^-(S, \mathcal{A}^o)\) and \(L \in D^-(S, \mathcal{A})\), we denote by \(K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} L\) the total tensor product of \(K\) and \(L\). The beam \(H_k(K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} L) = H^{-k}(K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} L)\) is designated by \(\operatorname{Tor}_k(K,L)\) and is called the \(k^{\text{ième}}\) local hypertor of \(K\) and \(L\).

Let \(K \in D^-(S, \mathcal{A}^o)\) and \(L \in D^-(S, \mathcal{A})\). If \(H^i(K) = 0\) for \(i > k\) and \(H^i(L) = 0\) for \(i > \ell\), we check that \(H^i(K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} L) = 0\) for \(i > k + \ell\) and that \(H^{k+\ell}(K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} L) = H^k(K) \otimes H^\ell(L)\). Let's set \(K\) and look at \(K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} L\) as a functor in \(L\). If we apply definition 1.2.9 of the cohomological amplitude as an interval of \(\mathbb{Z}\), the upper bound of this interval is of a trivial nature. It is the largest integer \(i\) such that \(H^i(K) \neq 0\). The lower bound is more interesting:

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.1.9

We say that a complex \(K \in \operatorname{Ob} D^-(S, \mathcal{A}^o)\) is of Tor-dimension \(\leq n\) if the following equivalent conditions are verified:

(i) For any \(\mathcal{A}\)-module \(L\), \(\operatorname{Tor}_k(K,L) = 0\) for \(k > n\).

(ii) For any complex of \(\mathcal{A}\)-modules \(L\) such as \(H^i(L) = 0\) for \(i < \ell\), \(H^i(K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} L) = 0\) for \(i < \ell-n\).

(iii) There exists a quasi-isomorphism of goal \(K\) and source a complex \(K'\), with flat and zero components for \(i < -n\).
4.1.10. Here are some possible variations:

(i) If \(K \in \operatorname{Ob} D^b(S, \mathcal{A}^o)\) is of finite tor-dimension and if \(L \in \operatorname{Ob} D(S, \mathcal{A})\), then the functor \(\overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes}\) is defined (1.2.1) in \((K,L)\).
(ii) If \(K \in \operatorname{Ob} K^b(S, \mathcal{A}^o)\) has finite-dimensional Tor components, the functor \(L \longmapsto K \otimes L\) \((L \in K(S, \mathcal{A}))\) is left differentiable.
(iii) If any Module is of finite Tor-dimension, the functor \(\otimes\) derives into a functor
\[\overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} : D(S, \mathcal{A}^o) \times D(S, \mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow D(S, C) \, .\]
4.2. Godement's Flabby Resolutions.

4.2.1. Let \(S\) be a topos and \(X\) be a set. It is the same to give a point family of \(S\) indexed by \(X\) or to give a morphism \(k : \operatorname{Top}(X) \longrightarrow S\) of the topos defined by the discrete topological space \(X\) in \(S\). For the said family to be conservative (IV 6.4.1), it is necessary and sufficient that for any sheaf \(F\) of \(S\), the addition arrow of \(F\) in \(k_* k^*F\) is a monomorphism (see IV 6.4.0).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.2.2

Let \(k : \operatorname{Top}(X) \longrightarrow S\) be a conservative family of points of a topos \(S\). We call Godement resolution or canonical flaccid resolution (relative to \(X\)) of an abelian sheaf \(F\) of \(S\), and we designate by \(\mathscr{C}^*_X(F)\) (or simply \(\mathscr{C}^*(F)\)), the following resolution to the right of \(F\):

1) \(\mathscr{C}^0(F) = k_* k^*F\) and \(\varepsilon : F \longrightarrow \mathscr{C}^0(F)\) is the addition arrow.

2) Let's set \(d^{-1} = \varepsilon\); we define by recurrence, for \(n \geq 0\)

\[\mathscr{C}^{n+1}(F) = \mathscr{C}^0(\operatorname{coker} d^{n-1}), \quad \text{et } d^n \text{ comme flèche composée}\]

\[d^n : \mathscr{C}^n(F) \longrightarrow \operatorname{coker}(d^{n-1}) \longrightarrow \mathscr{C}^0(\operatorname{coker}(d^{n-1})) \, .\]

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 4.2.3

Under the assumptions of 4.2.2,

(i) \(\mathscr{C}^n(F)\) is a flaccid beam (V 3.7),

(ii) \(\mathscr{C}^n(F)\) is an exact functor in \(F\),

(iii) the fiber at a point \(x \in X\) of the complex \(\mathscr{C}^*(F)\) is a canonically split resolution of \(F_x\).
The beams \(\mathscr{C}^n(F)\) are flaccid as a direct image of (automatically flaccid) beams on the discrete topological space \(X\).

The functors \(k^*\) and \(k_*\) are exact. The functor \(\mathscr{C}^0\) is therefore exact. Let us prove by induction on \(n \geq 0\) that the functor \(\mathscr{Z}^n(F) = \operatorname{coker}(d^{n-1})\) is exact, therefore also \(\mathscr{C}^{n+1} = \mathscr{C}^0 \mathscr{Z}^n\). Let's set \(\mathscr{Z}^{-1}(F) = F\). An exact sequence of beams
\[0 \longrightarrow F' \longrightarrow F \longrightarrow F'' \longrightarrow 0\]
defines for \(n \geq 0\) a diagram

\[\xymatrix@C=1.4em@R=1.4em{ & 0 \ar[d] & 0 \ar[d] & 0 \ar[d] & \\ 0 \ar[r] & \mathscr{Z}^{n-1}(F') \ar[r] \ar[d] & \mathscr{Z}^{n-1}(F) \ar[r] \ar[d] & \mathscr{Z}^{n-1}(F'') \ar[r] \ar[d] & 0 \\ 0 \ar[r] & \mathscr{C}^n(F') \ar[r] \ar[d] & \mathscr{C}^n(F) \ar[r] \ar[d] & \mathscr{C}^n(F'') \ar[r] \ar[d] & 0 \\ 0 \ar[r] & \mathscr{Z}^n(F') \ar[r] \ar[d] & \mathscr{Z}^n(F) \ar[r] \ar[d] & \mathscr{Z}^n(F'') \ar[r] \ar[d] & 0 \\ & 0 & 0 & 0 & }\]
whose columns are exact, as well as, according to the recurrence hypothesis, that the first 2 lines. The 3rd line is therefore correct, and (ii) is proven.
Short exact sequences
\[0 \longrightarrow \mathscr{Z}^{n-1}(F) \longrightarrow \mathscr{C}^n(F) \longrightarrow \mathscr{Z}^n(F) \longrightarrow 0\]
are special cases of the short exact sequence
\[(4.2.3.1) \qquad 0 \longrightarrow F \xrightarrow{\;\varepsilon\;} \mathscr{C}^0(F) \longrightarrow \mathscr{Z}^0(F) \longrightarrow 0\]
(make the substitution \(F \longmapsto \mathscr{Z}^{n-1}(F)\)). To prove (iii), it remains to prove that the reciprocal image sequence of (4.2.3.1) by \(k\) is canonically split. The compound arrow
\[k^*F \xrightarrow{\;k^*(\varepsilon)\;} k^*\mathscr{C}^0 F = k^*(k_* k^*)F = (k^* k_*)k^*F \longrightarrow k^*F\]
is indeed identity.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 4.2.4

If \((x_i)_{i \in X}\) is a conservative family of points of a site, this family corresponds to a conservative family of points of the generated topos, which still makes it possible to define the canonical flaccid resolutions.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 4.2.5

If \(S\) is a ringed topos, the functors \(\mathscr{C}^n\) transform Modules into Modules.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 4.2.6

Let \(\mathcal{S}\) be a category made up of sites. Suppose given, for each \(S \in \operatorname{Ob} \mathcal{S}\), a set \(S^d\) and a conservative family \(k_S : \operatorname{Top}(S^d) \longrightarrow S\) of points of \(S\) indexed by \(S^d\). Suppose that \(S^d\) is functorial in \(S\) and that the diagrams

\[\xymatrix@C=2.4em@R=2em{ \operatorname{Top}(S^d) \ar[r]^{f^d} \ar[d]_{k_S} & \operatorname{Top}(T^d) \ar[d]^{k_T} \\ S \ar[r]_{f} & T }\]

be commutative. The corresponding canonical flaccid resolutions are then "functional in \(S\)".
If \(\mathcal{S}\) is the category of topological spaces, we can take \(S^d\) = underlying set.

If \(\mathcal{S}\) is the category of slack sites of schemas \(S\) locally of finite type on a schema \(S_0\), and if for each \(s \in S_0\), \(\overline{k(s)}\) is an algebraic closure of \(k(s)\), we can take for \(S^d\) the set of points of \(S\) with values in one of \(\overline{k(s)}\).
We can replace the "finite type" condition by a cardinal condition if the algebraic closures \(\overline{k(s)}\) are replaced by sufficiently large algebraically closed extensions.

4.2.7. One of the advantages of canonical flaccid resolutions is that they make it possible to construct complexes deployed both for functors of the "tensor product" type and for functors of the "direct image" type. They thereby allow a perhaps more understandable construction of base change morphisms (4.1.5).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.2.8

Let \(f : (S, \mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow (S', \mathcal{B})\) a morphism of ringed sites, \(K \in \operatorname{Ob} K(S, \mathcal{A}^o)\) and \(L \in \operatorname{Ob} K(S', \mathcal{B})\) complexes and \(k : \operatorname{Top}(X) \longrightarrow S\) a conservative set of points of \(S\). One of the following conditions is assumed to be met:

(a) The cohomologies of \(K\) and \(L\) are upper bounded;

(b) \(\mathcal{A}\) is finite-dimensional Tor on \(f^*\mathcal{B}\) and \(K \in \operatorname{Ob} D^b(S, \mathcal{A}^o)\) is finite-dimensional Tor ;

(c) \(L \in \operatorname{Ob} D^b(S', \mathcal{B})\) is finite-dimensional Tor.

Then the functor \((K,L) \longmapsto K \otimes f^*L\) is differentiable (1.2.1) in \((K,L)\); or \(\overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} Lf^*\) its derivative.

(i) If \(x\) is a point of \(S\), then \((K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} Lf^*L)_x \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} K_x \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes}_{\mathcal{B}_{f(x)}} L_{f(x)}\).

(ii) The pair \((K,L)\) is deployed on the left for \(\overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} Lf^*\) (1.2.1) if and only if, for all \(x \in X\), the pair \((K_x, L_{f(x)})\) is deployed on the left for the functor \(\otimes_{\mathcal{B}_{f(x)}}\).

(iii) Under the hypotheses (a) or (b), the functor \(K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} Lf^*L\) in \(L\) is of cohomological amplitude \(\subset d\) (1.2.9) if and only if, \(\forall x \in X\), the complex \(K_x\) of \(\mathcal{B}_{f(x)}\)-modules is of Tor-amplitude \(\subset d\).
This lemma, whose proof is standard, expresses the "point nature" of the functor considered.

4.2.9. Let \(L\) be a complex. We call Godement resolution (relative to \(X\)) of \(L\) the simple complex (defined in terms of sums) associated with the double complex \(\mathscr{C}^*L\) (1.1.4). We call Godement resolution truncated to the order \(n\) \(\tau''_{\leq n} \mathscr{C}^*L\) the simple complex associated with the double complex deduced from \(\mathscr{C}^*L\) by truncation (1.1.15) in the direction of the Godement differential. By virtue of 4.2.3 (iii), \(L\) is point of \(X\) by point of \(X\) homotopic to the complexes \(\tau''_{\leq n} \mathscr{C}^*L\) and \(\mathscr{C}^*L\); these complexes are therefore "as good as \(L\)" with respect to functors of the "tensor product" type (4.2.8).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 4.2.10

Let \((S, \mathcal{A})\) a ringed site, \(X\) a conservative set of points of \(S\) and \(N,M \in \mathbb{N} \cup \{\infty\}\) with \(N\) or \(M < \infty\). Let \(A\) the subcategory of \(K(S)\) formed by complexes \(L\) such that

(a) If \(N = \infty\), then \(H^i(L) = 0\) for \(i\) large enough; if \(M = \infty\), then \(H^i(L) = 0\) for \(i\) small enough.

(b) Whatever \(x \in X\) and the \(\mathcal{A}_x\)-module \(Q\) Tor-dimension \(\leq N\), the complex \(L_x\) is deployed at left for the functor \(* \otimes_{\mathcal{A}_x} Q\).

(c) Whatever the morphism of topos \(f : \widetilde{X} \longrightarrow E\), of cohomological dimension \(\leq M\), the complex \(L\) is deployed on the right for the functor \(f_*\).

Then, the inclusion of \(A\) in \(K(S)\) induces an equivalence between the category deduced from \(A\) by reversing the quasi-isomorphisms and the category \(D^*(S)\), \(* = +\) if \(N < M = \infty\), \(* = -\) if \(M < N = \infty\) and \(* =\) blank if \(N,M < \infty\).

Let \(K\) be a complex satisfying (a). If \(N = \infty\), then the canonical arrow \(a : K_1 = \tau_{\leq i} K \longrightarrow K\) is quasi-isomorphism for \(i\) large enough. If \(N < \infty\), we set \(K_1 = K\). Let \(K_1^*\) be a flat Cartan-Eilenberg resolution and set \(K_2 = \tau''_{\leq 2N} K_1\) (truncation in the direction of the new differential). The compound arrow of \(K_2\) in \(K\) is a quasi-isomorphism, and \(K_2\) satisfies (b).

Let \(K\) be a complex satisfying (a) and (b). If \(M = \infty\), then \(N < \infty\); for \(i\) large enough, \(b : K \longrightarrow \tau_{\geq i} K_1 = K_3\) is a quasi-isomorphism of \(K_3\) still satisfies (a) and (b). If \(M < \infty\), we set \(K = K_3\). Or \(K_4 = \tau''_{\leq M} \mathscr{C}^*K_3\). Then \(K_4\) verifies (a) (c), and (b) by 4.2.3 (iii).
The proposal therefore results from two successive applications of [CD I 2.4.2].

blocks.title: Variants
blocks.html:
Variants 4.2.11

If \(N < \infty\) (resp. if \(M < \infty\), resp. if \(N, M < \infty\)) then we could have worked in the same way with complexes bounded lower (resp. higher, resp. bounded).
4.2.12. Here is how to use these constructions to define the base change morphism 4.1.4 under more general assumptions than those of loc. cit., when the sites considered have enough points.
Let us be a commutative diagram (cf. (0.11)) of sites
\[(4.2.12.1) \qquad \xymatrix@C=2.4em@R=2em{ X' \ar[r]^{g'} \ar[d]_{f'} & X \ar[d]^{f} \\ S' \ar[r]_{g} & S } \qquad ,\]
\(\mathcal{A}\) a ring bundle on \(S\), \(\mathcal{A}'\) a ring bundle on \(S'\) and \(L\) a complex of \((\mathcal{A}', g^*\mathcal{A})\)-bimodules on \(S'\). We assume that:
(i) the sites \(X\) and \(X'\) admit conservative families of points, \(k : Q \longrightarrow X\) and \(k' : Q' \longrightarrow X'\), fitting into a commutative diagram
\[(4.2.12.2) \qquad \xymatrix@C=2.4em@R=2em{ X' \ar[r]^{g'} \ar[d]_{k'} & X \ar[d]^{k} \\ Q' \ar[r]_{g_0} & Q } \qquad .\]
(ii) One of the following assumptions is met:
(a) The functors \(Rf_*\) and \(Rf'_*\) are of finite cohomological dimension, and \(L\) is upper bounded. We set \(s = -\).
(b) \(L\) is lower bounded, with uniformly bounded Tor-dimensional components (like \(g^*\mathcal{A}\)-modules). We set \(s = +\).
(c) \(L\) is bounded, and the assumptions of (a) and (b) are met. We put \(s =\) white.

We denote by \(L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} Lg^* : D^s(S, \mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow D^s(S', \mathcal{A}')\) the left derivative functor (1.2.1) of the functor \(K \longmapsto L \otimes_{g^*\mathcal{A}} g^*K\) of \(K^s(S, \mathcal{A})\) in \(K^s(S', \mathcal{A}')\).
We propose to define a base change arrow
\[(4.2.12.3) \qquad \varphi : L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} Lg^* Rf_* K \longrightarrow Rf'_*(f'^*L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} Lg'^*K)\]
for \(K\) in \(D^s(X, \mathcal{A})\).
Under 4.2.10, it is sufficient to define it, functionally as \(K\), for complexes belonging to the category \(A\) of 4.2.10, for
\[N \geq \sup_i (\text{tor dim de } L^i \text{ sur } g^*)\] \[M \geq \text{dimension cohomologique de } Rf_* \, .\]
Such a complex is deployed both for the functor \(Rf_*\) and for the functor \(f'^*L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes}_{\mathcal{A}} g'^*\) (4.2.8). Canonical Arrows
\[\alpha : f_*K \longrightarrow Rf_*K\] \[\beta : f'^*L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} Lg'^*K \longrightarrow L \otimes g'^*K\]
are therefore quasi-isomorphisms. To calculate the left hand side \(I\) of (4.2.12.3), it is therefore sufficient to take a left resolution (i.e. a quasi-isomorphism)
\[\gamma : K_1 \longrightarrow f_*K \, ,\]
with \(K_1\) deployed for \(L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} Lg^*\); we have
\[\mathrm{I} \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} L \otimes Lg^* K_1 \, .\]
Similarly, to calculate the right hand side \(\mathrm{II}\) of (4.2.12.3), it is enough to take a right resolution
\[\delta : f'^*L \otimes g'^*K \longrightarrow K_2 \, ,\]
with \(K_2\) deployed for \(Rf'_*\); we have
\[\mathrm{II} \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} f'_* K_2 \, .\]
We then define the base change arrow as the compound

\[\mathrm{I} \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} L \otimes g^*K_1 \xrightarrow{\;\gamma\;} L \otimes g^*f_*K \xrightarrow{\;c\;} f'_*(f'^*L \otimes g'^*K) \xrightarrow{\;\alpha\;} f'_* K_2 \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \mathrm{II} \, ,\]
where the arrow \(c\) is the usual base change arrow, at the level of real complexes.
This base change arrow has the following characteristic property, which in principle makes it easy to verify its compatibility properties.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 4.2.13

Under the previous hypotheses, let be \(K \in \operatorname{Ob} C^s(X, \mathcal{A})\), \(K_1 \in \operatorname{Ob} C^s(S, \mathcal{A})\) and \(K_2 \in \operatorname{Ob} C^s(X', \mathcal{A}')\). Be more \(u\) a \(f\)-morphism of \(K\) in \(K_1\), i.e. \(u \in \operatorname{Hom}(K_1, f_*K) \xrightarrow{\sim} \operatorname{Hom}(f^*K_1, K)\), and \(v\) a morphism of \(f'^*L \otimes g'^*K\) in \(K_2\). We deduce from \(u,v\) and from the usual base change arrow a morphism of complexes \(c_{u,v} : L \otimes g^*K_1 \longrightarrow f'_* K_2\). Let \(u'\), \(v'\) and \(c'_{uv}\) the compound arrows

\[u' : K_1 \xrightarrow{\;u\;} f_*K \longrightarrow Rf_*K\]

\[v' : L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} Lg'^*K \longrightarrow L \otimes g'^*K \xrightarrow{\;v\;} K_2\]

\[c'_{u,v} : L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} Lg^*K_1 \longrightarrow L \otimes g^*K_1 \xrightarrow{\;c_{uv}\;} f'_* K_2 \longrightarrow Rf'_*K_2 \, .\]
The following diagram is commutative

\[(4.2.13.1) \qquad \xymatrix@C=4em@R=2.6em{ L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} Lg^*Rf_*K \ar[r]^-{\varphi} \ar[d]_{L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} Lg^*(u')} & Rf'_*(f'^*L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} Lg'^*K) \ar[d]^{Rf'_*(v')} \\ L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} Lg^*K_1 \ar[r]_-{c'_{uv}} & Rf'_* K_2 } \qquad .\]
Let us first assume that \(K_1 = f_*K\) and that \(K_2 = f'^*L \otimes g'^*K\), and is a commutative diagram of complexes
\[\xymatrix@C=2.6em@R=2em{ K^a \ar[r] \ar[d] & K \ar[d] \\ K^c \ar[r] & K^b }\]

such that \(K^a\) is deployed for \(Lf'^*L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} Lg'^*\), that \(K^b\) is deployed for \(Rf_*\) and that \(K^c\) is deployed for these two functors: to construct this diagram, we start by constructing \(K^a\), then we apply a canonical flaccid resolution (possibly truncated) to \(K^a\) and \(K\).

Let us apply the functor \(f_*\) (resp. \(L \otimes g'^*\)) to this diagram, and solve the complexes of the diagram \((K_1, K_1^a, K_1^b, K_1^c)\) (resp. \((K_2, K_2^a, K_2^b, K_2^c)\)) so as to obtain a commutative cube, where \(\approx\) denotes a quasi-isomorphism(both cubes below are approximate reconstructions of the three-dimensional diagrams of the original):

\[\xymatrix@!0@C=3em@R=2.4em{ & K_1^{a} \ar[rr] \ar[dd] & & K_1 \ar[dd] \\ K_1^{a'} \ar[ur]^{\approx} \ar[rr] \ar[dd] & & K_1' \ar[ur]_(0.35){\approx} \ar[dd] & \\ & K_1^{c} \ar[rr] & & K_1^{b} \\ K_1^{c'} \ar[ur]^{\approx} \ar[rr] & & K_1^{b'} \ar[ur]_(0.35){\approx} & }\]
with \(K_1'\), \(K_1^{a'}\), \(K_1^{b'}\), \(K_1^{c'}\) deployed for the functor \(L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} Lg^*\) (resp.

\[\xymatrix@!0@C=3em@R=2.4em{ & K_2^{a} \ar[rr] \ar[dd] & & K_2 \ar[dd] \\ K_2^{a'} \ar[ur]^{\approx} \ar[rr] \ar[dd] & & K_2' \ar[ur]_(0.35){\approx} \ar[dd] & \\ & K_2^{c} \ar[rr] & & K_2^{b} \\ K_2^{c'} \ar[ur]^{\approx} \ar[rr] & & K_2^{b'} \ar[ur]_(0.35){\approx} & }\]

with \(K_2'\), \(K_2^a\), \(K_2^b\), \(K_2^c\) deployed for the functor \(Rf'_*\)). Using the usual base change morphisms, we obtain a commutative prism of complexes on \(S'\) (approximate reconstruction):
and we conclude by noting that the arrows in the diagram (4.2.13.1) are identified with the following compound arrows:
for \(\varphi\): the composite of arrows (1);
for \(L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} Lg^*(u')\): the composite of arrows (2), including an inverse arrow of a quasi-isomorphism;
for \(Rf'_*(v')\): the composite of arrows (3), including an inverse arrow of a quasi-isomorphism;
for \(c'_{uv}\): the composition of the arrows (4).
In the general case, let's set \(K_1' = f_*K\) and \(K_2' = f'^*L \otimes g'^*K\). We then have a diagram

\[\xymatrix@C=2.4em@R=3em{ & L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} Lg^*Rf_*K \ar[rr]^-{\varphi} & & Rf'_*(f'^*L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} Lg'^*K) & \\ L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} Lg^*K_1 \ar[ur] \ar[r] & L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} Lg^*K_1' \ar[u] \ar[rr] & & Rf'_* K_2' \ar[u] \ar[r] & Rf'_* K_2 \ar[ul] } \, ,\]
whose triangles are commutative, as well as the interior rectangle according to the above. The contour is therefore commutative, and this proves 4.2.13.
4.3. The change of basis theorem.
We propose to reformulate in terms of derived categories the base change theorem for proper morphisms XII 5.1 (iii).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.3.1

Let us be a Cartesian diagram of schemes

\[\xymatrix@C=2.4em@R=2em{ X' \ar[r]^{g'} \ar[d]_{f'} & X \ar[d]^{f} \\ S' \ar[r]_{g} & S }\]

with \(f\) own. Let \(\mathcal{A}\) and \(\mathcal{A}'\) beams of torsion rings on \(S\) and \(S'\) and \(L\) a complex bounded above \((\mathcal{A}', g^*\mathcal{A})\)-bimodules. We assume that the dimension of the fibers of \(f\) is bounded. Assumption 4.2.12 (a) is then fulfilled, and the base change arrow (4.2.12.3), between functors of \(D^-(X, f^*\mathcal{A})\) in \(D^-(S', \mathcal{A}')\):

\[\varphi_K : L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} Lg^* Rf_*K \longrightarrow Rf'_*(f'^*L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} Lg'^* K) \, ,\]

is an isomorphism.

The functors considered are triangulated and "way-out" [RD I 7], so that, to verify that \(\varphi_K\) is an isomorphism, it suffices to verify that the \(\varphi_{\underline{H}^i(K)}\) are isomorphisms. We can therefore assume that \(K\) is reduced to a beam of \(f^*\mathcal{A}\)-modules (of torsion) placed at degree 0.

To verify that \(\varphi_K\) is an isomorphism, simply check it after applying the forgetting functor from the category of \(\mathcal{A}'\)-Modules to that of \(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\)-Modules, where \(n\mathcal{A} = n\mathcal{A}' = 0\).

This allows us to return to the case where \(\mathcal{A}' = \mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\), with \(n\mathcal{A} = 0\). We can then replace \(L\) by an isomorphic complex in the derived category, with flat components on \(g^*\mathcal{A}\), and bounded above. The considered functors being triangulated and "way out" in \(L\), it suffices to verify that \(\varphi_K\) is an isomorphism after replacing \(L\) by one of its components. We can therefore assume that \(L\) is reduced to a bundle of \(g^*\mathcal{A}\)-flat modules placed at degree 0. It is necessary to prove that the arrows
\[\varphi^q : L \otimes g^*R^q f_*K \longrightarrow R^q f'_*(f'^*L \otimes g'^*K)\]

are isomorphisms. Let \(s\) be a geometric point of \(S'\), \(X_s\) the fiber of \(X'\) in \(s\) and \(K_s\) the reciprocal images of \(K\) on \(X_s\). Under XII 5.1 (iii), the fiber in \(s\) of \(\varphi^q\) is the arrow
\[\varphi^q_s : L_s \otimes H^q(X_s, K) \longrightarrow H^q(X_s, L_s \otimes K) \, .\]

By virtue of D. Lazard's theorem [1], the flat \(\mathcal{A}_s\)-module \(L_s\) is the filtering inductive limit of \(\mathcal{A}_s\)-free modules \(L_{s,i}\); the functor \(H^q(X_s, *)\) commutes at the filtering inductive limits. The arrow \(\varphi^q\) is therefore the inductive limit of the arrows
\[\varphi^q_i : L_{s,i} \otimes H^q(X_s, K) \longrightarrow H^q(X_s, L_{s,i} \otimes K) \, .\]
The latter are obviously isomorphisms, so also \(\varphi^q_s\), and \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.

blocks.title: Variants
blocks.html:
Variants 4.3.2

(i) When \(L\) is a bounded complex of finite-dimensional Tor components on \(g^*\mathcal{A}\), the assumptions (c) of 4.2.12 are met, and one can work in the entire derivative category.

(ii) If \(L\) lower bounded, with finite-dimensional Tor components on \(g^*\mathcal{A}\), assumptions (b) of 4.2.12 are met, even if \(\mathcal{A}\) and \(\mathcal{A}'\) are not torsion; the base change arrow \(\varphi_K\) (\(K \in D^+(X, f^*\mathcal{A})\)) will be an isomorphism if the beams \(\underline{H}^i(K)\) are torsional.

pages.title: 5. Direct image functors with proper support.

blocks.html: 5. Direct image functors with proper support.
5.1. The fundamental construction.

We propose to follow the program indicated in 3.1 and to define the functor \(Rf_!\) for \(f\) a compactifiable morphism (3.2). We will show in the appendix how to treat the case of a separate morphism of any finite type.

5.1.1. Let \(X\) be a topos, \(U\) an open of \(X\) (IV 8.3) and \(j\) the inclusion morphism of \(U\) in \(X\). We defined in IV 5.2 a functor "vacuum extension" of the category of sheaves of sets on \(U\) in that of sheaves of sets on \(X\). This functor \(j_!\) is appended on the left to the restriction functor \(j^*\).

We also designate by \(j_!\) the functor of the category of sheaves of sets pointed on \(U\) (resp. sheaves in groups, resp. sheaves of \(j^*\mathcal{A}\)-modules for \(\mathcal{A}\) sheaf of rings on \(X\)) in the analogous category on \(X\), left adjoint to the restriction functor \(j^*\) (IV ). These three functors \(j_!\) are compatible with forgetting functors, of the category of Modules in that of Groups, and of the category of Groups in that of Pointed Sets (∗). If \(i : F \longrightarrow X\) is the inclusion of the closed complement of \(U\), then \(j^*j_!\) is (canonically isomorphic to) the identity and \(i^*j_!\) is the constant functor of value of the initial (or final, it amounts to the same thing here). This also characterizes \(j_!\). We deduce from this that the three \(j_!\) functors considered are exact and are injected into the corresponding \(j_*\) functors. They are called zero extension functors. The formula \((j_1 j_2)^* = j_2^* j_1^*\) transposes into a transitivity isomorphism
Functors \(j_!\) are compatible with base changes:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.1.2

Let \(f : X \longrightarrow Y\) a morphism of topos, \(V\) an open of \(Y\) and \(U\) its reciprocal image in \(X\):

\[\xymatrix@C=2.4em@R=2em{ U \ar[r]^{f'} \ar[d]_{j'} & V \ar[d]^{j} \\ X \ar[r]_{f} & Y }\]

There then exists one and only one base change isomorphism \(f^*j_! \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} j'_! f'^*\) making the diagram commutative

\[(5.1.2.1) \qquad \xymatrix@C=2.4em@R=2.4em{ f^*j_! \ar[r]^-{\sim} \ar[d] & j'_! f'^* \ar[d] \\ f^*j_* \ar[r]_-{\alpha} & j'_* f'^* } \, ,\]

where \(\alpha\) is the change of base arrow.

Proof. The base change arrow \(\varphi : j^*f_* \longrightarrow f'_*j'^*\) is trivially an isomorphism. It transposes into a base change isomorphism \(\varphi' : j'_! f'^* \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} f^*j_!\). To verify that the diagram (5.1.2.1) is commutative, it suffices to see that its restriction to \(U\) is, which is trivial.

5.1.3. Under the assumptions of 5.1.1, with \(X\) ringed by a sheaf of rings \(\mathcal{A}\), the functor \(j_!\) on the Modules is exact, so passes trivially to the derived categories and defines a functor, again denoted \(j_!\), of \(D(U, j^*\mathcal{A})\) in \(D(X, \mathcal{A})\). We still have transitivity isomorphisms
We still have, at the level of the derived categories, an addition formula
\[(5.1.3.2) \qquad \operatorname{Hom}_{D(X)}(j_! K, L) \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \operatorname{Hom}_{D(U)}(K, j^*L) \, .\]

5.1.4. Let \(f : X \longrightarrow Y\) be a proper morphism, \(\mathcal{A}\) a sheaf of torsion rings on \(Y\), and let us anneal \(X\) by the sheaf of rings reciprocal image of \(\mathcal{A}\). Suppose that the dimension of the fibers of \(f\) is increased by an integer \(n\) (which is the case if \(Y\) is quasi-compact). We then know that for each bundle of \(f^*\mathcal{A}\)-modules \(F\) on \(X\), the bundles \(R^q f_* F\) are zero for \(q > 2n\) (XII 5.3 bis). The functor \(f_*\) therefore admits a derived functor (1.2.10)
\[Rf_* : D(X, f^*\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow D(Y, \mathcal{A}) \, .\]
If \(f\) is the composite \(g\, h\) of two proper morphisms, we have a transitivity formula \(Rf_* = Rg_* Rh_*\), which we can as usual express in terms of cofibered categories (Scholie 5.1.3).
5.1.5. Consider a commutative diagram of schemes

in which \(j_1\) and \(j_2\) are open immersions and \(f\), \(g\) are proper morphisms whose fiber dimension is increased by an integer \(n\). Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be a bundle of torsion rings on \(Y\), and let us anneal \(U\), \(V\) and \(X\) by the reciprocal images of \(\mathcal{A}\). Under 5.1.3.2, if \(K \in D(U)\), define an arrow
\[(5.1.5.2) \qquad d : j_{2!}\, Rg_* K \longrightarrow Rf_* j_{1!}\, K\]
is equivalent to defining an arrow
\[d' : Rg_* K \longrightarrow j_2^* Rf_* j_{1!}\, K \, .\]
The fiber product \(X' = V \times_Y X\) is inserted into a commutative diagram
and, trivially, we have \(j_2^* Rf_* j_{1!}\, K \simeq Rf'_*(j'^*_2 j_{1!}\, K) \simeq Rf'_* k_! K\).

Arrow \(k\) is a clean open dip; we therefore have \(k_! = k_*\), hence an isomorphism \(Rf'_* k_! K \simeq Rf'_* k_* K \simeq Rg_* K\), which provides us with the isomorphism \(d'\) sought and the morphism \(d\). We further see that the restriction of \(d\) to \(V\) is an isomorphism.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.1.6

Morphism 5.1.5.2 is an isomorphism.

It remains to verify that the restriction of \(d\) to \(Y - V = F\) is an isomorphism. Let \(X'' = X \times_Y F\) and apply the base change theorem for a proper morphism (4.3.1) to the Cartesian square
\[\xymatrix@C=2.4em@R=2em{ X'' \ar[r]^{i'} \ar[d]_{f''} & X \ar[d]^{f} \\ F \ar[r]_{i} & Y } \, ,\]
for \(\mathcal{A}' = i^*\mathcal{A}\) and \(L = \mathcal{A}'[0]\). We find:
so that both sides of (5.1.5.2) are zero apart from \(U\) and \(d\) is an isomorphism.

5.1.7. Let \(S\) be a diagram ringed by a beam of torsion rings \(\mathcal{A}\). For all \(S\)-schema \(\varphi : X \longrightarrow S\), we set \(\mathcal{A}_X = \varphi^*\mathcal{A}\). Let us place ourselves in the category \((S)\) of \(S\)-quasi-separated quasi-compact schemes and \(S\)-compactifiable morphisms (3.2). By virtue of 3.2.3, this category, the subcategory \((S,i)\) of open immersions, and the subcategory \((S,p)\) of proper morphisms satisfy assumptions 3.2.4.
We propose to apply theory 3.3.2 to the following data (notations of 3.3.1)
\(\alpha\)) \(F(X) = D(X, \mathcal{A}_X)\) for \(\varphi : X \longrightarrow S\) in \(S\):
(i) For proper \(p : X \longrightarrow Y\), we take as functor \(p_*\) the functor \(Rp_*\).
(ii) We use the usual transitivity isomorphisms \(R(pq)_* = Rp_* Rq_*\).
(i') For an open immersion \(i : X \longrightarrow Y\), we take as functor \(i_*\) the functor \(i_!\).
(ii') We use the transitivity isomorphisms (5.1.3.1).
(iii) For any commutative diagram (5.1.5.1) we take as isomorphism \(d\) the isomorphism (5.1.5.2).
We verify the conditions (a) (b) (c) (a') (b') (c') of 3.3.2, which proves point (a) of:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.1.8

(a) In the category \((S)\) defined in 5.1.7, we can in one, and essentially only one way, define for any arrow \(f : X \longrightarrow Y\) a functor

\[Rf_! : D(X, \mathcal{A}_X) \longrightarrow D(Y, \mathcal{A}_Y) \, ,\]

and for any compound morphism \(f = g h : X \xrightarrow{\;h\;} Y \xrightarrow{\;g\;} Z\) transitivity isomorphisms \(c_{f,g}\) between \(Rf_!\) and \(Rg_! Rh_!\) so that

(i) The \(c_{f,g}\) check the "cocycle condition"

\[(c_{f,g} * Rh_!) \circ c_{fg,h} = (Rf_! * c_{g,h}) \circ c_{f,gh} \, .\]

(ii) When we restrict ourselves to proper morphisms (resp. open immersions) we find variance theory 5.1.4 (resp. 5.1.3).

(iii) For any diagram (5.1.5.1), the isomorphism \(d\) (5.1.5.2) is the compound isomorphism \(c_{f,j'} \circ c_{j,f'}^{-1}\).

(b) The functors \(Rf_!\) are "way out" [RD I 7] and triangulated.

Every functor \(Rf_!\) is composed of a functor \(Rg_*\), for \(g\) specific to a quasi-compact goal, therefore with fibers of limited dimension, and of a functor \(j_!\), for \(j\) open immersion. These functors are way out and triangulated, so also \(Rf_!\).
5.1.8.1. The transitivity isomorphisms 5.1.8 (a) are often used via the spectral sequence "compound functors" which is deduced from them, valid for \(K \in \operatorname{Ob} D(X, \mathcal{A}_X)\):
\[(5.1.8.2) \qquad E_2^{pq} = R^p g_! R^q h_!(K) \Longrightarrow R^{p+q} f_!(K) \, .\]
To obtain it, we note that according to 5.1.8 b) and [VERDIER 2], for all \(L \in \operatorname{Ob} D(Y, \mathcal{A}_Y)\), we have a spectral sequence
\[(5.1.8.3) \qquad E_2^{pq} = R^p g_! (\underline{H}^q(L)) \Longrightarrow R^{p+q} g_!(L) \, ,\]
and (5.1.8.2) is the special case of (5.1.8.3) for \(L = Rh_!\, K\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 5.1.9

(i) We call "total eigensupport direct image functors" the functors constructed in 5.1.8. We will omit the adjective “total” when there is no risk of confusion.

(ii) We call \(q^{\text{ième}}\) direct image functor with proper support, and we denote by \(R^q f_!\), the compound functor \(\mathscr{H}^q \circ Rf_!\).

(iii) If \(X\) is a diagram on a body \(k\) separably closed, immersible in a proper diagram on \(k\), we identify bundles of modules on \(\operatorname{Spec}(k)\) and usual modules, and we designate the functor \(R^q f_!\) by one of the notations \(H^q_c(X, \ )\), \(H^q_!(X, \ )\), or simply \(H^q_c(\ )\) if there is no danger of confusion. It is then called "\(q^{\text{ième}}\) hypercohomology group with proper support", or "\(q^{\text{ième}}\) cohomology group with proper support" if it is applied to a complex reduced to the degree \(0\).
5.1.9.1. The notation \(Rf_!\) is abusive in that the functor \(Rf_!\) is not the right derivative of the functor \(f_!\) which will be defined in 6.1.2. Some prefer the notation \(R_! f\).

5.1.10. The ingredients in the proof of 5.1.8 are the proper base change theorem and the identity between \(j_*\) and \(j_!\) when \(j\) is a proper open immersion. This allows various variations to 5.1.8 to be stated; we leave it to the reader to give them a precise meaning and to verify them.

blocks.title: Variant
blocks.html:
Variant 5.1.11

In category \((S)\), we can in one, and essentially only one way, define for any arrow \(f : X \longrightarrow Y\) a functor \(f_!\) of the category of bundles of sets pointed at \(X\) in the category of bundles of sets pointed at \(Y\) and for any compound morphism \(f = g h\) transitivity isomorphisms satisfying conditions (i), (ii) (iii) similar to those of 5.1.4.
See 6.1.2 for a direct description of this functor.

blocks.title: Variant
blocks.html:
Variant 5.1.12

We can, for any compactifiable morphism \(f : X \longrightarrow Y\), define a functor \(R^1 f_!\) from the category of bundles of Ind-finite groups on \(X\) in the category of bundles of sets pointed at \(Y\), with for any compactification \(f = \bar{f} j\) of \(f\), an isomorphism \(R^1 f_! \simeq R^1 \bar{f}_* \circ j_!\).

We do not postulate any transitivity formula here; the only problem is to show that the functor \(R^1 f_!\) does not depend on the chosen compactification of \(f\). Let \(p : (\bar{f}_1, i_1) \longrightarrow (\bar{f}_2, i_2)\) be a compactification morphism, i.e. a diagram
\[(5.1.12.1) \qquad \xymatrix@C=3.5em@R=2.5em{ X \ar[r]^{i_1} \ar[dr]^{i_2} \ar[dd]_{f} & \bar{X}_1 \ar[d]^{p} \\ & \bar{X}_2 \ar[dl]^{\bar{f}_2} \\ Y & } \, ,\]
where \(\bar{f}_1 = \bar{f}_2 \circ p\), \(p\) being clean. If \(G\) is a bundle in groups on \(X\), we have an exact sequence of pointed sets (with \(\alpha\) injective)
\[0 \longrightarrow R^1 \bar{f}_{2*}(p_* i_{1!}\, G) \xrightarrow{\;\alpha\;} R^1 \bar{f}_{1*}(i_{1!}\, G) \longrightarrow \bar{f}_{2*} R^1 p_*(i_{1!}\, G) \, ,\]

and an arrow from \(i_{2!}\, G\) in \(p_* i_{1!}\, G\), hence by composition an arrow \(\beta\) from \(R^1 \bar{f}_{2*} i_{2!}\, G\) in \(R^1 \bar{f}_{1*} i_{1!}\, G\). Reasoning as in 3.3.2.1, we see that the problem is to prove that this arrow is always an isomorphism when \(G\) is Ind-finite.
If we apply the base change theorem specific to \(p\) and to the sheaf of sets \(i_{1!}\, G\), we verify that \(p_* i_{1!}\, G = i_{2!}\, G\), hence the exact sequence
\[0 \longrightarrow R^1 \bar{f}_{2*}(i_{2!}\, G) \xrightarrow{\;\beta\;} R^1 \bar{f}_{1*}(i_{1!}\, G) \longrightarrow \bar{f}_{2*} R^1 p_* i_{1!}\, G \, .\]

The arrow \(\beta\) is injective (because \(\alpha\) is). If we apply the base change theorem specific to \(p\) and to the group sheaf \(G\), assumed Ind-finite, we find that \(R^1 p_* i_{1!}\, G = 0\). So \(\beta\) is an epimorphism, which completes the demonstration of 5.1.12.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 5.1.13

For any site \(X\), we designate by \(D_{\mathrm{tors}}(X)\) the full subcategory of the category derived from the category of abelian sheaves on \(X\), formed by complexes whose cohomology sheaves are torsion.

blocks.title: Variant
blocks.html:
Variant 5.1.14

In the category \((S)\), we can in one, and essentially only one way, define for any arrow \(f : X \longrightarrow Y\) a triangulated and "way out" functor [RD, I 7]

\[Rf_! : D^+_{\mathrm{tors}}(X) \longrightarrow D^+_{\mathrm{tors}}(Y)\]

and for any compound morphism \(f = g h\) transitivity isomorphisms satisfying conditions (i) (ii) (iii) analogous to those of 5.1.4.

blocks.title: Variant
blocks.html:
Variant 5.1.15

Let us place ourselves in the category of \(S\)-ringed schemes by torsion beams, the arrows being the morphisms of ringed schemes inducing (0.8) a morphism \(S\)-compactifiable of schemes. For any arrow \(f : (X, \mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow (Y, \mathcal{B})\), let \(Rf_!\) be the compound of the forgetting functor of the category of \(\mathcal{A}\)-modules in that of \(f^*\mathcal{B}\)-modules, and of \(Rf_!\). These functors benefit from the variance properties of \(Rf_!\) usual functors.

Note, however, that the notation \(Rf_!\) is only reasonable here if \(\mathcal{A}\) is finite on \(f^*\mathcal{B}\), as we can see by taking \(X = Y = \operatorname{Spec}(k)\), \(k\) algebraically closed.

5.1.16. Let \(f : X \longrightarrow S\) be a compactifiable morphism, \(j : U \longrightarrow X\) an open of \(X\), \(i : F \longrightarrow X\) the complementary closed, \(\mathcal{A}_S\) a bundle of torsion rings on \(S\), \(\mathcal{A}_X\) its reciprocal image on \(X\) and \(K \in \operatorname{Ob} D(X, \mathcal{A}_X)\).
The exact sequence of complexes
\[0 \longrightarrow j_! j^*K \longrightarrow K \longrightarrow i_* i^*K \longrightarrow 0\]
gives rise to a distinguished triangle in \(D(X, \mathcal{A}_X)\) [VERDIER C.D. I 2.4]. Its image by \(Rf_!\) is a distinguished triangle (5.1.8 (b))
\[(5.1.16.1) \qquad R(fj)_! j^*K \longrightarrow Rf_! K \longrightarrow R(fi)_! i^*K \longrightarrow R(fj)_! j^*K[1] \cdots\]
which defines a long exact cohomology sequence
\[(5.1.16.2) \qquad \cdots R^q(fj)_! j^*K \longrightarrow R^q f_! K \longrightarrow R^q(fi)_! i^*K \xrightarrow{\;\partial\;} R^{q+1}(fj)_! j^*K \longrightarrow \cdots\]
In the particular case where \(S\) is the spectrum of a separably closed body and where \(K\) is reduced to a beam \(\underline{G}\) placed in degree 0, the sequence (5.1.16.2) is written
\[(5.1.16.3) \qquad \cdots H^q_c(U, \underline{G}) \longrightarrow H^q_c(X, \underline{G}) \longrightarrow H^q_c(F, \underline{G}) \xrightarrow{\;\partial\;} H^{q+1}_c(U, \underline{G}) \cdots\]
5.1.17. Consider a diagram of \(S\)-compactifiable schemas
\[\xymatrix@C=3em@R=2.5em{ X \ar[rr]^{u} \ar[dr]_{f} & & Y \ar[dl]^{g} \\ & S & } \, ,\]

with \(u\) own. We anneal \(X\) and \(Y\) by the reciprocal image of a beam of torsion rings \(\mathcal{A}_S\) on \(S\). For \(K \in D(Y, \mathcal{A}_Y)\), the obvious (addition) arrow \(K \longrightarrow Ru_* u^*K\) defines by application of the functor \(Rg_!\) an arrow
\[(5.1.17.1) \qquad u^* : Rg_! K \longrightarrow Rg_! Ru_* u^*K = Rg_! Ru_! u^*K \simeq Rf_! u^*K\]
(contravariance of cohomology with proper support with respect to proper morphisms).
For \(S\) spectrum of an algebraically closed body and \(K\) reduced to a beam \(\underline{G}\) placed in degree 0, this arrow is written
\[(5.1.17.2) \qquad u^* : H^q_c(Y, \underline{G}) \longrightarrow H^q_c(X, \underline{G}) \, .\]
5.2. The change of base theorem.

5.2.1. Let \(f : X \longrightarrow Y\) be a morphism of topos, \(V\) an open of \(Y\), \(U = f^{-1}(V)\) its reciprocal image in \(X\), \(\mathcal{A}\) a sheaf of rings on \(Y\), \(\mathcal{A}'\) a bundle of rings on \(X\) and \(L\) an upper bounded complex of \((\mathcal{A}', f^*\mathcal{A})\)-bimodules:
\[(5.2.1.1) \qquad \xymatrix@C=2.4em@R=2em{ U \ar[r]^{f'} \ar[d]_{j'} & V \ar[d]^{j} \\ X \ar[r]_{f} & Y } \, .\]
We then have a base change isomorphism (5.1.2.1) between functors of \(D(V, \mathcal{A})\) in \(D(X, f^*\mathcal{A})\): \(f^*j_! \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} j'_! f'^*\).

In addition, we have an obvious isomorphism between functors of \(D^-(U, f'^*\mathcal{A})\) in \(D^-(X, \mathcal{A}')\): \(L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes}_{f^*\mathcal{A}} j'_! K \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} j'_!(j'^*L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes}_{f'^*\mathcal{A}} K)\). To construct it, simply note that if a complex \(K\) is deployed for the functor \(j'^*L \otimes_{f'^*\mathcal{A}} {\ }\), then the complex \(j'_! K\) is deployed for the functor \(L \otimes_{f^*\mathcal{A}} {\ }\), and that we have an isomorphism of the previous type at the level of the complexes. Composing these two isomorphisms, we obtain a base change isomorphism between functors of \(D^-(V, \mathcal{A})\) in \(D^-(X, \mathcal{A}')\):
\[(5.2.1.2) \qquad L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes}_{f^*\mathcal{A}} f^* j_! K \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} j'_!(j'^*L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes}_{f'^*\mathcal{A}} f'^*K) \, .\]
If \(L\) is bounded to finite tor-dimensional component on \(f^*\mathcal{A}\), this isomorphism is still defined for \(K \in \operatorname{Ob} D(V, \mathcal{A})\).

5.2.2. Let \(f : X \longrightarrow S\) and \(g : S' \longrightarrow S\) be schema morphisms, and suppose that \(f\) is the compound of an open immersion \(j\) and a proper morphism \(p\), whose fiber dimension is bounded by an integer \(n\) (automatic for \(S\) quasi-compact). These morphisms fit into a Cartesian square diagram

\[\xymatrix@C=2.5em@R=2em{ X' \ar[rr]^{g'} \ar[dr]^{j'} \ar[dddr]_{f'} & & X \ar[dr]^{j} & \\ & \bar{X}' \ar[rr]^(0.32){\bar{g}} \ar[dd]^(0.35){p'} & & \bar{X} \ar[dd]^{p} \\ & & & \\ & S' \ar[rr]_{g} & & S }\]

Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be a bundle of torsion rings on \(S\), \(\mathcal{A}'\) a bundle of torsion rings on \(S'\) and \(L\) a top-bounded complex of \((\mathcal{A}', g^*\mathcal{A})\)-bimodules. We anneal \(X\) and \(\bar{X}\) (resp. \(X'\) and \(\bar{X}'\)) by the reciprocal images \(\mathcal{A}_X\) and \(\mathcal{A}_{\bar{X}}\) (resp. \(\mathcal{A}'_{X'}\) and \(\mathcal{A}'_{\bar{X}'}\)) of \(\mathcal{A}\) (resp. \(\mathcal{A}'\)) on \(X\) and \(\bar{X}\) (resp. \(X'\) and \(\bar{X}'\)). We then have base change isomorphisms (5.2.1.2) between the functors \(p'^*L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} \bar{g}^* j_! K\) and \(j'_!(f'^*L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} g'^*K)\) of \(D^-(X, \mathcal{A}_X)\) in \(D^-(\bar{X}', \mathcal{A}'_{\bar{X}'})\), and base change isomorphisms (4.3.1) between the functors \(L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} g^* Rp_* K\) and \(Rp'_*(p'^*L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} \bar{g}^*K)\) from \(D^-(\bar{X}, \mathcal{A}_{\bar{X}})\) in \(D^-(S', \mathcal{A}')\). By composition, we deduce a base change isomorphism between functors of \(D^-(X, \mathcal{A}_X)\) in \(D^-(S', \mathcal{A}')\):
\[(5.2.2.1) \qquad L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} g^* Rp_* j_! K \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} Rp'_* j'_!(f'^*L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} g'^*K) \, .\]
When \(L\) is bounded to finite tor-dimensional components, the preceding arguments and 4.3.2 (i) allow defining the base change isomorphism for \(K \in \operatorname{Ob} D(X, \mathcal{A}_X)\).

To be able to refer to 5.1.8, suppose that \(S\) is quasi-compact quasi-separated. The functors \(Rp_* j_! = Rf_!\) and \(Rp'_* j'_! = Rf'_!\) then only depend on \(f\) and \(f'\), which gives meaning to

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.2.3

The base change isomorphism (5.2.2.1) does not depend on the chosen compactification \(f = p j\) of \(X\).
Let be a compactification morphism \(q : \bar{X}_1 \longrightarrow \bar{X}_2\)

The base change isomorphism \(c_1\) relative to \(\bar{X}_1\) is obtained by "composing" those relating to \(j_1\) and \(p_1\), or by composing those relating to \(j_1\), \(q\) and \(p_2\). The isomorphism \(c_2\) relating to \(\bar{X}_2\) is obtained by composing those relating to \(j_2\) and \(p_2\). To verify that \(c_1 = c_2\), it suffices to verify that the isomorphism \(c'_1\) relative to \(j_2\) is obtained as the compound \(c'_2\) of those relative to \(j_1\) and \(q\). Let us designate with a \('\) the arrows deduced by the change of base \(g\) of the arrows (5.2.3.1); \(c'_1\) and \(c'_2\) are isomorphisms
\[c'_1, c'_2 : L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} \bar{g}_2^* j_{2!} K \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} j'_{2!}(j'^*_2 L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} g'^*K) \, .\]
According to (5.1.3.2), to check that \(c'_1 = c'_2\), it is enough to check it after having restricted ourselves to \(X'_2\), which is trivial.
The base change isomorphisms relating to two compactifications between which there is a morphism are therefore equal, and we conclude with 3.2.6 (ii).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.2.4

Let \(f = u v : X \longrightarrow Y \longrightarrow S\) be a compound of morphisms \(S\)-compactifiable. Base change isomorphisms (5.2.2.1) relative to \(u\), \(v\) and \(f\) give rise to a commutative diagram (notations of (5.2.4.1) below):

\[\xymatrix@C=2em@R=3em{ L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} g^* R(uv)_! K \ar[rr]^-{\sim} \ar@{=}[d] & & R(u'v')_!((u'v')^*L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} g''^*K) \ar@{=}[d] \\ L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} g^* Ru_! Rv_! K \ar[r]^-{\sim} & Ru'_!(u'^*L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} g'^* Rv_! K) \ar[r]^-{\sim} & Ru'_! Rv'_!(v'^*u'^*L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} g''^*K) } \, .\]
Let's choose a compactification of \((u,v)\) (3.2.5):

Lemma 5.2.4 expresses that the base change isomorphism \(c_1\) relative to \(f\) is the composite \(c_2\) of those relative to \(u\) and \(v\). The isomorphism \(c_1\) is obtained by composing the base change isomorphisms relative to \(j_1\), \(j_2\), \(p_3\) and \(p_2\). For \(c_2\), we compose those relating to \(j_1\), \(p_1\), \(j_3\), \(p_2\). It is therefore sufficient to compare the isomorphisms \(c'_1\), composed of those relating to \(p_3\) and \(j_2\), and \(c'_2\), composed of those relating to \(j_3\) and \(p_1\).
Let us consider the Cartesian diagram

\[\xymatrix@C=1.6em@R=2em{ \bar{X}' \ar[rrr]^{\bar{g}''} \ar[dr]^{j'_2} \ar[dd]_{p'_1} & & & \bar{X} \ar[dr]^{j_2} & \\ & \bar{\bar{X}}' \ar[rrr]^(0.3){\bar{\bar{g}}} \ar[drr]_(0.4){p'_3} & & & \bar{\bar{X}} \\ Y' \ar[rrr]^(0.25){g'} \ar[dr]^{j'_3} \ar[dd]_{v'} & & & Y \ar[dr]^{j_3} \ar[dd]^(0.3){v} & \\ & \bar{Y}' \ar[rrr]^(0.3){\bar{g}'} \ar[dl]_{p'_2} & & & \bar{Y} \ar[dl]^{p_2} \\ S' \ar[rrr]_{g} & & & S & \\ }\]
\[c'_1, c'_2 : L' \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} \bar{g}'^* R(j_3 p_1)_! K \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} R(j'_3 p'_1)_!((j'_3 p'_1)^*L' \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} \bar{g}''^*K) \, .\]
Under (5.1.3.2), to check that \(c'_1 = c'_2\), it is enough to check that \(j'^*_3(c'_1) = j'^*_3(c'_2)\), which is trivial.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.2.5

Let us be morphisms of schemes ringed by sheaves of torsion rings

\[(S'', \mathcal{A}'') \xrightarrow{\;g_2\;} (S', \mathcal{A}') \xrightarrow{\;g_1\;} (S, \mathcal{A}) \, ,\]

and \(f : X \longrightarrow S\) a compactifiable morphism, hence a diagram:
We anneal \(X\), \(X'\), \(X''\) by \(f^*\mathcal{A}\), \(f'^*\mathcal{A}'\) and \(f''^*\mathcal{A}''\). So, the diagram
is commutative.
This lemma is immediately deduced from the analogous results for the functors \(j_!\) and \(Rp_*\) for \(p\) proper. We leave it to the reader to modulate it.
We summarize the preceding statements in

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.2.6

The functor \(Rf_!\) commutes on base changes.

blocks.title: Variants
blocks.html:
Variants 5.2.7

(i) The functors \(f_!\) (for sheaves of pointed sets: 5.1.11) and the functors \(R^1 f_!\) (for sheaves in groups: 5.1.12) commute on change of base.

(ii) Functors \(Rf_!\) (5.1.14) commute on base changes.

(iii) Under the hypotheses (5.2.2), if \(L\) is bounded and with finite tor-dimensional components, the functors \(Rf_! : D(X) \longrightarrow D(S)\), commute to base changes.
Theorem 5.2.6 is essentially equivalent to the conjunction of the following two corollaries 5.2.8 and 5.2.9.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 5.2.8

Let \(f : X \longrightarrow S\) a compactifiable morphism, and \(\mathfrak{F}\) a torsion beam on \(X\). For any geometric point \(s\) of \(S\), let \(X_s\) the geometric fiber of \(X\) in \(s\) and \(\mathfrak{F}_s\) the induced beam. We canonically

\[(R^q f_!(\mathfrak{F}))_s = H^q_c(X_s, \mathfrak{F}_s) \, .\]

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.2.8.1

If the fibers of \(f\) are of dimension \(\leq d\), the functor \(Rf_!\) is of cohomological dimension \(\leq 2d\) (1.2.9): for any sheaf of twist \(F\), we have \(R^i f_! F = 0\) for \(i > 2d\). The functor \(R^{2d} f_!\) is therefore right-exact on the category of torsion beams.
