We return by change of base (5.2.8) to the case where \(S\) is the spectrum of an algebraically closed body. If then \(\bar{X}\) is a proper schema on \(S\) containing \(X\) as a dense Zariski open, \(j : X \longrightarrow \bar{X}\), we have \(\dim(\bar{X}) = \dim(X) \leq d\). For \(\underline{F}\) torsion beam on \(X\) and \(i > 2d\), we therefore have, by X 4.3, 5.2
\[H^i_c(X, \underline{F}) = H^i(\bar{X}, j_! \underline{F}) = 0 \, .\]

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 5.2.9

Let \(f : X \longrightarrow S\) a compactifiable morphism, \(\mathcal{A}\) a sheaf of torsion rings on \(S\), \(K\) a complex bounded above \(f^*\mathcal{A}\)-modules on the left and \(L\) a complex bounded above by \(\mathcal{A}\)-modules on the right. We canonically

\[L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes}_{\mathcal{A}} Rf_!(K) = Rf_!(f^*L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes}_{f^*\mathcal{A}} K) \, .\]
This proposition formally implies the following result:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.2.10

Let \(f : X \longrightarrow S\) a compactifiable morphism, \(\mathcal{A}\) a bundle of torsion rings on \(S\), \(K \in D^-(X, f^*\mathcal{A})\) and \(d \in \mathbb{Z}\). If \(K\) is finite-dimensional Tor \(\leq d\) (4.1.9), then \(Rf_! K\) is finite-dimensional Tor \(\leq d\).
It suffices to check that for any bundle of \(\mathcal{A}\)-modules to the right \(L\) on \(S\), we have
\[H^{-i}(L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} Rf_! K) = 0 \quad \text{pour } i > d \, .\]
According to 5.2.9, we know that
\[H^{-i}(L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} Rf_! K) = R^{-i} f_!(f^*L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} K) \, ,\]
moreover, by hypothesis,
\[H^{-i}(f^*L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} K) = 0 \quad \text{pour } i > d \, ,\]
and we conclude by noting that if a complex \(M\) of bundles of modules on \(X\) satisfies \(H^{-i}(M) = 0\) for \(i > d\), then we still have \(H^{-i}(Rf_! M) = 0\) for \(i > d\).
In the particular case of constant and Noetherian ring sheaves, we have the more general result:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.2.11

Let be \(f : X \longrightarrow S\) a site morphism, \(A\) a ring, \(C\) its center and \(d \in \mathbb{Z}\). It is assumed that

(i) the functor \(Rf_* : D^+(X,C) \longrightarrow D^+(S,C)\) is of finite cohomological dimension.

(ii) \(A\) is Noetherian on the right.

(iii) \(S\) has enough points.

For any complex \(K \in \operatorname{Ob} D^-(X,A)\) of bundles of \(A\)-modules on the left, if \(K\) is of finite Tor-dimension \(\leq d\), then the complex \(Rf_* K \in \operatorname{Ob} D^-(S,A)\) is from Tor-dimension \(\leq d\).
Proof. By virtue of (i), the functor \(f_*\) is differentiable into functors
\[Rf_* : D(X,A) \longrightarrow D(S,A)\]
and
\[Rf_* : D(X,C) \longrightarrow D(S,C)\]

According to 4.2.8, it suffices to check that for any point \(s\) of \(S\), the complex \((Rf_* K)_s\) of \(A\)-modules is of Tor-dimension \(\leq d\); for this it suffices that for every \(A\)-module on the right \(L\) of finite type we have
\[H^{-i}(L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} Rf_* K) = 0 \quad \text{pour } i > d \, .\]
Reasoning as in 5.2.10, we see that the "change of base arrow" is sufficient for this
\[(5.2.11.1) \qquad L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} Rf_* K \longrightarrow Rf_*(L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} K)\]
or an isomorphism.

Let \(L^*\) be a resolution of \(L\) by \(A\)-free modules of finite type. The considered functors being way-out to the right, it suffices to prove that (5.2.11.1) is an isomorphism after replacing \(L\) by any component of \(L_*\), and we are brought back to the trivial case where \(L\) is free of finite type.

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 5.2.12

(i) Assumption (iii) of 5.2.11 is probably unnecessary.

(ii) If we omit the Noetherian hypothesis on \(A\), 5.2.11 remains valid when the functors \(R^i f_*\) commute at filtering inductive limits.

(iii) The assumption that \(S\) is ringed by a constant ring bundle is essential. We would obtain a counter-example by taking for \(S\) the spectrum of a strictly local discrete valuation ring, for \(f\) the inclusion of the generic point \(\eta\), for sheaf of rings \(\mathcal{A}\) the sheaf worth \(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\) at the generic point and \(\mathbb{Z}/n^2\mathbb{Z}\) at the closed point \(s\) (\(n > 1\) first with the residual characteristics) and for beam the module \(f^*\mathcal{A}\).
5.3. Comparison theorem and finiteness theorem.
5.3.1. A schema \(X\) of finite type on \(\mathbb{C}\) is associated with its etal site \(X_{\mathrm{ét}}\), its transcendent site \(X_{\mathrm{cl}}\) and a morphism of sites (XI 4)
\[\varepsilon : X_{\mathrm{cl}} \longrightarrow X_{\mathrm{ét}} \, .\]
If \(j : U \longrightarrow X\) is an open immersion, the commutative diagram
\[\xymatrix@C=2.4em@R=2em{ U_{\mathrm{cl}} \ar[r] \ar[d] & U_{\mathrm{ét}} \ar[d] \\ X_{\mathrm{cl}} \ar[r] & X_{\mathrm{ét}} }\]

identifies \(\widetilde{U_{\mathrm{cl}}}\) with the open of \(\widetilde{X_{\mathrm{cl}}}\) reciprocal image of the open \(\widetilde{U_{\mathrm{ét}}}\) of \(\widetilde{X_{\mathrm{ét}}}\). If \(p : Y \longrightarrow X\) is a proper morphism, the continued application \(p^{\mathrm{an}} : Y^{\mathrm{an}} \longrightarrow X^{\mathrm{an}}\) is clean and separate. To see this, we return using Chow's lemma to the projective case \(p\).
Let \(f : X \longrightarrow S\) be a compactifiable morphism of finite type schemes on \(\mathbb{C}\) and let \(f = p j\) be a compactification of \(f\).

\[(5.3.1.1) \qquad \xymatrix@C=2em@R=2em{ X_{\mathrm{cl}} \ar[rrr]^{\varepsilon} \ar[dr]^{j_{\mathrm{cl}}} \ar[dddr]_{f_{\mathrm{cl}}} & & & X \ar[dr]^{j} & \\ & \bar{X}_{\mathrm{cl}} \ar[rrr]^(0.35){\varepsilon} \ar[dd]^(0.35){p_{\mathrm{cl}}} & & & \bar{X} \ar[dd]^{p} \\ & & & & \\ & S_{\mathrm{cl}} \ar[rrr]_{\varepsilon} & & & S }\]

The functor \(p_{\mathrm{cl}*} j_{\mathrm{cl}!}\) is none other than the direct image functor with proper support \(f_{\mathrm{cl}!}\) (see VERDIER [3]). If \(S_{\mathrm{cl}}\), therefore \(X_{\mathrm{cl}}\) is separated, the functor \(j_{\mathrm{cl}!}\) transforms soft beams on \(X_{\mathrm{cl}}\) into soft beams on \(\bar{X}_{\mathrm{cl}}\) [TF II 3.5.5]. The question being local on \(S\), it in any case transforms flaccid sheaves on \(X_{\mathrm{cl}}\) into acyclic sheaves on \(X_{\mathrm{cl}}\) for the functor \(p_{\mathrm{cl}*}\). We therefore have (cf. VERDIER [3])
\[Rf_{\mathrm{cl}*} = Rp_{\mathrm{cl}*} j_{\mathrm{cl}!} \, .\]
5.3.2. According to 5.1.2, we have a base change isomorphism, between functors of \(D(X)\) in \(D(\bar{X}_{\mathrm{cl}})\)
\[\varepsilon^*j_! \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} j_{\mathrm{cl}!} \varepsilon^* \, .\]
According to 4.2.12 or 4.1.5, we have a base change morphism between functors of \(D^+(\bar{X})\) in \(D^+(S_{\mathrm{cl}})\):
\[\varepsilon^*Rp_* \longrightarrow Rp_{\mathrm{cl}*} \varepsilon^* \, .\]
By composition, we deduce a base change morphism, between functors of \(D^+_{\mathrm{tors}}(X)\) in \(D^+_{\mathrm{tors}}(S_{\mathrm{cl}})\) (5.1.13)
\[(5.3.2.1) \qquad \varepsilon^*Rf_! \longrightarrow Rf_{\mathrm{cl}!} \varepsilon^* \, .\]
We check as in 5.2.3 that it does not depend on the chosen compactification of \(f\); it verifies a transitivity formula of type 5.2.4.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.3.3

(Comparison theorem). Morphism (5.3.2.1) is an isomorphism.

By construction, it is sufficient to deal with the case where \(f\) is clean. The functors considered are triangulated and “way-out” [RD I 7]. It is therefore sufficient to treat the case where \(K\) reduced to a torsion beam placed in degree 0, and we conclude with XVI 4.1 (i).

blocks.title: Variants
blocks.html:
Variants 5.3.4

(i) If \(\mathcal{A}\) is a sheaf of torsion rings on \(S_{\mathrm{ét}}\), we similarly obtain an isomorphism (5.3.2.1) between functors of \(D(X, f^*\mathcal{A})\) in \(D(S, \varepsilon^*\mathcal{A})\).

(ii) In low degree, we find the usual variants for sheaves of pointed sets and sheaves of Ind-finite groups.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.3.5

Let \(X\) a separate finite type scheme on \(\mathbb{C}\), which can be compacted into a clean diagram on \(\mathbb{C}\), And \(F\) a torsion beam on \(X\). We then have

\[H^q_c(X, F) \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} H^q_c(X_{\mathrm{cl}}, \varepsilon^*F) \, .\]

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.3.6

Let \(f : X \longrightarrow S\) a compactifiable morphism of finite presentation and \(A\) a left-hand Noetherian torsion ring. The functor \(Rf_! : D(X,A) \longrightarrow D(S,A)\) transforms complexes with constructible cohomology into complexes with constructible cohomology.

The assertion is local to \(S\), which can be assumed to be affine. By passing to the limit, we return to the \(S\) Noetherian case. Complexes with constructible cohomology form a triangulated subcategory of \(D(S,A)\). The functor \(Rf_!\) being "way-out", we come down to verifying that if \(\mathfrak{F}\) is a constructible bundle of \(A\)-modules, the bundles \(R^q f_! \mathfrak{F}\) are constructible. Let \(f = pj\), with \(p\) clean and \(j\) an open immersion. We have \(R^q f_! \mathfrak{F} = R^q p_*(j_! \mathfrak{F})\). The beam \(j_! \mathfrak{F}\) is constructible and we conclude with XIV 1.1.
5.3.7. In lower degrees, we have analogous statements for direct images with proper support of a constructible sheaf of Ind-finite sets or groups.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.3.8

Let \(X\) a separate finite type scheme on a separably closed body \(k\) and \(\mathfrak{F}\) a constructible torsion beam on \(X\). We assume that \(X\) can be compactified into a clean schema on \(k\). So, the groups \(H^q_c(X, \mathfrak{F})\) are finished.
5.4. Künneth formula.
5.4.1. Consider a commutative diagram (0.11) of sites

\[(5.4.1.1) \qquad \xymatrix@C=1.6em@R=2em{ & & Z' \ar[dll]_{p'} \ar[d]^{h} \ar[drr]^{q'} & & \\ X' \ar[dr]_{f} & & Z \ar[dl]_(0.4){p} \ar[dr]^(0.4){q} & & Y' \ar[dl]^{g} \\ & X \ar[dr] & & Y \ar[dl] & \\ & & S & & }\]

We suppose \(S\) ringed by a bundle of rings \(\mathcal{A}\), with center \(\mathcal{C}\); we will again designate by \(\mathcal{A}\) and \(\mathcal{C}\) the reciprocal images of these beams on the sites of (5.4.1.1).
If \(K\) is a bundle of \(\mathcal{A}\)-modules on the right on \(X\) and \(L\) a bundle of \(\mathcal{A}\)-modules on the left on \(Y\), we set
\[K \boxtimes_{\mathcal{A}} L = p^*K \otimes_{\mathcal{A}} q^*L\]

(external tensor product). The same for \(K\) (resp. \(L\)) on \(X'\) (resp. \(Y'\)). Let then \(K\) be a bundle of \(\mathcal{A}\)-modules on the right on \(X'\) and \(L\) be a bundle of \(\mathcal{A}\)-modules on the left on \(Y'\). Base change arrows (XII 4.1) define
\[(5.4.1.2) \qquad \begin{cases} p^*f_* K \longrightarrow h_* p'^*K \\[4pt] q^*g_* L \longrightarrow h_* q'^*L \, , \end{cases}\]
hence, by tensor product, an arrow
\[(5.4.1.3) \qquad f_*K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\boxtimes}_{\mathcal{A}} g_*L \longrightarrow h_* p'^*K \otimes_{\mathcal{A}} h_* q'^*L \longrightarrow h_*(K \boxtimes_{\mathcal{A}} L) \, .\]
Assume that the functors

\[\begin{aligned} Rf_* &: D^+(X', \mathcal{A}^o) \longrightarrow D^+(X, \mathcal{A}^o) \\ Rg_* &: D^+(Y', \mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow D^+(Y, \mathcal{A}) \\ Rh_* &: D^+(Z', \mathcal{C}) \longrightarrow D^+(Z, \mathcal{C}) \end{aligned}\]

are of finite cohomological dimension, and therefore extend to integer derived categories. It is then necessary, for \(K \in \operatorname{Ob} D^-(X', \mathcal{A}^o)\) and \(L \in \operatorname{Ob} D^-(Y', \mathcal{A})\), to define a Künneth arrow
\[(5.4.1.4) \qquad Rf_* K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\boxtimes}_{\mathcal{A}} Rg_* L \longrightarrow Rh_*(K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\boxtimes}_{\mathcal{A}} L) \, .\]

We will only define it if the sites considered have enough points, so as to be able to use 4.2.10 and 4.2.8. Under 4.2.10, it is sufficient to define it for a pair \((K,L)\) deployed on the left for the functor \((K,L) \longmapsto K \boxtimes_{\mathcal{A}} L\), \(K\) and \(L\) being deployed on the right for the functors \(f_*\) and \(g_*\) and bounded superiorly.

Let \(K_1\) be a flat resolution of \(f_* K\), \(L_1\) be a flat resolution of \(g_*L\) and \(u : K \boxtimes L \longrightarrow M\) be a resolution of \(K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\boxtimes}_{\mathcal{A}} L\), deployed for the functor \(Rh_*\). We have morphisms

\[Rf_*K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\boxtimes}_{\mathcal{A}} Rg_*L \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} K_1 \boxtimes_{\mathcal{A}} L_1 \longrightarrow f_*K \boxtimes_{\mathcal{A}} g_*L \longrightarrow h_*(K \boxtimes_{\mathcal{A}} L) \longrightarrow h_*M \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} Rh_*(K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\boxtimes}_{\mathcal{A}} L)\]
and we define (5.4.1.4) as their compound.
This arrow has the following characteristic property, very analogous to that of 4.2.13:

5.4.1.5. Let \(K \in \operatorname{Ob} C^-(X', \mathcal{A}^o)\) and \(L \in \operatorname{Ob} C^-(Y', \mathcal{A})\). Let's set \(K_1 = f_*K\), \(L_1 = g_*L\), and \(M_2 = K \boxtimes L\). The following diagram is then commutative

\[\xymatrix@C=2.6em@R=3em{ Rf_*K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\boxtimes}_{\mathcal{A}} Rg_* L \ar[rrr] & & & Rh_*(K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\boxtimes}_{\mathcal{A}} L) \ar[dd] \\ & & & \\ K_1 \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\boxtimes}_{\mathcal{A}} L_1 \ar[uu] \ar[r] & K_1 \boxtimes_{\mathcal{A}} L_1 \ar[r] & h_* M_2 \ar[r] & Rh_* M_2 } \, .\]
We have an analogous commutativity (see 4.2.13) starting instead from morphisms \(K_1 \longrightarrow f_*K\), \(L_1 \longrightarrow g_*L\) and \(K \boxtimes L \longrightarrow M_2\).
Commutativity 5.4.1.5 makes it in principle easy to check the compatibility properties of the Künneth morphism.

5.4.2. Let be a commutative diagram (5.4.1.1) of schemes and \(\mathcal{A}\) a torsion beam on \(S\). Suppose that \(f\) and \(g\) are compactifiable (3.2.1), that \(Z = X \times_S Y\) and that \(Z' = X' \times_S Y'\). Let us factor \(f\) and \(g\) into a proper morphism and an open immersion; we obtain a diagram

\[\xymatrix@C=2.2em@R=2.2em{ X' \ar[d]_{j_1} \ar@/_2.6em/[dd]_{f} & Z' \ar[l]_{p'} \ar[r]^{q'} \ar[d]^{j_3} & Y' \ar[d]^{j_2} \ar@/^2.6em/[dd]^{g} \\ \bar{X}' \ar[d]_{\bar{f}} & \bar{Z}' \ar[l]_(0.45){\bar{p}} \ar[r]^(0.45){\bar{q}} \ar[d]^{\bar{h}} & \bar{Y}' \ar[d]^{\bar{g}} \\ X & Z \ar[l]^{p} \ar[r]_{q} & Y }\]
in which we put \(\bar{Z}' = \bar{X}' \times_S \bar{Y}'\). We have an isomorphism of a trivial nature, for complex \(K\) and \(L\) on \(X'\) and \(Y'\)
\[(5.4.2.1) \qquad j_{3!}(K \boxtimes_{\mathcal{A}} L) \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} j_{1!} K \boxtimes_{\mathcal{A}} j_{2!} L \, .\]

By "composing" the inverse of this isomorphism and the Künneth arrow (5.4.1.4) relative to \((\bar{f}, \bar{g}, \bar{h}, j_{1!} K, j_{2!} L)\), we obtain, for \(K \in \operatorname{Ob} D^-(X', \mathcal{A})\) and \(L \in \operatorname{Ob} D^-(Y', \mathcal{A})\), a Künneth arrow
\[(5.4.2.2) \qquad Rf_! K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\boxtimes}_{\mathcal{A}} Rg_! L \longrightarrow Rh_!(K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\boxtimes}_{\mathcal{A}} L) \, ,\]
which we verify that it does not depend on the chosen compactifications.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.4.3

Under the assumptions of 5.4.2, the Künneth arrow in cohomology with proper supports (5.4.2.2) is an isomorphism.
Proof. (a) By construction, it is enough to verify this result when \(f\) and \(g\) (therefore also \(h\)) are clean.
(b) Consider a commutative diagram
\[(5.4.3.1) \qquad \xymatrix@C=2.4em@R=2em{ X_1 \ar[r]^{p_1} \ar[d] & S_1 \ar[d] & Y_1 \ar[l]_{q_1} \ar[d] \\ X \ar[r]_{p} & S & Y \ar[l]^{q} }\]
and are \(Z_1 = X_1 \times_{S_1} Y_1\), \(X'_1 = X' \times_X X_1\), \(Y'_1 = Y' \times_Y Y_1\) and \(Z'_1 = X'_1 \times_{S_1} Y'_1 = Z' \times_Z Z_1\), so that we have a diagram morphism (5.4.1),
whose arrows will be uniformly designated by \(C\).
We will admit that:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.4.3.2

The following diagram is commutative:

\[\xymatrix@C=1.4em@R=3.5em{ C^*(Rf_! K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\boxtimes}_{\mathcal{A}} Rg_! L) \ar@{=}[r] \ar[d] & C^*Rf_!K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\boxtimes}_{\mathcal{A}} C^* Rg_! L \ar[r]^-{\sim} & Rf_{1!} C^*K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\boxtimes}_{\mathcal{A}} Rg_{1!} C^* L \ar[d] \\ C^*(Rh_!(K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\boxtimes}_{\mathcal{A}} L)) \ar[r]^-{\sim} & Rh_!(C^*(K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\boxtimes}_{\mathcal{A}} L)) \ar@{=}[r] & Rh_!(C^*K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\boxtimes}_{\mathcal{A}} C^* L) } \, .\]
In this diagram, the horizontal arrows are base change arrows and the vertical arrows are Künneth arrows for the two diagrams (5.4.1.1) considered.

To prove 5.4.3, it is enough to prove that the Künneth arrow is an isomorphism at any geometric point of \(Z\). According to 5.4.3.2, it is enough to check it after any change of basis (5.4.3.1) such that \(S_1\) is the spectrum of an algebraically closed field and \(p_1\), \(q_1\) isomorphisms.
After such a change of base, the diagram (5.4.1.1) is reduced to a diagram
\[(5.4.3.3) \qquad \xymatrix@C=1.8em@R=2em{ & X \times_S Y \ar[dl]_{p} \ar[dd]^(0.32){f \times g} \ar[dr]^{q} & \\ X \ar[dr]_{f} & & Y \ar[dl]^{g} \\ & S & }\]
and the one-arrow Künneth arrow
\[(5.4.3.4) \qquad k : Rf_! K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\boxtimes}_{\mathcal{A}} Rg_! L \longrightarrow R(f\times g)_!(K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\boxtimes}_{\mathcal{A}} L) \, .\]
(c) Consider a diagram (5.4.3.3) in which \(f\) and \(g\) are proper. We will admit that:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.4.3.5

The following diagram is commutative

\[\xymatrix@C=3em@R=3em{ Rf_! K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\boxtimes}_{\mathcal{A}} Rg_! L \ar[r] \ar[d] & R(f\times g)_!(K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\boxtimes}_{\mathcal{A}} L) \ar@{=}[d] \\ Rf_!(K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes}_{\mathcal{A}} f^* Rg_! L) \ar[r] & Rf_!(Rp_!(p^*K \otimes q^*L)) } \, .\]

In this diagram, the horizontal arrows are the Künneth arrows and the base change arrow. The vertical arrows are a base change arrow by \(f\) and a transitivity isomorphism. Theorem 5.4.3 is a consequence of this lemma and 4.3.1.
The courageous reader will be able to verify that this lemma remains valid if \(f\) and \(g\) are not necessarily clean.

5.4.4. Let \(S\) be a schema, \(f_i : X'_i \longrightarrow X_i\) (\(i \in I\)) a finite family of compactifiable morphisms of \(S\)-schemes, \(X\) the product on \(S\) of \(X_i\), \(X'\) the product of \(S\) of \(X'_i\) and \(f : X' \longrightarrow X\) the product of \(f_i\). If \(S\) is annealed by \(\mathcal{A}\), as in 5.4.1, and if we anneal the \(X_i\) and \(X'_i\) by the reciprocal image of \(\mathcal{A}\), we more generally have an isomorphism
\[(5.4.4.1) \qquad \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\underset{i \in I}{\boxtimes}}\, Rf_{i!}(K_i) \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} Rf_!\Bigl(\overset{\mathrm{L}}{\underset{i \in I}{\boxtimes}}\, K_i\Bigr) \, ,\]
for \(K_i \in \operatorname{Ob} D^-(X'_i, \mathcal{A})\). The definition of this isomorphism, as well as that of the two members of (5.4.4.1) does not require the choice of a total order on \(I\).
5.5. Cohomology of symmetric powers.

The main result of this issue is theorem 5.5.21, giving in particular the cohomology with proper supports of a symmetric power of a scheme \(X\) on an algebraically closed field \(k\), for suitable coefficients (for example \(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\)) in terms of the cohomology with proper supports of \(X\). The analogue of this theorem in semi-simplicial topology is well known, at least for cohomology with coefficients in a commutative ring \(A\); it is verified at the level of semi-simplicial chains. Indeed, if \(X_\bullet\) is a semi-simplicial set, there exists a canonical continuous bijection of the geometric realization of the symmetric power \(n\)-th \((X_\bullet)^n/\mathfrak{S}_n\) of \(X_\bullet\) into the symmetric power \(n^{\text{ième}}\) of the geometric realization of \(X_\bullet\), and this bijection induces an isomorphism on the cohomology.

5.5.1. Let us recall some properties (due to D. LAZARD) of flat modules, some properties (due to ROBY) of the functors \(\mathrm{TS}^n\) and \(\Gamma^n\), and some properties (due to DOLD and PUPPE) of functors derived from non-additive functors.
5.5.1.1. Any flat module on a ring \(A\) is filtering inductive limit of free modules of finite type (LAZARD [1, I 1.2]). This result implies the following:

5.5.1.2. The functor \(\varinjlim\) is an equivalence of the category of Ind-objects (I 8.1) of the category of \(A\)-free modules of finite type (resp. projectives of finite type) with the category of \(A\)-flat modules.

5.5.1.3. If \(A\) and \(B\) are two rings, any functor \(T : \operatorname{Mod}(A) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Mod}(B)\) which transforms free finite type module into flat module and switches to filtering inductive limits, transforms flat module into flat module (LAZARD [1, I 1.5]).
5.5.1.4. Any exact sequence of \(A\)-modules, with \(L''\) flat,
\[0 \longrightarrow L' \longrightarrow L \longrightarrow L'' \longrightarrow 0\]
is inductive limit of split exact sequences (LAZARD [1, I 2.3]).
5.5.2. Let \(A\) be a commutative ring and \(M\) be a \(A\)-module.

5.5.2.1. For any integer \(n\), we denote by \(\mathrm{TS}^n(M)\) (or \(\mathrm{TS}^n_A(M)\)) the module of the symmetric tensors of degree \(n\) of \(M\). Designating by \(\mathfrak{S}_n\) the symmetric group of degree \(n\), we have by definition
\[\mathrm{TS}^n(M) = (\otimes^n M)^{\mathfrak{S}_n} \, .\]

If \(B\) is a \(A\)-algebra, then \(\mathrm{TS}^n(B)\) is a subalgebra of \(\otimes^n B\). If further \(B\) is commutative, then \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathrm{TS}^n(B))\) is by definition the symmetric power \(n^{\text{ième}}\) of \(\operatorname{Spec}(B)\) on \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\):

\[\operatorname{Spec}(\mathrm{TS}^n_A(B)) = \operatorname{Sym}^n_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)}(\operatorname{Spec}(B)) \underset{\mathrm{dfn}}{=} (\operatorname{Spec}(B)/\operatorname{Spec}(A))^n/\mathfrak{S}_n \, .\]

5.5.2.2. Recall (ROBY [1, I 1 pg.226]) that a polynomial law \(f\) of a \(A\)-module \(M\) in a \(A\)-module \(N\) is the data, for any \(A\)-commutative algebra \(A'\), of an application \(f_{A'} : M \otimes_A A' \longrightarrow N \otimes_A A'\), these applications being such that for any homomorphism of \(A\)-algebras \(u : A' \longrightarrow A''\), the diagram
\[\xymatrix@C=3em@R=2.5em{ M \otimes_A A' \ar[r]^{f_{A'}} \ar[d]_{u} & N \otimes_A A' \ar[d]^{u} \\ M \otimes_A A'' \ar[r]_{f_{A''}} & N \otimes_A A'' }\]
be commutative.
A polynomial distribution \(f\) is said to be homogeneous of degree \(n\) (ROBY [1, I 1 pg.226]) if, for all \(a' \in A'\), we have identically
Such a polynomial law will also be called application \(n^{\text{ique}}\) (quadratic, cubic, ...); we will note their together
\[\operatorname{Hom} n^{\text{ique}}(M,N) \, .\]

More generally, if \((M_i)_{i \in I}\) is a finite family of \(A\)-modules and \(\underline{n} = (n_i)_{i \in I}\) a family of integers, a multihomogeneous polynomial law of multidegree \(\underline{n}\) of the modules \(M_i\) in \(N\) is a polynomial distribution of \(\sum M_i\) in \(N\) satisfying identically, for \(a_i \in A'\),
\[f_{A'}((a_i x_i)_{i \in I}) = \prod_{i \in I} a_i^{n_i} \cdot f_{A'}((x_i)_{i \in I}) \, .\]
We will note \(\operatorname{Hom} \underline{n}^{\text{ique}}((M_i),N)\) all of these polynomial laws.
5.5.2.3. For any module \(M\), the covariant functor of the \(A\)-modules in the sets
\[N \longmapsto \operatorname{Hom} n^{\text{ique}}(M,N)\]

is representable by a \(A\)-module \(\Gamma^n(M)\) (or \(\Gamma^n_A(M)\)), equipped with a application \(n^{\text{ique}}\) universal \(\gamma^n : M \longrightarrow \Gamma^n(M)\) (ROBY [1, IV 1 pg.226]). Given \((M_i)_{i \in I}\) and \(\underline{n}\) as in 5.5.2.2, the covariant functor
\[N \longmapsto \operatorname{Hom} \underline{n}^{\text{ique}}((M_i),N)\]

is represented by the module \(\bigotimes_{i \in I} \Gamma^{n_i}(M_i)\), equipped with the multihomogeneous polynomial law (of multidegree \(\underline{n}\)) universal \((m_i) \longmapsto \bigotimes_{i \in I} \gamma^{n_i}(m_i)\).

5.5.2.4. The functors \(\mathrm{TS}^n\) and \(\Gamma^n\) commute at the filtering inductive limits (for the second, (ROBY [1, IV 6 pg.275])). They transform free modules into free modules, therefore flat modules into flat modules (5.5.1.2).

5.5.2.5. The application \(n^{\text{ique}}\) of \(M\) in \(\mathrm{TS}^n(M)\) given by \(m \longmapsto \otimes^n m\) defines a so-called canonical application \(\varphi\), of \(\Gamma^n(M)\) in \(\mathrm{TS}^n(M)\), checking
\[\varphi(\gamma^n(m)) = \otimes^n m \, .\]
This application is an isomorphism for free \(M\) of finite type (ROBY [1,IV pg.272]) therefore also for \(M\) flat (5.5.2.4 and 5.5.1.1).
Let \(M\) be a \(A\)-module and \(P\) be an exact sequence
\[P : P_1 \xrightarrow{\;d\;} P_0 \xrightarrow{\;\varepsilon\;} M \longrightarrow 0 \, .\]
Let \(P'_1 = P_1 \times P_0\), and let \(j_0\) and \(j_1\) be the morphisms of \(P'_1\) in \(P_0\) with coordinates \((d, \operatorname{Id})\) and \((0, \operatorname{Id})\). The rest
\[\Gamma^n(P'_1) \mathrel{\overset{\Gamma^n(j_0)}{\underset{\Gamma^n(j_1)}{\rightrightarrows}}} \Gamma^n(P_0) \longrightarrow \Gamma^n(M) \longrightarrow 0\]
is then exact (ROBY [1 pg.284]). For \(P\) a flat presentation of \(M\), we deduce a description of the functor \(\Gamma^n\) via the functor \(\mathrm{TS}^n\)
\[(5.5.2.5.1) \qquad \mathrm{TS}^n(P'_1) \rightrightarrows \mathrm{TS}^n(P_0) \longrightarrow \Gamma^n(M) \longrightarrow 0 \, .\]
5.5.2.6. If \(M = M' \oplus M''\), the application \(n^{\text{ique}}\)
\[m \longmapsto (\gamma^{n'} m \otimes \gamma^{n''} m)_{n'+n''=n}\]
defines an isomorphism, called canonical (ROBY [1, III 4])
\[(5.5.2.6.1) \qquad \Gamma^n(M) \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \sum_{n'+n''=n} \Gamma^{n'}(M') \otimes \Gamma^{n''}(M'') \, .\]
If \(\Sigma\) is an exact sequence
\[\Sigma : 0 \longrightarrow M' \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow M'' \longrightarrow 0 \, ,\]
the module \(\Gamma^n(M)\) is provided with canonical (decreasing) filtration and morphisms
\[(5.5.2.6.2) \qquad \Gamma^i(M') \otimes \Gamma^{n-i}(M'') \longrightarrow \operatorname{Gr}^i(\Gamma^n(M)) \, .\]

This data is characterized by the properties of being functorial in \(\Sigma\) and of checking obvious compatibility with (5.5.2.6.1) for \(\Sigma\) splittable. The arrows (5.5.2.6.2) are isomorphisms for splittable \(\Sigma\), hence also for flat \(M'\) (5.5.1.4).
5.5.2.7. The functor \(\Gamma^n\) commutes to the extension of scalars (ROBY [1, III 3 pg.262]) (the analogous property for \(\mathrm{TS}^n\) is generally false).
5.5.2.8. If \(u : M_1 \times M_2 \longrightarrow N\) is a bilinear map, then \(\gamma^n \circ u\) is a bihomogeneous polynomial law of bidedegree \((n,n)\) and therefore defines
\[(5.5.2.3') \qquad u^n : \Gamma^n(M_1) \otimes \Gamma^n(M_2) \longrightarrow \Gamma^n(N) \quad \text{vérifiant} \quad u^n(\gamma^n x \otimes \gamma^n y) = \gamma^n u(x,y) \, .\]

In particular, if \(B\) is a \(A\)-algebra, the product: \(B \times B \longrightarrow B\) induces a product on \(\Gamma^n(B)\). This product makes \(\Gamma^n(B)\) a \(A\)-algebra, and the canonical map of \(\Gamma^n(B)\) into \(\mathrm{TS}^n(B)\) is a homomorphism of algebras.

5.5.3. Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category. We will designate by \(\operatorname{ssimpl}^+(\mathcal{A})\) the category of co-semi-simplicial objects of \(\mathcal{A}\), by \(C^{\geq 0}(\mathcal{A})\) the category of differential complexes \(K\) with \(K^i = 0\) for \(i < 0\), and by \(K^{\geq 0}(\mathcal{A})\) the image of \(C^{\geq 0}(\mathcal{A})\) in \(K(\mathcal{A})\). If \(K \in \operatorname{Ob} \operatorname{ssimpl}^+(\mathcal{A})\), the associated normalized complex \(N(K) \in \operatorname{Ob} C^{\geq 0}(\mathcal{A})\) is defined by
\[(5.5.3.1) \qquad \begin{cases} N(K)^n = \text{intersection des noyaux des opérateurs de dégénérescence de } K^n \text{ dans } K^{n-1} \\[4pt] d = \sum (-1)^i \partial_i \end{cases}\]

If we further denote by \(K\) the complex of components \(K^n\), and differential \(d = \sum (-1)^i \partial_i\), then \(N(K)\) is a direct factor subcomplex in \(K\), and \(K/N(K)\) is homotopic to zero.
According to DOLD-PUPPE [1], the functor \(N\) is an equivalence of categories
\[N : \operatorname{ssimpl}^+(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow C^{\geq 0}(\mathcal{A}) \, .\]

This equivalence and its inverse transform homotopic morphisms into homotopic morphisms. Furthermore, for all \(K \in \operatorname{Ob} \operatorname{ssimpl}^+(\mathcal{A})\), \(K^n\) is sum of copies of \(N(K)^i\) (\(i \leq n\)); if \(\mathbb{P}\) is a property of objects of \(\mathcal{A}\), stable by finite sums and direct factors, we deduce that the \(K^i\) have the property \(\mathbb{P}\) for \(i \leq n\) if and only if the \(N(K)^i\) have this property for \(i \leq n\).

The equivalence \(N\) is used by DOLD and PUPPE to extend any (not necessarily additive) functor \(T : \mathcal{A} \longrightarrow \mathcal{B}\) of \(\mathcal{A}\) in any category \(\mathcal{B}\) into a functor \(C^{\geq 0}(T)\) (or simply \(T\)) of \(C^{\geq 0}(\mathcal{A})\) in \(C^{\geq 0}(\mathcal{B})\)
The functor \(C^{\geq 0}(T)\) passes to the quotient to define
\[(5.5.3.3) \qquad T \text{ ou } K^{\geq 0}(T) : K^{\geq 0}(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow K^{\geq 0}(\mathcal{B}) \, .\]

5.5.3.4. If \(T\) is of degree \(\leq n\), i.e. if the \((n+1)^{\text{ième}}\) "cross effect" is zero (DOLD-PUPPE [1, 4.18 pg.232]) then, for all \(K \in \operatorname{Ob} C^{\geq 0}(\mathcal{A})\) such that \(K^i = 0\) for \(i > m\), we have \([T(K)]^i = 0\) for \(i > nm\) (DOLD-PUPPE [1, 4.2.3 pg.233]).

5.5.3.5. In particular, if \(T\) is a finite degree functor from the category of \(A\)-modules in that of \(B\)-modules, which transforms flat object into flat object, then a flat bounded complex has as its image a flat bounded complex.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.5.4

Let \(\mathcal{A}\) an abelian category.

(i) Quasi-isomorphisms in \(K^{\geq 0}(\mathcal{A})\) allow calculation of fractions on the left and right.

(ii) The obvious functor is an equivalence between the category deduced from \(K^{\geq 0}(\mathcal{A})\) by reversing the quasi-isomorphisms and the category \(D^{\geq 0}(\mathcal{A})\).

The functor \(\tau_{\geq 0} : C(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow C^{\geq 0}(\mathcal{A})\) (1.1.13) passes to the quotient to define a functor \(\tau_{\geq 0} : K(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow K^{\geq 0}(\mathcal{A})\), left adjoint to the inclusion of \(K^{\geq 0}(\mathcal{A})\) in \(K(\mathcal{A})\). The functor \(\tau_{\geq 0}\) transforms quasi-isomorphisms into quasi-isomorphisms, and the addition arrow \(X \longrightarrow \tau_{\geq 0}(X)\) is a quasi-isomorphism if and only if \(H^i(X) = 0\) for \(i < 0\). Lemma 5.5.4 therefore results from VERDIER [CD] and points (i) and (ii) of the following lemma, the proof of which is left to the reader:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.5.4.1

Let \(K\) a category, \(K'\) a full subcategory, \(S\) a set of morphisms of \(K\) equal to the inverse image of the set of isomorphisms of \(K(S^{-1})\) (cf. [GAB.ZIS 1, chap 1]) and \(S'\) its trace in \(K'\). We assume that the inclusion \(i\) of \(K'\) in \(K\) admits a left adjoint \(\tau\), and that \(\tau(S) \subset S'\).

(i) The obvious functor of \(K'(S'^{-1})\) in \(K(S^{-1})\) is fully faithful, and admits \(\tau\) as left adjoint.

(ii) If \(K(S^{-1})\) can be calculated by calculating fractions on the left (resp. on the right), then \(K'(S'^{-1})\) has the same property.

If the inclusion \(i\) does not necessarily have a left adjoint, and if \(\tau\) designates the non-starting functor defined as left adjoint to \(i\), we have:

(iii) If \(K(S^{-1})\) can be calculated by calculating fractions on the left (resp. on the right) and if for all \(u \in S\), of goal (resp. of source) in \(K'\), \(\tau(u)\) is defined and in \(S'\), then \(K'(S'^{-1})\) admits a calculation of fractions on the left (resp. on the right) and the functor \(K'(S'^{-1}) \longrightarrow K(S^{-1})\) is fully faithful.
5.5.5. Let \(T : \mathcal{A} \longrightarrow \mathcal{B}\) be a functor between abelian categories. As in 1.2.1, we define the derivatives of \(T\)

\[\begin{aligned} LT &: D^{\geq 0}(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Pro}(D^{\geq 0}(\mathcal{B})) \\ RT &: D^{\geq 0}(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Ind}(D^{\geq 0}(\mathcal{B})) \end{aligned}\]
by formulas
\[LT(K) = \varprojlim_{s : K' \to K} T(K') \, , \qquad RT(K) = \varinjlim_{s : K \to K'} T(K') \, ,\]
where \(s\) traverses the filtering category of goal (resp. source) quasi-isomorphisms \(K\).

We say that \(T\) is left differentiable (resp. right) in \(K\) if \(LT(K)\) (\(RT(K)\)) is representable. We say that \(K\) is deployed on the left (resp. on the right) for \(T\) (or, by misnomer, deployed for \(LT\) (resp. \(RT\))) if the following canonical arrow is an isomorphism:
\[LT\, K \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} TK \qquad (\text{resp. } TK \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} RT\, K) \, .\]

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 5.5.6

Let \(F\) a functor from the category of modules on a ring \(A\) in an abelian category \(\mathcal{B}\). Suppose that the filtering inductive limits in \(\mathcal{B}\) are representable and are exact, and that \(F\) commutes to the filtering inductive limits. Then any bounded complex \(M^\bullet \in \operatorname{Ob} K^{\geq 0}(A)\) of flat modules is deployed for \(LF\).

The proposition follows immediately from the definitions, from the following lemma, which will be proven in 5.5.6.4, and from the fact that the quasi-isomorphisms \(u : N^\bullet \longrightarrow M^\bullet\), with \(N^\bullet \in C^{b,\geq 0}(A)\) with flat components, form a cofinal set in the category of goal quasi-isomorphisms \(M^\bullet\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.5.6.1

Let \(u : N^\bullet \longrightarrow M^\bullet\) a quasi-isomorphism between flat complexes, objects of \(C^{b,\geq 0}(A)\). Then, \(F(u)\) is a quasi-isomorphism.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.5.6.2

Let \(\mathcal{A}\) an additive category and \(d\) an upper bounded interval of \(\mathbb{Z}\). We denote by \(C^d\) (resp. \(C^{d,b}\), resp. \(K^d\), resp. \(K^{d,b}\)) the category of zero complexes in degrees \(i \notin d\) (resp. and bounded, resp. to near homotopy). We have an equivalence

\[\ell : \operatorname{Ind}(C^{d,b}(\mathcal{A})) \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} C^d(\operatorname{Ind} \mathcal{A}) \, ;\]

the inverse of this equivalence induces a functor

\[\ell' : K^d(\operatorname{Ind} \mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Ind}(K^{d,b}(\mathcal{A})) \, .\]
A) Let's prove that \(\ell\) is fully faithful. Let \(K\) therefore be a bounded complex of objects of \(\mathcal{A}\) and \(L = \varinjlim L_\alpha\) be an Ind-complex. We have

\[\begin{aligned} \operatorname{Hom}(K,L) &\underset{\mathrm{dfn}}{=} \varinjlim \operatorname{Hom}(K,L_\alpha) = \varinjlim Z^0(\operatorname{Hom}^\bullet(K,L_\alpha)) \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} Z^0 \varinjlim_\alpha \operatorname{Hom}^\bullet(K,L_\alpha) \\ &\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} Z^0 \operatorname{Hom}^\bullet(K, \ell L) = \operatorname{Hom}(K, \ell L) \, , \end{aligned}\]
the penultimate isomorphism coming from the fact that \(K\) is finite, so that the \(\varinjlim\) switches when going from double to simple complex. This proves the assertion.

B) We leave it to the reader (quite tedious) to verify that \(\ell\) is essentially surjective by constructing, from left to right, from an arbitrarily chosen degree, "sufficient" bounded complexes being sent into a higher bounded complex of Ind-objects \(K\); and to similarly develop the following point:

C) If \(\ell f : \ell \varinjlim K_\alpha \longrightarrow \ell \varinjlim L_\beta\) is homotopy to zero: \(f = d H + Hd\), then, for all \(\alpha\), \(H\) rises to \(H^i_\alpha : K^i_\alpha \longrightarrow L^i_{\beta(\alpha)}\) which, for \(\beta\) large enough, defines a homotopy to zero of \(f_\alpha : K_\alpha \longrightarrow L_{\beta(\alpha)}\).

5.5.6.3. Let \(A\) be a ring, \(d\) an interval bounded above by \(\mathbb{Z}\), \(P(A)\) the category of \(A\)-projective modules of finite type, \(P\ell(A)\) the category of \(A\)-flat modules. Let us denote by \(D^{\mathrm{Tor} \subset d}(A)\) the image of \(C^d(P\ell(A))\) in \(D(A)\) and by \(D^{\mathrm{parf} \subset d}(A)\) the image of \(C^{d,b}(P(A))\) in \(D(A)\). According to 5.5.1.2, we have \(P\ell(A) \simeq \operatorname{Ind} P(A)\). We also have \(K^{d,b}(P(A)) \simeq D^{\mathrm{parf} \subset d}(A)\). We therefore deduce from 5.5.6.2, and from the fact that \(H^i\) commutes at filtering inductive limits, that there exists a commutative diagram of functors

\[\xymatrix@C=3em@R=2.5em{ C^d(P\ell(A)) \ar[d] & \operatorname{Ind} C^{d,b}(P(A)) \ar[l]_-{\varinjlim} \ar[d] \\ K^d(P\ell(A)) \ar[r]^-{\ell'} \ar[d] & \operatorname{Ind} K^{d,b}(P(A)) \ar[d] \\ D^{\mathrm{Tor} \subset d}(A) \ar[r]_-{\ell''} & \operatorname{Ind} D^{\mathrm{parf} \subset d}(A) }\]
5.5.6.4. Let's prove 5.5.6.1. With the notations of 5.5.6.3, if \(d\) is an interval \([0,k]\), we have (because \(F\) commutes to \(\varinjlim\)) commutative diagrams

\[\xymatrix@C=3em@R=2.5em{ K^d(P\ell(A)) \ar[r]^-{\ell'} \ar[d]_{K^{\geq 0}(F)} & \operatorname{Ind} K^d(P(A)) \ar[d]^{\operatorname{Ind}(K^{\geq 0}(F))} \\ K^+(\mathcal{B}) \ar[r] \ar[d]_{H^i} & \operatorname{Ind} K^+(\mathcal{B}) \ar[d]^{\operatorname{Ind}(H^i)} \\ \mathcal{B} & \operatorname{Ind} \mathcal{B} \ar[l]^-{\varinjlim} }\]
If \(u\) is as in 5.5.6.1, then, according to 5.5.6.3, \(\ell'(u)\) is an isomorphism, and \(H^i K^{\geq 0}(F)(u)\) is therefore an isomorphism, which was what had to be proven.

5.5.7. Let \(X\) be a schema over a schema \(S\) and \(k\) be an integer. It is assumed that the symmetric power \(\operatorname{Sym}^k_S(X)\) is defined. The reader who would like not to make this hypothesis can place himself in the category of algebraic spaces (ARTIN [1]). We ring \(S\) by a bundle of commutative rings \(\mathcal{A}\). From 5.5.19, \(\mathcal{A}\) will be assumed torsion. We will also designate by \(\mathcal{A}\), by abuse of notations, the reciprocal image of \(\mathcal{A}\) on any \(S\)-schema.

Let \(\underline{F}\) be a bundle of modules on \(X\); the sheaf \(\boxtimes^k \underline{F}\), external tensor product of \(k\) copies of \(\underline{F}\), is a sheaf on \(X^k/S\), equivariant by the action of the symmetric group \(\mathfrak{S}_k\) on \(X^k/S\). By passing to invariants, it defines a sheaf \(\mathrm{TS}^k_{\mathrm{ext}}(\underline{F})\) on \(\operatorname{Sym}^k_S(X)\), called external symmetric tensor power of \(\underline{F}\).
To reason "point by point", it is useful to work with the points of \(\operatorname{Sym}^k_S(X)\) described as follows.

5.5.7.1. Let \(s\) be a geometric point of \(S\). A point \(x\) of \(\operatorname{Sym}^k_S(X)\) with values ​​in \(k(s)\) defines a geometric point of \(\operatorname{Sym}^k_S(X)\). We will designate by \((x_1 \ldots x_k)\) a point of \((X/S)^k\) with values in \(k(s)\) of image \(x\), by \(y_1 \ldots y_\ell\) the distinct elements of the sequence \(x_1 \ldots x_k\) and by \(n_i\) (\(1 \leq i \leq \ell\)) the multiplicity of \(y_i\) in this sequence (\(\sum n_i = k\)).
With the notations of 5.5.7.1, and designating by \(\mathfrak{S}\) a symmetric group, we have
\[(5.5.7.2) \qquad \mathrm{TS}^k_{\mathrm{ext}}(F)_x \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \Bigl( \bigotimes_1^\ell \bigl( \otimes_1^{n_i} F_{y_i} \bigr) \Bigr)^{\prod_1^\ell \mathfrak{S}_{n_i}} \, ,\]
the tensor products being taken on \(\mathcal{A}_S\). In particular, for \(F\) flat, we have, thanks to 5.5.2.5,
\[(5.5.7.3) \qquad \mathrm{TS}^k_{\mathrm{ext}}(F)_x \xleftarrow{\;\sim\;} \bigotimes_1^\ell \Gamma^{n_i}(F_{y_i}) \, ,\]

using an argument by induction on \(\ell\), using the platitude of \(\Gamma^{n_i}(F_{y_i})\) (5.5.2.4) and the following fact: if \(G\) (resp. \(H\)) is a finite group operating on a \(A\)-module \(M\) (resp. \(N\)), and if \(M\) and \(N^H\) are flat, then
\[M^G \otimes_A N^H \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} (M \otimes_A N)^{G \times H} \, .\]
5.5.8. Let \(F\) be a Module on \(X\), and \(P\) be a flat presentation of \(F\)
\[P : P_1 \longrightarrow P_0 \longrightarrow F \longrightarrow 0 \, .\]

We define \(P'_1\), \(j_0\) and \(j_1\) as in 5.5.2.5, and we define \(\Gamma^k_{\mathrm{ext}}(F)\) (functor \(\Gamma\) external) as the cokernel of the double arrow \((\mathrm{TS}^k_{\mathrm{ext}}(j_0), \mathrm{TS}^k_{\mathrm{ext}}(j_1))\). With the notations of 5.5.7.1, we deduce isomorphisms from 5.5.7.3 and 5.5.2.5
\[(5.5.8.1) \qquad \Gamma^k_{\mathrm{ext}}(F)_x \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \bigotimes_1^\ell \Gamma^{n_i}(F_{y_i}) \, .\]
From this formula, we deduce that \(\Gamma^k_{\mathrm{ext}}(F)\), which a priori depends functionally on \(F\), only depends, up to a single isomorphism, on \(F\).
5.5.9. We have a functorial morphism
\[\alpha : \Gamma^k_{\mathrm{ext}}(F) \longrightarrow \mathrm{TS}^k_{\mathrm{ext}}(F) \, .\]
The pair \((\Gamma, \alpha)\) is characterized by the two properties:
(i) For flat \(F\), \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism
(ii) With the notations of 5.5.2.5, for any presentation
\[P_1 \xrightarrow{\;d\;} P_0 \xrightarrow{\;\varepsilon\;} F \longrightarrow 0\]
from \(F\), the sequel
\[\Gamma^k_{\mathrm{ext}}(P'_1) \rightrightarrows \Gamma^k_{\mathrm{ext}}(P_0) \longrightarrow \Gamma^k_{\mathrm{ext}}(F) \longrightarrow 0\]
is correct.
We also have:
(iii) The functors \(\mathrm{TS}^k\) and \(\Gamma^k\) commute at the filtering inductive limits (from 5.5.7.2, 5.5.8.1, 5.5.2.4).
(iv) The functor \(\Gamma^k\) commutes at any extension \(\mathcal{A} \longrightarrow \mathcal{A}'\) of the base ring.
(v) The functor \(\Gamma^k\) commutes at any change of base \(S' \longrightarrow S\): for any Cartesian square
\[\xymatrix@C=2.4em@R=2em{ X' \ar[r]^{g'} \ar[d]_{f'} & X \ar[d]^{f} \\ S' \ar[r]_{g} & S } \, ,\]
we have
\[\operatorname{Sym}^k_S(g')^* \Gamma^k_{\mathrm{ext}} \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \Gamma^k_{\mathrm{ext}} g'^* \, .\]
(vi) The functor \(\Gamma^k\) commutes to inverse images by any radical morphism \(i : X' \longrightarrow X\) (for example a closed immersion): We have
\[\operatorname{Sym}^k_S(i)^* \Gamma^k_{\mathrm{ext}} \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \Gamma^k_{\mathrm{ext}} i^*\]
(vii) For \(j : X' \longrightarrow X\) an immersion, we have
\[\operatorname{Sym}^k_S(j)_! \Gamma^k_{\mathrm{ext}} \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \Gamma^k_{\mathrm{ext}} j_!\]
5.5.10. Let \(k_1\), \(k_2\) and \(k = k_1 + k_2\) be three integers \(\geq 0\). We then have a finite morphism ( ):
\[\vee_{k_1 k_2} : \operatorname{Sym}^{k_1}_S(X) \times_S \operatorname{Sym}^{k_2}_S(X) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Sym}^k_S(X) \, .\]
If \(F_i\) (\(i = 1,2\)) is a Module on \(\operatorname{Sym}^{k_i}_S(X)\), we denote by \(F_1 \vee F_2\) the beam on \(\operatorname{Sym}^k_S(X)\)
5.5.11. Or an exact sequence of Modules on \(X\)
\[\Sigma : 0 \longrightarrow F' \longrightarrow F \longrightarrow F'' \longrightarrow 0 \, .\]
With the 5.5.7.1 notations, we have, thanks to 5.5.8.1 and 5.5.2.6, filtration on \(\Gamma^k_{\mathrm{ext}}(F)_x\), and morphisms:

\[\bigotimes_{\substack{1 \leq \alpha \leq \ell \\ \sum i_\alpha = i}} \Gamma^{i_\alpha}(F'_{y_\alpha}) \otimes \bigotimes_{1 \leq \alpha \leq \ell} \Gamma^{n_\alpha - i_\alpha}(F''_{y_\alpha}) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Gr}^i(\Gamma^k_{\mathrm{ext}}(F)_x) \, .\]

The first member is none other than \((\Gamma^i_{\mathrm{ext}}(F') \vee \Gamma^{k-i}_{\mathrm{ext}}(F''))_x\). We easily verify that these data, constructed point by point, come from a (unique) filtration in \(k+1\) steps of \(\Gamma^k_{\mathrm{ext}}(F)\), and from (unique) morphisms
\[(5.5.11.1) \qquad \Gamma^i_{\mathrm{ext}}(F') \vee \Gamma^{k-i}_{\mathrm{ext}}(F'') \longrightarrow \operatorname{Gr}^i(\Gamma^k_{\mathrm{ext}}(F)) \, .\]
If the exact sequence \(\Sigma\) is split, we have more precisely (point-by-point verification from 5.5.2.6.2).
\[(5.5.11.2) \qquad \Gamma^k_{\mathrm{ext}}(F' \oplus F'') \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \sum_{k_1+k_2=k} \Gamma^{k_1}_{\mathrm{ext}}(F') \vee \Gamma^{k_2}_{\mathrm{ext}}(F'') \, .\]
If the exact sequence \(\Sigma\) is splittable point by point, or if \(F''\) is flat, the morphisms (5.5.11.1) are isomorphisms (see 5.5.1.4).

5.5.12. Let \(u : X \longrightarrow Y\) be a morphism of \(S\)-schemas. We assume that the symmetric powers \(\operatorname{Sym}^k_S(X)\) and \(\operatorname{Sym}^k_S(Y)\) exist. Let \(F\) be a Module on \(X\), \(G\) be a Module on \(Y\) and \(v \in \operatorname{Hom}(u^*G, F) \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \operatorname{Hom}(G, u_* F)\) be a \(u\)-homomorphism of \(F\) in \(G\) (0.2). If \(\prod^n u\) is the Cartesian power \(n^{\text{ième}}\) of \(u\), the homomorphism \(v\) induces a \(\prod^n u\)-homomorphism \(\boxtimes^n v\) of \(\boxtimes^n F\) in \(\boxtimes^n G\).
By passing to the invariants, this defines
\[\mathrm{TS}^n(v) : \mathrm{TS}^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(F) \xrightarrow[\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(u)]{} \mathrm{TS}^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(G) \, .\]
We deduce from definition 5.5.8 that there exists one and only one \(\operatorname{Sym}^n(u)\)-homomorphism \(\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(v)\), functorial in \((F, G, v)\),
\[(5.5.12.0) \qquad \Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(v) : \Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(F) \xrightarrow[\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(u)]{} \Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(G)\]
making the following diagram commutative (see (0.2) for the (unusual) direction of the arrows):

\[\xymatrix@C=5em@R=4em{ \Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(F) \ar[r]^-{\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(v)}_-{\text{sur } \operatorname{Sym}^n_S(u)} \ar[d]_{\alpha \text{ sur Id}} & \Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(G) \ar[d]^{\alpha \text{ sur Id}} \\ \mathrm{TS}^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(F) \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{TS}^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(v)}_-{\text{sur } \operatorname{Sym}^n_S(u)} & \mathrm{TS}^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(G) }\]
We will use this arrow mainly as a "symmetric Künneth arrow": for \(G = u_*F\), and \(v\) the canonical arrow, the arrow \(\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(v)\) is identified with a homomorphism of Modules
\[(5.5.12.1) \qquad k^n : \Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(u_*F) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Sym}^n(u)_* \Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(F) \, .\]
5.5.13. Let the condition be:

(5.5.13.1). \(K \in \operatorname{Ob} K^{b,\geq 0}(X, \mathcal{A})\) and, for any geometric point \(x\) of \(X\), the complex \(K_x\) is homotopic to a complex of flat modules \(L_x \in \operatorname{Ob} C^{b,\geq 0}(\mathcal{A}_x)\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.5.13.2

The subcategory \(D^{b,\mathrm{tor} \leq 0}(X,\mathcal{A})\) of \(D^b(X,\mathcal{A})\) formed by Tor-dimension complexes \(\leq 0\) is equivalent to the category which is deduced from the category of complexes \(K \in \operatorname{Ob} K^{b,\geq 0}(X,\mathcal{A})\) verifying (5.5.13.1) by reversing the quasi-isomorphisms. This construction can be done either by calculating fractions on the left or on the right.

Let \(P\) be the subcategory of \(K^-(X,\mathcal{A})\) formed from point-by-point complexes homotopic to a complex of flat modules. We know (VERDIER [C.D.]) that \(D^-(X,\mathcal{A})\) is deduced from \(P\) by inverting the quasi-isomorphisms, by calculating fractions on the right or on the left. We then apply 5.5.4.1 to the inclusion of \(K^{b,\geq 0}(X,\mathcal{A}) \cap P\) in \(P\). Indeed, if \(K\) is of Tor-dimension \(\leq 0\), and point by point homotope to complexes of flat modules \(L_x\), then \(\tau_{\geq 0}(K)\) is point by point homotope to \(\tau_{\geq 0}(L_x)\), and these are still complexes of flat modules, so that \(\tau_{\geq 0}(K)\) checks 5.5.13.1.
5.5.14. According to 5.5.6.1, 5.5.7.2 or 5.5.8.1 and 5.5.2.4, any quasi-isomorphism \(v : K' \longrightarrow K\) between complexes satisfying (5.5.13.1) induces quasi-isomorphisms

\[\begin{aligned} \mathrm{TS}^k(v) &: \mathrm{TS}^k_{\mathrm{ext}}(K') \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \mathrm{TS}^k_{\mathrm{ext}}(K) \\ \Gamma^k(v) &: \Gamma^k_{\mathrm{ext}}(K') \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \Gamma^k_{\mathrm{ext}}(K) \end{aligned}\]

It follows that the functors \(\Gamma^k\) and \(\mathrm{TS}^k\) are differentiable (5.5.5) on the subcategory \(D^{b,\mathrm{tor} \leq 0}(X,\mathcal{A})\) of \(D^b(X,\mathcal{A})\), and that the complexes satisfying (5.5.13.1) are deployed on the left for these functors. According to 5.5.2.4, \(\Gamma^k\) and \(\mathrm{TS}^k\) induce functors

\[(5.5.14.1) \qquad L\mathrm{TS}^k : D^{b,\mathrm{tor} \leq 0}(X,\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow D^{b,\mathrm{tor} \leq 0}(\operatorname{Sym}^k_S(X), \mathcal{A})\] \[(5.5.14.2) \qquad L\Gamma^k : D^{b,\mathrm{tor} \leq 0}(X,\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow D^{b,\mathrm{tor} \leq 0}(\operatorname{Sym}^k_S(X), \mathcal{A}) \, .\]
In addition, the canonical morphism \(\alpha\) (5.5.9) induces according to 5.5.9 (i) an isomorphism between the functors 5.5.14.1 and 5.5.14.2
\[(5.5.14.3) \qquad \alpha : L\Gamma^k \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} L\mathrm{TS}^k \, .\]
5.5.14.4. The isomorphisms of 5.5.9 (iv) (v) (vi) (vii) move to the derived category and define isomorphisms:
(iv') For \(\mathcal{A}'\) a \(\mathcal{A}\)-algebra, we have

\[L\Gamma^k_{\mathrm{ext}}(K) \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes}_{\mathcal{A}} \mathcal{A}' \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} L\Gamma^k_{\mathrm{ext}}(K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes}_{\mathcal{A}} \mathcal{A}') \, .\]
(v') For any change of base \(g : S' \longrightarrow S\), we have
\[\operatorname{Sym}^k_S(g')^* L\Gamma^k_{\mathrm{ext}} K \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} L\Gamma^k_{\mathrm{ext}}(g'^*K) \, .\]
(vi') For any radical morphism \(i : X' \longrightarrow X\), we have
\[\operatorname{Sym}^k_S(i)^* L\Gamma^k_{\mathrm{ext}} K \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} L\Gamma^k_{\mathrm{ext}} i^*K \, .\]
(vii') For \(j : X' \longrightarrow X\) an immersion, we have:
\[\operatorname{Sym}^k_S(j)_! L\Gamma^k_{\mathrm{ext}} \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} L\Gamma^k_{\mathrm{ext}} j_! \, .\]
5.5.15. Consider an exact sequence of flat complexes of \(C^{\geq 0}(X,\mathcal{A})\),
\[0 \longrightarrow K' \longrightarrow K \longrightarrow K'' \longrightarrow 0 \, ,\]

and apply the functor \(\Gamma^k_{\mathrm{ext}}\) (5.5.3). We find (as in 5.5.11) that the semi-simplicial Module \(\Gamma^k_{\mathrm{ext}}(N^{-1}K)\) on \(\operatorname{Sym}^k_S(X)\) admits a filtration whose successive quotients are the Semi-simplicial Modules
\[\Gamma^{k'}_{\mathrm{ext}}(N^{-1}K') \vee \Gamma^{k''}_{\mathrm{ext}}(N^{-1}K'') \qquad (k'+k'' = k) \, .\]

Passing to the derived category we find that \(L\Gamma^k_{\mathrm{ext}}(K)\) is represented by a filtered complex whose successive quotients represent the objects \(L\Gamma^i_{\mathrm{ext}}(K') \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\vee} L\Gamma^{k-i}_{\mathrm{ext}}(K'')\) of the derived category (apply 5.5.2.4 and the EILENBERG-ZILBER-CARTIER theorem (isomorphism in \(\mathcal{K}\) of a double semi-simplicial object and its diagonal: DOLD PUPPE [1.2.9 pg.213]).
In the derived category, this construction can already be done from an exact sequence of complexes verifying 5.5.13.1.

∗ In more intrinsic terms, let \(\Delta\) be a "real triangle" of objects of \(D^{b,\mathrm{tor} \leq 0}(X,\mathcal{A})\), i.e. an object of the "filtered derived category" with \(\operatorname{Gr}^i(\Delta) = 0\) for \(i \neq 0,1\) and \(\operatorname{Gr}^i(\Delta) \in \operatorname{Ob} D^{b,\mathrm{tor} \leq 0}(X,\mathcal{A})\) (DELIGNE, in Appendix to VERDIER [2], or VI B n° 2.5.1, 2.5.2).
The previous construction then provides an object \(L\Gamma^k_{\mathrm{ext}}(\Delta)\) of the filtered derived category of \(\operatorname{Sym}^k_S(X)\), isomorphisms

\[\operatorname{Gr}^i(L\Gamma^k_{\mathrm{ext}}(\Delta)) \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} L\Gamma^i_{\mathrm{ext}}(\operatorname{Gr}^1(\Delta)) \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\vee} L\Gamma^{k-i}_{\mathrm{ext}}(\operatorname{Gr}^0(\Delta))\]
and, denoting by \(|\ \ |\) the complex underlying a filtered complex, an isomorphism
\[|\, L\Gamma^k_{\mathrm{ext}}(\Delta)\, | \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} L\Gamma^k_{\mathrm{ext}}(|\Delta|) \, . \qquad *\]

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.5.16

Let \(N\) be an integer. The category \(D^{b,\mathrm{tor} \leq 0}(X,\mathcal{A})\) is deduced from the category of complexes \(K \in \operatorname{Ob} K^{b,\geq 0}(X,\mathcal{A})\) verifying the conditions (i) and (ii) below by reversing the quasi-isomorphisms:

(i) Condition (5.5.13.1).

(ii) The complex \(K\) is deployed for any direct image functor of cohomological dimension \(\leq N\), relative to an isomorphism of topos \(f : \widetilde{X}_{\mathrm{ét}} \longrightarrow T\).

That there is a calculation of fractions on the right results from 5.5.13 and from the fact that, via the truncated canonical flaccid resolutions (4.2.9), any complex \(K\) satisfying (i) is sent into a complex satisfying (i) and (ii).

5.5.17. Consider a morphism of \(S\)-schemas \(u : X \longrightarrow Y\). We assume that \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(X)\) and \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(Y)\) exist, that there exists an integer \(N\) such that the functor \(u_*\) is of dimension \(\leq N\) on the category of \(\mathcal{A}\)-Modules, and that the functor \(Ru_*\) transforms bounded Tor-dimension complexes \(\leq 0\) into Tor-dimension complexes \(\leq 0\). These last two conditions are automatic if \(u\) is clean, \(\mathcal{A}\) is torsional and \(Y\) coherent (i.e. quasi-compact, quasi-separated) (5.2.8.1, 5.2.10).
Let \(K \in \operatorname{Ob} K^{b,\geq 0}(X, \mathcal{A})\) be a complex satisfying conditions (i) and (ii) of 5.5.16, with \(N\) as above. According to 5.5.14, the arrow
\[\beta : L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(K) \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(K)\]
is a quasi-isomorphism. Likewise, the arrow
\[\gamma : u_*K \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} Ru_*K\]
is a quasi-isomorphism.
We define the symmetric Künneth arrow of \(L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} Ru_*(K)\) in \(R\operatorname{Sym}^n(u)_* L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(K)\) as the compound

\[(5.5.17.1) \qquad \begin{aligned} k^n : L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} Ru_*K &\xleftarrow[\gamma]{\;\sim\;} L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} u_*K \longrightarrow \Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} u_*K \xrightarrow{(5.5.12.1)} \operatorname{Sym}^n_S(u)_* \Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(K) \\ &\longrightarrow R\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(u)_* \Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(K) \xleftarrow[\beta]{\;\sim\;} R\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(u)_* L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(K) \, . \end{aligned}\]
It follows from 5.5.16 that the morphisms (5.5.17.1) define a morphism between functors of \(D^{b,\mathrm{tor} \leq 0}(X, \mathcal{A})\) in \(D^+(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(Y), \mathcal{A})\)
\[(5.5.17.2) \qquad k^n : L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} Ru_* \longrightarrow R\operatorname{Sym}^n_S u_* L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} \, ,\]
also called symmetric Künneth morphism.
This morphism is characterized by property 5.5.18 below.

5.5.17.3. Let \(S\), \(X\), \(Y\) and \(u\) as above, \(K \in \operatorname{Ob} C^{b,\geq 0}(X, \mathcal{A})\) be a Tor-dimension complex \(\leq 0\), \(K_1 \in \operatorname{Ob} C^{b,\geq 0}(Y, \mathcal{A})\) and \(K_2 \in \operatorname{Ob} C^b(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(X), \mathcal{A})\). Let \(v\) be a \(u\)-homomorphism (0.2) from \(K\) into \(K_1\), and let \(t\) be a homomorphism (in the ordinary sense, like all those that follow) from \(\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(K)\) into \(K_2\). We have morphisms
a) \(\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(v) : \Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(K_1) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Sym}^n_S(u)_* \Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(K)\) (5.5.12.0)
b) \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(u)_*(t) : \operatorname{Sym}^n_S(u)_* \Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(K) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Sym}^n_S(u)_* K_2\),
of compound
\[\varphi' : \Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(K_1) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Sym}^n_S(u)_* K_2 \, ,\]
defining, by composition
\[\varphi : L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(K_1) \longrightarrow R\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(u)_* K_2 \, .\]
In addition, \(v\) and \(t\) induce:
\[\begin{cases} v' : K_1 \longrightarrow Ru_*K \\[4pt] t' : L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} K \longrightarrow K_2 \end{cases} \, .\]
We then have:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 5.5.18

Under assumptions 5.5.17.3, the following diagram is commutative in \(D^+(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(Y), \mathcal{A})\):

\[\xymatrix@C=5em@R=4em{ L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} Ru_*K \ar[r]^-{k^n}_-{\text{(Künneth symétrique)}} & R\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(u)_* L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} K \ar[d]^{R\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(u)_*(t')} \\ L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} K_1 \ar[u]^{L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(v')} \ar[r]_-{\varphi} & R\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(u)_* K_2 }\]
The demonstration is exactly the same as that of 5.4.1.5 and left to the reader.
5.5.19. We now assume \(\mathcal{A}\) torsion.
Let \(u : X \longrightarrow Y\) be a compactifiable morphism of \(S\)-schemas:
\[\xymatrix@C=2.5em@R=2em{ X \ar@{^{(}->}[r]^{j} \ar[dr] \ar@/_1.6em/[rr]_{u} & \bar{X} \ar[r]^{\bar{u}} & Y \ar[dl] \\ & S & }\]
It is assumed that \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(X)\), \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(\bar{X})\) and \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(Y)\) exist. For any Module \(F\) on \(X\), we then have
\[(5.5.19.1) \qquad \Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(j_!F) \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \operatorname{Sym}^n_S(j)_! \Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(F) \, ,\]
and this isomorphism passes trivially to derived categories.
We define the symmetric Künneth morphism between functors of \(D^{b,\mathrm{tor} \leq 0}(X, \mathcal{A})\) in \(D^{b,\mathrm{tor} \leq 0}(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(Y), \mathcal{A})\)
\[(5.5.19.2) \qquad k^n_u \text{ ou } k^n : L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} Ru_! \longrightarrow R\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(u)_! L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}\]
as being the compound

\[\begin{aligned} L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} Ru_! &\underset{\mathrm{dfn}}{=} L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} R\bar{u}_* j_! \xrightarrow{(5.5.17.2)} R\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(\bar{u})_* L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} j_! \\ &\xrightarrow[\sim]{(5.5.19.1)} R\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(\bar{u})_* \operatorname{Sym}^n_S(j)_! L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} \\ &\xrightarrow{\text{(5.5.14.4 (vii'))}} R\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(u)_! L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} \, . \end{aligned}\]
5.5.20. We leave it to the reader to make the following checks, easy in principle using 5.5.18.
5.5.20.1 (Intrinsic character). The morphism (5.5.19.2) is independent of the choice of compactification \(\bar{u}\) of \(u\).
5.5.20.2 (Transitivity). Consider a diagram of \(S\)-schemas
\[X \xrightarrow{\;u\;} Y \xrightarrow{\;v\;} Z \, ,\]

with \(u\) and \(v\) quasi-projective; we assume that \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(Z)\) exists (therefore also \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(Y)\) and \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(X)\)) (SGA 1 V 1.7, EGA II 4.5.4). The following functor diagram is then commutative

\[\xymatrix@C=1.6em@R=3.5em{ L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} R(vu)_! \ar[rr]^-{k^n_{vu}} \ar@{=}[d] & & R\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(vu)_! L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} \ar@{=}[d] \\ L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} Rv_! Ru_! \ar[r]^-{k^n_v} & R\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(v)_! L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} Ru_! \ar[r]^-{k^n_u} & R\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(v)_! R\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(u)_! L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} }\]

5.5.20.3. The symmetric Künneth morphism is compatible with base or space changes of the type 5.5.9 (iv) (v) (vi). More precisely, we have the following compatibilities with the morphisms 5.5.14.4 (iv') (v') (vi').
(iv") For any \(\mathcal{A}\)-algebra \(\mathcal{A}'\), and any \(S\)-quasi-projective morphism \(u : X \longrightarrow Y\) the diagram

\[\xymatrix@C=1.2em@R=3.5em{ L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} Ru_!(K) \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes}_{\mathcal{A}} \mathcal{A}' \ar[r]^-{\sim} \ar[d]_{k^n_u} & L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(Ru_!(K) \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes}_{\mathcal{A}} \mathcal{A}') \ar[r]^-{\sim} & L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} Ru_!(K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes}_{\mathcal{A}} \mathcal{A}') \ar[d]^{k^n_u} \\ R\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(u)_! L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(K) \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes}_{\mathcal{A}} \mathcal{A}' \ar[r]^-{\sim} & R\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(u)_! \bigl(L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(K) \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes}_{\mathcal{A}} \mathcal{A}'\bigr) \ar[r]^-{\sim} & R\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(u)_! L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes}_{\mathcal{A}} \mathcal{A}') }\]
is commutative.
(v") For any change of base \(g : S' \longrightarrow S\), giving rise to a Cartesian diagram
\[\xymatrix@C=1.8em@R=1.8em{ X' \ar[rr]^{g'} \ar[dr]_{u'} \ar[dd] & & X \ar[dr]^{u} \ar[dd] & \\ & Y' \ar[rr]^(0.35){g''} \ar[dl] & & Y \ar[dl] \\ S' \ar[rr]_{g} & & S & }\]
the functor diagram, just like (iv"), is commutative:

\[\xymatrix@C=1.2em@R=3.5em{ \operatorname{Sym}^n_S(g'')^* L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} Ru_! \ar[r]^-{\sim} \ar[d]_{k^n_u} & L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} g''^* Ru_! \ar[r]^-{\sim} & L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} Ru'_! g'^* \ar[d]^{k^n_{u'}} \\ \operatorname{Sym}^n_S(g'')^* R\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(u)_! L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} \ar[r]^-{\sim} & R\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(u')_! g'^* L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} \ar[r]^-{\sim} & R\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(u')_! L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} g'^* }\]
(vi") For any radical basis change \(g : Y' \longrightarrow Y\), giving rise to a Cartesian diagram of \(S\)-schemas
\[\xymatrix@C=2.4em@R=2em{ X' \ar[r]^{g'} \ar[d]_{u'} & X \ar[d]^{u} \\ Y' \ar[r]_{g} & Y } \, ,\]
the following functor diagram is commutative:

\[\xymatrix@C=1.2em@R=3.5em{ \operatorname{Sym}^n_S(g)^* L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} Ru_! \ar[r]^-{\sim} \ar[d]_{k^n_u} & L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} g^* Ru_! \ar[r]^-{\sim} & L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} Ru'_! g'^* \ar[d]^{k^n_{u'}} \\ \operatorname{Sym}^n_S(g)^* R\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(u)_! L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} \ar[r]^-{\sim} & R\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(u')_! g'^* L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} \ar[r]^-{\sim} & R\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(u')_! L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} g'^* } \, .\]

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.5.21

(Symmetric Künneth formula). Let \(S\) a coherent scheme (= quasi-compact quasi-separated) ringed by a bundle of commutative torsion rings \(\mathcal{A}\), with fibers of Noetherian rings. We anneal each \(S\)-schema by the reciprocal image of \(\mathcal{A}\). Let \(u : X \longrightarrow Y\) a morphism of \(S\)-quasi-projective schemes. Whatever the integer \(n \geq 0\) and \(K \in \operatorname{Ob} D^{b,\mathrm{tor} \leq 0}(X, \mathcal{A})\), the symmetric Künneth arrow (5.5.19.2)

\[k^n : L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} Ru_!K \longrightarrow R\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(u)_! L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} K\]

is an isomorphism in \(D^{b,\mathrm{tor} \leq 0}(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(Y), \mathcal{A})\).
The proof of this theorem will occupy the entire remainder of 5.5.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 5.5.21.1

The assumption of quasi-projectivity only serves to ensure that \(u\) admits compactification \(\bar{u}\)

\[X \xrightarrow{\;j\;} \bar{X} \xrightarrow{\;\bar{u}\;} Y\]

such that the schemas \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(X)\), \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(\bar{X})\) and \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(Y)\) exist for all \(n\). The language of algebraic spaces should make it possible to replace this hypothesis by the sole hypothesis that \(u\) is compactifiable.
The first part of the demonstration consists of reducing 5.5.21 to an "irreducible" case by unscrewing:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.5.22

Theorem 5.5.21 is implied by the following special case:

(*) For any algebraically closed field \(k\), any projective and smooth curve \(X\) on \(k\) and any prime number \(\ell\), symmetric Künneth arrows (obtained for \(Y = S = \operatorname{Spec}(k)\), \(\mathcal{A} = \mathbb{Z}/\ell\), \(K = \mathbb{Z}/\ell\); see explanations below)

\[(5.5.22.1) \qquad L\Gamma^n(R\Gamma(X, \mathbb{Z}/\ell)) \longrightarrow R\Gamma(\operatorname{Sym}^n_k(X), \mathbb{Z}/\ell)\]

are isomorphisms (\(n \geq 0\)).

In 5.5.22.1, the symbol \(\Gamma\) has an unfortunate double use: in the expression \(R\Gamma\), it designates the "global sections" functor; in the expression \(L\Gamma^n\), it designates the functor 5.5.2.3. In this formula we use the isomorphism \(\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(\mathbb{Z}/\ell) \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \mathbb{Z}/\ell\).
Given \(S\), \(X\), \(Y\), \(u\) and \(\mathcal{A}\) as in 5.5.21.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.5.22.2

Let us be an exact sequence of complexes in \(C^{b,\geq 0}(X, \mathcal{A})\), tor-dimension \(\leq 0\):

\[0 \longrightarrow K' \longrightarrow K \longrightarrow K'' \longrightarrow 0 \, .\]

If the Künneth arrows \(k^n\) (\(n \geq 0\)) relative to two of the complexes \(K'\), \(K\) and \(K''\) are all isomorphisms, then the arrows of Künneth relating to the third are also isomorphisms.

We return to the case where \(u\) is projective, and where the complexes \(K'\), \(K\), \(K''\) satisfy the conditions (i) and (ii) of 5.5.16, for \(N\) at least equal to twice the dimension of the arrows of \(u\). The symmetrical Künneth arrows are then given by 5.5.17.1.

Let us show by induction on \(n\) that if for \(i \leq n\) and two of the complexes \(K'\), \(K\), \(K''\), the arrows \(k^i\) are isomorphisms, then the arrows \(k^i\) (\(i \leq n\)) are isomorphisms for the third.
To see this, we note that thanks to 5.5.14, the morphism
\[(a) \qquad k^n : L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} Ru_*K \longrightarrow R\operatorname{Sym}^n(u)_* L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} K\]
can be represented by a morphism of filtered complexes
\[k'^n : K_0 \longrightarrow K_1\]
of finite filtration, this morphism inducing on the successive quotients of isomorphic arrows to compound arrows

\[(b_i) \qquad L\Gamma^i_{\mathrm{ext}} Ru_*K' \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\vee} L\Gamma^{n-i}_{\mathrm{ext}} Ru_*K'' \longrightarrow R\operatorname{Sym}^i(u)_*(L\Gamma^i_{\mathrm{ext}} K' \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\vee} L\Gamma^{n-i}_{\mathrm{ext}} K'')\]
following:

\[\begin{aligned} L\Gamma^i_{\mathrm{ext}} Ru_*K' \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\vee} L\Gamma^{n-i}_{\mathrm{ext}} Ru_*K'' &\xrightarrow{k^i_v\, k^{n-i}} R\operatorname{Sym}^i(u)_* L\Gamma^i_{\mathrm{ext}} K' \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\vee} R\operatorname{Sym}^{n-i}(u)_* L\Gamma^{n-i}_{\mathrm{ext}}(K'') \\ &\underset{\mathrm{dfn}}{=} \vee_{i,n-i\, *}\bigl(R\operatorname{Sym}^i(u)_* \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\boxtimes} R\operatorname{Sym}^{n-i}(u)_*\bigr)(L\Gamma^i_{\mathrm{ext}} K' \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\boxtimes} L\Gamma^{n-i}_{\mathrm{ext}} K'') \\ &\xrightarrow[\sim]{5.4.3} \vee_{i,n-i\, *}\bigl(R\operatorname{Sym}^i(u)_* \times R\operatorname{Sym}^{n-i}(u)_*\bigr)(L\Gamma^i_{\mathrm{ext}} K' \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\boxtimes} L\Gamma^{n-i}_{\mathrm{ext}} K'') \\ &= R\operatorname{Sym}^n(u)_* \vee_{i,n-i\, *}(L\Gamma^i_{\mathrm{ext}} K' \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\boxtimes} L\Gamma^{n-i}_{\mathrm{ext}} K'') \underset{\mathrm{dfn}}{=} R\operatorname{Sym}^n(u)_*(L\Gamma^i_{\mathrm{ext}} K' \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\vee} L\Gamma^{n-i}_{\mathrm{ext}} K'') \end{aligned}\]

According to the recurrence hypothesis (and Künneth's formula), these arrows are quasi-isomorphisms for \(i \neq 0,n\). Any two of the following assertions therefore imply the third: \((a)\) is a quasi-isomorphism; \((b_0)\) is a quasi-isomorphism; \((b_n)\) is a quasi-isomorphism. These are the three arrows that were in question, cqfd.

5.5.22.3. Let the assertion (*) considered in 5.5.22 be true, and prove 5.5.21. Let us therefore be \(S\), \(X\), \(Y\), \(\mathcal{A}\), \(K\) and \(u\) as in 5.5.21. The problem is local on \(S\), which can be assumed to be affine. Any complex \(K\) is a successive extension of complexes of the form \(i_! i^*K\) for \(i\) inclusion in \(X\) of a locally closed affine part. By unscrewing (5.5.22.2), we can therefore assume \(K\) of the form \(i_! K_1\), for an immersion \(i : X_1 \longrightarrow X\) of affine source. Compatibility 5.5.20.2 allows replacing \(X\) by \(X_1\), \(K\) by \(K_1\) and therefore assuming \(u\) affines. Let us factor \(u\) as a composite \(u = u_1 \ldots u_k\) of fiber morphisms of dimension \(\leq 1\). Compatibility 5.5.20.2 shows that it is enough to prove that the arrows \(k^n\) relating to \(u_i\) and to any complex \(K\) are isomorphisms. We are thus brought back to the case of \(S\) affine, \(u\) of relative dimension \(\leq 1\).

5.5.22.4. For \(k^n\) to be an isomorphism, it is enough that its restrictions on the geometric fibers of \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(Y)\) are; compatibility 5.5.20.3 (v") therefore brings us back to assuming \(S\) spectrum of an algebraically closed field \(k\).

5.5.22.5. For every rational point \(y\) of \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_k(Y)\), there exists a finite reduced part \(Y'\) of \(Y\) such that \(y\) is in the image of \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_k(Y')\). Compatibility 5.5.20.3 (vi") therefore allows us to assume that \(Y\) is the sum of a finite number of copies of \(\operatorname{Spec}(k)\).

5.5.22.6. If \(Y = \coprod Y_i\), then any complex \(K\) over \(X\) is the sum of complexes concentrated on one of the schemes \(X_i = u^{-1}(Y_i)\). By unscrewing (5.5.22.2) and according to the compatibility 5.5.20.3 (vi), it is enough to prove 5.5.21 for each of the morphisms \(u_i : X_i \longrightarrow Y_i\). By 5.5.22.5, this brings us back to the case
\[Y = S = \operatorname{Spec}(k) \, , \quad k \text{ algébriquement clos}, \quad \dim X \leq 1 \, .\]
The beam \(\mathcal{A}\) is now reduced to a Noetherian ring \(A\). The following proposition will allow us to return to the case where \(A\) is a body.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 5.5.23

Let \(A\) a Noetherian ring (commutative), \(K \in \operatorname{Ob} D^-(A)\) an upper bounded complex of \(A\)-modules and \(n\) an integer. The following conditions are equivalent

(i) \(K\) is from Tor-dimension \(\leq n\)

(ii) For all \(x \in \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and all \(i < -n\), \(H^i(K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes}_A k(x)) = 0\).

We have (i) \(\Longrightarrow\) (ii) trivially, Suppose (ii). To prove (i), we will prove by Noetherian induction on the closed \(F\) of \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) that if a module \(N\) satisfies \(N_x = 0\) for \(x \notin F\), then \(H^i(K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes}_A N) = 0\) for \(i < -n\).

Let \(I\) be the unique ideal which defines \(F\) such that \(A/I\) is reduced, and let \(N_k\) be the submodule of \(N\) canceled by \(I^k\). We have \(N = \varinjlim N_k\). Since the Tor functors commute at filtering inductive limits, we return to the case where there exists \(k\) such that \(N = N_k\), so that \(N\) is a successive extension of modules killed by \(I\), and the long exact cohomology sequence brings us back to the case where \(N\) is already a \(A/I\)-module.
Let \(\eta\) be a maximum point of \(F\), and define \(A/I\)-modules \(M_1\) and \(M_2\) in the exact sequence
\[(5.5.23.1) \qquad 0 \longrightarrow M_1 \longrightarrow N \xrightarrow{\;i\;} N \otimes k(\eta) \longrightarrow M_2 \longrightarrow 0 \, .\]

We have \((M_j)_\eta = 0\) (\(j = 1,2\)). If for each closed \(G \subset F\) with \(\eta \notin G\) we designate by \(M_{j,G}\) the submodule of \(M_j\) formed of sections of \(M_j\) with support in \(G\), we have
\[M_j = \varinjlim_{\eta \notin G} M_{j,G} \, .\]
According to the recurrence hypothesis and a passage to the limit, we therefore have
\[H^i(K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes}_A M_j) = 0 \quad \text{pour } i < -n \, .\]
The module \(N \otimes k(\eta)\) is a vector over \(k(\eta)\), therefore a sum of copies of \(k(\eta)\). By hypothesis, we have
\[H^i(K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes}_A (N \otimes k(\eta))) = 0 \quad \text{pour } i < -n \, .\]
If \(I\) is the image of the arrow \(i\) of (5.5.23.1), we have exact sequences

\[\begin{aligned} H^{i-1}(K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} M_2) &\longrightarrow H^i(K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} I) \longrightarrow H^i(K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} (N \otimes k(\eta))) \\ H^i(K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} M_1) &\longrightarrow H^i(K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} N) \longrightarrow H^i(K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} I) \, , \end{aligned}\]
from which we derive that \(H^i(K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes} N) = 0\) for \(i < -n\), cqfd.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.5.24

For an arrow \(u : K \longrightarrow L\) in \(D^-(A)\) to be an isomorphism, it is sufficient that for all \(x \in \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), the arrow

\[u_x : K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes}_A k(x) \longrightarrow L \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes}_A k(x)\]

be an isomorphism in \(D^-(k(x))\).

Let \(M\) be the cone (mapping cylinder) of \(u\). By hypothesis, we have \(M \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes}_A k(x) = 0\) for all \(x \in \operatorname{Spec}(A)\). According to 5.5.23, we therefore have \(M = 0\), and \(u\) is an isomorphism.

5.5.25.1. Let's continue the demonstration of 5.5.22, interrupted in 5.5.23. According to compatibility 5.5.20.3 (iv") and 5.5.24, the morphism \(k^n\) relative to \(K \in \operatorname{Ob} D^{b,\mathrm{tor} \leq 0}(X,A)\) is an isomorphism if and only if the morphisms \(k^n\) relative to \(K \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes}_A k(x) \in \operatorname{Ob} D^{b,\mathrm{tor} \leq 0}(X, k(x))\) are isomorphisms, for \(x \in \operatorname{Spec}(A)\). This brings us back to the case where \(A\) is a feature body \(\ell > 0\).
5.5.25.2. The exact sequence (where \(C\) denotes a cone)
\[0 \longrightarrow K[-1] \longrightarrow C K[-1] \longrightarrow K \longrightarrow 0\]
shows that the arrows \(k^n\) are isomorphisms for \(K\) if and only if they are for \(K[-1]\) (5.5.22.2).
5.5.25.3. Exact sequences
\[0 \longrightarrow \sigma_{\geq n} K \longrightarrow K \longrightarrow \sigma_{\leq n} K \longrightarrow 0\]
show that for the Künneth arrows to be an isomorphism, for \(K\) flat complex, it is enough that they are for the components of \(K\), placed in degree 0 (5.5.22.2) and (5.5.25.2).

5.5.25.4. Let \(F\) be a beam of \(A\)-vectors. This beam is the inductive limit of constructible beams (IX 2.7.2), automatically flat since \(A\) is a body, and by passing to the limit, we return to the case \(K\) constructible, reduced to degree zero.

5.5.25.5. If a complex \(K\) is a direct factor of a complex \(L\), the morphism \(k^n\) relative to \(K\) is a direct factor of the morphism \(k^n\) relative to \(L\), therefore a isomorphism if the latter is one. The trace method (IX 5.8) therefore applies and it is enough to treat the case where \(F\) is of the form \(j_! A_{X'}\), for \(j : X' \longrightarrow X\) almost finished. Compatibility 5.5.20.2 allows replacing \(X\) with \(X'\), hence the reduction
\[X \text{ est de dimension} \leq 1 \text{ et } K = A_X \, .\]
5.5.25.6. We can assume \(X\) reduced. If \(j : U \longrightarrow X\) is the smoothness open of \(X\), of complement \(i : F \longrightarrow X\), the exact sequence
\[0 \longrightarrow j_! j^* A_X \longrightarrow A_X \longrightarrow i_! i^*A_X \longrightarrow 0\]
brings us back by unscrewing to cases where \(X\) is either a smooth curve or a finite set. Additionally, the formula
\[A_X = A \overset{\mathrm{L}}{\otimes}_{\mathbb{Z}/\ell} (\mathbb{Z}/\ell)_X\]
bring back to 5.5.20.3 (iv") in case \(A_X = \mathbb{Z}/\ell\).
If \(X\) is a curve, we plunge \(X\) into the corresponding projective and smooth curve, and a new unscrewing leads to one of the following two cases
(i) \(X\) is a smooth projective curve, \(Y = S = \operatorname{Spec}(k)\) with \(k\) algebraically closed, \(\mathcal{A} = \mathbb{Z}/\ell\), \(K = \mathbb{Z}/\ell\).

(ii) \(X\) is a sum of a finite number of copies of \(\operatorname{Spec}(k)\), \(Y = S = \operatorname{Spec}(k)\) (\(k\) algebraically closed), \(\mathcal{A} = \mathbb{Z}/\ell\), \(K = \mathbb{Z}/\ell\).

A final unscrewing of \((\mathbb{Z}/\ell)_X\) into the bundles \((\mathbb{Z}/\ell)_{X'}\), for \(X'\) connected component of \(X\), brings back into (i) and (ii) assuming \(X\) connected. Case (ii) is then trivial. This proves 5.5.22.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.5.26

Theorem 5.5.21 is a consequence of the following two special cases of the assertion (*) of 5.5.22:

Case 1. It is assumed that \(k = \mathbb{C}\).

Case 2. We assume that \(k\) is of characteristic \(p > 0\) and that \(\ell = p\).

With the notations of 5.5.22, let us denote by \(p\) the characteristic of \(k\). If \(p = 0\), the Lefschetz principle (based here on compatibility 5.5.20.3 (v"), and on XII 5.4) allows us to return to the case where \(k = \mathbb{C}\).

It remains to deal with the case where \(p > 0\) and where \(\ell \neq p\). The curve \(X\) rises into a curve \(X'\) on the ring \(W(k)\) of Witt vectors on \(k\) (SGA 1 III 7.4). Let us set \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(W(k))\), i.e. the diagram
\[\xymatrix@C=2.4em@R=2em{ X'_\eta \ar[r] \ar[d]_{f_\eta} & X' \ar[d]^{f'} & X \ar[l] \ar[d]^{f} \\ \eta \ar[r] & S & \operatorname{Spec}(k) \ar[l] } \, ,\]

and let \(f_n\), \(f'_n\) and \(f_{\eta,n}\) be the respective projections of \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_k(X)\), \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(X')\) and \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_\eta(X'_\eta)\) on \(\operatorname{Spec}(k)\), \(S\) and \(\eta\). We know that \(f'_n\) is a projective and smooth morphism (the projectivity is obvious; for smoothness, the simplest thing is to reduce it to the case where \(X' = \mathbb{P}^1_S\), in which case \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(X') \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \mathbb{P}^n\) as it results from the theorem of elementary symmetric functions). According to the specialization theorem XVI 2.2, the sheaves \(R^i f_{n*}(\mathbb{Z}/\ell)\) are locally constant on \(S\). It is the same for beams \(L^i \Gamma^n(Rf'_{n!} \mathbb{Z}/\ell)\). To verify that the arrow of (5.5.22.1) relative to \(X\) on \(k\) is an isomorphism, it is therefore sufficient according to 5.5.20.3 (v") to prove the analogous statement for the generic geometric fiber \(X_{\bar{\eta}}\) of \(X\) on \(S\). The body \(k(\bar{\eta})\) has characteristic 0, and, given the first reduction, this proves 5.5.26.

To prove 5.5.21 in these two particular cases 5.5.26, we will compare the symmetric Künneth morphism on the one hand to its transcendent analogue (5.5.27 and 5.5.28) and on the other hand to its coherent analogue (5.5.29 to 5.5.37).

5.5.27. If \(p : X \longrightarrow S\) is a continuous map between topological spaces, with \(S\) ringed by a sheaf of commutative rings \(\mathcal{A}\), constructions 5.5.7, 5.5.8 apply as they are to define a functor \(\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}\) of the modules on \(X\) in modules on \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(X) = X^n/\mathfrak{S}_n\). Sorites 5.5.7 to 5.5.18 are transposed. Similarly, if \(S\), \(X\) and \(Y\) are separate locally compact topological spaces
\[\xymatrix@C=2em@R=2em{ X \ar[rr]^{u} \ar[dr] & & Y \ar[dl] \\ & S & } \, ,\]
and if the fibers of \(u\) are of bounded cohomological dimension, the sorite 5.5.19, 5.5.20 of the symmetric Künneth morphism in cohomology with proper support is transposed.

If \(p : X \longrightarrow S\) is a finite type schema morphism on \(\mathbb{C}\), the functors \(\Gamma^n\) and \(L\Gamma^n\) commute to the "passage to transcendent" functors. Finally, if \(X\), \(Y\), \(S\), \(\mathcal{A}\), \(K\) are like in 5.5.21, with \(S\) separated from finite type on \(\mathbb{C}\), and if we denote generically by \(\varepsilon\) site morphisms of type \(\varepsilon : X_{\mathrm{cl}} \longrightarrow X_{\mathrm{ét}}\), the following diagram is commutative:

\[(5.5.27.1) \qquad \xymatrix@C=4em@R=3.5em{ \varepsilon^* L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} Ru_! K \ar[r]^-{k^n} \ar[d] & \varepsilon^* R\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(u)_! L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} K \ar[d] \\ L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} R u^{\mathrm{an}}_! \varepsilon^*K \ar[r]_-{k^{u^{\mathrm{an}}}} & R\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(u^{\mathrm{an}})_! L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} \varepsilon^* K }\]

5.5.28. According to compatibility 5.5.27.1, and an elementary case of the comparison theorem (XI 4.4 or XVI 4.1), to treat case 1 of 5.5.26, it suffices to verify that if \(X\) is a complex non-singular projective curve, then the topological analogue of 5.5.22.1 is an isomorphism.

The topological space \(X^{\mathrm{an}}\) is triangulable (this fact is trivial here, because \(X\) is a ramified covering of the Riemann sphere). The topological analog of the unscrewing lemma 5.5.22.2 then brings us back to prove
(*) If \(D\) is a closed simplex (of dimension 0, 1 or 2), the topological symmetric Künneth arrow
\[(5.5.28.1) \qquad k^n : L\Gamma^n R\Gamma(D, \mathbb{Z}/\ell) \longrightarrow R\Gamma(\operatorname{Sym}^n(D), \mathbb{Z}/\ell)\]
is an isomorphism.
We have
