\[R\Gamma(D, \mathbb{Z}/\ell) = \mathbb{Z}/\ell \text{ placé en degré 0} \, ,\]
and therefore
\[L\Gamma^n R\Gamma(D, \mathbb{Z}/\ell) = \mathbb{Z}/\ell \text{ placé en degré 0} \, .\]
On the other hand, \(\operatorname{Sym}^n(D)\) is contractible, and we still have
\[R\Gamma(\operatorname{Sym}^n(D), \mathbb{Z}/\ell) = \mathbb{Z}/\ell \text{ placé en degré 0} \, .\]
The \(H^i\) of the two members of 5.5.28.1 are therefore zero, and the arrow induced on the \(H^0\) is identified with the identity of \(\mathbb{Z}/\ell\).
5.5.29. For the application we have in mind, it is interesting to look at a quasi-coherent beam on a diagram \(X\) as being a quasi-coherent beam of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules on the flat site of \(X\).
Soit \(p : X \longrightarrow S\) un morphisme quasi-projectif de schémas (cf. 5.5.21.1(∗)). If \(F\) is a bundle of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules on \(X_{\mathrm{ét}}\), then

a) We designate by \(\otimes^n F\) the bundle of modules on \((X/S)^n_{\mathrm{ét}}\) tensor product of \(\mathcal{O}_{(X/S)^n}\) \(n\)-modules reciprocal images of \(F\) on \((X/S)^n_{\mathrm{ét}}\);

b) the beam \(\otimes^n F\) is an \(\mathfrak{S}_n\)-equivariant beam. By passing to invariants, it defines a beam \(\mathrm{TS}^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(F)\) on \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(X)\): if \(\sigma\) is the projection of \((X/S)^n\) on \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(X)\), we have
\[(5.5.29.1) \qquad \mathrm{TS}^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(F) = (\sigma_* \otimes^n F)^{\mathfrak{S}_n} \, .\]

Suppose that \(p\) is flat; as in 5.5.8, we then define the functor \(\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}\) as being the \(0\)-th left derivative of the functor \(\mathrm{TS}^n_{\mathrm{ext}}\). With notations of 5.5.2.5 and 5.5.8, if
\[P_1 \longrightarrow P_0 \longrightarrow F \longrightarrow 0\]
is a flat presentation of \(F\), so the rest
\[\mathrm{TS}^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(P'_1) \overset{j_0}{\underset{j_1}{\rightrightarrows}} \mathrm{TS}^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(P_0) \longrightarrow \Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(F)\]
is correct. If \(x\) is a geometric point of \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(X)\) as in 5.5.7.1, we have (see 5.5.8.1)

\[(5.5.29.2) \qquad \Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(F)_x = \mathcal{O}_x \otimes_C \Bigl( \bigotimes_1^\ell \Gamma^{n_i}_{\mathcal{O}_S}(F_{y_i}) \Bigr) \, , \quad \text{avec} \quad C = \bigotimes_1^\ell \operatorname{Sym}^{n_i}_{\mathcal{O}_S}(\mathcal{O}_{y_i}) \, .\]

It is sufficient to verify this formula in the trivial case where the fibers of \(F\) are free. Note that \(\mathcal{O}_x\) is flat on \(C\), being the strict henselization of \(C\) at the closed point image of \(x\).

5.5.30. Assume \(X\) and \(S\) affines, \(p\) flat and \(F\) quasi-consistent. Let \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), \(X = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) and let \(F = H^0(X, F)\), so that \(F\) is the beam \(\widetilde{F}\) defined by the \(B\)-module \(F\). We then read on (5.5.29.1) that
\[\mathrm{TS}^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(F) = \widetilde{\mathrm{TS}^n_A(F)} \, ,\]
and, by derivation, we therefore have
\[(5.5.30.1) \qquad \Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(F) = \widetilde{\Gamma^n_A(F)} \, .\]
The functor \(\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}\) therefore appears as a functor \(\Gamma^n\) "relative to \(A\)", which (5.5.29.2) already expressed in local form.

5.5.31. We will designate by \(D^{b,\mathrm{tor}_S \leq 0}(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) the subcategory of \(D^b(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) formed of complexes of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules which, as complexes of \(p^{-1}(\mathcal{O}_S)\)-modules are of Tor-dimension \(\leq 0\).
Let be the condition

(5.5.31.1) \(K \in \operatorname{Ob} C^{b,\geq 0}(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) and, at each geometric point \(x\) of \(X\), \(K_x\) is homotopic to a complex of \(\mathcal{O}_{X,x}\)-modules, with positive degrees, flat on \(\mathcal{O}_{S,p(x)}\).

The morphism \(p\) being flat, so that there are "enough" flat \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules on \(S\), we check as in 5.5.13.2 that \(D^{b,\mathrm{tor}_S \leq 0}(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) is deduced from the category of complexes \(K \in \operatorname{Ob} K^{b,\geq 0}(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) verifying (5.5.31.1) by reversing the quasi-isomorphisms.

We read on (5.5.29.2) and 5.5.6.1 that if \(u : K \longrightarrow L\) is a quasi-isomorphism between complexes satisfying (5.5.31.1), then \(\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}(u)\) (in the sense of DOLD-PUPPE) is still a quasi-isomorphism. The functor \(\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}}\) is then derived in

\[(5.5.31.2) \qquad L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} : D^{b,\mathrm{tor}_S \leq 0}(X, \mathcal{O}_X) \longrightarrow D^{b,\mathrm{tor}_S \leq 0}(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(X), \mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(X)})\]

5.5.32. We will designate by \(D^{b,\mathrm{tor}_S \leq 0}_{\mathrm{qcoh}}(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) the subcategory of \(D^{b,\mathrm{tor}_S \leq 0}(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) formed by complexes with quasi-coherent cohomology.
Standard arguments allow, as in 5.5.17, to define a symmetrical Künneth arrow of the following type:

Let \(u : X \longrightarrow Y\) be a morphism of \(S\)-quasi-projective and flat schemes on \(S\); for \(K \in \operatorname{Ob} D^{b,\mathrm{tor}_S \leq 0}_{\mathrm{qcoh}}(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\), the symmetric Künneth arrow \(k^n\) in \(D^{b,\mathrm{tor}_S \leq 0}_{\mathrm{qcoh}}(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(Y), \mathcal{O})\) is an arrow
\[(5.5.32.1) \qquad k^n : L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} Ru_*K \longrightarrow R\operatorname{Sym}^n_S u_* L\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} K \, .\]
We leave it to the reader to verify the formal properties of 5.5.32.1 which will be used, in particular the following:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.5.33

If \(S\) is characteristic \(p > 0\), and if \(u : X \longrightarrow S\) is clean and flat, the diagram of symmetrical Künneth arrows 5.5.17.2 And 5.5.32.1 is commutative (\(Y = S\), \(K = \mathbb{Z}/p\) Or \(\mathcal{O}_X\)):

\[\xymatrix@C=4em@R=3.5em{ L\Gamma^n_{\mathbb{Z}/p} Ru_*(\mathbb{Z}/p) \ar[r]^-{k^n} \ar[d] & R\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(u)_*(\mathbb{Z}/p) \ar[d] \\ L\Gamma^n_{\mathcal{O}} Ru_*(\mathcal{O}) \ar[r]_-{k^n} & R\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(u)_*(\mathcal{O}) } \, .\]

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 5.5.34

Under the assumptions of 5.5.32, the symmetric Künneth arrow (5.5.32.1) is an isomorphism.
a) The question is local on \(S\), which can be assumed to be affine.

b) For any point \(x\) of \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(Y)\), there exists an open affine \(Y_1\) of \(Y\) such that \(x\) is in \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(Y_1)\). By localization, we can therefore assume \(Y\) affine.

c) Any complex \(K\) of the type considered can be represented by a complex \(K_0\) of quasi-coherent modules on \(S\), zero in degrees \(i < 0\). If \(\mathcal{U}\) is an affine covering of \(X\), then \(K\) admits a finite resolution by sums of complexes of the form \(j_* j^*K\) for \(j\) inclusion of an open affine (alternating Čech complex). Using the quasi-consistent analogue of the unscrewing lemma 5.5.22.2, this brings us back to the case where there exists an open affine \(j : X_1 \longrightarrow X\) of \(X\) and \(K_1 \in D^{b,\mathrm{tor}_S \leq 0}_{\mathrm{qcoh}}(X_1, \mathcal{O})\) such that \(K = Rj_* K_1\).

d) Given the quasi-consistent analog of transitivity 5.5.20.2, it is enough to prove 5.5.34 for \(uj\) and \(j\). Reduction b), applied to \(j\), finally brings us back to the case where \(X\), \(Y\) and \(S\) are affine. We can then represent \(K\) by a complex \(K_2 \in C^{b,\geq 0}(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) with flat and quasi-coherent components ( ). The functors \(Ru_*\) and \(L\Gamma^n\) are then calculated without having to solve \(K_2\), and it remains to be noted that, according to 5.5.30.1, for \(F\) quasi-consistent on \(X\), we have
\[\Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} u_* F \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \operatorname{Sym}^n_S(u)_* \Gamma^n_{\mathrm{ext}} F \, ,\]
both members identifying themselves as
\[\widetilde{\Gamma^n_A(F)} \quad \text{pour } F = \widetilde{F} \, ,\]
cqfd.
5.5.35. Let's deal with case 2 of 5.5.26. With the notations of 5.5.22, the following diagram is commutative (5.5.33):

\[(5.5.35.1) \qquad \xymatrix@C=4em@R=3.5em{ L\Gamma^n(R\Gamma(X, \mathbb{Z}/p)) \ar[r]^-{k^n}_-{\text{①}} \ar[d] & R\Gamma(\operatorname{Sym}^n_k(X), \mathbb{Z}/p) \ar[d] \\ L\Gamma^n(R\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O})) \ar[r]^-{k^n}_-{\text{②}} & R\Gamma(\operatorname{Sym}^n_k(X), \mathcal{O}) } \, ,\]

and according to 5.5.34, arrow ② is an isomorphism. The Frobenius endomorphism \(F : x \longrightarrow x^p\) of \(\mathcal{O}\) acts \(p\)-linearly on \(R\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O})\) and on \(R\Gamma(\operatorname{Sym}^n_k(X), \mathcal{O})\). It therefore also acts \(p\)-linearly on \(L\Gamma^n(R\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}))\). We check that \(k^n\) and \(F\) commute.
The Artin-Schreier theory provides us with exact sequences
\[0 \longrightarrow H^i(X, \mathbb{Z}/p) \longrightarrow H^i(X, \mathcal{O}) \xrightarrow{\;F-I\;} H^i(X, \mathcal{O}) \longrightarrow 0 \, ,\]
and the same for \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(X)\). To deduce that ① is an isomorphism from the fact that ② is one, we still have to check the

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.5.36

Let \(k\) a perfect field of characteristic \(p > 0\), \(K \in C^{\geq 0}(\mathbb{Z}/p)\) a complex of \(\mathbb{Z}/p\)-vectors of finite dimension, \(L \in C^{\geq 0}(k)\) a complex of \(k\)-finite-dimensional vectors, \(F\) an endomorphism \(p\)-linear of \(L\) and \(u : K \longrightarrow L\) a morphism. We assume that \(H^*(Fu) = H^*(u)\), and that the sequences

\[0 \longrightarrow H^i(K) \longrightarrow H^i(L) \xrightarrow{\;F-I\;} H^i(L) \longrightarrow 0\]

are correct. So, the sequels

\[0 \longrightarrow H^i(\Gamma^n K) \longrightarrow H^i(\Gamma^n L) \xrightarrow{\;F-I\;} H^i(\Gamma^n L) \longrightarrow 0\]

are accurate.
Since \(H^*(Fu) = H^*(u)\) and \(\mathbb{Z}/p\) is a body, there exists a homotopy
The body \(k\) being perfect, we can write
Replacing \(u\) with \(u - (dH_0 + H_0 d)\), we can assume that \(Fu = u\).

The functor \(S\) which for each \(k\)-vector \(V\) provided with an endomorphism \(p\)-linear \(F\) associates the \(\mathbb{Z}/p\)-vector \(V^S\) \(v \in V\) such that \(Fv = v\) is an exact functor. The hypothesis therefore means that morphism
\[K \longrightarrow L^S\]

is a quasi-isomorphism. It remains to prove that the functor \(S\) commutes to the functor \(\Gamma^n\). This point easily follows from the following well-known lemma, the proof of which is left to the reader:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.5.37

A finite-dimensional vector \(V\) on a perfect body \(k\), equipped with an endomorphism \(p\)-linear \(F\), admits one and only one decomposition

\[V = V_0 + V_n\]

with \(V^S \otimes k \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} V_0\), \(F(V_n) \subset V_n\) and \(F|_{V_n}\) nilpotent.
This completes the demonstration of 5.5.21.

footnotes.html: (∗) But not in that of Ensembles!
(∗) Everything that follows, including 5.5.34, is valid assuming only that \(X\) and \(Y\) admit large invertible sheaves relative to \(S\) (without assuming \(X\) and \(Y\) of finite type on \(S\)).

blocks.html: 6. The functor \(f_!\).

We propose to give a direct construction of the functor \(f_!\) of 5.1.11. We will then use this construction to define the "trace morphism" (6.2.3) for quasi-finite flat morphisms of finite presentation.
6.1. New construction of the functor \(f_!\).
Let \(f : X \longrightarrow S\) be a locally finite type morphism. A part \(P\) of \(X\) is said to be proper on \(S\) if it is the image of a subschema of \(X\) proper on \(S\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.1.1

Let \(f : X \longrightarrow S\) a locally finite and quasi-separate morphism.

(i) Part of \(X\) proper on \(S\) is locally closed, and is closed if \(f\) is separated.

(ii) If \(f\) is separated, the union of two parts of \(X\) proper on \(S\) is proper on \(S\).

(iii) For all \(S\)-schema \(U\), let us designate by \(\varphi_X(U)\) the set of parts of \(U \times_S X\) proper on \(U\). The functor \(\varphi_X\) is a sheaf for the fpqc topology. If \(f\) is separated, \(\varphi\) is a bundle of ordered sets filtering for the inclusion relation.

Assertions (i) and (ii) are trivial, and the second assertion of (iii) follows from (ii). The functor \(U \longmapsto \mathfrak{P}(U \times_S X)\) is a sheaf for any topology for which the overlaps are surjective. It follows from EGA IV 2.3.14 and EGA IV 2.7.1 (vii) that \(\varphi_X\) is a sub-bundle.

If \(\mathfrak{F}\) is a flat sheaf of sets pointed to a diagram \(X\), recall (IV 8.5.2) that the support of a section \(s\) of \(\mathfrak{F}\) is the complement of the largest open where \(s = 0\), \(0\) designating the marked section.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 6.1.2

Let \(f : X \longrightarrow S\) a locally finite and separated morphism, and \(\mathfrak{F}\) a sheaf of sets pointed to \(X\). We designate by \(f_! \mathfrak{F}\) the sub-beam of \(f_* \mathfrak{F}\) whose sections on \(U\) (spread over \(S\)) are the sections of \(\mathfrak{F}\) on \(U \times_S X\) on own support on \(U\).
It follows from 6.1.1 that \(f_! \mathfrak{F}\) is indeed a beam.
We easily verify that:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.1.3

If \(f\) is the compound \(g h\) of two locally separated morphisms of finite type, there exists one and only one isomorphism of functor \(f_! \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} g_! h_!\) making the diagram commutative
\[\xymatrix@C=2.4em@R=2.4em{ f_! \ar[r]^-{\sim} \ar[d] & g_! h_! \ar[d] \\ f_* \ar@{=}[r] & g_* h_* }\]

If \(f\) is an open immersion (resp. a proper morphism) the functor \(f_!\) is the zero extension functor (resp. the functor \(f_*\)). According to 6.1.3, definitions 6.1.2 and 5.1.11 therefore coincide in their common domain of validity.
The properties of the functor \(f_!\) verified in § 5 remain valid in the more general case 6.1.2.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.1.4

Let \(f : X \longrightarrow S\) a locally separated morphism of finite type.

(i) The functor \(f_!\) is left exact (i.e. commutes to finite projective limits) and commutes to filtering inductive limits.

(ii) If \(f\) is quasi-finite, the functor \(f_!\) is an exact functor of the category of abelian sheaves on \(X\) in that of abelian sheaves on \(S\).

(iii) For any Cartesian diagram

\[\xymatrix@C=2.4em@R=2em{ X' \ar[r]^{g'} \ar[d]_{f'} & X \ar[d]^{f} \\ S' \ar[r]_{g} & S }\]

there exists a base change isomorphism making the diagram commutative

\[\xymatrix@C=2.4em@R=2.4em{ g^*f_! \ar@{=}[r] \ar[d] & f'_! g'^* \ar[d] \\ g^*f_* \ar[r] & f'_* g'^* }\]

in which the second horizontal arrow is the base change arrow XII 4.1.
Proof. Whatever \(U \in \operatorname{Ob} S_{\mathrm{ét}}\), we have
\[f_! \mathfrak{F}(U) = \varinjlim_{P \in \varphi(U)} \Gamma_P(\mathfrak{F}) \, ,\]

and for quasi-separated quasi-compact \(U\), the functor \(\mathfrak{F} \longmapsto f_! \mathfrak{F}(U)\) commutes at finite projective limits and filtering inductive limits, as the filtering inductive limit (6.1.1) (iii) of functors having these properties.
This proves (i). Assertion (ii) immediately follows from (iii) (take as base changes the geometric points of \(S\)).

Assertion (iii) is local in nature on \(S\), which can be assumed to be affine. Let \(I\) be the filtering ordered set of open sets of \(X\) of finite type on \(S\). If \(U \xrightarrow{\;j\;} X\) is an open of \(X\), \((fj)_!(\mathfrak{F}|U) = f_!(j_!(\mathfrak{F}|U))\) is sent to \(f_!(\mathfrak{F})\). In addition
\[f_!(\mathfrak{F}) = \varinjlim_{U \in I} (fj)_!(\mathfrak{F}|U) \, .\]

This allows us to assume \(X\) of finite type on \(S\) and to apply Chow's lemma (EGA II 2nd ed.) to find a projective and surjective morphism: \(p^0 : X^0 \longrightarrow X\) such that \(f p^0\) is compactifiable. Let \(X^1 = X^0 \times_X X^0\) and \(p^1\) be the natural arrow of \(X^1\) in \(X\). The rest
\[0 \longrightarrow \mathfrak{F} \longrightarrow p^0_* p^{0*}\mathfrak{F} \rightrightarrows p^1_* p^{1*}\mathfrak{F}\]
is correct (VII 9.2).

Given the functionality of the base change arrow, and (i), it suffices to check (iii) for the beams \(p^0_* p^{0*}\mathfrak{F}\) and \(p^1_* p^{1*}\mathfrak{F}\). By changing notations (\(p^1 \longmapsto p^0\)) we are therefore reduced to assuming \(\mathfrak{F}\) of the form \(p^0_* \mathfrak{G}\). Assertion (iii) for the pair \((f, p^0_* \mathfrak{G})\) is equivalent to assertion (iii) for the pairs \((p^0, \mathfrak{G})\) and \((f p^0, \mathfrak{G})\). We conclude with 5.2.7 (i), applicable because \(p^0\) and \(fp^0\) are compactifiable.

blocks.title: Counterexample
blocks.html:
Counterexample 6.1.6

The functor \(Rf_!\) generally does not coincide with the functor derived from the functor \(f_!\).
If \(X\) is affine on an algebraically closed field \(k\), we indeed have
\[f_! \mathfrak{F} = \sum_{x \in X(k)} H^0_x(\mathfrak{F}) \, ,\]
and the \(i^{\text{ème}}\) functor derived from \(f_!\) is therefore worth \(\mathfrak{F}\)
\[\sum_{x \in X(k)} H^i_x(\mathfrak{F}) \, .\]
On the other hand, if \(X\) is smooth and irreducible, and of dimension 1,
\[H^2_c(X, \mu_{\ell^n}) \simeq \mathbb{Z}/\ell^n\]
for \(\ell\) first to the characteristic of \(k\).
6.2. The morphism traces in relative dimension 0.

The main result of this issue is Theorem 6.2.3. The reader is recommended not to read technical preliminaries 6.2.1, 6.2.2. These allow, in the proof of 6.2.3, to consider only finite \(f\) morphisms, a reduction which, via EGA IV 18.12, is in any case quite obvious.

6.2.1. Let \(f : X \longrightarrow S\) be a separate quasi-finite morphism. Whatever \(U \in \operatorname{Ob} S_{\mathrm{ét}}\), let us designate by \(\psi_f(U)\) the category of couples formed of a finite pointed set \(I\), of marked point denoted \(0\), and of a decomposition of \(f^*U\) into open and closed parts \(f^*U = \coprod_{i \in I} V_i\), with \(V_i\) ended up on \(U\) for \(i \neq 0\). A morphism from \(\varphi = (V_i)_{i \in I}\) into \(\varphi' = (V'_i)_{i \in I'}\) is a morphism \(\sigma\) from \(I'\) into \(I\) such that for \(i \in I\), we have \(V_i = \bigcup_{j \in \sigma^{-1}(i)} V'_j\). We say that \(\varphi'\) refines \(\varphi\) if there exists a morphism of \(\varphi\) into \(\varphi'\).

Let \(\mathfrak{F}\) be a sheaf of abelian groups on \(S\), \(U \in \operatorname{Ob} S_{\mathrm{ét}}\), \(\varphi = (V_i)_{i \in I} \in \operatorname{Ob} \psi_f(U)\), \(I^* = I \setminus \{0\}\), and \(f_i\) be the projection of \(V_i\) on \(U\). We have
\[f_! f^* \mathfrak{F}|U = f_{0!} f_0^*(\mathfrak{F}|U) \oplus \bigoplus_{i \neq 0} f_{i*} f_i^*(\mathfrak{F}|U) \, ,\]
hence a compound arrow
\[(6.2.1.1) \qquad \tau_\varphi : \mathfrak{F}^{I^*}|U \longrightarrow \bigoplus_{i \neq 0} f_{i*} f_i^*(\mathfrak{F}|U) \longrightarrow f_! f^* \mathfrak{F}|U \, .\]

If \(\sigma : I \longrightarrow J\) is a morphism of pointed sets, we denote by \(t_\sigma : \mathfrak{F}^{J^*} \longrightarrow \mathfrak{F}^{I^*}\) the arrow with coordinates \(t_\sigma^i = \operatorname{Id}\) if \(\sigma(i) = j\), and \(t_\sigma^i = 0\) if \(\sigma(i) \neq j\). If \(\sigma : I' \longrightarrow I\) is a morphism of \(\varphi\) into \(\varphi'\), the diagram
\[(6.2.1.2) \qquad \xymatrix@C=3em@R=2.5em{ \mathfrak{F}^{I^*}|U \ar[rr]^{t_\sigma} \ar[dr]_{\tau_\varphi} & & \mathfrak{F}^{I'^*}|U \ar[dl]^{\tau_{\varphi'}} \\ & f_! f^* \mathfrak{F}|U & }\]

is commutative. Additionally, \(t_{(\sigma\rho)} = t_\rho t_\sigma\). The \(\psi_f(U)\) form a fibered category \(\psi_f\) on \(S_{\mathrm{ét}}\); the previous constructions commute to localization and therefore define a \(S_{\mathrm{ét}}\)-functor \(\varphi = (V_i)_{i \in I} \in \psi_f(U) \longmapsto \mathfrak{F}^{I^*}|U\) of \(\psi\) in the \(S_{\mathrm{ét}}\)-category of sheaves on open open of \(S\), and a morphism of functors \(\tau_\varphi : \mathfrak{F}^{I^*}|U \longrightarrow f_! f^*\mathfrak{F}|U\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.2.2

(i) The category \(\psi_f\) is locally filtering on the right (IV 14) on \(S_{\mathrm{ét}}\).

(ii) The arrows (6.2.1.1) define an isomorphisms

\[\tau : \varinjlim_{\varphi = (V_i)_{i \in I} \in \psi_f(U)} \mathfrak{F}^{I^*}|U \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} f_! f^*\mathfrak{F} \, .\]

If \(S\) is strictly local to closed point \(s\), the \(S\)-schema \(X\) admits a decomposition \(\varphi \in \psi_f(S)\) into open and closed parts \(X = \coprod_{i \in I} X_i\) indexed by a pointed set \(I\) such that fiber of \(X_0\) in \(s\) is empty, that the \(X_i\) (\(i \neq 0\)) are finite on \(S\) and that the fiber of \(X_i\) (\(i \neq 0\)) in \(s\) is reduced to one point (EGA IV 18.12.1). Such decomposition is a final element of \(\psi(S)\). Additionally, by virtue of 6.1.4 (iii) (or because it's obvious) the fiber at \(s\) of the arrow \(\tau_\varphi\) (6.2.1.1) is an isomorphism.
In the general case, let \(x\) be a geometric point of \(S\) and \(\psi_x\) the fiber of \(\psi_f\) in \(x\),
\[\psi_x = \varinjlim_{U(x)} \psi_f(U) \, ,\]

for \(U\) slack neighborhood of \(x\). Let \(S_x\) be the strict localization of \(S\) in \(x\), \(X_x = X \times_S S_x\) and \(f_x : X_x \longrightarrow S_x\). The sorites of passage at the limit show that the category \(\psi_x\) is equivalent to the category \(\psi_{f_x}(S_x)\), therefore filtering on the right. It follows that \(\psi\) is locally right-filtering. Additionally, the fiber in \(x\) of \(\tau\) identifies with the arrow
\[\tau_x : \varinjlim_{\psi_x} \mathfrak{F}^{I_x}_x \longrightarrow (f_! f^*\mathfrak{F})_x\]
and, according to the above, this arrow is an isomorphism.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 6.2.3

We can define, in one and only one way, for any morphism \(f : X \longrightarrow S\), separate, flat of finite and quasi-finite presentation, and any abelian sheaf \(\mathfrak{F}\) on \(S\), a trace morphism

\[\operatorname{Tr}_f : f_! f^*\mathfrak{F} \longrightarrow \mathfrak{F}\]

so that the following conditions are verified:

(Var 1) \(\operatorname{Tr}_f\) is functorial in \(\mathfrak{F}\).

(Var 2) \(\operatorname{Tr}_f\) is compatible with any change of base, i.e. for any Cartesian diagram

\[\xymatrix@C=2.4em@R=2em{ X' \ar[r]^{g'} \ar[d]_{f'} & X \ar[d]^{f} \\ S' \ar[r]_{g} & S }\]

the following functor diagram is commutative:

\[\xymatrix@C=2.4em@R=3em{ f'_! f'^* g^* \ar@{=}[r] \ar[d]_{\operatorname{Tr}_{f'} * g^*} & f'_! g'^* f^* \ar@{=}[r] & g^* f_! f^* \ar[d]^{g^* * \operatorname{Tr}_f} \\ g^* \ar@{=}[rr] & & g^* }\]

(Var 3) \(\operatorname{Tr}_f\) is compatible with composition, i.e. if \(f\) is the compound \(f = gh\) of two separate, flat morphisms, of finite and quasi-finite presentation, the following diagram is commutative:

\[\xymatrix@C=2.4em@R=2.5em{ f_! f^* \ar@{=}[r] \ar[ddr]_{\operatorname{Tr}_f} & g_! h_! h^* g^* \ar[d]^{g_! * \operatorname{Tr}_h * g^*} \\ & g_! g^* \ar[d]^{\operatorname{Tr}_g} \\ & \operatorname{Id} }\]

(Var 4) (Normalization). If \(f\) is finite of constant rank \(n\), the compound homomorphism

\[\mathfrak{F} \longrightarrow f_* f^*\mathfrak{F} = f_! f^*\mathfrak{F} \xrightarrow{\operatorname{Tr}_f} \mathfrak{F}\]

is multiplication by \(n\).
Let us first note that the conditions (Var 2) to (Var 4) imply that

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.2.3.1

If \(X = X' \sqcup X''\) then, setting \(f' = f|X'\) and \(f'' = f|X''\), the morphism \(\operatorname{Tr}_f\):

\[f_! f^*\mathfrak{F} = f'_! f'^*\mathfrak{F} \oplus f''_! f''^*\mathfrak{F} \longrightarrow \mathfrak{F}\]

is sum of \(\operatorname{Tr}_{f'}\) and \(\operatorname{Tr}_{f''}\).
\[\xymatrix@C=2.4em@R=1.6em{ X' \ar[dr]^{j'} & & \\ & X \ar[r]^{f} & S \\ X'' \ar[ur]_{j''} & & }\]
If we apply (Var 2) to \(j'\), for the change of base by \(j'\), and (Var 4) for the identical application, we see that
\[\operatorname{Tr}_{j'} : j'_! j'^* f^*\mathfrak{F} \longrightarrow f^*\mathfrak{F}\]
is the canonical map defined by the decomposition of \(f^*\mathfrak{F}\) into direct sum
\[f^*\mathfrak{F} = j'_! j'^* f^*\mathfrak{F} \oplus j''_! j''^* f^*\mathfrak{F} \, .\]
If we apply (Var 3) to \(f' = f \circ j'\), we see that the following diagram is commutative
\[\xymatrix@C=3em@R=2.5em{ f'_! f'^*\mathfrak{F} \ar[rr] \ar[dr]_{\operatorname{Tr}_{f'}} & & f_! f^*\mathfrak{F} \ar[dl]^{\operatorname{Tr}_f} \\ & \mathfrak{F} & } \, ,\]
which, together with the analogous statement for \(f''\), is none other than 6.2.3.1.

Let \(f : X \longrightarrow S\) be a separate flat quasi-finite morphism of finite presentation. Whatever \(U \in \operatorname{Ob} S_{\mathrm{ét}}\), let \(\psi'_f(U)\) be the full subcategory of \(\psi_f(U)\) (6.2.1) formed from the decompositions \((V_i)_{i \in I}\) such that, for \(i \neq 0\), \(V_i\) is locally free of constant rank on \(U\). The categories \(\psi'_f(U)\) form a fibered subcategory of \(\psi_f\), cofinal in \(\psi_f\).

Let \(\mathfrak{F}\) and \(\mathfrak{G}\) be abelian sheaves on \(S\). According to 6.2.2 (ii), it is the same thing to give oneself a morphism \(g : f_! f^*\mathfrak{F} \longrightarrow \mathfrak{G}\) or to give oneself, for each decomposition \(\varphi = (V_i)_{i \in I} \in \psi'_f(U)\), a morphism \(g_\varphi : \mathfrak{F}^{I^*}|U \longrightarrow \mathfrak{G}|U\), compatible with the morphisms \(t_\sigma\) of (6.2.1.2). If \(f_i\) is the projection of \(V_i\) onto \(U\), \(g_\varphi\) is the coordinate arrow the compound arrows:
\[g_\varphi^i : \mathfrak{F}|U \longrightarrow f_{i*} f_i^* \mathfrak{F}|U \longrightarrow f_! f^*\mathfrak{F}|U \xrightarrow{\;g\;} \mathfrak{G}|U\]

We deduce that there exists one and only one arrow \(\operatorname{Tr}_f\), compatible with equal localization and verifying (Var 4) and 6.2.3.1. It is clear that this arrow verifies (Var 1) and (Var 2).
To check (Var 3), we return by change of base to the easy case where \(S\) is the spectrum of an algebraically closed field.

6.2.4. A weighting of a separate quasi-finite morphism \(f : X \longrightarrow S\) is the data, for each geometric point \(x\) of \(X\) of an integer \(n(x)\), depending only on the point of \(X\) image of \(x\), this data verifying:

(*) Whatever \(U \in \operatorname{Ob} S_{\mathrm{ét}}\) and the open and closed part \(X_1\) of \(f^{-1}(U)\), finite on \(U\), the function which at each geometric point \(s\) of \(U\) associates the number \(\sum_{x \in X_1(s)} n(x)\) is locally constant.
If \(f = g h\) is the composite of two weighted morphisms, we weight \(f\) by setting \(n_f(x) = n_h(x) \cdot n_g(h(x))\).
If the morphism \(f'\) is deduced by a change of base \(g\) of a weighted morphism \(f\), we weight \(f'\) by setting \(n_{f'}(x) = n_f(g'(x))\).
The preceding arguments actually prove:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.2.5

We can define in one and only one way, for any weighted separate quasi-finite morphism \(f : X \longrightarrow S\) and any abelian sheaf \(\mathfrak{F}\) on \(S\), a trace morphism \(\operatorname{Tr}_f : f_! f^*\mathfrak{F} \longrightarrow \mathfrak{F}\), which is functorial in \(\mathfrak{F}\), compatible with base changes and composition, and verifies:

(Var'4) If \(f\) is finite and if there exists an integer \(n\) such that, for every geometric point \(s\) of \(S\), we have \(\sum_{x \in X(s)} n(x) = n\), then the compound arrow

\[\mathfrak{F} \longrightarrow f_* f^*\mathfrak{F} = f_! f^*\mathfrak{F} \xrightarrow{\operatorname{Tr}_f} \mathfrak{F}\]

is multiplication by \(n\).

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
Example 6.2.6

Let \(S\) be a geometrically unibranched Noetherian scheme (for example normal), and \(f : X \longrightarrow S\) be a separate quasi-finite morphism. For any geometric point \(x\) of \(X\) of image \(s\) in \(S\), let \(\mathcal{O}_x\) (resp. \(\mathcal{O}_s\)) be the strictly local ring of \(X\) in \(x\) (resp. from \(S\) to \(s\)). The schema \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal{O}_s)\) is reduced to a single maximal point \(\eta\). Additionally, \(\mathcal{O}_x\) is finished on \(\mathcal{O}_s\). We set \(n(x) = \operatorname{rg}_\eta(\mathcal{O}_x)\). We verify that \(n(x)\) is a weighting of \(f\), hence a trace morphism \(\operatorname{Tr}_f\).

6.2.7. Let \(u : X \longrightarrow Y\) be a flat quasi-finite separate morphism of finite presentation. The trace morphism (6.2.3) being functorial, it defines, for any complex of abelian sheaves \(K\) on \(Y\), a morphism of complexes
\[(6.2.7.1) \qquad \operatorname{Tr}_u : u_! u^* K \longrightarrow K \, .\]
We will also call, as desired, trace morphism, Gysin morphism or morphism \(u_*\) any morphism deduced from (6.2.7.1) or 6.2.3 by application of a functor. For example the following:
Let a commutative diagram
\[\xymatrix@C=2em@R=2em{ X \ar[rr]^{u} \ar[dr]_{f} & & Y \ar[dl]^{g} \\ & S & }\]
with locally separated \(g\) of finite type. For any abelian sheaf \(\mathfrak{F}\) on \(Y\), the trace morphism defines a morphism
\[(6.2.7.2) \qquad \operatorname{Tr}_u \text{ ou } u_* : f_! u^* \mathfrak{F} \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} g_! u_! u^* \mathfrak{F} \xrightarrow{g_!(\operatorname{Tr}_u)} g_! \mathfrak{F} \, .\]

If \(g\) is compactifiable, and if \(K\) is a complex lower bounded with torsion cohomology, or a complex of \(g^*\mathcal{A}\)-modules for \(\mathcal{A}\) bundle of torsion rings on \(S\), the trace morphism (6.2.7.1) induces same

\[(6.2.7.3) \qquad \operatorname{Tr}_u \text{ ou } u_* : Rf_! u^*K \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} Rg_! u_! u^*K \xrightarrow{Rg_!(\operatorname{Tr}_u)} Rg_! K \, ,\] \[(6.2.7.4) \qquad \operatorname{Tr}_u \text{ ou } u_* : R^q f_! u^*K \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} R^q g_! u_! u^*K \xrightarrow{R^q g_!(\operatorname{Tr}_u)} R^q g_! K \, .\]
For \(S\) spectrum of an algebraically closed field, (6.2.7.4) is a morphism
\[(6.2.7.5) \qquad u_* : H_c^q(X, u^*K) \longrightarrow H_c^q(Y, K)\]
We therefore see that cohomology with proper supports presents a covariant character with respect to quasi-finite morphisms.

6.2.8. Let \(u : X \longrightarrow Y\) as in 6.2.7, \(\Delta_n = [0,n]\) be the standard set with \(n + 1\) elements and \(X_n\) be the iterated fiber product \((n + 1)\)-uple of \(X\) above \(Y\). The \(X_n\) form a simplicial diagram augmented to \(Y\) (cf. VI\(_B\)). We will denote by \(u_n : X_n \longrightarrow Y\) the augmentation morphism. If \(\mathfrak{F}\) is an abelian sheaf on \(Y\), the sheaves \(\mathfrak{F}_n = u_{n!} u_n^*\mathfrak{F}\) form, by virtue (6.2.7.2) (applied after the language change \(S \longrightarrow Y\), \(X \longrightarrow X_n\), \(Y \longrightarrow Y\) or \(X_m\)), a simplicial beam increased towards \(\mathfrak{F}\). The differential sheaf of "non-degenerate chains" of this simplicial sheaf has by definition for \(n^{\text{ième}}\) composing the quotient of \(\mathfrak{F}_n\), cokernel of the direct sum of the goal degeneracy operators \(\mathfrak{F}_n\) and of the morphisms \(\sigma \sim \varepsilon(\sigma)\) for \(\sigma\) symmetry operator.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.2.9

Under the previous hypotheses, for \(u\) separate smear of finite surjective type and for any abelian sheaf \(\mathfrak{F}\) on \(Y\), we have

(i) The beams \(\mathfrak{F}_n\) form a resolution (left) of \(\mathfrak{F}\).

(ii) The differential complex of non-degenerate chains of the simplicial bundle \((\mathfrak{F}_n)\) is a resolution (left) of \(\mathfrak{F}\). If the geometric fibers of \(u\) all have at most \(d\) points, this resolution is of length \(\leq d\).

By change of base, it suffices to verify this statement for \(Y\) the spectrum of an algebraically closed field, in which case 6.2.8 reduces to the calculation of the simplicial homology of a simplex.

Let \((U_i)_{i \in I}\) be a finite open covering of the scheme \(Y\), \(\mathfrak{F}\) an abelian sheaf on \(Y\) and, for each part \(P\) of \(I\), let \(j_P\) be the inclusion of \(U_P = \bigcap_{i \in P} U_i\) in \(X\). Suppose you choose a total order on \(I\). If \(X = \coprod_{i \in I} U_i\) and if \(u\) is the projection of \(X\) onto \(Y\), the resolution 6.2.9 (ii) of \(\mathfrak{F}\) is given by
\[(6.2.9.1) \qquad (\mathfrak{F}_n)_{n\ \mathrm{dég}} = \bigoplus_{|P|=n+1} j_{P!} j_P^* \mathfrak{F} \, .\]
This resolution retains meaning for \(I\) infinity.

6.2.10. Under the assumptions of 6.2.9, let \(\mathcal{A}\) be a beam of torsion rings on \(Y\) and \(K \in \operatorname{Ob} D^-(Y, \mathcal{A})\). We anneal \(X\) by \(u^*\mathcal{A}\). Resolution 6.2.9 (i) (resp. 6.2.9 (ii)), being functorial in \(\mathfrak{F}\), defines a resolution of \(K\) by a double complex \(K_1\). This resolution is called the Čech extraordinary resolution (resp. alternating extraordinary) of \(K\).
Let a commutative diagram
\[\xymatrix@C=2em@R=2em{ X \ar[rr]^{u} \ar[dr]_{f} & & Y \ar[dl]^{g} \\ & S & }\]

with \(g\) compactifiable. With the notations of 6.2.8, if \(f_n\) is the projection of \(X_n\) onto \(S\), we have \(Rf_{n!} = Rg_! u_{n!}\). The complex \(K_1\), filtered by the semi-simplicial degree, therefore defines a spectral sequence
\[(6.2.10.1) \qquad E_1^{-p,q} = R^q f_{p!} (u_p^* K) \Longrightarrow R^{-p+q} g_! K \, .\]
The differentials \(d_1\) come from the simplicial arrows, so that
\[(6.2.10.2) \qquad E_2^{-p,q} = \mathscr{H}^p (R^q f_{p!} (u_p^* K)) \Longrightarrow R^{-p+q} g_! K \, .\]
Under the hypotheses of (6.2.9.1), we also find a spectral sequence
\[(6.2.10.3) \qquad E_1^{-p,q} = \bigoplus_{|P|=p+1} R^q f_{P!} (j_P^* K) \Longrightarrow R^{-p+q} g_! K \, .\]
The spectral sequences (6.2.10.1) and (6.2.10.3) are called the top localization spectral sequences.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.2.11

If \(f\) is a slack morphism, separated from finite type, the functor \(f_!\) is a left adjoint of the functor \(f^*\), and admits the trace morphism for arrow addition.

We know that if \(X\) is an object of a site \(S\), the functor \(f^*\) restricting the abelian sheaves to \(X\) admits a left adjoint \(f_!\). If \(F\) is an abelian beam on \(X\), \(f_! F\) is the beam generated by the prebeam
\[V \longrightarrow \bigoplus_{\xi \in \operatorname{Hom}(V,X)} F(\xi) \, .\]

In the flat case, let us provisionally denote this functor \(f^0_!\). From its property of addition it follows immediately that its formation commutes at any change of base. The trace morphism defines a morphism \(t\) from \(f_! \mathfrak{F}\) into \(f^0_! \mathfrak{F}\), compatible with any base change. It suffices to see that \(t\) is a fiber by fiber isomorphism, i.e. for \(S\) spectrum of a separably closed body, where this is trivial.
6.2.11.1. Please note that this interpretation of \(f^0_!\) is specific to the case of the slack site and does not extend to the fpqf site of a pre-schema, for example.
6.3. Variant of the trace for continuous coefficients.
In this issue we demonstrate the assertions of GROTHENDIECK [1, n°6], and we apply these results to the definition of a "trace morphism" for suitable fppf beams.

6.3.1. Let \(A\) be a commutative ring. If \(L\) and \(M\) are two \(A\)-projective modules of finite type, the map \(u \longmapsto \wedge^m u\), of \(\operatorname{Hom}(L,M)\) in \(\operatorname{Hom}(\wedge^m L, \wedge^m M)\), is compatible with any change of basis and \(m\)-ique (5.5.2). According to 5.5.2, it defines a morphism
\[(6.3.1.1) \qquad \wedge^m : \Gamma^m \operatorname{Hom}(L,M) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}(\wedge^m L, \wedge^m M)\]

such that, after any change of base, for \(u \in \operatorname{Hom}(L,M)\), we have \(\wedge^m(\gamma^m(u)) = \wedge^m u\). Since \(\operatorname{Hom}(L,M)\) is flat, this morphism is identified (5.5.2) with a morphism
\[(6.3.1.2) \qquad \wedge^m : \mathrm{TS}^m \operatorname{Hom}(L,M) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}(\wedge^m L, \wedge^m M) \, .\]

N.B. We can also define such a homomorphism as soon as 2 is invertible in \(A\): a symmetric element of \(\otimes^m \operatorname{Hom}(L,M)\) defines a symmetric homomorphism of \(\otimes^m L\) in \(\otimes^m M\), and this passes to the quotient to define a homomorphism of \(\wedge^m L\) in \(\wedge^m M\).
Given three \(A\)-projective modules of finite type, the diagram

\[(6.3.1.3) \qquad \xymatrix@C=2em@R=3em{ \Gamma^m \operatorname{Hom}(L,M) \otimes \Gamma^m \operatorname{Hom}(M,N) \ar[r] \ar[d]_{\wedge^m \otimes \wedge^m} & \Gamma^m \operatorname{Hom}(L,N) \ar[d]^{\wedge^m} \\ \operatorname{Hom}(\wedge^m L, \wedge^m M) \otimes \operatorname{Hom}(\wedge^m M, \wedge^m N) \ar[r] & \operatorname{Hom}(\wedge^m L, \wedge^m N) } \, ,\]

in which the horizontal arrows are deduced from the composition arrows (and, for the first, from (5.5.2.8)), is commutative. This translates the formula \(\wedge^m(vu) = \wedge^m v \circ \wedge^m u\).
If \(L\) is a \(A\)-free module of rank \(n\), (6.3.1.2) defines a canonical morphism
\[(6.3.1.4) \qquad \det : \Gamma^n \operatorname{End}_A(L) = \mathrm{TS}^n \operatorname{End}_A(L) \longrightarrow A \, ,\]
which is a homomorphism of algebras according to 6.3.1.3. By definition, we have
\[(6.3.1.5) \qquad \det(u^{\otimes n}) = \det(u ; L) \, .\]
If \(L\) is also a module on a \(A\)-algebra \(B\), (6.3.1.4) defines by composition a homomorphism of algebras
\[(6.3.1.6) \qquad \det_L : \mathrm{TS}^n_A(B) \longrightarrow A \, .\]

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.3.2

Suppose that \(L\) fits into an exact sequence

\[F : 0 \longrightarrow L' \longrightarrow L \longrightarrow L'' \longrightarrow 0\]

with \(L'\) (resp. \(L''\)) locally rank free \(n_1\) (resp. \(n_2\)). Let \(n = n_1 + n_2\), \(\alpha\) the "associated graded" morphism of the group \(\operatorname{End}(E)\) endomorphisms of the extension \(E\) in \(\operatorname{End}(L') \oplus \operatorname{End}(L'')\) and, for \(X\) and \(Y\) two \(A\)-flat modules, i.e. \(\beta\) the morphism of \(\mathrm{TS}^n(X \oplus Y)\) in \(\mathrm{TS}^{n_1}(X) \otimes \mathrm{TS}^{n_2}(Y)\) deduced from isomorphism (5.5.2.6)

\[\mathrm{TS}^n(X \oplus Y) \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \sum_{n'+n''=n} \mathrm{TS}^{n'}(X) \otimes \mathrm{TS}^{n''}(Y) \, .\]

The following diagram is commutative:

\[\xymatrix@C=1.6em@R=3em{ \mathrm{TS}^n(\operatorname{End}(E)) \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{TS}^n(\alpha)} \ar[d] & \mathrm{TS}^n(\operatorname{End}(L') \oplus \operatorname{End}(L'')) \ar[r]^-{\beta} & \mathrm{TS}^{n_1}(\operatorname{End}(L')) \otimes \mathrm{TS}^{n_2}(\operatorname{End}(L'')) \ar[d]^{\det \otimes \det} \\ \mathrm{TS}^n(\operatorname{End}(L)) \ar[rr]_-{\det} & & A }\]

According to universal property 5.5.2 of \(\mathrm{TS}^n\), it suffices to check that for \(u \in \operatorname{End}(E)\) the two images of \(u^{\otimes n}\) coincide. If \(\alpha(u) = (u', u'')\), these images are respectively \(\det(u)\) and \(\det(u').\det(u'')\), hence the conclusion.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.3.3

Let \(A\) a ring, \(B\) a \(A\)-algebra, \(C\) a \(B\)-algebra not necessarily commutative, \(E\) a \(B\)-rank free module \(n\) on \(B\), and \(F\) a \(C\)-rank free module \(m\) on \(C\). We assume \(C\) flat on \(A\). According to the universal property 5.5.2.2 of \(\Gamma^{nm}\) and 5.5.2.3, there exists a unique arrow \(\sigma : \mathrm{TS}^{nm}_A(C) \longrightarrow \mathrm{TS}^n_A \mathrm{TS}^m_B(C)\) such that after any change of rings \(A \longrightarrow A'\), we have \(\sigma(c^{\otimes nm}) = (c^{\otimes m})^{\otimes n}\). The following diagram is then commutative:

\[\xymatrix@C=3em@R=3em{ \mathrm{TS}^n_A(B) \ar[d]_{\det_E} & \mathrm{TS}^n_A \mathrm{TS}^m_B(C) \ar[l]_-{\mathrm{TS}^n_A(\det_F)} \\ A & \mathrm{TS}^{nm}_A(C) \ar[l]^-{\det_{E \otimes_B F}} \ar[u]_{\sigma} }\]

Proof. According to 5.5.2.2 and 5.5.2.3, it is sufficient to show that the two images of an element \(u^{\otimes nm}\) of \(\mathrm{TS}^{nm}_A(C)\) coincide. These images are \(\det_E(\det_F(u))\) and \(\det_{E \otimes_B F}(u)\); they are expressed in terms of \(A\), \(B\), \(E\), \(F\) and \(u \in \operatorname{End}_B(F)\) and, to check that they coincide, we can first replace \(B\) by its (commutative) image \(B_1\) in \(\operatorname{End}(E)\), and \(F\) by \(F \otimes_B B_1\). The formula to prove is then the block calculation formula for the determinants (BOURBAKI, Alg. Ch. 8 § 12 lemma 2 p.140).
When \(C\) is no longer necessarily flat on \(A\), the same proof more generally provides a commutative diagram of algebras

\[(6.3.3.1) \qquad \xymatrix@C=3em@R=3em{ \Gamma^n_A(B) \ar[d]_{\det_E} & \Gamma^n_A \Gamma^m_B(C) \ar[l]_-{\Gamma^n_A(\det_F)} \\ A & \Gamma^{nm}_A(C) \ar[l]^-{\det_{E \otimes_B F}} \ar[u]_{\sigma} } \, .\]

6.3.4. If \(B\) is a \(A\)-commutative algebra, \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathrm{TS}^n(B))\) is none other than the symmetric power \(n^{\text{ième}}\) of \(\operatorname{Spec}(B)\) on \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), i.e. the quotient of \((\operatorname{Spec}(B)/\operatorname{Spec}(A))^n\) by the symmetric group (SGA 1 IV 3).

If \(X\) is a separate \(S\)-schema, we will say that a \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-quasi-consistent module \(\mathfrak{F}\) on \(X\) is finite locally free (resp. and of rank \(n\)) on \(S\) if its support is finite on \(S\) and if its direct image is locally free of finite rank (resp. of rank \(n\)) on \(S\). By support of \(\mathfrak{F}\), we will again understand the sub-schema of \(X\) defined by the cancellation of \(\mathfrak{F}\) (which is a quasi-consistent Ideal, \(\mathfrak{F}\) being quasi-consistent of finite type).

If \(\mathfrak{F}\) is finite locally free on \(S\), of rank \(n\) and of support \(E\), the scheme \(E\) is finite on \(S\); by globalization on \(S\), \(\mathfrak{F}\) defines, via (6.3.1.6), a section \(\sigma_E(\mathfrak{F})\) of \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(E)\), hence, by functoriality, a section \(\sigma(\mathfrak{F})\) of the algebraic space(∗) \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(X)\),
\[(6.3.4.1) \qquad \sigma(\mathfrak{F}) \in \Gamma(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(X)/S) \, .\]
In particular, if \(f : S' \longrightarrow S\) is finite locally free of rank \(n\), we obtain, for \(\mathfrak{F} = \mathcal{O}_{S'}\), a canonical section
\[(6.3.4.2) \qquad t \in \Gamma(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(S')/S) \, .\]

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.3.5

Let \(X\) a separate diagram on \(S\) And \(\mathfrak{F}_1\), \(\mathfrak{F}_2\) two \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-locally rank-free finite modules \(n_1\) And \(n_2\) on \(S\). We pose \(n = n_1 + n_2\). Either \(d\) the canonical arrow \(d : \operatorname{Sym}^{n_1}_S(X) \times \operatorname{Sym}^{n_2}_S(X) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Sym}^n_S(X)\). If \(\mathfrak{F}\) is an extension of \(\mathfrak{F}_1\) by \(\mathfrak{F}_2\), we have

\[\sigma(\mathfrak{F}) = d(\sigma(\mathfrak{F}_1), \sigma(\mathfrak{F}_2)) \, .\]
We can replace \(X\) by the support (finite on \(S\)) of \(\mathfrak{F}_1 \oplus \mathfrak{F}_2\) and assume \(S\) affine. Translated into terms of rings, the proposition then results from 6.3.2.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.3.6

If \(X\) is a separate scheme on \(S\), \(\mathfrak{F}\) a \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-locally rank-free finite module \(n\) on \(S\) and if \(\mathcal{L}\) is an invertible beam on \(X\), we have

\[\sigma(\mathfrak{F}) = \sigma(\mathfrak{F} \otimes \mathcal{L}) \, .\]
Let's replace \(X\) with support (6.3.4) for \(\mathfrak{F}\) locally on \(S\), \(\mathcal{L}\) is then isomorphic to \(\mathcal{O}_X\), and the assertion becomes trivial.
The reader will check:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.3.7

For any diagram of \(S\)-separate diagrams on \(S\)

\[\xymatrix@C=2em@R=2em{ X \ar[rr]^{u} \ar[dr]_{f} & & Y \ar[dl]^{g} \\ & S & }\]

and all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathfrak{F}\) finite locally rank-free \(n\) on \(S\), the \(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-module \(u_* \mathfrak{F}\) is finite locally rank-free \(n\) on \(S\), and

\[u(\sigma(\mathfrak{F})) = \sigma(u_* \mathfrak{F}) \, .\]
6.3.7.1. Finally, note that the construction of the morphisms \(\sigma\) (6.3.4.1) and \(t\) (6.3.4.2) is compatible with any change of base, which makes sense according to 5.5.2.7.
Application 1. The case of smooth curves.

6.3.8. Let \(f : X \longrightarrow S\) be a smooth and quasi-projective curve on \(S\), i.e. a smooth quasi-projective morphism purely of relative dimension 1. The theory of Hilbert's schemes [GROTHENDIECK [1]] shows that the sheaf fpqc \(\underline{\mathrm{Div}}^n_{X/S}\) which, at each \(S\)-scheme \(S'\), associates the set of relative divisors on \(X' = X \times_S S'\) which are finite on \(S\) and of degree \(n\), is a representable sheaf.

For each relative divisor \(E\) on \(X\), finite degree \(n\) on \(S\), we have a canonical element (6.3.4.2) \(t(E) \in \operatorname{Sym}^n_S(E)\), of image \(\sigma(E)\) in \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(X)\). This construction, being compatible with any change of base, defines
\[(6.3.8.1) \qquad \sigma : \underline{\mathrm{Div}}^n_{X/S} \longrightarrow \operatorname{Sym}^n_S(X) : E \longrightarrow \sigma(\mathcal{O}_E) \, .\]
Each section \(s\) of \(f\) defines a finite relative divisor on \(S\) of degree 1, the divisor \(s(S)\). Morphism
\[(X/S)^n \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Div}}^n_{X/S} : (s_i)_{0 < i \leq n} \longmapsto \sum s_i(S)\]
is symmetrical, and therefore induces a morphism
\[(6.3.8.2) \qquad \alpha : \operatorname{Sym}^n_S(X) \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Div}}^n_{X/S} \, .\]

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.3.9

The morphisms \(\sigma\) and \(\alpha\) from 6.3.8 are inverse isomorphisms of each other.
Proof. a) \(\sigma \alpha = \operatorname{Id}\). It is enough to prove that the compound arrow
\[(X/S)^n \xrightarrow{\;\pi\;} \operatorname{Sym}^n_S(X) \xrightarrow{\;\alpha\;} \underline{\mathrm{Div}}^n_{X/S} \xrightarrow{\;\sigma\;} \operatorname{Sym}^n_S(X)\]
is \(\pi\). For \(n = 1\), the assertion is clear; the general case is deduced via

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.3.9.1

With the notations of 6.3.5 and (6.3.8.1), we have

\[\sigma(E + F) = d(\sigma(E), \sigma(F)) \, .\]
This lemma follows from 6.3.5, 6.3.6 and the exact sequence
\[0 \longrightarrow \mathcal{O}(-F) \otimes \mathcal{O}_E \longrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{E+F} \longrightarrow \mathcal{O}_F \longrightarrow 0 \, .\]

b) We know that \(\underline{\mathrm{Div}}^n_{X/S}\) is smooth on \(S\) (there is no obstruction in finding infinitesimally divisors on a curve (cf. SGA 3 XI ​​1)). The morphism \(\alpha \pi : (X/S)^n \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Div}}^n_{X/S}\) is a quasi-finite surjective morphism between smooth schemes on \(S\) of equidimensional fiber sources. It is therefore flat (EGA \(\mathrm{O}_{IV}\) 17.3.5), so \(\alpha \pi\) is an epimorphism of fppf bundles, so \(\alpha\) is too. Considering a), this completes the proof of 6.3.9.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.3.10

Let \(k\) a commutative ring, \(M\) a \(k\)-free module and \(u\) an endomorphism of \(M\). Let us designate by \(N_{\det(u-T)=0}\) the norm of \(k[T]/\det(u-T)\) to \(k\). For \(P \in k[T]\), we then have

\[N_{\det(u-T)=0}(P(T)) = \det(P(u), M) \, .\]

This involves verifying an algebraic identity between the coefficients of the matrix of \(u\) and the coefficients of \(P\). To prove 6.3.10, it is therefore sufficient (thanks to familiar arguments) to treat the case where \(k\) is an algebraically closed field and where \(u\) is semi-simple, with eigenvalues ​​\(a_i\).
We then have
\[\det(u - T) = \prod (a_i - T)\] \[N_{\det(u - T) = 0}(P(T)) = \prod P(a_i) \qquad \text{et}\] \[\det(P(u), M) = \prod P(a_i) \, .\]

6.3.11.0. Let \(f : X \longrightarrow S\) be a quasi-projective morphism, of finite presentation, flat with fibers purely of dimension 1 and Cohen-Macaulay; these conditions are therefore verified if \(X\) is a quasi-projective smooth curve on \(S\). Let \(\mathfrak{F}\) be a \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, finite locally free on \(S\). The module \(\mathfrak{F}\) being zero on a dense open fiber by fiber of \(X\), we have a canonical arrow \(\psi : \mathcal{O}_X \longrightarrow \det{}^*(\mathfrak{F})\) (SGA 6 XI or MUMFORD [1 Ch. 5 § 3]). We will designate by \(\delta(\mathfrak{F})\) the divisor defined by the canceling ideal of \(\operatorname{coker}(\psi)\) (Ibidem); this divisor is finite on \(S\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.3.11.1

Under the assumptions 6.3.11.0 and with the notations of 6.3.8 and (6.3.4.1), if \(\mathfrak{F}\) is of rank \(n\), then \(\delta(\mathfrak{F})\) is of degree \(n\) and

\[\sigma(\mathfrak{F}) = \sigma(\delta(\mathfrak{F})) \, .\]

Demonstration. a) Case \(f\) smooth. We return in a standard way to the \(S\) local Artinian case with an algebraically closed residual body. The additivity (6.3.5) of \(\sigma\) allows us to assume the support of \(\mathfrak{F}\) concentrated at a rational point \(x\) of \(X\). We immediately check that if \(T\) is a standardizer in \(x\), defining \(p : X \longrightarrow \mathbb{E}^1_S\), then \(p_*(\mathcal{O}_{\delta(\mathfrak{F})}) \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \mathcal{O}_{\delta(p_*\mathfrak{F})}\) (because the constructions carried out switch to the completed one). We go back this way and 6.3.7 in case \(X = \mathbb{E}^1_S\).

If \(X = \mathbb{E}^1_S\) and if \(S\) is affine, \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(k)\), then \(\mathfrak{F}\) is identified with a \(k\)-locally free module \(M\), provided with an endomorphism \(u\) (the action of \(T\)). Module \(M\), as \(k[T]\)-module, supports resolution
\[0 \longrightarrow k[T] \otimes_k M \xrightarrow{\;\varphi\;} k[T] \otimes_k M \xrightarrow{\;\varepsilon\;} M \longrightarrow 0 \, ,\]

where \(\varepsilon(P \otimes m) = P(u)(m)\) and where \(\varphi(1 \otimes m) = 1 \otimes um - T \otimes m\). From the formula \(\varphi = u - T\), we deduce that the divisor \(\delta(\mathfrak{F})\) admits as equation \(\det(u - T, M) = 0\).
To verify 6.3.11, it suffices to show that, after any change of base and for any \(\varphi\) section of \(\mathcal{O}_X\), we have
\[\det_k(\varphi, M) = \det_k(\varphi, \mathcal{O}_{\delta(\mathfrak{F})}) \quad (= N_{\delta(\mathfrak{F})/S}(\varphi)) \, .\]
For \(f = P(T)\), this is what 6.3.10 asserts.
b) General case. Let us first explain the statement to be proven in the following more concrete form:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.3.11.2

Under the hypotheses 6.3.11.0 on \(f : X \longrightarrow S\), are \(M\) a locally free module of finite type on \(X\), and \(u\) an endomorphism of \(M\) such that \(\det u\) is a section of \(\underline{\mathcal{O}}_X\) regular on each fiber \(X_s\) (from which it follows that \(\operatorname{Coker} u = M/u(M)\) and \(\operatorname{Coker} \det u = \underline{\mathcal{O}}_X/\det u\) are both finished presentation dishes on \(S\) and with quasi-finished fibers). Suppose that the common support of these sheaves is finite on \(S\), so that they define locally free sheaves of finite type on \(S\). Then for any section \(\varphi\) of \(\underline{\mathcal{O}}_X\), the determinants with respect to \(\underline{\mathcal{O}}_S\) of \(\varphi\) operating on \(\operatorname{Coker} u\) and on \(\operatorname{Coker} \det u\) are equal:

\[(6.3.11.3) \qquad \det_S(\varphi, M/u(M)) = \det_S(\varphi, \underline{\mathcal{O}}_X/\det u) \, .\]

Note that this statement implies the equality of the ranks of the two locally free Modules considered on \(S\), for reasons of homogeneity (when we replace \(\varphi\) by \(T \varphi\), \(T\) an "indeterminate" section of \(\underline{\mathcal{O}}_S\)). Moreover, replacing \(\varphi\) by \(1 + T \varphi\), where \(T\) is such an indeterminate (precisely, we make a change of base \(S' = S[T] \longrightarrow S\)), we deduce from (6.3.11.3) the apparently more general formula
\[(6.3.11.4) \qquad \operatorname{Pol}_S(\varphi, M/uM)(T) = \operatorname{Pol}_S(\varphi, \underline{\mathcal{O}}_X/\det u)(T)\]
on characteristic polynomials.

To prove 6.3.11.3, we will return to the smooth case already proven. By localization equal to \(S\), we reduce to the case where \(S\) is strictly local to closed point \(s\), then by additivity we reduce to the case where the support of \(M/u(M)\) has a single point on \(X_s\), i.e. \(x\). Even if it means making a flat extension of the base, we can assume the residual extension \(k(x)/k(s)\) trivial, and even if it means replacing \(X\) by a fairly small neighborhood of \(x\), and adding to \(\varphi\) a function which induces the null operation on \(M/u(M)\) and on \(\underline{\mathcal{O}}_X/\det u\) (which does not modify the formula to be proven), we easily see that we can suppose that \(\varphi\) induces a section of \(\underline{\mathcal{O}}_{X_s}\) regular in \(x\), therefore (even if it means reducing \(X\)) defines a quasi-finite and flat morphism
\[p : X \longrightarrow X' = S[T] \, .\]

By flat localization on \(X\) and on \(X'\), we can therefore assume that we have a finite and flat morphism \(p\) as above, with \(X'\) smooth on \(S\), and \(\varphi = p^*(\varphi')\), where \(\varphi'\) is a section of \(\underline{\mathcal{O}}_{X'}\). Let's then ask

\[\begin{aligned} & M' = p_*(M) \, , \quad u' = p_*(u) \, , \\ & P = \operatorname{Coker} u, \quad Q = \operatorname{Coker} \det u \, , \\ & P' = \operatorname{Coker} u', \quad Q' = \operatorname{Coker} \det u' \, . \end{aligned}\]
We know we have
\[\det u' = N_{X'/X} \det u, \quad \text{donc} \quad Q' = \operatorname{Coker} N_{X'/X}(\det u) \, .\]
We have trivially
\[\det_S(\varphi, P) = \det_S(\varphi', P') \, ,\]
and according to the formula (6.3.11.3) proven in the smooth case,
\[\det_S(\varphi', P') = \det_S(\varphi', Q') \, ,\]
so that it remains to prove the formula
\[\det_S(\varphi', Q') = \det_S(\varphi, Q) \, ,\]

which is equivalent to the formula (6.3.11.3) to be established. We therefore see that the validity of this only depends on the section \(\det u\) of \(\underline{\mathcal{O}}_X\), or that it is equivalent to the same formula in the case where \(M\) is replaced by \(\underline{\mathcal{O}}_X\), and \(\varphi\) by \(\det u\). But in the case \(M = \underline{\mathcal{O}}_X\) the formula (6.3.11.3) is tautological! This completes the demonstration.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 6.3.11.5

In the case where \(S\) is artinian, the equality of ranks on \(S\) of \(\operatorname{Coker} u\) and \(\operatorname{Coker} \det u\) (notations of 6.3.11.2) is a special case of EGA IV 21.10.17.3, of which (6.3.11.3) can be considered a "relative" version.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.3.12

Let \(X\) and \(Y\) two quasi-projective curves of finite presentation, flat with Cohen Macaulay fibers on \(S\), and \(u : X \longrightarrow Y\) a quasi-finite and flat morphism.

The following diagram

\[\xymatrix@C=3em@R=2.5em{ \underline{\mathrm{Div}}^n_{X/S} \ar[r]^{u_*} \ar[d]_{\sigma_X} & \underline{\mathrm{Div}}^n_{Y/S} \ar[d]^{\sigma_Y} \\ \operatorname{Sym}^n_S(X) \ar[r]_{\operatorname{Sym}^n(u)} & \operatorname{Sym}^n_S(Y) }\]

is then commutative(∗).
Since \(u_*(D) = \delta(u_* \mathcal{O}_D)\), 6.3.12 follows from 6.3.7 and 6.3.11.1.
Application 2. Trace morphism.
6.3.13. Let \(G\) be an abelian beam on the large fppf site of a scheme \(S\) (0.10). Suppose \(G\) satisfies the following condition.
(6.3.13.1). For any flat affine morphism of finite presentation \(f : X \longrightarrow Y\) of \(S\)-schemes, and for any integer \(n \geq 1\), the morphism
\[\operatorname{Hom}_S(\operatorname{Sym}^n_Y(X), G) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}_S((X/Y)^n, G)\]
is injective and has as image the set of symmetric morphisms of \((X/Y)^n\) in \(G\).
This condition is stable by change of base.

Let \(f : T \longrightarrow S\) be a locally free finite morphism of rank \(n\). Let \(\operatorname{pr}_i\) be the \(i^{\text{ème}}\) projection of \((T/S)^n\) into \(T\), and \(t\) be the section (6.3.4.2) of \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(T)\). If \(u \in G(T)\), then \(\sum \operatorname{pr}_i^*(u)\) is a symmetric element of \(G((T/S)^n)\) and therefore defines
\[u_n \in G(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(T)) \, .\]
We pose
\[\operatorname{Tr}_f(u) = t^*(u_n) \, .\]

When \(f\) is finished locally free, we define \(\operatorname{Tr}_f(u)\), locally on \(S\), by the previous formula. This construction is compatible with any change of base and therefore defines a trace morphism
\[(6.3.13.2) \qquad \operatorname{Tr}_f : f_* f^*G \longrightarrow G \, .\]
6.3.14. The condition (6.3.13.1) is verified in the following cases
a) If \(G\) is representable, by definition of symmetric powers.
b) For \(G\) the reciprocal image on \(S_{\mathrm{fppf}}\) of a beam on the small flat site of \(S\) (0.10).
c) For \(G\) defined by a \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-quasi-consistent module \(G_0\).
Statement c) reduces to (5.5.2.7).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.3.15

For \(G\) verifying (6.3.13.1), we have:

(i) The morphism \(\operatorname{Tr}_f\) is functorial in \(G\), therefore additive.

(ii) His training is compatible with any change of base \(S' \longrightarrow S\).

(iii) If \(T = \coprod_i T_i\), so that \(f_* G \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \sum f_{i*} G\), we have \(\operatorname{Tr}_f = \sum \operatorname{Tr}_{f_i}\).

(iv) If \(T\) is of constant rank \(n\) on \(S\), the compound morphism

\[G \longrightarrow f_* f^*G \xrightarrow{\operatorname{Tr}_f} G\]

is multiplication by \(n\).

(v) The trace morphism is compatible with composition (cf. 6.2.3 Var 3).

Assertions (i) and (ii) are clear, and (iii) is an easy consequence of 6.3.5. With the notations of 6.3.13, if \(u \in G(S)\), then \(u_n\) is \(n\) times the reciprocal image of \(u\), so that \(\operatorname{Tr}_f(f^*u) = t^*(u_n) = n u\).
To prove (v), we reduce, by equal localization on \(S\) and using (iii), to prove that for
\[f = gh : S'' \xrightarrow{\;h\;} S' \xrightarrow{\;g\;} S \, ,\]
with finite locally free \(f\) and \(g\) of constant ranks \(n\) and \(m\), the diagram
\[(6.3.15.1) \qquad \xymatrix@C=3em@R=2.5em{ G(S'') \ar[rr]^{\operatorname{Tr}_h} \ar[dr]_{\operatorname{Tr}_f} & & G(S') \ar[dl]^{\operatorname{Tr}_g} \\ & G(S) & }\]
is commutative. For \(u \in G(S'')\), consider the diagram

\[(6.3.15.2) \qquad \xymatrix@C=2.6em@R=3em{ \operatorname{Sym}^n_S(S') \ar[rr]^{\operatorname{Sym}^n(t)} \ar[dr]|-{\operatorname{Tr}_g(u)_n} & & \operatorname{Sym}^n_S \operatorname{Sym}^m_{S'}(S'') \ar[dl]|-{(u_m)_n} \\ & G & \\ S \ar[uu]^{t} \ar[rr]_{t} \ar@/^0.7em/[ur]|-{\operatorname{Tr}_f \operatorname{Tr}_g(u)} \ar@/_0.9em/[ur]|-{\operatorname{Tr}_{fg}(u)} & & \operatorname{Sym}^{nm}_S(S'') \ar[uu]_{\sigma} \ar[ul]|-{u_{nm}} }\]
The right vertical arrow is defined because \(S'\) is flat on \(S\); each triangle is commutative and (v) will therefore be a consequence of the commutativity of the outer edge.
Translating in terms of rings, this is a consequence of the special case \(B = E\) and \(C = F\) of 6.3.3.

6.3.16. Let now be \(f : T \longrightarrow S\) a flat quasi-finite morphism of finite presentation. If \(\mathfrak{F}\) is an fppf beam on \(T\), we define its direct image with own support \(f_! \mathfrak{F}\) on \(S\) as the pre-beam which to \(S'/S\) associates all the sections of \(F\) on \(T'\) whose support is clean on \(S'\). This pre-beam is a beam according to 6.1.1. With the notation of 6.1.1, it can also be expressed as a local inductive limit (IV 14. ).
\[f_! F = \varinjlim_{V \in \varphi(U)} f_*(F|V) \, ,\]
formula which accounts for the fairly obvious fact that the properties of \(f_!\) are reduced to those of \(f'_*\) for \(f'\) finished. We find thus:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.3.17

We can define in one and only one way, for any morphism \(f : T \longrightarrow S\) quasi-finite flat of finite presentation and for any fppf sheaf \(G\) on \(S\) verifying (6.3.13.1), a morphism trace

\[\operatorname{Tr}_f : f_! f^*G \longrightarrow G\]

checking:

(i) For \(f\) finished we find the morphism (6.3.13.2).

(ii) The conditions (Var 1) to (Var 4) of 6.2.3 are met, mutatis mutandis.
The quasi-finite morphism \(f\) induces a commutative diagram (cf. (0.11)) of sites (in which \(\alpha_*\) is the "restriction" functor):
\[\xymatrix@C=2.4em@R=2.5em{ T_{\mathrm{fppf}} \ar[r]^{\alpha} \ar[d]_{f} & T_{\mathrm{ét}} \ar[d]^{f_{\mathrm{ét}}} \\ S_{\mathrm{fppf}} \ar[r]_{\alpha} & S_{\mathrm{ét}} }\]
We have, for \(G\) on \(T_{\mathrm{fppf}}\),
\[f_! \alpha_* \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \alpha_* f_! \, .\]
We also have a “base change” morphism
\[c : f_{\mathrm{ét}}^* \alpha_* \longrightarrow \alpha_* f^* \, .\]

We leave it to the reader to check the following compatibility between trace morphisms 6.2.3 and 6.3.17. For \(G\) on \(S_{\mathrm{fppf}}\) satisfying (6.3.13.1), the following diagram is commutative:

\[(6.3.17.1) \qquad \xymatrix@C=2.6em@R=3em{ f_{\mathrm{ét}!} f_{\mathrm{ét}}^* \alpha_* G \ar[r]^-{f_! \mathrm{ch}} \ar[d]_{\operatorname{Tr}_f} & f_{\mathrm{ét}!} \alpha_* f^*G & \alpha_* f_! f^*G \ar[l]_-{\sim} \ar[d]^{\alpha_* \operatorname{Tr}_f} \\ \alpha_* G \ar@{=}[rr] & & \alpha_* G }\]

In more concrete terms, this means that if \(G\) is an fppf beam on \(S\), which by restriction to the slack site defines a slack beam \(\alpha_*G\), and if \(u\) is a self-supported section of the reciprocal image \(f_{\mathrm{ét}}^*(\alpha_*G)\), of image \(u_1\) in \(f^*G\), the elements \(\operatorname{Tr}_f(u)\) (in the sense 6.2.3) and \(\operatorname{Tr}_f(u_1)\) (in the sense 6.2.17) coincide in \(G(S)\).

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
Example 6.3.18

Let \(f : S' \longrightarrow S\) be a locally free finite morphism of rank \(n\) and \(u \in \Gamma(S', \mathcal{O}_{S'}) = \operatorname{Hom}_S(S', \mathbb{E}^1_S)\). The morphism \(u\) defines a morphism

\[\operatorname{Sym}(u) : \operatorname{Sym}^n_S(S') \longrightarrow \operatorname{Sym}^n_S(\mathbb{E}^1_S) = \operatorname{Spec}(S[\sigma_1 \ldots \sigma_n]) \, ,\]

where the \(\sigma_i\) are elementary symmetric functions (cf. BOURBAKI, Alg. 5, App. I n°2). We then have (by an easy calculation left to the reader):

\[(6.3.18.1) \qquad [\operatorname{Sym}(u) \circ t]^*(\sigma_i) = \sigma_i(u) \, ,\]

where \(t \in \Gamma(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(S')/S)\) is the canonical element (6.3.4.2), and where we denote by \(\sigma_i(u)\) the \(i^{\text{ème}}\) coefficient of the polynomial characteristic of the multiplication by \(u\) in \(f_* \mathcal{O}_{S'}\). We deduce that the "trace" applications

\[\begin{cases} \mathbb{G}_a(S') \longrightarrow \mathbb{G}_a(S) \\[4pt] \mathbb{G}_m(S') \longrightarrow \mathbb{G}_m(S) \end{cases}\]

are respectively the usual trace and norm; indeed, the morphism \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(\mathbb{E}^1_S) \longrightarrow \mathbb{E}^1_S\) deduced from the additive (resp. multiplicative) law on \(\mathbb{E}^1_S\) is given by the function \(\sigma_1\) (resp. \(\sigma_n\)) on \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(\mathbb{E}^1_S) \simeq \operatorname{Spec}(S[\sigma_1,\ldots,\sigma_n])\).

We then deduce from (6.3.17.1) that for \(n\) invertible on \(S\) the spread beam diagram

\[(6.3.18.2) \qquad \xymatrix@C=2em@R=3em{ 0 \ar[r] & f_* \mu_n \ar[r] \ar[d]_{\operatorname{Tr}_f} & f_* \mathbb{G}_m \ar[r] \ar[d]^{N_f} & f_* \mathbb{G}_m \ar[r] \ar[d]^{N_f} & 0 \\ 0 \ar[r] & \mu_n \ar[r] & \mathbb{G}_m \ar[r] & \mathbb{G}_m \ar[r] & 0 }\]

is commutative.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.3.19

Let be a commutative diagram

\[\xymatrix@C=2em@R=2em{ X \ar[rr]^{u} \ar[dr]_{p} & & Y \ar[dl]^{q} \\ & S & }\]

in which \(X\) and \(Y\) are quasi-projective smooth curves on \(S\) and \(u\) a quasi-finite morphism.

(i) If \(D\) is a divisor on \(X\) finite on \(S\) (see note p.192) and \(g\) a section of \(G_Y\) on \(Y\), we have

\[\operatorname{Tr}_{D/S}(u^*g) = \operatorname{Tr}_{(u_* D)/S}(g) \, .\]

(ii) If \(E\) is a divisor on \(Y\) finitely on \(S\), \(g\) a section of \(G_X\) on \(X\) and if \(u\) is finished, we have

\[\operatorname{Tr}_{E/S}(\operatorname{Tr}_{X/Y}(g)) = \operatorname{Tr}_{(u^* E)/S}(g) \, .\]
Proof. (i) Let \(g_n\) be the section of \(G\) over \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(Y)\) deduced from \(g\) (6.3.13). According to 6.3.12, we have
\[\operatorname{Tr}_{D/S}(u^*g) \underset{\mathrm{dfn}}{=} \sigma(D)^*(u^*g)_n = \sigma(D)^*(u^*g_n) = (u \circ \sigma(D))^*(g_n) = \sigma(u_* D)^*(g_n) = \operatorname{Tr}_{u_* D}(g_n) \, .\]
(ii) Formula (ii) results from 6.3.15 (ii) and (v):
\[\operatorname{Tr}_{E/S}(\operatorname{Tr}_{X/Y}(g)) = \operatorname{Tr}_{E/S}(\operatorname{Tr}_{u^*E/E}(g)) = \operatorname{Tr}_{u^*E/S}(g) \, .\]
Application 3. Trace of a torso.

6.3.20. Let \(G\) be a beam on the large fppf site of \(S\) which satisfies (6.3.13.1). Let \(u : X \longrightarrow Y\) be a locally free finite morphism of \(S\)-schemes and \(K\) be a \(G_X\)-torsor on \(X\). Suppose that, locally fppf on \(Y\), the torso \(K\) on \(X\) is trivial. This is the case if \(K\) is locally trivial for the sparse topology.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.3.21

Under hypotheses 6.3.20, there exists with unique isomorphism one and only one torsor \(\operatorname{Tr}_u(K)\) under \(G_Y\) on \(Y\), provided with a morphism \(\operatorname{Tr}_u\) or \(\operatorname{Tr}_u^K : u_*K \longrightarrow \operatorname{Tr}_u(K)\), who, for \(g\) local section (in the fppf sense) of \(G\) on \(Y\) and \(k\) local chapter of \(u_*K\), checks

\[\operatorname{Tr}_u^K(g\, k) = \operatorname{Tr}_u(g) \cdot \operatorname{Tr}_u^K(k) \, .\]

The problem is local to \(Y\); we can therefore assume \(K\) trivial: \(K = G_X\). A solution to the problem is to take \(K = G_Y\) and for morphism trace the morphism (6.3.13.2). We immediately check that it’s the only one.
The trace functor 6.3.20 is defined in particular when \(G\) satisfies the following condition
(6.3.21.1) For all \(S\)-schema \(X\), all torso under \(G_X\) on \(X\) is locally trivial for the sparse topology.
This condition is verified for \(G\) a smooth group, for \(G\) reciprocal image of a beam on the small flat site of \(S\), or for \(G\) defined by a quasi-coherent beam on \(S\).
We have by construction
\[(6.3.21.2) \qquad \operatorname{Tr}_u(K) = u_*(K) \times_{u_* G_X} G_Y \, .\]

6.3.22. To define a trace functor under more general hypotheses, the key point is, for \(f : X \longrightarrow T\) a flat quasi-projective morphism of finite presentation of \(S\)-schemas, to define a reasonable functor \(K \longmapsto K_n\) of the \(G_X\)-torsors on \(X\) in the \(G_Z\)-torsors on \(Z = \operatorname{Sym}^n_T(X)\).
We first have, \(f_n\) designating the projection of \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_T(X)\) on \(T\), the following lemma, which is proven as 6.3.21:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.3.22.1

With the previous notations and if, locally on \(T\), the torso \(K\) is trivial, then there exists, up to a unique isomorphism, one and only one torsor \(K_n\) on \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_T(X)\), provided with a morphism \(\alpha : f_*K \longrightarrow f_{n*}(K_n)\), who for \(g\) local section (fppf) of \(f_* G_X\) And \(k\) local section of \(u_*K\) check

\[\alpha(g\, k) = g_n \circ \alpha(k)\]

(rating of 6.3.13 For \(g_n\) ; the definition of \(g_n\) here uses flatness assumption etc.).
The general case will be treated by a slightly cannulated descent. First of all:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.3.23

Let be a commutative diagram

\[(6.3.23.1) \qquad \xymatrix@C=2.4em@R=2em{ X \ar[rr]^{u} \ar[dr] & & Y \ar[dl] \\ & T & }\]

of \(T\)-flat diagrams of finite presentation and quasi-projective, with \(u\) surjective spread. Then, the semi-simplicial system of schemas \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_T((X/Y)^{k+1})\) is a hypercovering for the sparse topology of the schema \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_T(Y)\).
Proof. a) We will have to use the auxiliary result:

6.3.23.2. Let \((X,Y,T)\) as above, \(t \in T\), \(Z\) a \(T\)-sum schema of a finite number of \(T\)-schemes \(\operatorname{Spec}(k_i)\), for \(k_i\) finite extension of \(k_t\), and a commutative diagram of \(T\)-schemes
\[(6.3.23.3) \qquad \xymatrix@C=2em@R=2em{ & Z \ar[dl]_{x} \ar[dr]^{y} & \\ X_t \ar[rr]_{u} & & Y_t } \, ,\]

such that \(x\) and \(y\) are closed immersions. Let \(z \in \operatorname{Sym}^n_T(Z)\), and be \(x(Z)\) and \(y(Z)\) its images in \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_T(X)\) and \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_T(Y)\). Then, \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_T(u)\) is spread at \(x(Z)\).

The formation of the schema \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_T(X)\) is compatible with any base change \(S' \longrightarrow S\) (5.5.2.7). By reduction to the Noetherian case, we deduce that \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_T(X)\) is of finite presentation on \(S\); it is otherwise flat on \(S\) (5.5.2.4). Thanks to the flatness criterion fiber by fiber, we return to prove (6.3.23.2) to the case of \(S\) spectrum of a body. The completion of \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_T(X)\) in \(Z\) then has for affine algebra the projective limit of the affine algebras located in \(x(Z)\) of the schemes \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_T(Z_k)\), for \(Z_k\) infinitesimal neighborhood of \(Z\) in \(X\). By hypothesis, \(u\) induces isomorphisms between the infinitesimal neighborhoods of \(Z\) in \(X\) and in \(Y\). The completion of \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_T(u)\) in \(x(z)\) and \(y(z)\) is therefore an isomorphism, and this proves (6.3.23.2).

We deduce from 6.3.23.2 that the sum schema, for \(z \in \operatorname{Sym}^n_T(Z)\), of the henselized in \(y(z)\) of \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_T(Y)\) only depends on \(Z\) and the \(T\)-schema \(X_Z^h\) sum of the henselized of \(X\) at the various points of \(Z\). We will designate this diagram by the notation \(\operatorname{Sym}^{h,n}_T(X_Z^h)\); it is functorial in \(X_Z^h\).

b) By passing to the limit, to prove 6.3.23, it suffices to show that for \(y \in \operatorname{Sym}^n_T(Y)\), closed in its fiber, the reciprocal image on the henselized of \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_T(Y)\) in \(y\) of the simplicial diagram \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_T((X/Y)^{k+1})\) is a hypercovering for the eta topology. This problem is local on \(T\) for the sparse topology; This reduces us to the case where there exists a diagram (6.3.23.3) with \(y = x(Z)\) (by killing the residual extensions with a separable extension of \(k_t\)). The semi-simplicial system considered is then deduced from the system
\[\operatorname{Sym}^{h,n}_T((X^h_{u^{-1}(Z)}/Y^h_Z)^{k+1}), \quad \text{augmenté vers} \quad \operatorname{Sym}^{h,n}_T(Y^h_Z)\]

by change of base, so it suffices to consider the latter. The semi-simplicial system of \((X^h_{u^{-1}(Z)}/Y^h_Z)^{k+1}\) is semi-simplicially homotopic to the constant system \(Y^h_Z\), because \(X^h_{u^{-1}(Z)}/Y^h_Z\) admits a section. The system \(\operatorname{Sym}^{h,n}_T((X^h_{u^{-1}(Z)}/Y^h_Z)^{h+1})\) is therefore equally homotopic to \(\operatorname{Sym}^{h,n}_T(Y^h_Z)\), and in particular is a hyperoverlap of it. This proves 6.3.23.
6.3.24. Let \(f : Y \longrightarrow T\) be a flat quasi-projective morphism of finite presentation of \(S\)-schemas, and \(K\) be a \(G_Y\)-torsor on \(Y\). Consider the following problem:

6.3.24.0. Define, for any morphism \(u : X \longrightarrow Y\), with \(X\) flat quasi-projective of finite presentation on \(T\), a torsor \((u^*K)_n\) on \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_T(X)\), and for all \(v : X' \longrightarrow X\) a isomorphism \(c_v : (v^*u^*K)_n \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \operatorname{Sym}^n_T(v)^*K_n\), so that
(i) isomorphisms \(c_v\) are compatible with composition;
(ii) for \(u^*K\) locally trivial on \(T\), we find the torso of 6.3.22.1, and its variance.

According to 6.3.23, the question of existence and uniqueness of a solution to 6.3.24.0, for any torso deduced from \(K\) by a reciprocal image of the type considered in 6.3.24.0, is a local question for the topology spread over \(Y\). In particular, if \(G\) satisfies (6.3.21.1), problem 6.3.24.0 admits one and only one solution.
Now suppose that \(G\) checks:
(6.3.24.1) For any integer \(n\) and for any diagram of \(S\)-affine diagrams
\[\xymatrix@C=2em@R=2em{ X \ar[rr]^{u} \ar[dr]_{f} & & Y \ar[dl]^{g} \\ & T & }\]

with \(f\) and \(g\) finite presentation dishes and \(u\) finite locally free surjective, the truncated semi-simplicial schema \((\operatorname{Sym}^n_T((X/Y)^{k+1}))_{0 \leq k \leq 2}\), augmented towards \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_T(Y)\), is of descent (GIRAUD [1]) for the category of \(G\)-tortors.

6.3.24.1 bis. We deduce in a standard way from 6.3.23 that the condition (6.3.24.1) implies the analogous condition, where we assume only \(f\) and \(g\) quasi-projective finite presentation flats and \(u\) surjective flat. Locally for the flat topology, \(u\) indeed admits locally free finite quasi-sections and we conclude with 6.3.23 and GIRAUD [1] applied to a square diagram of descent data.

Given a \(G_Y\)-torsor \(K\) on a \(T\)-schema \(Y\) flat quasi-projective of finite presentation, there exists by definition a faithfully flat morphism of finite presentation \(u : X \longrightarrow Y\) such that \(u^*K\) is trivial. If \(u_i\) designates the projection of \((X/Y)^{i+1}\) on \(Y\) (\(i \geq 0\)), the torsors \((u_i^*K)_n\) (6.3.22.1) on the diagrams \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_T((X/Y)^{i+1})\) form a descent data (\(i=0,1,2\)) on this semi-simplicial schema augmented to \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_T(Y)\). Suppose \(G\) satisfies the condition:
(6.3.24.2) The descent data constructed above are all effective (see 6.3.24.1).
Then, we will designate by \(K_n\) the torsor on \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_T(Y)\) (determined up to a unique isomorphism) defined by this descent data.
6.3.25. Suppose \(G\) satisfies (6.3.21.1) or conditions (6.3.24.1) (6.3.24.2). We leave it to the reader to make sense of the following form and to verify it:
6.3.25.1. Change of base. Consider a commutative square
\[\xymatrix@C=2.4em@R=2em{ X' \ar[r]^{g'} \ar[d]_{f'} & X \ar[d]^{f} \\ T' \ar[r]_{g} & T }\]
Let us denote by \(g'_n\) the arrow of \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_{T'}(X')\) in \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_T(X)\). For \(K\) torso under \(G\) on \(X\), we have
\[g'^*_n K_n \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} (g'^*K)_n \, .\]
Let now \(f : X \longrightarrow T\) and \(K\) be a \(G_X\)-torsor on \(X\).
6.3.25.2. Group change. Let \(r : G_1 \longrightarrow G_2\) be a morphism between groups both satisfying (6.3.21.1) or (6.3.24.1), (6.3.24.2). We have
\[r(K_n) \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} r(K)_n \, .\]
6.3.25.3. Additivity in \(K\): We have
\[(K' + K'')_n \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} K'_n + K''_n \, .\]
6.3.25.4. Additivity in \(n\). Let \(n_1\) and \(n_2\) be two integers, \(n = n_1+n_2\) and
\[d : \operatorname{Sym}^{n_1}_T(X) \times_T \operatorname{Sym}^{n_2}_T(X) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Sym}^n_T(X)\]
the canonical arrow; we then have
\[d^*K_n \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \operatorname{pr}_1^* K_{n_1} + \operatorname{pr}_2^* K_{n_2} \, .\]

6.3.25.5. Multiplicativity in \(n\). Let \(n\) and \(m\) be two integers and \(\alpha\) be the canonical arrow of \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_T(\operatorname{Sym}^m_T(X))\) in \(\operatorname{Sym}^{nm}_T(X)\) (see 6.3.3). We have
\[\alpha^*(K_{nm}) \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} (K_m)_n \, .\]

6.3.26. Suppose \(G\) satisfies (6.3.21.1) or (6.3.24.1), (6.3.24.2). If \(f : X \longrightarrow T\) is a locally free finite morphism of rank \(n\) between \(S\)-schemas, if \(t\) is the section (6.3.4.2) of \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_T(X)\), and if \(K\) is a \(G_X\)-torsor on \(X\), we define a \(G_T\)-torsor \(\operatorname{Tr}_f(K)\) on \(T\) by the formula
\[(6.3.26.1) \qquad \operatorname{Tr}_f(K) = t^*(K_n) \, .\]

If \(f\) is finite locally free, we define \(\operatorname{Tr}_f(K)\), locally on \(T\), by the same formula. This definition, via a canonical and functorial isomorphism, is equivalent to definition 6.3.21 in their common domain of validity (definition 6.3.21 providing a solution to the descent problem posed). We immediately deduce from (6.3.25.1), (6.3.25.2) and (6.3.25.3) that the functor \(\operatorname{Tr}_f\) is compatible with changes of base \(T' \longrightarrow T\), compatible with changes of group \(r : G_1 \longrightarrow G_2\), compatible with the addition of torsors.
The commutativity of the outer edge of the diagram (6.3.15.2), and (6.3.25.1) (6.3.25.4) provide compatibility to the composition of morphisms:
\[\operatorname{Tr}_{fg}(K) \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \operatorname{Tr}_f(\operatorname{Tr}_g(K)) \, .\]
6.3.27. In the particular case of relative curves, we also have:

blocks.title: Construction
blocks.html:
Build 6.3.27.1

If \(f : X \longrightarrow S\) is a smooth, quasi-projective curve on \(S\), \(D_1\) and \(D_2\) two finite relative divisors on \(S\) and \(K\) a \(G\)-torsor on \(X\), we have, setting \(\operatorname{Tr}_{D/S}(K) = \operatorname{Tr}_{D/S}(K|D)\), a canonical isomorphism

\[\operatorname{Tr}_{D_1+D_2}(K) \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \operatorname{Tr}_{D_1}(K) + \operatorname{Tr}_{D_2}(K) \, .\]
This isomorphism is immediately deduced from the isomorphism (6.3.25.4) and 6.3.9.1. Finally the arguments of 6.3.19 still provide:

blocks.title: Construction
blocks.html:
Build 6.3.27.2

Let be a commutative diagram

\[\xymatrix@C=2em@R=2em{ X \ar[rr]^{u} \ar[dr]_{p} & & Y \ar[dl]^{q} \\ & S & }\]

in which \(X\) and \(Y\) are quasi-projective smooth curves on \(S\) and \(u\) a quasi-finite morphism.

(i) If \(D\) is a divisor over \(X\) finitely over \(S\) and \(K\) a torsor under \(G\) over \(Y\), we have (see note p.192):

\[\operatorname{Tr}_{D/S}(u^*K) \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \operatorname{Tr}_{(u_* D)/S}(K) \, .\]

(ii) If \(E\) is a divisor over \(Y\) finitely over \(S\), \(K\) a torsor under \(G\) over \(X\) and if \(u\) is finished, we have

\[\operatorname{Tr}_{E/S}(\operatorname{Tr}_{X/Y}(K)) \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} \operatorname{Tr}_{u^*E/S}(K) \, .\]

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.3.28

Let \(A\) and \(B\) two \(R\)-algebras (commutative) and \(u : A \longrightarrow B\) one homomorphism which makes of \(B\) a \(A\)-locally free module of finite and faithful type. Then the semi-simplicial complex of algebras \((\mathrm{TS}^n_R(\otimes_A^{k+1} B))_{k \geq 0}\) is, as \(\mathrm{TS}^n_R(A)\)-semi-simplicial differential module, a split resolution of \(\mathrm{TS}^n_R(A)\).

Proof. The complex of modules associated with the semi-simplicial module \(\mathrm{TS}^n_R(\otimes_A^{k+1} B)\) only depends on the \(A\)-module \(B\), provided with a homomorphism of modules \(u : A \longrightarrow B\). As \(A\)-module, \(B\) can be written \(B = A \oplus L\), and this makes it possible to explain a homotopy operator.
Remember that:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.3.29

Let \(A\) a ring, \(B^*\) a \(A\)-co-semi-simplicial algebra, and \(c : A \longrightarrow B^*\) the structural morphism. For the reciprocal image functor of modules (= extension of scalars) \(\varepsilon^*\) to be faithful (resp. fully faithful) it is sufficient that the sequence of \(A\)-modules

\[0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow B^0\]

(resp. \(0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow B^0 \xrightarrow{j_0 - j_1} B^1\))

is exact, and the rest after tensorization by any \(A\)-module.
The demonstration is trivial and left to the reader.
We deduce in a standard way from (6.3.28) (6.3.29) the

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.3.30

The condition (6.3.24.1) is satisfied by any affine group \(G\).
(Use that a torsor under \(G\) is representable by an affine diagram on \(T\).)

It should not be difficult to extend 6.3.30 to any group \(G\) representable by an algebraic space(∗). I conjecture that the condition (6.3.24.2) is satisfied by any group representable by an algebraic space, and flat. We have the hoax statement:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 6.3.31

Given an exact sequence on \(S\)

\[0 \longrightarrow G \xrightarrow{\;r\;} G' \xrightarrow{\;u\;} H \longrightarrow 0 \, .\]

Suppose that \(G'\) and \(H\), therefore \(G\) verifies (6.3.13.1), that \(G'\) verifies (6.3.21.1) and that \(G\) verifies (6.3.24.1). Then, \(G\) check (6.3.24.2).
This applies for example to an affine kernel group of an epimorphism of representable and smooth groups.

Proof of 6.3.31. Let \(K\) be a \(G\)-torsor on \(X\). By 6.3.23, the question is local to \(X\) for the sparse topology; it is therefore sufficient to treat the case where \(r(K)\) is trivial. \(G\)-torsors \(K\) such that \(r(K)\) is trivial are identified with \(G'\)-torsors \(K'\) with an isomorphism \(k : H \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} u(K')\). Let's set \(Z = \operatorname{Sym}^n_T(X)\).
