The trivial torsor morphism \(k\) induces \(k_n : H_Z \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} u(K'_n)\), and the corresponding \(G\)-torsor on \(\operatorname{Sym}^n(X)\) represents the descent data considered.

footnotes.html: (∗) M. ARTIN, Algebraization of formal moduli I. We will only have to use the previous construction when \(\operatorname{Sym}^n_S(X)\) exists as a schema, which is the case for \(X\) quasi-projective on \(S\).

(∗) The morphism \(u_*\) is defined because we limit ourselves to finite divisors on \(S\). For example, we take for definition \(u_*(D) = \delta(u_* \mathcal{O}_D)\), formula which coincides with EGA IV 21.5.5 for \(u\) finished.
(∗) Algebraic Spaces by M. ARTIN. Yale University, Whittemore Lectures, May 1969.

blocks.html: 7. Appendix (by B. Saint-Donat)
7.0. Foreplay.
In this appendix we will use the results of \(\mathrm{VI_B}\), whose notations we keep.

7.0.1. Let \(\mathrm{Sch}_!\) be the category whose objects are quasi-compact and quasi-separated schemas, the morphisms being the morphisms of separate schemas of finite type: we designate by \(\mathscr{E}\) the bifibered category above \(\mathrm{Sch}_!\), with fiber categories of categories opposed to topos, defined by the spread beams on the diagrams \(\mathrm{VI_B}\) (4.3.0). We fix once and for all an object \(R\) of \(\mathrm{Sch}_!\) and we designate by \(\mathscr{E}_R\) the bifibred category above \(\mathrm{Sch}_{!R}\) deduced from \(\mathscr{E}\). Finally, let \(\mathscr{A}\) be a ring of the topos \(\mathscr{E}^{\circ}_R\) which satisfies the following conditions:

1. (i) \(\mathscr{A}\) is a CoCartesian section of \(\mathscr{E}^{\circ}_R\) above \(\mathrm{Sch}_{!R}^{\circ}\).
  2. (ii) For any scheme \(X\) of \(\mathrm{Sch}_{!R}\), \(\mathscr{A}_X\) is a torsion beam.
That said, we will work with the category \(\underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\mathscr{E}^{\circ}_R, \mathscr{A})\) (\(\mathrm{VI_B}\) 1.3.5).

We propose to define, for any morphism \(f : X \longrightarrow Y\) of \(\mathrm{Sch}_{!R}\), a functor \(Rf_! : D(X, \mathscr{A}_X) \longrightarrow D(Y, \mathscr{A}_Y)\), coinciding with the functor previously defined when \(f\) is compactifiable, and making categories \(D(X, \mathscr{A}_X)\) for \(X\) variable a category cofibered above \(\mathrm{Sch}_{!R}\).

7.0.2. Although all the results of this appendix are stated in the framework explained in 7.0.1, we could transcribe them with obvious modifications in the following framework: we take as the section of \(\Gamma(\mathscr{E}^{\circ}_R)\) the constant section of value the constant beam \(\mathbb{Z}\) and we limit ourselves for any diagram \(X\) to the derived category \(D^{*}_{\mathrm{tors}}(X)\) defined by the complexes of sheaves of abelian groups whose cohomology sheaves are torsion (\(\mathrm{VI_B}\) 4.3.1).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.0.3

Let be a commutative diagram of schemes

\[(7.0.3.1) \qquad \xymatrix@C=3em@R=2.5em{ X' \ar[r]^{j'} \ar[d]_{f'} & X \ar[d]^{f} \\ Y' \ar[r]_{j} & Y }\]

where \(f\), \(f'\) are clean and \(j\), \(j'\) are immersions. So for any complex \(K^{\bullet}\) of \(D^{+}_{\mathrm{tors}}(X')\), the canonical arrow

\[j_! \circ R^+f'(K^{\bullet}) \longrightarrow R^+f \circ j'_!(K^{\bullet})\]

is an isomorphism.
By virtue of the "lemma on way-out functors" (R.D. I (7.1)), it suffices to show that for any integer \(i\) and for any sheaf \(F\) of torsion, the arrow
\[(7.0.3.2) \qquad j_! \circ R^i f'(F) \longrightarrow R^i f \circ j'_!(F)\]
is an isomorphism.

By taking the closed images of \(j\) and \(j'\) we are brought back to the case where \(j\) and \(j'\) are open immersions whose images are everywhere dense: the cleanliness of \(f\) and \(f'\) then leads to the diagram (7.0.3.1) is Cartesian. Under these conditions, the change of base theorem for a proper morphism, in the form XII 5.2, shows that, for any geometric point \(\xi\) of \(Y\), the arrow \((j_! \circ R^i f'(F))_{\xi} \longrightarrow (R^i f \circ j'_!(F))_{\xi}\) is an isomorphism.

Construction 7.0.4. Let \(D\) be a category, \(X\) and \(Y\) two \(D\)-objects of \(\mathrm{Sch}_{!R}\) such that for any arrow \(\alpha \longrightarrow \beta\) of \(D\), the morphisms \(X_{\beta} \longrightarrow X_{\alpha}\) and \(Y_{\beta} \longrightarrow Y_{\alpha}\) are clean. Let \(j : X \longrightarrow Y\) be a morphism such that for any object \(\alpha\) of \(D\), \(j_{\alpha} : X_{\alpha} \longrightarrow Y_{\alpha}\) or an immersion: the isomorphism (7.0.3.2) for \(i = 0\) verifies the usual compatibility conditions and makes it possible to construct a functor correct:
\[j_! : \underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\underline{\Gamma}(\bar{X}), \mathscr{A}) \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Mod}}(\underline{\Gamma}(\bar{Y}), \mathscr{A})\]
(see \(\mathrm{VI_B}\) 1.2.5 for notations).
which induces a triangulated functor, also denoted \(j_!\):
\[j_! : D(\underline{\Gamma}(\bar{X}), \mathscr{A}) \longrightarrow D(\underline{\Gamma}(\bar{Y}), \mathscr{A})\]
Then, for any object \(\alpha\) of \(D\), we have a commutative diagram, where \(j_{\alpha!}\) designates the usual "extension by \(0\)" functor:

\[\xymatrix@C=3em@R=2.5em{ D(\underline{\Gamma}(\bar{X}), \mathscr{A}) \ar[r]^{j_!} \ar[d]_{R\, e_{\alpha}^+} & D(\underline{\Gamma}(\bar{Y}), \mathscr{A}) \ar[d]^{R\, e_{\alpha}^+} \\ D(X_{\alpha}, \mathscr{A}_{X_{\alpha}}) \ar[r]_{j_{\alpha!}} & D(Y_{\alpha}, \mathscr{A}_{Y_{\alpha}}) }\]
Construction 7.0.5. Consider a commutative diagram in \(\mathrm{Sch}_{!R}\)
\[(7.0.5.1) \qquad \xymatrix@C=3em@R=2.5em{ X' \ar[r]^{i'} \ar[d]_{f'} & X \ar[d]^{f} \\ Y' \ar[r]_{i} & Y }\]
where \(i\), \(i'\) are immersions, \(f\) a proper morphism and \(f'\) a surjective proper morphism. It defines a commutative diagram of simplicial objects of \(\mathrm{Sch}_{!R}\)
\[(7.0.5.2) \qquad \xymatrix@C=3em@R=2.5em{ [X' \mid Y'] \ar[r]^{j} \ar[d]_{u_{f'}} & [X \mid Y] \ar[d]^{u_f} \\ C^{\Delta}_{Y'} \ar[r]_{i} & C^{\Delta}_Y }\]
where \(j_n : [X' \mid Y']_n \longrightarrow [X \mid Y]_n\) is an immersion for all \(n\). With these notations, we have a canonical isomorphism
\[i_! \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} R^+ \bar{u}_{f+} \circ j_! \circ L^+ \bar{u}_{f'}^{+} \, .\]

By taking the closed images of \(i\) and \(i'\) we can assume that \(i\) and \(i'\) are open immersions whose images are dense everywhere. The diagram (7.0.5.1) is then Cartesian and \(f\) is surjective. Moreover, for any integer \(n\), \(j_n\) is an open immersion and the diagram
\[\xymatrix@C=3em@R=2.5em{ [X' \mid Y']_n \ar[r]^{j_n} \ar[d] & [X \mid Y]_n \ar[d] \\ Y' \ar[r]_{i} & Y }\]
is Cartesian.

From then on \(L^+ \bar{u}_f^{+} \circ i_!\) is isomorphic to \(j_! \circ L^+ \bar{u}_{f'}^{+}\): using the fact that \(u_f\) is an increase of cohomological descent (\(\mathrm{VI_B}\) (4.3.2)), we find a sequence of isomorphisms
\[i_! \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} R^+ \bar{u}_{f+} \circ L^+(\bar{u}_f^{+}) \circ i_! \xrightarrow{\;\sim\;} R^+ \bar{u}_{f+} \circ j_! \circ L^+ \bar{u}_{f'}^{+}\]
hence the desired isomorphism.
Finally, we will use the following variant of Chow's lemma, the proof of which will be found in EGA II 2nd edition, and which is an improvement of (XII 7.1):

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.0.6

Let \(S\) a quasi-compact and quasi-separate scheme and \(f : X \longrightarrow S\) a separate morphism of finite type. Then there exists a commutative diagram

\[\xymatrix@C=3em@R=2.5em{ X' \ar[r]^{p} \ar[d]_{f'} & X \ar[dl]^{f} \\ S & }\]

checking the following conditions:

  1. (i) \(p\) is proper and surjective
  2. (ii) there exists a dense open \(U\) of \(X\) such that \(p\) induces an isomorphism of \(p^{-1}(U)\) on \(U\)
  3. (iii) \(f'\) is quasi-projective.
7.1. Definition of \(R^+f_!\)

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 7.1.1

Let \(f : X \longrightarrow S\) be a schema morphism. A pseudo-compactification of \(f\) consists of the data of a commutative diagram

\[(7.1.1.0) \qquad \xymatrix@C=3em@R=2.5em{ X_1 \ar[r]^{j_1} \ar[d]_{p_1} & T_1 \ar[ldd]^{f_1} \\ X \ar[d]_{f} & \\ S & }\]

where \(p_1\) is proper and surjective, \(j_1\) is an immersion and \(f_1\) is proper.

7.1.2. Let \(f : X \longrightarrow S\) be a morphism of \(\mathrm{Sch}_{!R}\): according to Chow's lemma (7.0.6), \(f\) has a pseudo-compactification \((p_1, j_1, f_1)\). The diagram (7.1.1.0) defines a morphism
\[[X_1 \mid X] \xrightarrow{\;j_1\;} [T_1 \mid S]\]

noted again \(j_1\), such that \(j_{1n}\) is an immersion for all \(n\). We then set \(R^{+(1)}f_! = L^+ \bar{u}_{p_1} \cdot j_{1!} \circ R^+ \bar{u}_{f_1}\) so that we obtain a triangulated functor of \(D^{+}(X, \mathscr{A}_X)\) in \(D^{+}(S, \mathscr{A}_S)\).

Construction 7.1.3. For any couple \(((p_1, j_1, f_1), (p_2, j_2, f_2))\) pseudo-compactifications of \(f\), we have an isomorphism \(\alpha_{12} : R^{+(1)}f_! \longrightarrow R^{+(2)}f_!\) such as for any other pseudo-compactification \((p_3, j_3, f_3)\), we have \(\alpha_{23} \circ \alpha_{12} = \alpha_{13}\).
a) We define a double simplicial system \([[X_1, X_2 \mid X]]\) by setting
\[[[X_1, X_2 \mid X]]_{[n] \times [m]} = [X_1 \mid X]_n \times [X_2 \mid X]_m\]
In the same way we pose

the morphisms \([[X_1 \mid X]]_{[n] \times [m]} \longrightarrow [[X_1 \mid X]]_{[n] \times [m']}\) induced by the morphisms \([m'] \longrightarrow [m]\) being all equal to the identity. We define the double simplicial systems \([[T_1, T_2 \mid S]]\) and \([[T_1 \mid S]]\) in the same way, so that we have a commutative diagram

\[\xymatrix@C=1.6em@R=2.2em{ [[X_1, X_2 \mid X]] \ar[rr]^{j_{12}} \ar[dr] \ar[ddd]_{u_{p_1 p_2}} & & [[T_1, T_2 \mid S]] \ar[dr]^{j} \ar[ddd]|(0.62){u_{f_1 f_2}} & \\ & [[X_1 \mid X]] \ar[rr]^(0.35){j} \ar[ddl]|{u_{p_1}} & & [[T_1 \mid S]] \ar[ddl]^{u_{f_1}} \\ \\ C^{\Delta \times \Delta}_X \ar[rr] & & C^{\Delta \times \Delta}_S & }\]
where \(j_{12}\) is degree-by-degree immersion. The calculation process exposed in \(\mathrm{VI_B}\) (2.3) makes it possible to construct a morphism
which is obtained from a morphism of triple complexes of sheaves on \(S\).
b) \(\alpha_1\) is an isomorphism:

To demonstrate this, we can fix the first index \(n\) coming from \([[T_1, T_2 \mid S]]_{[n] \times [m]}\) in the previous triple complex morphism and look at the double complex morphism thus obtained. We verify that this morphism is none other than the isomorphism defined in Lemma 7.0.5 for the diagram
\[\xymatrix@C=3em@R=2.5em{ [X_1 \mid X]_n \times X_2 \ar[r] \ar[d] & [T_1 \mid S]_n \times T_2 \ar[d] \\ [X_1 \mid X]_n \ar[r] & [T_1 \mid S]_n }\]
c) We construct an isomorphism in the same way
\[R^{+(2)}f_! \xrightarrow{\;\alpha_2\;} R^+ \bar{u}_{f_1 f_2} \circ j_{12!} \circ L^+ \bar{u}_{p_1 p_2}^{+}\]
and we set \(\alpha_{12} = \alpha_2^{-1} \circ \alpha_1\).
To verify the relation \(\alpha_{23} \circ \alpha_{12} = \alpha_{13}\), we use a triple simplicial object: we leave it to the reader to verify that we obtain a commutative diagram:

Construction 7.1.4. Let \(Z \xrightarrow{\;g\;} Y\) and \(Y \xrightarrow{\;f\;} X\) be two morphisms of \(\mathrm{Sch}_{!R}\). For any triple \(((p_1, j_1, f_1), (p_2, j_2, g_2), (p_3, j_3, (fg)_3))\), where \((p_1, j_1, f_1)\) is a pseudo-compactification of \(f\), \((p_2, j_2, g_2)\) a pseudo-compactification of \(g\) and \((p_3, j_3, (fg)_3)\) a pseudo-compactification of \(fg\), we have an isomorphism
\[R^{+(3)}fg_! \xleftarrow{\;\sim\;} R^{+(2)}g_! \circ R^{+(1)}f_! \quad \text{compatible avec les isomorphismes } \alpha_{ij} \text{ définis précédemment.}\]
We will proceed in several steps:
7.1.4.1. Let \((p_1, j_1, f_1)\) (resp. \((p_2, j_2, f_2)\)) be a pseudo-compactification of \(f : Y \longrightarrow X\) (resp. of \(g : Z \longrightarrow Y\)):
The morphism \(Z' = Z \times_Y Y_1 \longrightarrow Y_1\) deduced from \(g\) by base change is provided with a pseudo-compactification:
\[(7.1.4.1.2) \qquad \xymatrix@C=2.6em@R=2.4em{ T_2' = T_2 \times_Y Y_1 \ar[r]^-{j_2'} \ar[drr]_{g_2'} & Z_2 \times_Y Y_1 = Z_2' \ar[d]^{p_2'} \\ & Z \times_Y Y_1 = Z' \ar[d]^{g'} \\ & Y_1 }\]
We now choose a compactification of \(j_1 \cdot g_2'\).
which defines a pseudo-compactification of the compound \(fg\): we will note \(R^{+(12)}fg_!\) the corresponding functor.
7.1.4.2. The previous data allows us to define an isomorphism \(R^{+(12)}fg_! \xleftarrow{\;\sim\;} R^{+(1)}f_! \cdot R^{+(2)}g_!\):
We have a commutative diagram, where all the arrows are deduced naturally from (7.1.4.1.1), (7.1.4.1.2) and (7.1.4.1.3).

\[(7.1.4.2.1) \qquad \xymatrix@C=2.2em@R=2.6em{ [T_{12} \mid X] \ar[d]_{g_2''} & [T_2' \mid Y] \ar[l]_{j_1'} \ar[d]^{g_2'} & [Z_2' \mid Z] \ar[l]_{j_2'} \ar[dl]^{1} \ar[r]^{r} & C^{\Delta}_Z \ar[d]^{g} \\ [T_1 \mid X] \ar[drr]_{u_{f_1}} & [Y_1 \mid Y] \ar[l]^{j_1} \ar[r]_{u_{p_1}} & C^{\Delta}_Y \ar[d]^{f} & \\ & & C^{\Delta}_X & }\]
We are going to define a natural arrow:
\[\beta : R^{+(1)}f_! \circ R^{+(2)}g_! \longrightarrow R^{+(12)}fg_!\]
which we will show is an isomorphism.
For this we will use two lemmas:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.1.4.2.2

Let us consider a commutative diagram:

\[\xymatrix@C=3em@R=2.4em{ X_1 \ar[r]^{j_1} \ar[d]_{p_1} & T_1 \ar[d]^{h'} \\ X \ar[r]|{j} \ar[d]_{f} & T \ar[dl]^{h} \\ S & }\]

where \(h\), \(h'\) are proper, \(p_1\) is proper and surjective and \(j\), \(j_1\) are the immersions. So the canonical arrow

\[R^+h \circ j_! \longrightarrow R^{+(1)}(hh')_!\]

is an isomorphism.
Indeed, according to (7.0.5) the arrow
\[j_! \longrightarrow R^+ \bar{u}_{h'} \circ j_{1!} \circ L^+ \bar{u}_{p_1}^{+}\]
is an isomorphism. Hence the result by applying \(R^+h\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.1.4.2.3

With diagram notations (7.1.4.2.1), canonical arrows

\[R^{+(1)}f_! \circ R^+ \bar{u}_{p_1} \longrightarrow R^+ \bar{u}_{f_1} \circ j_{1!}\]

\[R^{+(2)}g_! \circ R^+ \bar{r} \longrightarrow R^+ \bar{u}_{p_1} \circ R^+ g_2' \circ j_{2!}'\]

are isomorphisms.
We will only establish the first isomorphism, leaving the reader to deduce the second.
With obvious notations, we set \(C = [Y_1 \mid Y] \times_X X_1\) and \(\bar{C} = [T_1 \mid S] \times_S T_1\), so that we have a commutative diagram:
\[\xymatrix@C=2.2em@R=2.4em{ & \bar{C} \ar[r] & T_1 \\ C \ar[ur]^{i} \ar[r] \ar[d]_{q} & Y_1 \ar[ur]|{j} \ar[d]^{p_1} \ar[u]|(0.35){f_1} & \\ [Y_1 \mid Y] \ar[r]_{u_{p_1}} & Y & }\]
where \(i\) is an immersion, \(q\) is proper and surjective. We then construct a diagram
\[\xymatrix@C=3em@R=2.4em{ [[C \mid [Y_1 \mid Y]]] \ar[r]^-{i} \ar[d]_{u_q} & [[\bar{C} \mid C^{\Delta}_Y]] \ar[ddl]^{k} \\ \lvert X_1 \mid X \rvert \ar[d] & \\ Y & }\]

and it follows from (7.1.4.2.2) that the functor \(R^+ \bar{u}_{j_1} \circ j_1\) is identified with the functor \(R^+ \bar{k} \circ i_1 \circ L^+ \bar{u}_q\) and, thanks to (7.0.3) and the base change theorem this last functor is identified with \(R^{+(1)}f_! \circ R^+ \bar{u}_{p_1}\). Lemma (7.1.4.2.3) being established, we have a series of isomorphisms

\[\begin{aligned} R^{+(3)}fg_! &= R^+ \bar{u}_{f_1} \circ R^+ g_2'' \circ j_{1!} \circ j_{2!}' \circ L^+ \bar{r}^{+} \\ &\xleftarrow{\phantom{\sim}} R^+ \bar{u}_{f_1 +} \circ j_{1!} \circ R^+ g_2' \circ j_{2!}' \circ L^+ \bar{r}^{+} \\ &\xleftarrow{\;\sim\;} R^{+(1)}f_! \circ R^+ \bar{u}_{p_1 +} \circ R^+ g_{2+}' \circ j_{2!}' \circ L^+ \bar{r} \\ &\xleftarrow{\;\sim\;} R^{+(1)}f_! \circ R^{+(2)}g_! \circ R^+ \bar{r} \circ L^+ \bar{r}^{+} \\ &\xleftarrow{\;\sim\;} R^{+(1)}f_! \circ R^{+(2)}g_! \end{aligned}\]
whose compound gives \(\beta\).
7.1.4.3. If \((p_3, j_3, (fg)_3)\) is a pseudo-compactification of \(fg\), we define an isomorphism
by composing \(\alpha_{(12)3}\) with \(\beta\).

7.1.4.4. Let now be \((p_1', j_1', f_1')\) and \((p_2', j_2', g_2')\) two other pseudo-compactifications of \(f\) and \(g\) and a diagram (7.1.4.3); we verify that the following diagram is commutative:
considering a diagram of the type (7.1.4.4) where the simplicial objects are replaced by double simplicial objects.
We conclude that the following diagram is commutative:
for any pseudo-compactification \((p_3', j_3', (fg)_{3'})\) of \(fg\), which completes the demonstration of 7.1.4.

7.1.5. By choosing for any morphism \(f\) of \(\mathrm{Sch}_{!R}\) a pseudo-compactification, such that an identical morphism is associated with the trivial pseudo-compactification, we define for any morphism \(f : X \longrightarrow Y\) a triangulated functor
\[R^+f_! : D^{+}(X, \mathscr{A}_X) \longrightarrow D^{+}(Y, \mathscr{A}_Y)\]
and any pair \((f, g)\) of morphism, an isomorphism
\[R^+fg_! \xrightarrow[\;\sim\;]{c_{fg}} R^+f_! \circ R^+g_!\]
Also if \(f\) is an identity, \(R^+f_!\) is the identical functor and \(c_{fg}\) is the identity.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 7.1.6

The correspondence \(f \longrightarrow R^+f_!\) defines a normalized pseudo-functor of \(\mathrm{Sch}_{!R}\) in the 2-category of categories. In other words, the categories \(D^{+}(X, \mathscr{A}_X)\) for \(X\) object of \(\mathrm{Sch}_{!R}\) can be organized into a cofibered category on \(\mathrm{Sch}_{!R}\) by setting, for any morphism \(f : X \longrightarrow Y\) from \(\mathrm{Sch}_{!R}\):

\[\operatorname{Hom}_f(K, L) = \operatorname{Hom}(R^+f_! K, L)\]

with \(K \in D^{+}(X, \mathscr{A}_X)\) and \(L \in D^{+}(Y, \mathscr{A}_Y)\).

This involves seeing that the identification \(R^+fg_! \xrightarrow[\;\sim\;]{c_{fg}} Rf_! \circ Rg_!\) is compatible with triple compounds. Let therefore be \(h : T \longrightarrow Z\), \(g : Z \longrightarrow Y\) and \(f : Y \longrightarrow X\) three morphisms of \(\mathrm{Sch}_{!R}\): by virtue of the above, it suffices, to verify this compatibility, to verify it after an arbitrary choice of pseudo-compactifications for \(h\), \(g\) and \(f\).
We have a diagram:

\[(7.1.6.1) \qquad \xymatrix@C=1.8em@R=2.2em{ T_5 & T_4 \ar[l] \ar[dr] & T_3 \ar[l] & T' \ar[l] \ar[r]^{p_3} \ar[d] & T \ar[d]^{h} \\ & T_3 \ar[dr] & T_2 \ar[l] \ar[dr] & Z' \ar[l] \ar[r]^{p_2} \ar[d] & Z \ar[d]^{g} \\ & & T_1 & Y_1 \ar[l] \ar[r]_{p_1} \ar[d] & Y \ar[d]^{f} \\ & & & & X }\]
where all oblique arrows are proper and where \(p_1\), \(p_2\) and \(p_3\) are proper and surjective.
A diagram of type (7.1.4.4) built on (7.1.6.1) then allows us to conclude.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 7.1.7

If \(f : X \longrightarrow S\) is a compactifiable morphism, the functor \(Rf_!\) coincides with that defined in presentation XVII.
Indeed, there is a commutative diagram:
\[\xymatrix@C=3em@R=2.4em{ X_1 \ar[r]^{j_1} \ar[d]_{p_1} & T_1 \ar[d]^{h'} \\ X \ar[r]|{j} \ar[d]_{f} & T \ar[dl]^{h} \\ S & }\]
to which we apply (7.1.4.2.2).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 7.1.8

If \(f : X \longrightarrow Y\) is a morphism of \(\mathrm{Sch}_!\), the functor \(R^+f_!\) that we have just defined will be called the direct image functor with proper supports (in the sense of derived categories). The functor \(\mathscr{H}^q \circ R^+f_!\), noted \(R^q f_!\), will be called the \(q^{\text{ième}}\) direct image functor with proper supports.
With these notations, we leave it to the reader to verify the

Construction 7.1.9. Let \((\pi_1, j_1, f_1)\) be a pseudo-compactification of \(f : X \longrightarrow Y\) and let us denote by \(\pi_1^n\) the canonical morphism \([X_1 \mid X]_n \longrightarrow X\). Then, for any complex \(K^{\bullet}\) of \(D^{+}(X, \mathscr{A}_X)\), there exist two biregular spectral sequences:

\[(7.1.9.1) \qquad E_1^{pq} = R^q f_!(K^p) \Longrightarrow R^{p+q} f_!(K^{\bullet})\] \[(7.1.9.2) \qquad E_1^{pq} = R^q (f \circ \pi_1^p)_! (\pi_1^{p*}(K^{\bullet})) \Longrightarrow R^{p+q} f_!(K^{\bullet})\]
(Take for example the canonical flaccid resolution of \(j_{1!} \circ L^+ \bar{u}_{p_1}^{+}(K^{\bullet})\) (cf. XVII (4.2)), then its direct image by \(u_{f_1}\).)
7.2. Some properties of \(R^+f_!\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 7.2.1

Let \(f : X \longrightarrow Y\) be a morphism of \(\mathrm{Sch}_{!R}\) of relative dimension \(\leqslant d\). Then the functor \(R^+f_!\) is of cohomological dimension \(\leqslant 2d\).

This involves showing that for any bundle \(F\) of \(\mathscr{A}_X\)-modules on \(X\), we have \(R^i f_!(F) = 0\) for \(i > 2d\). (We then deduce, by a standard argument, that for any complex \(K^{\bullet}\) of \(D^{+}(X, \mathscr{A}_X)\) such that \(H^i(K^{\bullet}) = 0\) for \(i > k\), we have \(R^i f_!(K^{\bullet}) = 0\) for \(i > k + 2d\)).
Let \(\mathscr{U} = (U_i)_{i \in I}\) be a finite covering of \(X\) by open affines. We define (XVII 6.2.9) a left resolution \(K^{\bullet}_{\mathscr{U}} \longrightarrow F\) of \(F\) by setting:
\[K^n_{\mathscr{U}} = \bigoplus_{|P| = n+1} j_{P!}\, j_P^*(F)\]
where for each part \(P\) of \(I\), \(j_P\) is the inclusion of \(\bigcap_{i \in P} U_i\) in \(X\).
We deduce from (7.1.9.1) a spectral sequence
\[E_1^{pq} = \bigoplus_{\substack{|P| = p+1 \\ P \subset I}} R^q (fj_P)_!\, j_P^*(F) \Longrightarrow R^{p+q} f_!(F)\]

and the fact that \(fj_P\) is compactifiable, a relevant case for presentation XVII, shows that \(E_1^{pq} = 0\) for \(p > 0\) and \(q > 2d\); we then deduce that \(R^n f_!(F) = 0\) for \(n > 2d\), what had to be demonstrated.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 7.2.2

Let \(f : X \longrightarrow Y\) a morphism of \(\mathrm{Sch}_{!R}\) and \(K^{\bullet}\) an object of \(D^b(X, \mathscr{A}_X)\) of finite Tor-dimension. Then \(R^+f_!(K^{\bullet})\) is of finite Tor-dimension.

As before, let \(\mathscr{U} = (U_i)_{i \in I}\) be a finite covering of \(X\) by open affines. The functoriality of \(K^n_{\mathscr{U}}\) makes it possible to produce a left resolution bounded by \(K^{\bullet}\) by finite dimension Tor-complexes, because this last property is verified point by point (XVII (4.1.9)): we will note \(K^{\bullet\bullet}_{\mathscr{U}}\) the double complex thus obtained, the second index corresponding to that of \(K^{\bullet}\). With the conventions of (\(\mathrm{VI_B}\) (2.3.2)), we have for any integer \(n\) an exact sequence, where the operation \(\sigma_{\leqslant n}\) is taken in \(C^{+}(C^{+}(\operatorname{Mod}(X, \mathscr{A}_X)))\):

\[0 \longrightarrow (\sigma_{\leqslant n}(K^{\bullet\bullet}_{\mathscr{U}}))_s \longrightarrow (\sigma_{\leqslant n+1}(K^{\bullet\bullet}_{\mathscr{U}}))_s \longrightarrow (-1)^{n+1} K^{n+1, \bullet}_{\mathscr{U}} \longrightarrow 0\]
hence a distinguished triangle in \(D^{+}(X, \mathscr{A}_X)\):

\[\xymatrix@C=1.4em@R=2.4em{ & K^{n+1, \bullet}_{\mathscr{U}} \ar[dl] & \\ (\sigma_{\leqslant n}(K^{\bullet\bullet}_{\mathscr{U}}))_s \ar[rr] & & (\sigma_{\leqslant n+1}(K^{\bullet\bullet}_{\mathscr{U}}))_s \ar[ul] }\]

Using the fact that \(R^+f_!(K^{n+1, \bullet}_{\mathscr{U}})\) is finite Tor-dimensional for all \(n\) (XVII (5.2.10)), we deduce that \(R^+f_!((\sigma_{\leqslant 0}(K^{\bullet\bullet}_{\mathscr{U}}))_s) = R^+f_!(K^{\bullet})\) is finite Tor-dimensional.

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 7.2.3

The preceding argument does not allow us to assert that \(R^+f_!(K^{\bullet})\) is of Tor-dimension \(\leqslant k\) as soon as \(K^{\bullet}\) is of Tor-dimension \(\leqslant k\) (cf.(3.7)).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 7.2.4

Consider a Cartesian diagram in \(\mathrm{Sch}_{!R}\):

\[\xymatrix@C=3em@R=2.5em{ X' \ar[r]^{g'} \ar[d]_{f'} & X \ar[d]^{f} \\ S' \ar[r]_{g} & S }\]

Then for any complex \(K^{\bullet}\) of \(D^{+}(X, \mathscr{A}_X)\), the canonical morphism

\[g^* \circ R^+f_!(K^{\bullet}) \xrightarrow{\;ch_{K^{\bullet}}\;} R^+f_! \circ g'^*(K^{\bullet})\]

is an isomorphism.
Let \((\pi_1, j_1, f_1)\) be a pseudo-compactification of \(f\): we deduce by change of base a pseudo-compactification \((\pi_1', j_1', f_1')\) of \(f'\) and a commutative diagram:

\[\xymatrix@C=1.8em@R=1.8em{ X_1' \ar[rr]^{j_1'} \ar[dr] \ar[dd]_{\pi_1'} & & T_1' \ar[dr] \ar[dd]|(0.7){f_1'} & \\ & X_1 \ar[rr]^(0.35){j_1} \ar[dd]|(0.3){\pi_1} & & T_1 \ar[dd]^{f_1} \\ X' \ar[rr]^(0.65){g'} \ar[dd]_{f'} & & X \ar[dd]^(0.3){f} & \\ & S' \ar[rr]_{g} & & S \\ & & & }\]
which allows you to define the arrow \(ch_K\).
We then have, according to (7.1.9.2), a morphism of biregular spectral sequences:

\[\xymatrix@C=2em@R=2.8em{ g^* \circ R^q(f \circ \pi_1^p)_!\, (\pi_1^{p*}(K^{\bullet})) \ar[r] \ar[d]_{u_1^{pq}} & g^* \circ R^{p+q} f_!(K^{\bullet}) \ar[d]^{ch_K} \\ R^q(f' \circ \pi_1'^p)_!\, (\pi_1'^{p*}(g'^*(K^{\bullet}))) \ar[r] & R^+f_! \circ g'^*(K) }\]
and \(ch_K\) is an isomorphism since it is the same for the \(u_1^{pq}\) according to (XVII (5.2.6)).
7.3. Definition of \(Rf_!\)
We now propose to "reasonably" extend the functor \(R^+f_!\) into a functor \(Rf_! : D(X, \mathscr{A}_X) \longrightarrow D(Y, \mathscr{A}_Y)\), for any morphism \(f\) of \(\mathrm{Sch}_{!R}\).

7.3.1. Let \((p_1, j_1, f_1)\) be a pseudo-compactification of \(f\) and \(F\) be a \(\mathscr{A}_X\)-module: taking the canonical flaccid resolution of \(j_{1!} \circ L^+ \bar{u}_{p_1}^{+}(F)\) (cf. XVII (4.2)), then its direct image by \(f_1\), then the simple complex associated with the double complex thus constructed, we obtain a complex of \(\mathscr{A}_Y\)-modules:
\[(7.3.1.1) \qquad 0 \longrightarrow \mathscr{X}^0(F) \xrightarrow{\;S^0(F)\;} \mathscr{X}^1(F) \; \cdots \; \mathscr{X}^n(F) \xrightarrow{\;S^n(F)\;} \mathscr{X}^{n+1}(F) \; \cdots\]
functional in \(F\) and isomorphic in \(D^{+}(Y, \mathscr{A}_Y)\) to \(R^+f_!(F)\).
From 7.2.1 \(H^i(\mathscr{X}^{\bullet}(F)) = 0\) for \(i > 2d\), where \(d\) is an integer such that the relative dimension of \(f\) is \(\leqslant d\), so that the complex
\[(7.3.1.2) \qquad 0 \longrightarrow \mathscr{X}^0(F) \longrightarrow \mathscr{X}^1(F) \longrightarrow \cdots \mathscr{X}^{2d-1}(F) \longrightarrow \operatorname{Ker} S^{2d}(F) \longrightarrow 0\]
is canonically isomorphic in \(D^{+}(Y, \mathscr{A}_Y)\) to the complex (7.3.1.1). Additionally:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.3.2

Functors \(\mathscr{X}^0\), \(\mathscr{X}^1\), \(\ldots\), \(\mathscr{X}^{2d-1}\), \(\operatorname{Ker} S^{2d}\) are accurate in \(F\).

The exactness of the functors \(\mathscr{X}^n\) results from the exactness of the canonical flaccid resolution and from the fact that the direct image by a morphism of a short exact sequence of flaccid sheaves is still a short exact sequence.

To see the accuracy of \(\operatorname{Ker} S^{2d}\), we consider, for a short exact sequence \(0 \longrightarrow F' \longrightarrow F \longrightarrow F'' \longrightarrow 0\) of beams on \(X\), the diagram

\[\xymatrix@C=2.6em@R=2em{ \operatorname{Ker} S^{2d}(F') \ar[r] \ar[d] & \mathscr{X}^{2d+1}(F') \ar[r] \ar[d] & \mathscr{X}^{2d+2}(F') \ar[r] \ar[d] & \cdots \\ \operatorname{Ker} S^{2d}(F) \ar[r] \ar[d] & \mathscr{X}^{2d+1}(F) \ar[r] \ar[d] & \mathscr{X}^{2d+2}(F) \ar[r] \ar[d] & \cdots \\ \operatorname{Ker} S^{2d}(F'') \ar[r] & \mathscr{X}^{2d+1}(F'') \ar[r] & \mathscr{X}^{2d+2}(F'') \ar[r] & \cdots }\]
and the exact sequence of homology associated with the exact sequence

\[0 \longrightarrow \sigma_{\geqslant 2d+1}(\mathscr{X}^{\bullet}(F')) \longrightarrow \sigma_{\geqslant 2d+1}(\mathscr{X}^{\bullet}(F)) \longrightarrow \sigma_{\geqslant 2d+1}(\mathscr{X}^{\bullet}(F'')) \longrightarrow 0\]
let us conclude.
We now have the following general lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.3.3

Let \(A\) and \(B\) two abelian categories and \(Je^{\bullet}\) an object of \(C^b(\operatorname{Fex}(A, B))\) where \(\operatorname{Fex}(A, B)\) designates the category of exact functors of \(A\) in \(B\). Then, by associating with any complex \(K^{\bullet}\) of \(C(A)\) the simple complex associated with the double complex \(Je^{\bullet}(K^{\bullet})\) we obtain a triangulated functor \(K(A) \longrightarrow K(B)\) which preserves the quasi-isomorphisms.
We write the functor in question as a compound
\[C(A) \longrightarrow C^b(C(B)) \xrightarrow{\;(\ )_s\;} C(B)\]
where the functor \((\ )_s\) is the associated simple complex functor and we use (\(\mathrm{VI_B}\) (2.3.2.2). Note): the details are left to the reader.

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 7.3.4

Show that for any complex \(K^{\bullet}\) of \(D^{+}(X, \mathscr{A}_X)\), the canonical arrow

\[\tau_{\leqslant 2d}\, \mathscr{H}^{\bullet}(K^{\bullet}) \longrightarrow \mathscr{H}^{\bullet}(K^{\bullet})\]

induces a quasi-isomorphism on the associated simple complexes. Note for this that the canonical arrow

\[\varinjlim_{k \geqslant 2d} (\tau_{\leqslant k}\, \mathscr{H}^{\bullet}(K^{\bullet}))_s \longrightarrow (\mathscr{H}^{\bullet}(K^{\bullet}))_s\]

is an isomorphism, since the cohomology sheaves of a complex commute at filtering inductive limits.

7.3.5. Let \(R^{(1)}f_! : D(X, \mathscr{A}_X) \longrightarrow D(Y, \mathscr{A}_Y)\) be the functor defined by (7.3.1.2) thanks to 7.2.3. By virtue of 7.2.4 we can calculate it directly from (7.3.1.1) and we can verify that it "does not depend" on the chosen pseudo-compactification. It would then be necessary to verify statements 7.1.3, 7.1.4 and 7.1.6 by removing the exponents \(+\), which the editor did not have the courage to do.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 7.3.6

The functor \(Rf_! : D(X, \mathscr{A}_X) \longrightarrow D(Y, \mathscr{A}_Y)\) built in 7.3.5 is still called direct image functor with proper supports.

7.3.7. We can then show that the functor \(Rf_!\) commutes at base changes and that the statements (XVII (5.2.9) and (5.2.10)) remain true for any morphism of \(\mathrm{Sch}_{!R}\), which justifies the constructions that we have just made.
